author_bio: Abydenus is a Hellenistic historian and chronographer, known from fragments preserved in later sources such as Eusebius. He wrote on Assyrian and Babylonian history.
author_notes: Often mentioned in the context of Near Eastern historiography; some doubts about whether he is a distinct figure or a pseudonym.

author_bio: Acacius is a name attached to fragments of a commentary on the Epistle to the Romans preserved in Greek catenae. The author may be Acacius of Caesarea or another exegete of the period.
author_notes: Identification uncertain; several figures named Acacius are known from church history.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis)

author_bio: Acesander is a shadowy figure known only from fragments preserved in papyri, including a citation in P. Oxy. 32.2637. His identity and dates are unknown, and the works attributed to him may derive from a larger historical or geographical compilation.
author_notes: The assigned fragments are brief and lack firm attribution; the author may be a pseudoepigraphic name.

author_bio: Achaeus of Eretria was a Greek tragedian, known from fragments and ancient testimonia.
author_notes: Identified as Achaeus of Eretria, a known dramatic poet.

author_bio: Achilles Tatius of Alexandria was a late antique Greek scholar and astronomer. He is known for his 'Isagoga excerpta', an introduction to the Phaenomena of Aratus.
author_notes: Disambiguated by 'Astron.' to distinguish from the novelist of the same name. Attribution is based on manuscript tradition.

author_bio: Achilles Tatius of Alexandria was a Greek novelist of the 2nd century CE, author of the romance Leucippe and Clitophon. His work is a key example of the ancient Greek novel, marked by rhetorical sophistication and erotic themes.
author_notes: The label 'Scr. Erot.' disambiguates from the astronomer Achilles Tatius. URN identifier used.

author_bio: A collection of texts known as the Acts of the Alexandrians, preserving accounts of disputes and trials involving Alexandrian Greeks under Roman rule. The corpus exists as a named work occupying the author slot.
author_notes: The label 'Acta Alexandrinorum' refers to a corpus of fragmentary papyri, not a personal author. Used as a work title.

author_bio: Acta Barnabae is a late antique apocryphal text recounting the missionary journeys and martyrdom of Barnabas, the companion of Paul. The work is anonymous and extant in a single manuscript tradition; the title serves as the author heading.
author_notes: The heading 'Acta Barnabae' is a modern or manuscript-derived title for an anonymous Christian text. No historical author is known. Agent kind set to work_title.

author_bio: A Greek account of the trial and martyrdom of the Christian apologist Apollonius, dating to the early third century CE. The text, transmitted under the title 'Acta et martyrium Apollonii', is a key witness to early Christian hagiography and legal proceedings in the Roman world.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the work itself; no individual author is identified.

author_bio: The Acta Eupli is a martyrdom account of the deacon Euplus, who was executed under Diocletian. The narrative is hagiographic and the author slot is taken by the title.
author_notes: The TLG entry treats the anonymous acta as a textual unit. The heading is the title of the work, not an author.

author_bio: An apocryphal Acts of John, composed in Greek in the early Christian period. The work is anonymous, and the author slot is filled by the title.
author_notes: Standard catalog entry for an anonymous early Christian text; the label 'Acta Joannis' represents the work itself.

author_bio: Acta Justini et septem sodalium are Christian martyr acts recounting the trial and execution of Justin Martyr along with seven companions. Multiple recensions exist, reflecting hagiographical development. The texts are anonymous and transmitted under this conventional title.
author_notes: This entry is a title-based author heading in the corpus. The work is anonymous and the heading reflects that.
representative_titles: Acta Justini et septem sodalium (recensio C) | Acta Justini et septem sodalium (recensio B) | Acta Justini et septem sodalium (recensio A)

author_bio: The Acts of Paul is an early Christian apocryphal text, traditionally dated to the 2nd century CE, detailing the missionary travels and miracles of the Apostle Paul. The text circulated in various recensions and includes the well-known Acts of Paul and Thecla.
author_notes: The title occupies the author slot because the work is anonymous; multiple manuscripts and versions are extant.
representative_titles: Acta Pauli et Theclae | Acta Pauli | Martyrium Pauli | Pauli et Corinthiorum epistulae (P. Bodmer 10) | Acta Pauli et Theclae (partis finalis recensio e codice G)

author_bio: The Acts of Peter is an early Christian apocryphal text recounting the deeds and martyrdom of the apostle Peter. It survives in Greek fragments and Latin translations.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the text, as the work is anonymous. The text is known from various manuscript traditions.

author_bio: An apocryphal Christian text recounting the trial and martyrdom of Phileas, bishop of Thmuis. Survives in a single papyrus codex (P. Bodmer 20) and is cited as a work-title author in the TLG corpus.
author_notes: The name designates the text itself, not a person; classified as work title.

author_bio: An early Christian apocryphal Acts describing the missionary travels and martyrdom of the apostle Philip. The text survives in Greek in several recensions and epitomes.
author_notes: Author field occupied by the title of the text; it is an anonymous apocryphal work.
representative_titles: Acta Philippi | Acta Philippi (recensio) | Acta Philippi (epitome)

author_bio: An early Christian martyr act recounting the trial and execution of a group of Christians from Scilli in North Africa under the Emperor Marcus Aurelius. This is one of the earliest surviving Latin martyr accounts and provides important evidence for the spread of Christianity in Roman Africa.
author_notes: The 'author' field is occupied by the title itself because the text is anonymous; no personal author is identified. The account is a documentary record preserved in Greek transmission as well as Latin. Author_agent_kind is set to 'work_title' accordingly.
representative_titles: Acta Scillitanorum martyrum sive Passio Sperati et sociorum

author_bio: The Acts of Thomas is an early Christian apocryphal text narrating the missionary journeys and miracles of the apostle Judas Thomas in India. It survives in several Greek recensions with Syriac and other versions.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work itself; it is not attributed to a named person. The entry represents a corpus of manuscripts under this title.
representative_titles: Acta Thomae | Acta Thomae (recensio) | Carmen animae (De margarita) (cod. Rom. vallicellanus B 35) | Carmen animae (De margarita) (paraphrasis Nicetae) | Actorum Thomae consummatio

author_bio: The Acts of Xanthippe and Polyxena is an apocryphal Christian text recounting the adventures and conversions of two noblewomen, associated with the apostle Paul. The work is attributed in the source to a certain 'Onesimus', but the traditional indexing uses the title as the author slot.
author_notes: Source (TLG 2248) lists one work (9,652 words) with the note 'sub auctore Onesimo'. The author slot is occupied by the title of the work itself; the actual author ('Onesimus') is pseudonymous or legendary. Classification as 'work_title' reflects this convention.
representative_titles: Acta Xanthippae et Polyxenae (sub auctore Onesimo)

author_bio: Acusilaus of Argos was an early Greek mythographer and logographer of the 6th century BCE. His 'Genealogies' systematized Greek myths, drawing on Hesiod and oral traditions. His works survive only in fragments and testimonies, but were influential for later historians.
author_notes: Acusilaus is often cited alongside Hecataeus of Miletus. The fragments are preserved in later scholia and compilations.

author_bio: Adamantius Judaeus was a Jewish physician and physiognomist active in Alexandria. His contributions include a work on physiognomics, a treatise on winds, and an epitome. His writings blend Hellenistic medical science with Jewish cultural elements.
author_notes: The label 'Judaeus Med.' indicates Jewish medical writer; three works survive, suggesting a more significant corpus than many fragmentary authors.

author_cultural_context: Early Christian theology; anti-Marcionite dialogue tradition
author_bio: Adamantius is the purported author of the Greek dialogue 'De recta in deum fide', a work long misattributed to Origen Adamantius. The text is a 4th-century anti-Marcionite and anti-Gnostic theological dialogue. The authorial label 'theol.' indicates a tradition that distinguishes this figure from Origen. The work has been transmitted under this name, representing an active Christian intellectual milieu in late antique Anatolia.
author_notes: Source label 'theol.' differentiates from Origen; the dialogue's authorship remains uncertain but is traditionally assigned to a distinct 'Adamantius' in theological literature.
representative_titles: De recta in deum fide (olim sub auctore Origene Adamantio)

author_cultural_context: Anonymous Greek papyrus fragments, multiple genres
author_bio: Collection of anonymous Greek papyrus fragments, including hexameter poems, epigrams, elegiac verses, and miscellanea of uncertain authorship.
author_notes: Explicitly anonymous: 'Adespota' means 'without a master'; works of unknown authorship grouped under this heading.

author_bio: Adrianus was a Greek rhetorician of the 1st-2nd centuries CE, known for his declamation. He is possibly identical with the sophist Adrianus of Tyre, though the attribution of the surviving declamation remains uncertain.
author_notes: The name is common and identification with known sophists is plausible but not certain.

author_bio: Legendary figure to whom a lost epic poem (the *Aegimius*) was sometimes attributed; possibly a Dorian hero or early poet.
author_notes: Likely a pseudepigraphic name attached to an epic work; historicity uncertain.

author_bio: Claudius Aelianus (Aelian) was a Roman‑era Greek author and rhetorician active during the Severan period. He is best known for his miscellanies 'On the Nature of Animals' and 'Various History', which compile curious anecdotes about natural history, moral exempla, and historical snippets. His work is a major source for ancient zoological lore and for the Greek intellectual world of the Second Sophistic.
author_notes: Well‑attested historical figure with multiple surviving works and extensive manuscript tradition. Date range well‑established from historical references. Prominence is high within the Greek classical and Natural History tradition.
representative_titles: De natura animalium | Varia historia | Fragmenta | Epistulae rusticae | Titulus

author_bio: Aelianus (Aelian) is the name associated with a Greek manual on military tactics (Tactica), probably dating to the Hellenistic period. The identity of the author is otherwise unknown; he may be distinct from the more famous Aelian the Sophist.
author_notes: The attribution is uncertain; the Tactica is often referred to as 'Aelian's Tactics' but the author's historicity and identity are not secure.

author_bio: Aelius Aristides was a prominent Greek rhetorician and sophist of the Second Sophistic, celebrated for his ornate orations and his polemical defense of rhetoric against Plato.
author_notes: Well-documented life and extensive corpus. The 'Ars rhetorica Sp.' is likely spurious. His works include the Hieroi Logoi, a unique autobiographical account of his illnesses and dream therapy.
representative_titles: Πρὸς Πλάτωνα ὑπὲρ τῶν τεττάρων | Πρὸς Πλάτωνα περὶ ῥητορικῆς | Παναθηναϊκός | Ars rhetorica Sp. | Περὶ τοῦ παραφθέγματος | Πρὸς Λεπτίνην ὑπὲρ ἀτελείας | Αἰγύπτιος | Πρὸς Δημοσθένη περὶ ἀτελείας

author_bio: Little-known Greek author of the Imperial period, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Very few fragments survive; almost no biographical information.

author_bio: Aelius Dionysius was a Greek grammarian and lexicographer of the Roman period, known for his Atticist lexicon Attika Onomata. This work, which survives only in fragments, was a key reference for Atticist revival in the Second Sophistic.
author_notes: His work is known through later lexicographers and epitomes.

author_bio: Aelius Herodianus was a Greek grammarian from Alexandria, author of major works on prosody, orthography, and inflection; his corpus includes many genuine and spurious attributions collectively transmitted under his name.
author_notes: The display name includes Pseudo-Herodianus notes due to the large corpus of spurious works; the historical Herodianus is well-known but many works are misattributed.
representative_titles: De prosodia catholica | Περὶ κλίσεως ὀνομάτων | Περὶ ὀρθογραφίας | Περὶ παθῶν | Περὶ Ἰλιακῆς προσῳδίας | Partitiones (= Ἐπιμερισμοί) [Sp.?] (e codd. Paris. 2543 + | Περὶ ῥημάτων | Περὶ παθῶν (supplementum)

author_bio: Aelius Theon was a Greek rhetorician and author of the Progymnasmata, a textbook of elementary rhetorical exercises that exerted strong influence on later Byzantine and Renaissance education.
author_notes: Theon (Theon) is the standard name; Aelius is the Roman nomen. Sometimes confused with other Theons.

author_bio: A Christian Neoplatonist rhetorician from Gaza, known for a theological dialogue on the immortality of the soul and resurrection, and for a collection of letters attesting to late antique intellectual culture.
author_notes: Standard TLG identifier; author is historical but not widely known outside patristic and philosophical circles.
representative_titles: Theophrastus sive de animarum immortalitate et corporum resurrectione dialogus | Epistulae

author_bio: Aeneas Tacticus was an early Hellenistic military writer known for his treatise on siegecraft, the 'Poliorcetica'. He is considered the earliest surviving author of a Greek tactical manual.
author_notes: The source identifier is stable; the single work is well-attested and of moderate length.

author_bio: Aeschines of Athens was a prominent Attic orator and rival of Demosthenes. Three of his speeches survive, along with spurious letters, providing insight into fourth-century Athenian political and legal life.
author_notes: A major canonical orator; the TLG source ID uses URN format.
representative_titles: In Ctesiphontem | In Timarchum | De falsa legatione | Epistulae Sp.

author_bio: A follower of Socrates and writer of Socratic dialogues, whose works survive only in fragments; distinct from the orator Aeschines.
author_notes: Historically attested figure; fragments are studied for their testimony on Socratic philosophy.

author_bio: Aeschrion was a Hellenistic Greek poet, known for epigrammatic and fragmentary works. His surviving output is minimal, but he is recognized within the Greek epigram tradition.
author_notes: The identification is based on fragmentary evidence; the name is poorly attested in literary sources.

author_bio: Founder of Greek tragedy, known for his dramatic innovations and surviving plays such as the *Oresteia* and *Persae*.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; corpus includes tragedies and fragments from lost plays.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Agamemnon | Prometheus vinctus | Choephoroe | Eumenides | Persae | Septem contra Thebas | Supplices

author_bio: Aeschylus Alexandrinus is a little-known Hellenistic poet, probably from Alexandria, of whom only a fragment and a title survive. His relationship to other writers named Aeschylus is unclear.
author_notes: The epithet 'Alexandrinus' distinguishes him from the famous tragedian; evidence is minimal.

author_bio: Aesop is the legendary fabulist credited with a corpus of beast fables that became foundational for Greek and later Western moral storytelling.
author_notes: Much of the biographical tradition is legendary. The fable collection under his name is a pseudepigraphic corpus accumulated over centuries.
representative_titles: Fabulae | Fabulae (dodecasyllabi) | Fabulae Syntipae philosophi | Proverbia | Fabulae Aphthonii rhetoris | Fabulae Dosithei | Sententiae | Fabulae Libanii

author_bio: A lost epic poem of the Epic Cycle, attributed to Arctinus of Miletus in ancient sources. It narrated events following the Iliad, including the death of Achilles.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the lost epic. Attribution to Arctinus is traditional but not certain.

author_bio: Aethlius is known only from fragments and may be a shadowy figure from the Greek historiographical or paradoxographical tradition. The name appears in a few citations; his identity and historical existence are very uncertain.
author_notes: Very fragmentary evidence from indirect citations. Insufficient to determine historicity; status marked as uncertain.

author_bio: Aëtius is a name given to a hypothetical Greek doxographer, whose lost work on the opinions of natural philosophers is reconstructed from later quotations, chiefly in Stobaeus, Theodoret, and Nemesius. The figure is a scholarly construct used to explain shared source material.
author_notes: The identification of Aëtius as a real person is uncertain; the name is a modern label for a reconstructed source anthology. The corpus consists of two sets of fragments preserved in Stobaeus and in Theodoret/Nemesius, sometimes called the Aëtian Placita.
representative_titles: De placitis reliquiae (Stobaei excerpta) | De placitis reliquiae (Theodoreti et Nemesii excerpta)

author_bio: Aëtius of Amida was a prominent Byzantine physician whose encyclopedic work 'Iatricorum' compiled medical knowledge from earlier Greek and Roman sources.
author_notes: Extensive extant corpus; key figure in the transmission of ancient medical lore.
representative_titles: Iatricorum liber viii | Iatricorum liber xvi | Iatricorum liber vii | Iatricorum liber ix | Iatricorum liber vi | Iatricorum liber i | Iatricorum liber v | Iatricorum liber ii

author_cultural_context: Fragmentary Greek author; possibly a grammarian or historian known only from a single quoted fragment.
author_bio: Agaclytus is known only from a single fragment, transmitted via later anthologies. His original context and genre are obscure.
author_notes: Only 207 words survive; the source label indicates fragmentum; no further biographical detail.

author_bio: Agamestor is known only from a single fragment, possibly from a lost epic or genealogical poem. The author's historicity cannot be confirmed.
author_notes: Single fragment of uncertain provenance.

author_bio: Agatharchides was a Greek historian and geographer active at Alexandria in the 2nd century BCE. He wrote a work on the Erythraean Sea, now surviving only in excerpts.
author_notes: The disambiguation '(Geogr.)' distinguishes him from other Agatharchides. Attested by later authors (e.g., Diodorus Siculus, Photius).
representative_titles: De mari Erythraeo (excerpta) | Fragmenta | Testimonia | Fragmenta sedis incertae

author_bio: Greek historian and geographer active in Alexandria during the 2nd century BCE. Known for his work on the Red Sea and fragments preserved by later authors.
author_notes: The label '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Agatharchides from other homonymous figures. The sole fragment is transmitted indirectly.

author_bio: Agathemerus is a little-known Greek geographer, likely active in the late Roman or early Byzantine period. His 'Geographiae informatio' (Hypotyposis geographiae) is a concise compendium of geography known from a single manuscript.
author_notes: Very little is known of Agathemerus beyond his work; the name is attested only in the manuscript tradition.

author_cultural_context: Greek historian, poet, and lawyer (scholasticus); author of the 'Histories' covering the reign of Justinian.
author_bio: Agathias was a Byzantine historian and poet. His 'Histories' (in five books) continue the work of Procopius and describe the wars and events of the mid-6th century CE.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; main work is the 'Histories' (61k+ words); also known for his poetry and the Cycle of Agathias.

author_bio: Agathocles is a name attached to a small number of Greek fragments; the identity and period of this author remain uncertain.
author_notes: Attestation is minimal; the fragments may belong to different homonymous authors.

author_bio: Agathodaemon is a legendary figure associated with the alchemical tradition of late antiquity, sometimes identified with the 'good spirit' of Alexandrian alchemy. The few surviving fragments are technical and oracular in style, attributed to his authority.
author_notes: The name is often a pseudepigraphic attribution in alchemical manuscripts; the historical existence is doubtful. The corpus includes a commentary on an oracle and an untitled alchemical fragment.
representative_titles: Ἀγαθοδαίμων εἰς τὸν χρησμόν: Ὀρφέως συναγωγὴ καὶ | Fragmentum alchemicum (sine titulo) (e cod. Venet. Marc. 299, fol. 95v)

author_bio: Agathon is a historian known only from fragments; possibly a later Hellenistic or imperial-era author confused with the homonymous tragic poet. The surviving fragments are too sparse to establish a corpus or dating.
author_notes: Label 'Hist.' distinguishes this Agathon from the tragedian; authorship is insecure.

author_bio: An Athenian tragic poet, contemporary with Euripides and celebrated for his stylistic innovations. Only fragments of his plays survive, known primarily through quotations in later authors such as Aristotle and Athenaeus.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments collected by Snell and others.

author_bio: A shadowy Greek literary figure known only from a single surviving fragment; nothing certain is known about his life or works. Possibly a poet or historian, but the fragment is too brief and uncontextualized for firm attribution.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary evidence; the name may be a corruption or a misattribution in later sources. Assigned to Greek literature based on language of the fragment.

author_bio: Agesilaus refers to the Spartan king Agesilaus II (c. 445–360 BCE), a prominent military and political figure, though the single fragment under this name is likely a later rhetorical exercise.
author_notes: The attribution of this fragment to Agesilaus is uncertain; possibly a later composition.

author_bio: The name (H)agias-Dercylus refers to a tradition or collaboration of two authors, possibly regarding local Boeotian histories. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: The compound name indicates joint authorship or a school attribution; the exact relationship is unknown.

author_bio: Aglaïs is a poorly attested Greek medical writer, known only from fragments. The work 'Adversus suffusiones incipientes' suggests ophthalmic interests, though no biographical details survive.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; biographical information is lacking. Included by TLG under medical corpus.

author_bio: Agl(a)osthenes is a Greek author known only from fragments. The variant spelling suggests uncertainty in the manuscript tradition, and the works attributed to him are lost except for scattered citations.
author_notes: The name is rare and the spelling is uncertain (Aglosthenes? Aglaosthenes?). Attribution is purely based on a few fragmentary references.

author_bio: Greek paradoxographer known for a collection of marvelous tales. His work survives only in fragments, and his biographical details are largely unknown.
author_notes: The name is rare and the figure is known only from a few fragments; historicity is uncertain, hence 'legendary' as a cautious classification.

author_bio: Middle Platonist philosopher, known for the Didaskalikos (handbook of Platonism) and an introduction to Plato's dialogues.
author_notes: Historicity is secure; sometimes identified as Alcinous in scholarship, but TLG uses Albinus; works survive.
representative_titles: Epitome doctrinae Platonicae sive Διδασκαλικός, | Introductio in Platonem

author_bio: A Greek comic poet, known only from fragments. The label 'Comic.' distinguishes him from the lyric poet Alcaeus. His works are cited by later writers such as Athenaeus.
author_notes: The cognomen 'Comic.' is a modern disambiguation; ancient sources provide little biographical detail.

author_bio: Lyric poet of Lesbos, contemporary of Sappho. Known for his political poems, hymns, and drinking songs. A canonical figure in the Greek lyric tradition.
author_notes: Alcaeus is a securely historical figure; the 'Lyr.' descriptor clarifies his role as a lyric poet.

author_bio: Alcibiades was an Athenian statesman and general known for his role in the Peloponnesian War and his association with Socrates. Only a single fragment of a speech is attributed to him.
author_notes: Only a single fragment survives attributed to him; doubtful authorship.

author_bio: A Greek rhetorician and sophist, pupil of Gorgias and rival of Isocrates. Known for advocating improvisation and natural talent; his surviving fragments and a few speeches show a concern with rhetorical theory and political ethics.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure in ancient sources. Fragments only.

author_bio: An obscure figure known only from a single inscribed titulus (inscription) of very brief length. Nothing else is securely known about Alcimenes; the name may belong to a historical person, but the evidence is extremely fragmentary.
author_notes: Only two works recorded, both of negligible word count. Author identification is purely based on the name in the inscription.

author_bio: Alcimus was a Greek epigrammatist from Sicily, active in the 5th century BCE, of whom only fragments survive.
author_notes: Minimal extant fragments; identification uncertain.

author_bio: Alciphron was a Greek sophist of the Imperial period, best known for his collection of fictional letters that offer vivid glimpses into the social and domestic life of classical Athens.
author_notes: Well-attested in the manuscript tradition; his letters are a key source for the Second Sophistic.

author_bio: A philosopher and medical theorist from Croton active in the early 5th century BCE. Alcmaeon wrote on physiology, sense perception, and the nature of the soul, and is associated with the Pythagorean and medical traditions.
author_notes: Well-known figure among Pre-Socratics; surviving testimonia and fragments attest to his historical reality.

author_bio: The Alcmaeonis is a lost archaic Greek epic poem focusing on the myth of Alcmaeon; surviving fragments are extremely few, and the original author is unknown.
author_notes: The name 'Alcmaeonis' is the title of the epic, not a personal author name; the agent is classified as work title because the text itself occupies the author slot in the source.

author_bio: Alcman was an early Greek lyric poet active in Sparta, famous for his choral poetry, especially partheneia (maiden songs). His works survive in papyrus fragments, showcasing a distinct dialect and rich mythological imagery.
author_notes: The traditional floruit is well-supported by ancient testimonia. His status as a historical figure is secure despite fragmentary works.

author_bio: Alexander was a Greek comic poet of the Hellenistic period, known only from fragmentary remains. His work belongs to the tradition of New Comedy.
author_notes: The identifier '(Comic.)' distinguishes this Alexander from other authors of the same name. Attribution is secure but the corpus is very limited.

author_bio: Alexander (Lyr. et Trag.) is a little-known poet associated with both lyric and tragic genres. Only fragments and epigrams survive.
author_notes: The label 'Lyr. et Trag.' indicates possible multi-genre activity; the identity is uncertain.

author_bio: Alexander of Tralles was a Greek physician active in the 6th century CE, whose medical writings, including Therapeutica and De febribus, were influential in Byzantine medicine.
author_notes: Historical physician; the abbreviation (Med.) indicates his profession.
representative_titles: Therapeutica | De febribus | De oculis libri tres | Epistula de lumbricis | Dedicatio ad Cosman

author_bio: Leading Peripatetic philosopher and commentator on Aristotle, active in the early 3rd century CE; his extensive commentaries shaped later Aristotelianism.
author_notes: Well-attested figure with substantial surviving corpus; some treatises attributed as spurious.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis metaphysica commentaria | In Aristotelis topicorum libros octo commentaria | In Aristotelis analyticorum priorum librum i commentarium | In Aristotelis meteorologicorum libros commentaria | In librum de sensu commentarium | Ἀπορίαι καὶ λύσεις [Sp.] | De anima libri mantissa (= De anima liber alter) [Sp.] | De anima

author_bio: Alexander (of Numenius or Seleucia?) was a Greek rhetorician and sophist active in the Roman period. He wrote a treatise On Figures (De figuris) and other fragments on rhetorical theory, representing the Second Sophistic tradition.
author_notes: Distinguished from other homonymous Alexanders by the label 'Rhet. et Soph.'; the exact identification with Numenius' son remains debated.

author_bio: Alexander is a name attached to fragmentary rhetorical texts, possibly a Hellenistic rhetorician known only from citations.
author_notes: The identity is ambiguous; 'Rhet.' is a modern disambiguator. The fragments and title do not securely identify a known individual.

author_bio: Alexander the Scribe (Scr. Eccl.) is a Byzantine author possibly of monastic background, associated with hagiographical and liturgical works including the 'Inventio crucis' and a 'Laudatio Barnabae'. The identification remains uncertain.
author_notes: Multiple works dubiously attributed to an 'Alexander'; some may be by different authors.
representative_titles: Inventio crucis (fort. auctore Alexandro alio) | Laudatio Barnabae apostoli | Inventio crucis (epitome prioris libelli)

author_cultural_context: Christian theologian, likely a monk or cleric
author_bio: Alexander is a little-known Christian theologian, author of a treatise against the Manichaeans. The work shows engagement with contemporary heresiological debates.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Theol.)' indicates uncertain identification; several authors named Alexander exist in this period.

author_bio: The *Epistulae Alexandri Magni* is a collection of pseudepigraphical letters attributed to Alexander the Great, preserved in Greek manuscripts and often embedded in the Alexander Romance tradition.
author_notes: No single author; the letters are a corpus of spurious epistles varying in origin and date.

author_bio: An obscure author known only from a few fragmentary references; possibly a philosopher or historian.
author_notes: Very little is known. The name appears in lexicographical sources with minimal context.

author_bio: Hellenistic philosopher associated with the Megarian school, known for logical paradoxes and dialectical arguments. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: Attested in later sources; works lost except for a brief titulus.

author_bio: Greek comic playwright active from the late Classical to early Hellenistic period, known for Middle Comedy and fragments of numerous plays.
author_notes: Well-attested figure with many fragments and titles; source ID consistent with TLG author record.

author_bio: Alexis was a Hellenistic historian, possibly from Tarentum, who wrote about Samos and Italic history. Only fragments of his works survive.
author_notes: The identifier 'Hist.' distinguishes this Alexis from the more famous comic playwright of the same name.

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to Amasis, likely a fictional or pseudepigraphic epistolary corpus. The work reflects a tradition of composing imaginary or paradigmatic letters in the Byzantine period.
author_notes: The name 'Amasis' may refer to an Egyptian pharaoh or a legendary figure; the letters are probably not authentic. Cataloged as a work title.

author_bio: Amelesagoras is a shadowy early Greek author known from a few fragments in later writers. He is sometimes linked with the Atthidographic tradition and may have written an early chronicle of Athens.
author_notes: His historicity is uncertain; the attribution may be a later construct or a pseudepigraphon.

author_bio: The title 'Amelii Epistula' (Letter of Amelius) is a short text attributed to a figure named Amelius, possibly the Neoplatonist philosopher. The author entry is the title itself.
author_notes: Source uses the work title as the author heading; likely a single surviving letter.

author_bio: Amipsias was an Athenian comic poet of the 5th century BCE, contemporary with Aristophanes. Only fragments of his plays survive, notably from the *Konnos*, in which Socrates is said to have been lampooned.
author_notes: Well-attested in ancient testimonia and fragmentary citations.

author_bio: Ammonius is a name attached to a handful of epigrams and a fragment; his identity is unclear, possibly a poet or grammarian of uncertain date.
author_notes: The 'Epigr.' label distinguishes this figure from other homonymous Ammonii; no biographical details survive.

author_bio: Ammonius Grammaticus was a Greek scholar known for treatises on synonyms and lexical propriety. His works were influential in later Byzantine lexicography.
author_notes: The source label 'Ammonius (Gramm.)' distinguishes this grammarian from other Ammonii; the number of works and word count are moderate.
representative_titles: De adfinium vocabulorum differentia (= Περὶ ὁμοίων καὶ | De impropriis (= Περὶ ἀκυρολογίας) (quoque sub nominibus

author_bio: Ammonius Hermeiou was a Neoplatonic philosopher and commentator on Aristotle, head of the Alexandrian school. His commentaries on Aristotle and Porphyry were foundational for later Byzantine and Arabic philosophy.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; extensive surviving commentaries.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis librum de interpretatione commentarius | In Porphyrii isagogen sive quinque voces | In Aristotelis categorias commentarius | In Aristotelis analytica priora Sp. | In Aristotelis analyticorum priorum librum i commentarium

author_bio: Ammonius (the Presbyter) was a Christian exegete active perhaps in Alexandria, known for catena fragments on the Gospel of John. He may be identical with Ammonius of Alexandria, a 5th/6th-century commentator, though details are scarce.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Scr. Eccl.)' indicates an ecclesiastical writer. The attribution of the catena fragments to Ammonius is conventional but not completely secure. 'Scr. Eccl.' = scriptor ecclesiasticus.

author_bio: An obscure author known only from a single fragment; nothing else is known about this figure.
author_notes: The name appears only in a single fragmentary source; historicity and identity are uncertain.

author_bio: 'Amphiarai Exilium' is a Latin title referring to a lost work about the exile of the seer Amphiaraus. The text is extant only in a single fragment, and the author is unknown; the title itself occupies the author slot in the catalog.
author_notes: The entry 'Amphiarai Exilium' is a work title used as a placeholder author. No real author is associated with this fragment.

author_bio: Greek historian and rhetorician, known only from a single fragment preserved in later sources.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Amphicrates is a very fragmentary author; little is known about his life or works.

author_bio: Amphilochius of Iconium was a bishop, theologian, and orator, a contemporary of Basil the Great and Gregory of Nazianzus. He authored homilies, theological works, and the influential Iambi ad Seleucum, a didactic poem. His writings address Christological and ascetic themes.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; multiple works survive. The corpus includes authentic and spurious texts. Works are transmitted in Greek.
representative_titles: Contra haereticos | In mulierem peccatricem (orat. 4) | In illud: Pater si possibile est (orat. 6) | Fragmenta | In occursum domini (orat. 2) | In Zacchaeum (orat. 8) | Oratio in mesopentecosten Sp. | Iambi ad Seleucum

author_bio: Amphis was a poet of Middle Comedy active in Athens. Only fragments of his plays survive, noted for mythological and social themes.
author_notes: Fragments preserved chiefly by Athenaeus. Dates approximate.

author_bio: Amyntas is a shadowy figure known from a single literary papyrus fragment (P. Oxy. 4.662). The work may belong to the Hellenistic poetic tradition.
author_notes: The identification as an author is tentative; the fragment may be part of a larger work by an unknown poet. The name could be a character or a poet.

author_bio: The 'Letters of Anacharsis' are a pseudepigraphic collection of fictional letters attributed to the legendary Scythian sage Anacharsis. They belong to the tradition of Cynic epistolary fiction and are of uncertain date and authorship.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of a corpus, not by a person. The letters are rhetorical exercises or popular fiction, not genuine correspondence.

author_bio: A shadowy later poet composing elegiac verse in the style of Anacreon of Teos. Known only through a single fragment, the author is likely a pseudonymous imitator active in the post-Classical period.
author_notes: The label '(Junior Eleg.)' indicates a later imitator with uncertain identity; classified as pseudonymous person.

author_region: Teos, Ionia; court of Polycrates at Samos; Athens
author_bio: Anacreon was a celebrated lyric poet of the Archaic period, known for his songs on love, wine, and sympotic themes; his corpus survives in fragments and later imitations.
author_notes: The qualifier '(Lyr.)' distinguishes the lyric poet from the homonymous epigrammatist of the Palatine Anthology; only fragmenta and epigrammata are transmitted.

author_bio: A collection of late Hellenistic and Imperial Greek poems composed in the style and meter of Anacreon, transmitted under his name but mostly the work of later imitators.
author_notes: The corpus ascribes authorship to Anacreon, but the poems are now recognized as pseudepigrapha of diverse dates. The entry represents the collection itself, not a historical person.

author_bio: An early Greek iambic poet known from a few fragments, associated with the tradition of Hipponax and iambic satire.
author_notes: Traditionally named as a poet, but extant fragments are minimal. Historicity is uncertain; may be a legendary or pseudonymous figure.

author_bio: Anatolius of Alexandria, a bishop and scholar, was renowned for his work on arithmetic and theology, notably 'On the Decad and the Numbers Within It'.
author_notes: Distinct from other figures named Anatolius; this treatise is a key source for Neopythagorean number theory.
representative_titles: Περὶ δεκάδος καὶ τῶν ἐντὸς αὐτῆς ἀριθμῶν

author_bio: Anaxagoras of Clazomenae was a Presocratic philosopher who introduced Nous (Mind) as a cosmogonic principle. He taught in Athens and influenced Pericles, Euripides, and later philosophy.
author_notes: TLG collects testimonia and fragments. The biographical tradition is well-established.

author_bio: Anaxandridas was a Greek comic poet of the Middle Comedy period, active in Sicily. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Fragments attest historical existence; limited biographical information.

author_bio: Anaxandrides was a Greek comic poet of the Middle Comedy period, active in Athens during the 4th century BCE. Known for his social and mythological burlesques, he won multiple victories at dramatic festivals. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Fragments are preserved primarily via Athenaeus; limited biographical data. The corpus is small but significant for Middle Comedy studies.

author_bio: A Greek philosopher of the Democritean school, a companion of Alexander the Great, known for his ethical stance of apatheia; his teachings survive only in testimonia and a few fragments.
author_notes: Historical figure but limited authentic fragmentary material; often discussed in later doxography.

author_bio: Anaxicrates is known only from a single fragment, possibly a historian or poet. His identity and dates are not securely established.
author_notes: Only one extant fragment (171 words); insufficient evidence to determine historicity or context. Agent kind assigned as person tentatively.

author_bio: Anaxilas was a comic poet of the late Classical period, known from fragmentary remains. His work belongs to the tradition of Middle Comedy.
author_notes: Fragmentary preservation; biographical details uncertain.

author_bio: Anaximander of Miletus was a pre-Socratic philosopher and a student of Thales. He is credited with early cosmological theories, including the concept of the apeiron (the boundless). Only fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Well-documented by later doxographers; fragments collected in Diels-Kranz.

author_bio: Anaximenes of Lampsacus was a Greek historian and rhetorician of the 4th century BCE. He is credited with the 'Rhetorica ad Alexandrum', a manual long misattributed to Aristotle, as well as historical fragments covering the Hellenic and Persian worlds.
author_notes: The 'Hist. et Rhet.' label disambiguates from the earlier philosopher Anaximenes of Miletus. The attribution of the 'Rhetorica ad Alexandrum' is widely accepted.
representative_titles: Ars rhetorica vulgo Rhetorica ad Alexandrum | Fragmenta | Testimonia

author_bio: Greek rhetorician and historian from Lampsacus; pupil of Diogenes the Cynic and teacher of Alexander the Great. Known for rhetorical and historical works, many lost.
author_notes: Distinguished from the Presocratic philosopher Anaximenes (Milesian); extant under testimonia and fragmenta.

author_bio: This entry designates a collection of letters transmitted under the name of Anaximenes of Miletus, a rhetorician and historian. The letters are likely later rhetorical exercises or forgeries, not authentic correspondence.
author_notes: The author slot contains the title 'Anaximenis Milesii Epistulae' (Letters of Anaximenes of Miletus), indicating a corpus transmitted under his name. The origin and authenticity are uncertain.

author_bio: Anaxion is known only from a single inscribed title (Titulus) of two words, offering no biographical information.
author_notes: The name may be a variant of Anaxion or Anaxianus; the source provides no context for dating or genre.

author_bio: Anaxippus was a Hellenistic comic poet of the New Comedy tradition, active in Athens. Only fragments of his plays survive, known from later citations and papyri, illustrating the stock characters and domestic plots typical of the genre.
author_notes: The attribution is based on fragmentary evidence from comic poets. His name appears in the list of New Comedy poets.

author_bio: Andocides was an Athenian orator and politician, one of the ten Attic orators. He was involved in the mutilation of the Herms and profanation of the Mysteries in 415 BC, leading to exile. His surviving speeches—'On the Mysteries', 'On the Peace', 'On His Return', and 'Against Alcibiades' (the latter of disputed authenticity)—provide important insight into late 5th-century Athenian politics and legal practice.
author_notes: The orations are transmitted under his name, but 'Against Alcibiades' has at times been considered spurious or of later composition. Andocides is a canonical figure in the Attic orator tradition.
representative_titles: De mysteriis | In Alcibiadem Sp. | De pace | De reditu suo | Fragmenta

author_bio: A fragmentary Greek author, possibly a physician or scholar named Andreas. Only a single short fragment survives, making identification difficult.
author_notes: Very fragmentary (75 words). Multiple persons named Andreas exist in antiquity; the specific identity is uncertain.

author_region: unknown (possibly Rhodes or a Hellenistic center)
author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek literature (possible historian or peripatetic)
author_bio: Andriscus is a shadowy figure known only from fragments. He may have been a historian or philosophical author; the surviving fragments do not allow secure characterization.
author_notes: The 578 words are fragmentary; the author may be the same Andriscus mentioned by Diogenes Laertius, but the identification is tentative.

author_bio: Androetas is a name appearing in a single fragment of uncertain provenance. Nothing is known of this author, whose historicity remains unresolved.
author_notes: Label 'Androetas' is obscure; only a single fragment of 94 words survives. The name may be a corruption or a very rare attestation. Historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Greek physician and poet, known for his elegiac poem on theriac (the *Theriaca*), preserved in fragmentary form.
author_notes: Identity uncertain; possibly Andromachus the Elder, physician to Nero. Fragmentary survival limits biographical detail.

author_bio: Andron Alexandrinus was a Greek author from Alexandria, known only from a single fragment preserved in later sources, possibly a grammatical or historical work.
author_notes: Only one work (Fragmentum) is attested; his identity and exact period are uncertain. Source ID tlg2172.

author_bio: Andron Historicus was a Hellenistic historian, known only from fragments quoted by later authors.
author_notes: The epithet 'Historicus' distinguishes him from other homonyms; often confused with Andron of Teos.

author_bio: A Hellenistic geographer known from fragments. The epithet 'Geogr.' distinguishes him from other authors named Andron.
author_notes: The fragmentary nature of the evidence makes precise dating and identification difficult; the geographer may be identical with Andron of Halicarnassus or another figure.

author_bio: A Hellenistic historian, possibly from Alexandria, known from citations and fragments of his historical work.
author_notes: Distinguished from other figures named Andron by the '(Hist.)' label. Fragments survive in later compilers.

author_bio: Andronicus of Rhodes was a Peripatetic philosopher who organized and edited the works of Aristotle and Theophrastus, producing the first complete edition of the Aristotelian corpus. He is also known for a treatise on the passions, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Author_id tlg1124 is securely identified with the historical Andronicus of Rhodes. The two fragmentary works show low word count but the figure is important in the history of Aristotelian transmission.
representative_titles: De passionibus (lib. 2) Sp. | De passionibus (lib. 1) Sp.

author_bio: Androtion was an Athenian politician and one of the local historians (Atthidographers) whose work survives only in fragments. He wrote an Atthis, a local history of Attica, frequently cited by later authors such as Aristotle and Plutarch.
author_notes: Androtion is known primarily through fragments and citations. The name is attested as a historical figure active in 4th-century BCE Athens.

author_bio: Byzantine princess and historian, daughter of Emperor Alexios I Komnenos. She authored the 'Alexias', an epic account of her father's reign.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with secure authorship of the Alexias. The name 'Comnena' is a Latinized form of 'Komnene'.

author_bio: An anonymous treatise on music preserved in a manuscript edited by Bellermann. The work compiles musical theory from various sources, likely of late antique or Byzantine origin.
author_notes: The label 'Anonyma' is used as a generic author heading for the anonymous treatise; the works are attributed to no known author.

author_bio: Anonymous aulodic text preserved on papyrus (P.Oxy. 15.1795), likely a song for the double flute. Author unknown.
author_notes: The heading Anonymi Aulodia is a conventional label; the composition is unattributed.

author_bio: An anonymous commentary on Plato's Theaetetos, preserved on papyrus (P. Berol. inv. 9782). Possibly authored by Eudorus of Alexandria. It is a key witness for ancient Platonic exegesis.
author_notes: The heading is a descriptive label for an anonymous work; the author_agent_kind is set to 'work_title' because the entry refers to a specific textual corpus rather than a person.
representative_titles: Commentarius in Platonis Theaetetum (fort. auctore Eudoro Alexandrino) (P. Berol. inv. 9782)

author_bio: This is a cult hymn to the Kuretes from a late classical or Hellenistic period ritual context. The text exists only in a single fragmentary inscription.
author_notes: The author designation 'Anonymi Curetum Hymnus' is a conventional label for the anonymous hymn text. Not associated with any known named author.

author_bio: Anonymous Byzantine dialogue on astrology, sometimes attributed to a certain Hermippus or possibly John Catrarios. The work is a pseudo-Hermippean text.
author_notes: Stored under the anonymi label; authorship uncertain, possibly ascription to John Catrarios. The author slot is a work title.
representative_titles: De astrologia dialogus (= Hermippus) (fort. auctore Joanne Catrario),

author_bio: An anonymous Byzantine grammatical treatise on linguistic errors (barbarisms and solecisms). The author is unknown, and the work survives in multiple recensions.
author_notes: The descriptor 'Anonymi' explicitly indicates anonymity, classified as anonymous_label.

author_bio: Anonymi De Terrae Motibus is a conventional heading for an anonymous Greek treatise on earthquakes, surviving in a single manuscript and attributed to no known author.
author_notes: The label indicates a work transmitted anonymously; 'Anonymi' is a cataloging convention.

author_bio: The 'Anonymi Exegesis in Hesiodi Theogoniam' is an anonymous Byzantine commentary on Hesiod's Theogony. It survives in manuscript and represents a medieval scholarly tradition of interpreting classical mythology.
author_notes: The title is used as an author heading because the work is anonymous and the text itself occupies the author slot in the catalog.

author_bio: An anonymous Greek geographical work explaining the principles of understanding geography on a sphere. The author is unknown.
author_notes: Explicitly labeled 'Anonymi', indicating no known author.
representative_titles: Anonymi summaria ratio geographiae in sphaera intelligendae

author_bio: A Byzantine geographical compendium transmitted anonymously; the work itself occupies the author field in the catalog.
author_notes: This is a work title occupying the author slot; the actual author is unknown.

author_bio: A grouping of anonymous grammarians whose fragments survive on papyrus and in medieval manuscripts. Works include supplements to Dionysius Thrax and various grammatical fragments.
author_notes: This entry assembles multiple anonymous grammatical texts under a single label; no single author exists. The heading is a modern catalog convention.

author_bio: A collective label for anonymous historical texts, encompassing a range of fragments on diverse topics such as Alexander, the Parthian war, and Egyptian geography. The corpus represents the work of unknown authors.
author_notes: The label 'Anonymi Historici' is a modern collection of anonymous fragments. No single author exists; use anonymous label. Works span various periods and regions.

author_bio: A hexameter hymn inscribed on stone from Crete, addressed to the Idaean Dactyls, mythological figures associated with Mt. Ida. The authorship is unknown; the text is conventionally referred to by its title.
author_notes: The author entry is a placeholder for the inscribed text itself. The 'Anonymi' in the label indicates unknown authorship, but the entry is treated as a work title in the author index.

author_bio: Anonymous Byzantine prose commentaries on the progymnasmata of Aphthonius, transmitted under varying or no named authorship.
author_notes: The label 'Anonymi In Aphthonium' indicates anonymous authorship; some parts have been misattributed to John Doxapatres.
representative_titles: Incerti auctoris prolegomena in progymnasmata (olim sub auctore Joanne Doxapatre) | Prolegomena in progymnasmata

author_bio: An anonymous collection of commentaries and paraphrases on Aristotle's Rhetoric, compiled from various scholia and independent treatises.
author_notes: The heading designates a corpus of anonymous exegetical material, not a single author.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis artem rhetoricam commentarium | Fragmentum commentarii in Aristotelis rhetorica | Fragmentum paraphrasis in Aristotelis rhetorica

author_bio: A collection of anonymous Greek commentaries on Aristotle's Categories, preserved under the label 'Anonymi' to indicate unknown authorship.
author_notes: Author_agent_kind=anonymous_label because the display name explicitly uses 'Anonymi' for anonymous authorship.

author_bio: A set of anonymous commentaries and paraphrases on Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics, including a pseudepigraphon once attributed to Heliodorus, but actually compiled by Constantine Palaeocappa. The works are important for the reception of Aristotelian ethics.
author_notes: The heading uses 'Anonymi' as a collective label for a group of commentaries. The author_agent_kind is set to 'anonymous_label' because it is an explicit anonymous grouping.

author_canonical_name: Anonymus In Aristotelis Analytica Posteriora II
author_bio: An anonymous ancient or Byzantine commentary on the second book of Aristotle's Posterior Analytics, transmitted under this descriptive title.
author_notes: The label explicitly identifies the agent as 'Anonymi' (anonymous), indicating no known author.
representative_titles: Anonymi in analyticorum posteriorum librum alterum commentarium

author_display_name: Anonymi In Aristotelis Librum De Interpretatione
author_canonical_name: Anonymous, In Aristotelis Librum De Interpretatione
author_bio: An anonymous commentary on Aristotle's De Interpretatione preserved in a single Paris manuscript (Par. gr. 2064). The text may have been composed by an Alexandrian or Constantinopolitan scholar between the 5th and 7th centuries.
author_notes: The heading 'Anonymi In Aristotelis Librum De Interpretatione' is a modern cataloguing label for an anonymous work. The author is unknown.

author_canonical_name: Anonymi In Aristotelis Librum Primum Analyticorum Posteriorum Commentarium
author_bio: An anonymous commentary on Aristotle's Posterior Analytics, attributed to no known author. The work is typical of the Late Antique or Byzantine scholastic tradition, reflecting continuous study of Aristotelian logic.
author_notes: The heading 'Anonymi' explicitly indicates an anonymous author. This is classified as 'anonymous_label' because the author slot is occupied by a generic anonymous descriptor, not a specific individual or collective.
representative_titles: In primum librum analyticorum posteriorum commentarium

author_bio: Anonymous commentators on Aristotle's Sophistici Elenchi, represented by a series of paraphrases and scholia produced over a long period. Their identities are unknown.

author_bio: A corpus of anonymous commentaries on Hermogenes' rhetorical treatises, compiled by multiple unknown scholiasts; the label reflects collective or anonymized scholarly production.
author_notes: The author heading 'Anonymi In Hermogenem' is a catalog label for anonymous commentary material; some works may be attributable to Marcellinus or others.

author_bio: This is a set of anonymous scholia on Oppian's Halieutica, preserved in a single manuscript. The commentary is a product of the late antique scholarly tradition.
author_notes: The heading 'Anonymi In Oppiani Opera' refers to the collective commentary tradition, not a named author. The text is a scholiastic work.
representative_titles: In Oppiani halieutica exegesis (e cod. Paris. gr. 2735)

author_bio: An anonymous collection of rhetorical glosses. The title 'Anonymi Lexeis Rhetoricae' indicates an untitled work by an unknown author.
author_notes: The work is transmitted without author attribution; the label 'Anonymi' is conventional.

author_bio: Anonymi Medici is a modern cataloging label for a group of anonymous medical texts in Greek. These works, covering diagnosis, diet, fevers, and human anatomy, are part of the corpus of late antique and Byzantine medical literature, drawing on earlier Hippocratic and Galenic sources.
author_notes: The label explicitly indicates anonymity. The 24 works are attributed for convenience to this collective entry, as individual authors are unknown.

author_bio: A group of anonymous Greek paradoxographers who compiled collections of marvels and unusual phenomena. Their works are transmitted under various titles, including De incredibilibus and Tractatus de mulieribus.
author_notes: The label anonymi paradoxographi is a modern designation for unknown authors of paradoxographical texts; multiple anonymous compilations are involved.

author_bio: The unknown author of the spurious Book 15 of Euclid's Elements, which is a late addition to the Euclidean corpus, likely compiled by a Byzantine commentator or editor.
author_notes: The source label explicitly lists 'Anonymous' as the author; the work is a later interpolation, not by Euclid.
representative_titles: Euclidis elementorum qui fertur liber xv

author_display_name: Anonymus Ad Avircium Marcellum Contra Cataphrygas
author_canonical_name: Anonymus Ad Avircium Marcellum Contra Cataphrygas
author_bio: An anonymous Christian treatise against the Cataphrygian (Montanist) heresy, addressed to Avircius Marcellus. Author unknown.
author_notes: Conventional scholarly designation; author not identified.

author_bio: The work known as Anonymus Alexandri comprises fragments from an unidentified Hellenistic author, possibly associated with the Library of Alexandria. The text deals with historical or paradoxographical material, but the author's name is unknown.
author_notes: The label 'Anonymus Alexandri' indicates that the author is unnamed, with provenance inferred from content.

author_bio: An anonymous Byzantine grammatical treatise (or fragment) discussing metrical principles, surviving only in a single papyrus (P. Berol. 9734r). The work is of unknown authorship, conventionally referred to by its modern Latin title.
author_notes: The author slot is a conventional anonymous label for a grammatical fragment. The source heading explicitly marks the work as anonymous.

author_bio: A set of anonymous Prolegomena to Platonic philosophy, sometimes attributed to Elias or formerly to Olympiodorus; the text is a standard introduction in the Alexandrian Neoplatonic curriculum.
author_notes: Author heading is a modern descriptor for an anonymous text; the manuscript tradition does not supply an author name; attribution to Elias or Olympiodorus is conjectural.
representative_titles: Prolegomena philosophiae Platonicae (fort. auctore Elia, olim sub auctore Olympiodoro)

author_cultural_context: Greek scientific literature in Roman Egypt
author_bio: Anonymous author of a fragmentary papyrus treatise on Egyptian plants (P. Oxy. 15.1796). The work lists and describes local flora.
author_notes: The label 'Anonymus' indicates that the author's name is unknown; the descriptor 'De Plantis Aegyptiis' is derived from the content. Fragmentary papyrus.

author_bio: An anonymous dialogue on political science, formerly attributed to Peter the Patrician, but now considered a separate, possibly 6th-century work. It discusses ideal governance and imperial administration.
author_notes: The work is anonymous; the title 'De Scientia Politica' is a modern label. The manuscript tradition has confused it with works of Peter the Patrician, but it is now treated as a distinct anonymous treatise.
representative_titles: De scientia politica dialogus (olim sub auctore Petro Patricio) (e cod. Vat. gr. 1298)

author_bio: Anonymus De Viribus Herbarum is the conventional name for the unknown author of a fragmentary Greek poem on the properties of herbs. The work survives in a single fragment.
author_notes: The label 'Anonymus De Viribus Herbarum' is a modern editorial name. No author information exists.

author_bio: The 'Anonymus Dialogus cum Judaeis' is an anonymous Byzantine Greek Christian dialogue against Judaism, formerly attributed to John of Damascus. The text, preserved in a 10th-century manuscript from Vatopedi, presents theological arguments for the truth of Christianity and reflects the polemical genre of anti-Jewish literature.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the work itself, as the text is anonymous. The former attribution to John Damascene is rejected, but the work remains important for Byzantine theological and literary studies.
representative_titles: Anonymus dialogus cum Judaeis (e cod. Vatoped. 236) (olim sub auctore Joanne Damasceno)

author_bio: An anonymous author whose fragments are preserved within the tradition associated with the historian Diodorus Siculus. The text likely derives from a lost historiographical source used by Diodorus.
author_notes: The heading 'Anonymus Diodori' indicates anonymous fragments transmitted under Diodorus's name or in his works.

author_bio: An anonymous Epicurean author whose fragments survive among the Herculaneum papyri. The texts (P. Herc. 176 and associated fragments) discuss Epicurean doctrines, possibly from a lost work by a lesser-known adherent of the school.
author_notes: Name assigned by modern editors to a body of papyrus remains. The 'Epicureus' label reflects content, not a known historical figure.
representative_titles: Fragmenta (P. Herc. 176) | Fragmenta (ad P. Herc. 176 pertinentia)

author_bio: An unnamed philosophical author preserved only through quotations in the work of Iamblichus. The fragments deal with ethical and political themes, often attributed to a Pythagorean or Sophistic milieu.
author_notes: The label 'Anonymus Iamblichi' is a modern scholarly construct for a lost author known only via Iamblichus's Protrepticus. No actual author name survives.

author_bio: Anonymus Lexicographus is a scholarly label for an anonymous Byzantine lexicographer whose fragments are preserved in quotations by Eustathius.
author_notes: Label assigned by modern editors to unidentified lexicographical fragments.

author_bio: Conventional name for the unknown author of a medical doxographical papyrus (British Museum inv. 137). The work contains excerpts from earlier medical writers and is sometimes attributed to Meno, a pupil of Aristotle.
author_notes: The source label 'Anonymus Londinensis' is a modern scholarly designation; no authorial attribution is preserved.
representative_titles: Iatrica (fort. auctore Menone) (Brit. Mus. inv. 137) | Fragmenta

author_bio: This is a Greek Manichaean text, part of the Cologne Mani Codex. It contains a tract on the birth and body of Mani, reflecting Manichaean cosmology and theology.
author_notes: The text is anonymous; the label 'Anonymus Manichaeus' is a modern convention for this section of the Manichaean codex. It is a key source for early Manichaean thought in Greek.
representative_titles: Περὶ τῆς γέννης τοῦ σώματος αὐτοῦ (= Codex Manichaicus

author_bio: Anonymus Photii refers to one or more anonymous works preserved in the Bibliotheca of Photius, the 9th-century Byzantine patriarch. The fragmentary texts are unnamed and of uncertain origin.
author_notes: Label 'Anonymus Photii' indicates an anonymous fragment preserved through Photius. The author is unknown, making this an anonymous label.

author_cultural_context: Unknown Christian author (priest) from an uncertain period; his identity has not been recovered
author_bio: An unnamed priest from the Greek Christian tradition, known only from a single brief fragment. The text likely belongs to a homiletic or exegetical context, but the author's name and exact date are lost.
author_notes: The 'Anonymus Presbyter' label is a modern cataloguing convenience. The fragment (72 words) is of uncertain origin and date, possibly from a catena or codex unicus.

author_bio: An anonymous Greek rhetorician, known only through the manuscript tradition of the Ars rhetorica (formerly attributed to Cornutus).
author_notes: The label 'Anonymus Seguerianus' is a conventional modern name for an unknown author; the work has also been spuriously attributed to Cornutus.

author_bio: Antagoras of Rhodes was a Hellenistic epic poet and courtier, known for his epigrams and a lost epic on the Argonauts. He was associated with Antigonus II Gonatas and wrote witty, satirical verses. His epigrams survive in the Greek Anthology.
author_notes: The two works attributed to him are epigram collections. The biographical context is drawn from later sources like Athenaeus.

author_bio: Antenor is a Greek author of a single fragmentary work. The name could be a pseudonym or a historical figure from the Imperial period.
author_notes: Only one fragment survives; attribution to Antenor is based on manuscript tradition. The subject matter is unclear.

author_bio: Anthemius of Tralles was a renowned architect and mathematician, best known for designing the Hagia Sophia with Isidore of Miletus. He wrote on mechanical devices and mirrors, and his Greek works on burning mirrors and paradoxes survive fragmentarily.
author_notes: Well attested as a historical figure. The mathematical fragments are carefully transmitted.
representative_titles: Περὶ παραδόξων μηχανημάτων | Fragmentum mathematicum Bobiense

author_bio: A supplementary corpus of Greek epigrams and related texts not included in the main Palatine or Planudean anthologies, drawn from diverse sources and periods.
author_notes: This is a modern editorial collection, not an ancient author; the heading functions as a textual corpus.
representative_titles: Epigrammata sepulcralia | Epigrammata demonstrativa | Oracula | Epigrammata dedicatoria | Epigrammata exhortatoria et supplicatoria | Problemata et aenigmata | Epigrammatis addenda | Epigrammata irrisoria

author_bio: Antidotus is a shadowy figure known only from a few surviving fragments; possibly a Hellenistic comic or tragic poet, but the limited evidence makes a confident assessment impossible.
author_notes: The name appears in Hellenistic fragment collections; no firm biographical details are preserved.

author_bio: Antigenes is a Greek author known from a single surviving fragment. No biographical details or literary context can be securely established due to the scarcity of evidence.
author_notes: A ghost author or minor figure; the fragment is insufficient for meaningful classification. The name may be common, making identification uncertain.

author_bio: The title 'Antigoni Epistula' functions as an author heading in the manuscript tradition. The letter is attributed to a certain Antigonus, but the actual author is unknown.
author_notes: The entry is a title occupying the author slot. The attribution to an Antigonus is uncertain.

author_bio: Antigonus, often identified as Antigonus of Carystus or a later compiler, is the author of a collection of marvels (Historiarum mirabilium collectio), a Hellenistic paradoxographical work.
author_notes: The identification with Antigonus of Carystus is traditional but uncertain; the works include fragments, an epigram, and an inscription.
representative_titles: Historiarum mirabilium collectio | Fragmenta | Epigramma | Fragmentum et titulus

author_bio: Antigonus Historicus was a Greek historian or paradoxographer, possibly of the 3rd or 2nd century BCE, known only from a single fragment. The scope of his work is uncertain but may have concerned marvels or local history.
author_notes: The label 'Historicus' distinguishes him from other Antigoni; the fragmentary nature of the evidence precludes a firm characterization.

author_bio: Antileon is known only from a single fragment and is otherwise unattested; likely a minor figure of uncertain date and origin.
author_notes: Only 45 words survive; no biographical evidence exists beyond the name.

author_bio: Antimachus of Colophon was a poet and grammarian, author of the epic Thebais and elegiac Lyde; influential yet controversial in antiquity.
author_notes: Labeled 'Eleg. et Epic.' to specify his dual poetic output; fragments survive from both works.

author_bio: Antimachus of Colophon was an epic poet and grammarian, known for his long poem 'Thebaid' and an elegy 'Lyde.' His work was influential among Hellenistic scholars.
author_notes: The 'Epic.' disambiguator identifies him as an epic poet. Only fragments survive of his major works.

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to a king Antiochus of the Seleucid dynasty, offering insights into Hellenistic diplomacy and royal correspondence.
author_notes: The heading 'Antiochi Regis Epistulae' likely designates a small corpus of letters from a Seleucid king, but the specific king is uncertain; possibly Antiochus III or another. Single work with very few words.

author_bio: Antiochus, a historian of the Hellenistic period, known from fragments.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' identifies the genre; likely identical to Antiochus of Syria.

author_bio: Antiochus is a figure known only from a few surviving fragments, possibly a philosopher active in the late Hellenistic or early Roman period. The disambiguation 'Phil.' suggests a philosophical context.
author_notes: Very little is known; fragments indicate philosophical authorship.

author_bio: Antipater of Sidon was a Greek epigrammatist of the Hellenistic period, known for his contributions to the Greek Anthology. His works include epitaphs and dedicatory poems of high literary quality.
author_notes: The name 'Antipater' is common; this entry likely refers to Antipater of Sidon, as the works are epigrams. The fragments are preserved in the Palatine Anthology.

author_bio: One of the most prolific poets of Middle Comedy, Antiphanes from Athens (or perhaps Cyprus) wrote numerous plays. His fragments survive in citations and papyri, preserving a vivid picture of Athenian social and literary life.
author_notes: Well-attested figure in ancient comedy; substantial fragmentary corpus (ca. 13,800 words).

author_bio: Antiphon of Rhamnus was the earliest of the ten Attic orators, a logographer and oligarchic politician. His surviving works include court speeches and the Tetralogies, rhetorical exercises on homicide cases.
author_notes: Distinct from Antiphon the Sophist; label clarifies orator.
representative_titles: De caede Herodis | De choreuta | Fragmenta | Tetralogia 1 | Tetralogia 2 | Tetralogia 3 | In novercam

author_bio: Antiphon the Sophist was a 5th-century BCE Athenian intellectual, writer, and orator (possibly distinct from Antiphon of Rhamnus). His works include rhetorical, ethical, and cosmological fragments.
author_notes: Distinguished from Antiphon Orator by '(Soph.)' in modern scholarship; identification remains debated.

author_bio: Antiphon the Tragedian was a shadowy figure of 5th-century Athens, known only from a single tragic fragment. He may be distinct from the orator Antiphon of Rhamnus, but the evidence is too sparse to be certain.
author_notes: The disambiguating label 'Trag.' distinguishes this author from the orator Antiphon; only 39 words of text survive.

author_bio: Antisthenes was a Greek philosopher and rhetorician, a disciple of Socrates and a key figure in the development of Cynicism. His works are largely lost, but fragments and a few declamations survive, showing a focus on ethics and a critique of conventional values.
author_notes: The label '(Phil. et Rhet.)' distinguishes him from other Antisthenes. His works are fragmentary; the 'Declamationes' are attributed to him with varying certainty.

author_bio: Antisthenes of Athens was a Greek philosopher and a student of Socrates, considered the founder of the Cynic school. He wrote extensively on ethics and rhetoric, but only fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Phil.)' disambiguates from other Antisthenes figures. The surviving texts are testimonia only; direct works are lost.

author_bio: This entry represents the letter (epistula) of Emperor Antoninus Pius to the Koinon of Asia, a Greek inscription recording an imperial ruling on religious privileges. It is the text of the letter, not the emperor as author.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the document; the entry is a textual edition of the epigraphic text.

author_bio: Greek mythographer known only for his Metamorphoseon synagoge, a collection of transformation myths based on earlier sources.
author_notes: Little is known of his life; the name may be Latin. His sole work survives.

author_bio: Greek hagiographer known only from the Vita Symeonis Stylitae senioris, a life of the pillar saint Symeon the Elder. Likely active in Syrian monastic circles, drawing on oral or local traditions.
author_notes: Single-work author; the vita survives in a single manuscript tradition. Identity otherwise unattested.

author_bio: Antonius Diogenes was a Greek author of the Second Sophistic, best known for the fantastic travel novel The Incredible Wonders Beyond Thule (Ta hyper Thoulen apista). This work, now fragmentary, mixed paradoxography with romance and influenced later fiction.
author_notes: Knowledge of his work derives from papyrus fragments and the summary in Photius' Bibliotheca.
representative_titles: De incredibilibus vel Herpyllis (?) (fragmentum) (P. Dubl. C3) | De incredibilibus (fragmentum) (PSI 10.1177) | De incredibilibus (fragmentum) (P. Oxy. 42.3012)

author_bio: Antyllus was a Greek physician whose works on surgery and pharmacology survive only in fragments, often quoted by Oribasius and later medical compilers.
author_notes: The identification depends on quotations; the single surviving fragment is from a treatise on clysters, and the author's floruit is only approximate.
representative_titles: Ἐκ τοῦ περὶ κλυσμῶν ὅτι διὰ τρεῖς αἰτίας

author_bio: Aphareus was an Athenian orator and tragedian, a son of the sophist Isocrates. He is known from literary references and two brief epigraphic or epigrammatic works.
author_notes: Very little survives; the two works are fragmentary or uncertain in attribution.

author_bio: Aphthonius was a Greek rhetorician and author of the influential 'Progymnasmata,' a manual of rhetorical exercises that became a standard textbook in late antiquity and the Byzantine period.
author_notes: Attested in rhetorical tradition; the single work 'Progymnasmata' is securely attributed.

author_bio: Apion was an Alexandrian grammarian and rhetorician of the 1st century CE, known for his Homeric scholarship and anti-Jewish writings. His works survive in fragments.
author_notes: Known from citations; also referenced by Josephus and Pliny.

author_bio: A Greek text titled 'Revelation of Adam', likely a pseudepigraphic or apocryphal work attributed to Adam. Its exact date and provenance are uncertain, but it belongs to the broader tradition of apocalyptic literature.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by a title, not a person. The representative work 'Νυχθήμερον' is dubiously attributed to Apollonius of Tyana; the present entry is a separate anonymous text.

author_bio: The Greek Apocalypse of Baruch (3 Baruch) is an apocryphal apocalyptic text attributed to Baruch, the scribe of Jeremiah. It describes visions of heaven and the afterlife.
author_notes: The author slot is the title of the text; the work is anonymous. The 'iii Baruch' designation distinguishes it from other Baruch apocalypses.

author_bio: The Apocalypse of Elijah is a pseudepigraphic text attributed to the prophet Elijah, surviving only in Greek fragments that witness early apocalyptic literature.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of a work; the original author is unknown. Attributed pseudonymously to Elijah.

author_bio: A Jewish apocalyptic text attributed to Ezra (Esdras), extant in Greek. It is a pseudepigraphon concerning visions and revelations, and is not associated with a historical author. The title functions as the author entry in catalogues.
author_notes: Author entry is the work title itself; no historical author exists. The text is part of the Old Testament pseudepigrapha.

author_bio: A corpus of apocryphal apocalypses attributed to John (possibly John the Divine or John the Theologian). The texts are anonymous pseudepigrapha; the author field represents the title group.
author_notes: The name designates a set of apocryphal texts, not a person; classified as work title for the corpus.
representative_titles: Apocalypsis apocrypha Joannis | Apocalypsis apocrypha Joannis (versio tertia) | Apocalypsis apocrypha Joannis (versio altera)

author_bio: The 'Apocalypse of Sedrach' is an apocryphal text, likely of Christian origin, attributed to Sedrach (a variant of Zerah or connected to Ezra). The work title serves as the author entry.
author_notes: Source uses the work title as the author heading; the text is a pseudepigraphon.

author_bio: An apocryphal apocalyptic text attributed to the prophet Zephaniah, surviving only in fragments. It blends Jewish and Christian elements and is part of the Old Testament pseudepigrapha.
author_notes: The work is pseudepigraphical; the author field is occupied by the title of the text itself.

author_bio: The Syriac Apocalypse of Baruch is a Jewish pseudepigraphon surviving in Greek fragments. It is a title that occupies the author slot in TLG, as the real author is unknown.
author_notes: This is a textual corpus, not a person; author_agent_kind=work_title per instructions.

author_bio: An apocryphal text attributed to Ezekiel, surviving only in fragments. The work is a pseudepigraphon, not the product of a named historical author, and reflects early Jewish or Christian traditions expanding on biblical figures.
author_notes: 'Apocryphon Ezechiel' is the title of a fragmentary text, not a personal name. It is classified as 'work_title' because the author slot is occupied by the textual corpus itself, not a person or collective.

author_bio: An obscure Greek author known only from a few fragments. No biographical information survives.
author_notes: TLG lists a single work; the name is rare and no external attestation is clear.

author_bio: Apollinaris of Laodicea was a 4th-century Christian bishop and theologian, known for his biblical commentaries (surviving mostly in catenae) and his controversial Christological views.
author_notes: Well-known patristic figure. The fragments in catenae are attributed to him.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in Psalmos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in Joannem (in catenis) | Fragmenta in Matthaeum (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis)

author_bio: Apollodorus of Gela was a Greek comic poet of the Middle and New Comedy periods. Only fragments of his work survive, reflecting the transition from Middle to New Comedy traditions.
author_notes: Distinguished from the homonymous Apollodorus of Athens (mythographer) by the ethnic 'Gelous'; fragments are sparse.

author_bio: Apollodorus of Seleucia was a Stoic philosopher, a student of Diogenes of Babylon, known for his ethical writings that survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Known primarily through later doxographers; works survive only as fragments and testimonia.

author_bio: Apollodorus Carystius was a playwright of Greek New Comedy, active in the early 3rd century BCE. His works survive only in fragments, but he was a source for Roman comedy adaptors such as Terence.
