author_notes: The label 'Carystius vel Apollodorus' reflects uncertainty over whether he is the same as Apollodorus of Carystus or Apollodorus of Gela; modern scholarship favors the former.

author_bio: Apollodorus is the name of at least two comic poets of New Comedy in Athens, possibly including Apollodorus of Gela and Apollodorus of Carystus. Their works survive only in fragments and titles, and are cited in ancient sources such as Athenaeus. The abbreviation 'Comic.' distinguishes them from the mythographer Apollodorus.
author_notes: The label 'Comic.' is used in the TLG to differentiate from other Apollodori. The biographical data are uncertain; there may be conflation of multiple persons.

author_bio: Apollodorus (Gramm.) was a Greek grammarian and mythographer, likely the author of the 'Bibliotheca' (Library), a compendium of Greek mythology, though the attribution is disputed. His fragments (over 13,000 words) attest to scholarly activity in the Alexandrian tradition, including commentary on Homer and the tragic poets.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Gramm.)' distinguishes this Apollodorus from the Athenian painter of the same name. The attribution of the 'Bibliotheca' to this Apollodorus is conventional but contested; the TLG fragments may include works of uncertain authorship.

author_bio: Greek historian and grammarian of Athens, pupil of Aristarchus. Wrote a Chronicle in verse; fragments survive via later citations.
author_notes: Distinguished from Apollodorus of Athens the mythographer; the historical fragments are preserved in FGrHist.

author_bio: Apollodorus (Lyr.) is a lyric poet known from a single fragment. Almost nothing is known of his life or work; he may be one of several Apollodori recorded in the Greek poetic tradition.
author_notes: The surviving fragment of 8 words is the only record; the 'Lyr.' disambiguator indicates lyric poetry. Historicity is assumed but barely attestable.

author_region: Damascus (or elsewhere in the Hellenistic East)
author_cultural_context: Military engineering under the early Roman Empire
author_bio: Apollodorus Mechanicus (often identified with Apollodorus of Damascus) was a military engineer who wrote on siegecraft (Poliorcetica). Likely active in the early Imperial period, his work is a key text in the poliorcetic tradition.
author_notes: The label 'Mech.' distinguishes this Apollodorus from other authors of the same name; the attribution to Apollodorus of Damascus is widely accepted but not absolutely certain.

author_bio: Apollodorus of Athens is the name traditionally attached to the Bibliotheca, a comprehensive compendium of Greek mythology. The attribution is uncertain; the work likely dates to the 2nd century CE, not the 2nd century BCE, and the author is sometimes called Pseudo-Apollodorus. The text remains a key source for mythological narratives.
author_notes: The heading 'Apollodorus (Myth.)' distinguishes this figure from other Apollodori (e.g., the grammarian). The Bibliotheca is widely cited but the author's identity is debated; the 'Myth.' label signals the mythological tradition.

author_bio: Apollodorus the Philosopher was a Stoic thinker, possibly of the 2nd–1st century BCE, known from sparse testimonia. He may be identical with the author of a 'Chronicle' or an ethical treatise.
author_notes: The 'Phil.' disambiguates from the more famous Apollodorus of Athens.

author_bio: Apollodorus of Athens, a tragedian of the early Hellenistic period known from fragmentary remains. Only one attested work survives in scant fragments, placing him among the minor poets of the era.
author_notes: The source label 'Apollodorus (Trag.)' distinguishes this figure from other authors named Apollodorus; the surviving text count and word count are minimal, indicating a fragmentary transmission.

author_bio: Apollonides is a poorly attested Greek author known only from a single surviving fragment. It is uncertain whether he is a historian, poet, or writer of miscellaneous prose. Multiple persons named Apollonides existed in antiquity, making precise identification difficult.
author_notes: The name Apollonides is common in antiquity; without a patronymic or city of origin, identification remains uncertain. The fragmentary nature of the evidence cautions against confident categorization.

author_bio: Apollonius Dyscolus was a pioneering Greek grammarian active in Alexandria. He is regarded as the founder of systematic syntactic theory and authored extensive works on parts of speech, syntax, and linguistic analysis, including 'De constructione' and 'De pronominibus'.
author_notes: Well-attested author with multiple works. The traditional biographical dates are widely accepted.
representative_titles: De constructione | De pronominibus | De adverbiis | De conjunctionibus

author_bio: Apollonius Rhodius was a Hellenistic epic poet and scholar, best known as the author of the 'Argonautica', an epic poem recounting the voyage of the Argonauts. He served as librarian of the Library of Alexandria and is a central figure of Alexandrian literature.
author_notes: Well-attested historical person; major surviving work and fragments.

author_bio: Apollonius the biographer is known only from his life of Aeschines, a work that survives in a single manuscript. He likely wrote during the Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The label 'Biogr.' indicates the biographical tradition; very little is known about this Apollonius; the work is a short vita.

author_bio: Apollonius is a obscure comic poet known from a single fragment (12 words). His identity and dates are unknown; the parenthetical '(Comic.)' indicates his likely genre.
author_notes: Recorded only in fragments, possibly a misattributed name. The parenthetical is used to disambiguate from other Apollonii.

author_bio: Greek geometer and astronomer known for his work on conic sections, which laid the foundation for later mathematics.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Apollonius (Geom.) is a disambiguation label for Apollonius of Perga, the mathematician.

author_bio: A very obscure historian (Apollonius Historicus) known only from a few fragments. Almost nothing is known of his life, and his identity is uncertain among the many Apollonii in Greek literature.
author_notes: The '(Hist.)' disambiguator marks the historian. The attestation is minimal and identification is uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek medical writer known for a commentary on Hippocrates' On Joints, demonstrating the continuing Alexandrian tradition of Hippocratic exegesis.
author_notes: The label '(Med.)' distinguishes him from other Apollonii. His commentary survives in fragmentary form.

author_bio: Apollonius Paradoxographus, author of a collection of marvels (Θαυμάσια). His work survives in fragments and influenced later paradoxographical traditions.
author_notes: The epithet '(Paradox.)' distinguishes him from other Apollonii. His exact identity and dates are uncertain; he may be the same as Apollonius of Alexandria.

author_bio: A little-known philosopher (Apollonius Philosophus) whose works include astrological and textual fragments. He is one of several homonymous Apollonii, and his identity and dates are uncertain.
author_notes: The '(Phil.)' disambiguator marks the philosopher. The works are dubia and spuria, making the corpus unreliable.
representative_titles: Apollonii epistulae Dub. | Apotelesmata Sp. | De horis diei et noctis (fragmenta e cod. Berol. 26) Sp.

author_period: late second – early third century CE
author_bio: Apollonius was an early Christian writer known for his work against the Montanist movement, a prophetic and ascetic Christian sect. His fragments are preserved mainly by Eusebius, showing him as a rigorous critic of Montanist practices and a defender of ecclesiastical order. Little else is known of his life.
author_notes: The designation '(Scr. Eccl.)' identifies him as an ecclesiastical writer. Only fragments survive; the historical reality of the person is not in doubt but details remain sparse.
representative_titles: Fragmenta ex libro adversus Cataphrygas seu Montanistas

author_bio: Apollonius Sophista was a Greek grammarian, known as the author of the Homeric Lexicon. Active likely in the 2nd century CE, he compiled a valuable word-by-word glossary of Homeric vocabulary, drawing on earlier Hellenistic scholarship.
author_notes: The label '(Soph.)' indicates his profession as a sophist or grammarian; ‘tlg1168’ is a stable TLG identifier.

author_bio: A comic poet of uncertain date, known only from fragments and titles. His name appears in the tradition of Greek comedy, but few biographical details survive.
author_notes: The label '(Comic.)' identifies the genre; likely a historical poet but known only from minimal fragments.

author_bio: Apollophanes is a fragmentary philosopher known only from late-antique testimonia and sparse citations. His identity remains uncertain due to the ambiguous abbreviation 'Phil.' possibly denoting a philosopher or a work title.
author_notes: The label 'Phil.' may indicate a philosophical affiliation or a work title; the source is ambiguous.

author_bio: Abū Maʿshar (Latinized as Apomasar or Albumasar) was a Persian astrologer active in Abbasid Baghdad. A work attributed to him in Greek translation is De revolutionibus nativitatum, a treatise on revolutions of nativities based on Hellenistic sources.
author_notes: Although historically identified as Abū Maʿshar, the Greek tradition lists him under the Latinized name Apomasar. The single work is a Byzantine Greek translation of his Arabic original.

author_bio: The *Apophthegmata Patrum* ('Sayings of the Fathers') is a collection of wisdom sayings and anecdotes from the Desert Fathers of Egypt. It circulated in multiple recensions and was a foundational text for Eastern Christian monasticism.
author_notes: The author field lists a title: 'Apophthegmata' is a textual corpus, not a person. Use agent_kind=work_title accordingly.
representative_titles: Apophthegmata patrum (collectio alphabetica) | Apophthegmata (collectio anonyma) (e cod. Coislin. 126) | Apophthegmata patrum (collectio systematica) (cap. 1-9) | Quaestiones et responsa senum de tentationibus | Apophthegmata patrum (collectio anonyma) (e codd. Coislin. 126, 127 + Paris. gr. 1596, 2474) | Dialogus de contemplatione | De meditationibus duodecim anachoretarum | Apophthegma patrum (e collectione anonyma) (e codd. Vat. gr. 1599, 1632)

author_bio: An anonymous collection of Greek proverbs appended to the major paroemiographic corpora, likely compiled in the Byzantine period.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the work itself; authorship is not attributed to a specific person or group.

author_bio: Appian was a Greek historian from Alexandria who wrote a Roman History in Greek, covering the wars of the Republic. His work survives in substantial fragments and several complete books.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; major author for Roman history as seen from a Greek viewpoint. URN source.
representative_titles: Bellum civile | Libyca | Mithridatica | Iberica | Syriaca | Annibaica | Illyrica | Macedonica (fragmenta)

author_bio: Araethus (also spelled Ariaethus) is a fragmentarily preserved Greek author. The surviving fragments contain historical and antiquarian material, possibly from a work on local traditions. His exact dates and region are not securely established.
author_notes: The alternate spelling Ar(i)aethus reflects manuscript variation; fragments attributed to this author are limited in number.

author_cultural_context: Attic comedy; son of Aristophanes, active as a comic poet in Athens.
author_bio: Araros was a son of the great comic playwright Aristophanes and a poet of Middle Comedy. His works, now fragmentary, were known to later scholars.
author_notes: The source includes both 'Fragmenta' and 'Tituli', indicating some titles but minimal textual remains.

author_bio: Aratus of Soli was a Hellenistic poet and astronomer, best known for his didactic poem 'Phaenomena,' which versified the constellations and weather signs. The work, based on Eudoxus's prose treatise, achieved immense popularity in antiquity and was translated by Cicero and others. The TLG fragment of 627 words likely represents surviving portions of his oeuvre.
author_notes: The TLG entry includes only 'Fragmenta' (627 words), presumably from his minor works or lost poems; the main work 'Phaenomena' is not represented in this corpus, but the author's historicity and significance are well-attested.

author_region: Soli, Cilicia; active in Athens and Macedonia
author_bio: Aratus of Soli was a Hellenistic poet and scholar, best known for the 'Phaenomena,' a didactic hexameter poem on astronomy and weather signs, widely read and translated in antiquity.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; his 'Phaenomena' was a standard text. The source ID indicates a standard CTS URN.
representative_titles: Phaenomena | Fragmentum (e cod. Matrit. 4616) | Fragmentum (e cod. Vat. gr. 2130) | Fragmenta et tituli | Epigrammata

author_bio: Arcadius was a grammarian known for a treatise on Greek accents (De accentibus), reflecting the scholarly traditions of late antique grammar and education.
author_notes: The identity of this Arcadius is debated; he may be distinct from the better-known grammarian Arcadius of Antioch.

author_bio: The Epistula Arcesilai is a short letter attributed to the Academic skeptic philosopher Arcesilaus, but its authenticity is disputed.
author_notes: The source label uses the title as author heading. Attribution is doubtful; may be a later rhetorical exercise.

author_bio: Arcesilaus is a name associated with a fragment of comic verse. Little is known about this poet; the single surviving fragment suggests he was active in Hellenistic comedy. The attribution is uncertain and the name may be a pseudonym or a mistake.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg0520. The label 'Comic.' denotes a comic poet. The fragment is very short (10 words). Identity and dates are speculative.

author_bio: Arcesilaus is known primarily as a philosopher in the skeptical Academy, but this entry likely refers to a different Arcesilaus, given the disambiguation 'Phil.' and the minimal fragmentary remains.
author_notes: Possibly a homonym of the famous Academic; surviving fragments are very brief.

author_bio: An obscure Hellenistic poet known from a single fragment of seven words, included in later anthologies.
author_notes: Only one tiny fragment. The name may also appear as 'Archebulus' in some sources. Minimal evidence for biographical details.

author_bio: Archedemus is a name known from fragmentary works, possibly a Stoic or Academic philosopher. The surviving fragments (907 words) suggest engagement with philosophical topics, but his identity and dates remain uncertain.
author_notes: Single work 'Fragmenta' (907 words). The name Archedemus appears in various ancient contexts; the TLG entry likely refers to a philosophical author of the Hellenistic period.

author_bio: Archedicus is an ancient Greek comic poet, known only from fragments. His work may belong to New Comedy, but little is known about his life or career.
author_notes: Only fragments survive, preserved through citations by later authors. The precise dating and origin are uncertain.

author_bio: Archelaus is the name associated with a didactic poem on alchemy, 'On the Sacred Art' (περὶ τῆς θείας τέχνης). Nothing certain is known of his life; the work combines Hermetic and philosophical themes and may date to late antiquity.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Alchem. et Poeta)' distinguishes this Archelaus from others. The single surviving work is a hexameter poem on alchemical processes.
representative_titles: Ἀρχελάου φιλοσόφου περὶ τῆς θείας τέχνης διὰ στίχων ἰάμβων

author_bio: Author of paradoxographical (marvels and wonders) literature; a minor figure known only from fragments.
author_notes: The 'Paradox.' label indicates his genre: paradoxography. Very little is known about his life; authorship of the surviving epigram is uncertain.

author_bio: Archelaus was an early Greek philosopher of the Ionian school, a student of Anaxagoras and teacher of Socrates, known for advancing natural philosophy in Athens.
author_notes: The identifier '(Phil.)' denotes this entry as the philosopher Archelaus, distinguishing him from homonyms. Attestations are primarily fragmentary.

author_bio: Archemachus of Euboea was a Hellenistic historian and antiquarian, known for a local history of Euboea (Euboika). His work survives only in fragments, mainly cited by Athenaeus and Stephanus of Byzantium. He compiled details on mythology, cults, and local traditions of the island.
author_notes: Attribution of Euboika to Archemachus is based on citations; the exact scope of his work is not fully preserved.

author_bio: Archestratus of Syracuse (or Gela) composed a parodic gastronomic poem in hexameters, the Hedypatheia (Life of Luxury), influencing later comic and didactic poetry.
author_notes: Listed as 'Parodius' to distinguish from other authors named Archestratus; focal work is the Hedypatheia.

author_bio: A Greek tragic poet known only from a single titular fragment; extremely obscure.
author_notes: Everything attributed to this author is a single title ('Titulus'); the 'Trag.' label indicates his genre. Historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Archigenes was a Greek physician from Apamea in Syria, active in Rome during the reign of Trajan. A pupil of Agathinus, he belonged to the Eclectic school of medicine and wrote influential works on pathology, pharmacology, and surgery, surviving only in fragments and later quotations.
author_notes: The TLG author ID tlg0661 aggregates Archigenes' fragments from various sources; the attested corpus is limited, hence medium confidence.

author_bio: Early Greek lyric poet from Paros, known for his innovative iambic and elegiac poetry. His work survives in fragments and papyri.
author_notes: One of the earliest and most influential Greek poets; attribution of fragments is generally secure.

author_bio: Preeminent Greek mathematician, physicist, and engineer. His extant works treat geometry, hydrostatics, and mechanics, demonstrating profound originality.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; major influence on later science. (Med.) disambiguation is not needed; this entry is the mathematician.
representative_titles: De sphaera et cylindro | De conoidibus et sphaeroidibus | De lineis spiralibus | Ad Eratosthenem methodus | De planorum aequilibriis | De corporibus fluitantibus | Quadratura parabolae | Arenarius

author_bio: Archimelus is known from two works: a fragment that describes an enormous ship built for Hieron II of Syracuse (the Syracusia), and an epigram. He may have been a court poet or engineer associated with the Syracusan ruler.
author_notes: Some scholars identify Archimelus with the engineer Archimedes, but this is speculative; the connection to Hieron II is secure.
representative_titles: Fragmentum de Hieronis II navigio | Epigramma

author_bio: Archinus is a Greek author known only from fragments. The name is associated with Athens in the late 5th century BCE, possibly a politician or historian, but the surviving fragments do not provide clear identification. He may be the same Archinus mentioned by later sources in a political context.
author_notes: The identity is ambiguous; possibly the Athenian politician Archinus, but the attribution of these fragments is uncertain.

author_bio: Archippus was an Athenian comic poet active in the late 5th and early 4th centuries BCE, known from fragmentary plays and title references. He belonged to the tradition of Old Comedy.
author_notes: Identification as a historical comic poet is secure, but surviving fragments are limited; TLG source lists fragments and titles with few extant lines.

author_cultural_context: Epicurean philosopher known through sparse fragments; distinguished from the Pythagorean Archytas.
author_bio: Archytas the Epicurean is a minor Hellenistic philosopher known only from a 33-word fragment, possibly a doxographical citation.
author_notes: Disambiguated as Epicurean (not the Pythagorean); very short fragment.

author_bio: A corpus of pseudonymous philosophical fragments and testimonia attributed to the historical Archytas of Tarentum, likely composed by later Neopythagorean authors.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Phil.)' distinguishes this corpus of pseudonymous philosophical fragments from the historical Archytas of Tarentum. Author_historicity_status set to 'pseudonymous' because the works are attributed to a historical figure but are not authentic.

author_bio: Known only from a single fragment, Aresas is a shadowy figure in ancient Greek literary tradition. The extant work, titled Fragmentum, provides insufficient biographical details to confirm his historicity or precise cultural role.
author_notes: The author is known only through a single fragmentary work; no external attestations exist to confirm historicity.

author_bio: Aretades is a little-known Hellenistic author of paradoxographical works, attested solely through fragmentary citations. His identity and exact date are uncertain.
author_notes: The name is known only from a few fragments in later compilations; it may be a pseudonym or a misattribution.

author_cultural_context: Greek medicine under the Roman Empire
author_bio: Aretaeus of Cappadocia was a Greek physician of the Roman period, author of medical texts on acute and chronic diseases. His works are foundational for the history of medicine, blending Hippocratic and eclectic traditions.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with surviving works in multiple manuscripts.
representative_titles: De causis et signis acutorum morborum (lib. 2) | De curatione acutorum morborum libri duo | De causis et signis acutorum morborum (lib. 1) | De curatione diuturnorum morborum libri duo

author_bio: Byzantine scholar, archbishop of Caesarea, prolific writer of scholia, epigrams, and theological commentaries.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with substantial surviving corpus.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis) | Scholia Arethae in Cyrilli apologiam xii anathematismorum contra Theodoretum et in Theodoreti impugnationem | Fragmenta in epistulam ii ad Corinthios (in catenis) | Epigrammata | Fragmenta in epistulam i ad Corinthios (in catenis) | Fragmentum in epistulam i ad Timotheum (in catenis) | Fragmentum in epistulam ad Hebraeos (in catenis) | Fragmentum in epistulam ii ad Thessalonicenses (in catenis)

author_bio: Arimnestus is a name attached to a single fragmentary work (*Fragmentum*) of only 62 words. No reliable biographical details survive, and the historical reality of the author is uncertain.
author_notes: Minimal data; may be a name preserved only in a quotation or a misattribution. Historicity status set to uncertain due to lack of evidence.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek lyric poetry; Skolion tradition
author_bio: Ariphron was a Greek lyric poet from Sicyon, known solely from a single short fragment (a hymn to Health). His name appears in a few ancient sources, though no biographical detail survives. He is representative of the many minor lyric poets whose work has come down only in brief, isolated quotations.
author_notes: Very limited textual attestation; author is known from a single fragment and scattered ancient testimonia. The source id is unique to TLG.

author_bio: Aristaenetus is the author of a collection of fictional erotic letters (Epistulae), known only from this work. His dates and biography are obscure, but the letters place him in the late antique or early Byzantine period.
author_notes: Single surviving work (Epistulae) with 15,444 words. The name is attested only through the manuscript tradition; internal evidence suggests a late antique dating.

author_bio: Attributed author of a single fragmentary work on mythology or natural philosophy; likely a legendary figure associated with early Greek lore.
author_notes: All that survives is a single fragment; authorship and historicity are uncertain. The name is legendary, possibly conflated with the mythological hero Aristaeus.

author_bio: A minor Greek comic playwright, known from only a few fragments. Nothing certain is recorded about his life or career.
author_notes: The label (Comic.) distinguishes him from other figures named Aristagoras. Attribution of the fragments is tentative.

author_bio: Aristagoras (Hist.) is a Greek historian of the Classical or early Hellenistic period, known only from a few fragments. His work likely dealt with local history or genealogy.
author_notes: The epithet '(Hist.)' disambiguates from other homonyms. The fragments are very short and may be misattributed.

author_bio: Aristarchus of Samos was a Greek astronomer and mathematician who pioneered the heliocentric model and produced the only surviving ancient work on the sizes and distances of the sun and moon via geometric reasoning.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Astron.)' distinguishes this from the Homeric scholar Aristarchus of Samothrace. The identification is secure.
representative_titles: De magnitudinibus et distantiis solis et lunae

author_bio: Aristarchus (Trag.) was a tragic poet from the early Hellenistic period. Only fragments of his plays survive, including one quoted by the paroemiographer Zenobius.
author_notes: The 'Trag.' disambiguation separates this poet from the more famous Homeric scholar Aristarchus of Samothrace.

author_bio: A pseudonymous letter purporting to be from Aristeas, a courtier of Ptolemy II, describing the Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible (Septuagint). Composed in Greek as a work of Jewish apologetics.
author_notes: The 'author' is actually a text; the heading represents a work title in the author slot. The real author is unknown but the text is a key witness to Septuagint legend.

author_bio: Aristeas is the name associated with a fragment of epic poetry in Greek. The historical identity is unclear; the name may refer to a known figure or a pseudonym.
author_notes: The fragment is epic in nature but the name Aristeas is ambiguous; it could be a real person or a traditional attribution. Only 75 words survive.

author_bio: Aristias was a tragic poet, son of the dramatist Pratinas, and known to have competed in the Athenian dramatic festivals. Only fragments of his plays survive.
author_notes: Attested in ancient catalogues; fragmentary corpus.

author_bio: Aristides Quintilianus is the author of the De musica, a comprehensive treatise on music theory and its ethical and cosmological dimensions.
author_notes: Not to be confused with Aristides of Miletus; 'Quintilianus' is likely a cognomen, not related to the Roman rhetorician.

author_bio: Aristides was a 2nd-century Christian apologist from Athens, author of an 'Apology' addressed to Emperor Hadrian. His work is one of the earliest surviving defenses of Christianity, known in Greek fragments and a Syriac version.
author_notes: The label '(Apol.)' distinguishes him from the many other Aristides; identification with the apologist is secure.

author_bio: Aristides (Hist.) is a Hellenistic historian known only from fragments. His exact identity remains uncertain, but he is treated as a distinct person in scholarly catalogues.
author_notes: The epithet '(Hist.)' distinguishes him from other Aristides. His fragments are short and may be confused with other homonymous writers.

author_bio: Aristippus was a Greek philosopher from Cyrene, founder of the Cyrenaic school of hedonism; only a few fragments of his writings survive.
author_notes: This entity likely refers to the founder Aristippus the Elder, but fragments may be attributed to his grandson of the same name; attribution is ambiguous.

author_bio: Aristobulus (Hist.) was a historian who accompanied Alexander the Great on his campaigns and wrote an influential history of Alexander. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' disambiguation separates this historian from other figures named Aristobulus. His work was used by later Alexander historians like Arrian.

author_bio: Aristobulus was a Hellenistic Jewish philosopher of the 2nd century BCE, often considered a precursor to Philo. He wrote exegetical fragments combining Jewish scripture with Greek philosophy.
author_notes: Source label includes 'Judaeus Phil.' to distinguish from other Aristobuli; known only through fragments.

author_bio: Aristocles is known as a writer of paradoxography, a genre collecting marvels and wonders. Only fragments of his work survive, and little is known about his life.
author_notes: The label 'Paradox.' indicates his genre. This Aristocles is distinct from other figures of the same name; the attribution of the fragments is uncertain.

author_bio: Aristocles (Phil.) is a Greek philosopher known from fragments. The designation '(Phil.)' distinguishes him from other Aristides; he likely wrote on ethics and epistemology.
author_notes: The fragments are preserved primarily in later doxographers. The identification as a distinct philosopher is conventional but not entirely secure.

author_cultural_context: Greek author of uncertain identity, possibly an orator or historian.
author_bio: Aristocrates is a shadowy Greek writer known only by name and a few fragments, perhaps a logographer or minor historian.
author_notes: 239 words; no secure biographical details.

author_bio: Aristocreon was a Hellenistic Stoic philosopher, likely of the 3rd century BCE, known only from fragmentary references. He may have been a student of Chrysippus and is cited by later authors for dialectical arguments.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; attestation is indirect via later doxographers. TLG identifier tlg2455.

author_bio: Aristocritus is a poorly known Greek author, surviving only through a single fragment. Neither his literary genre nor his exact period can be reliably determined.
author_notes: No additional information beyond the fragment; uncertain identification.

author_bio: Obscure author of historical and mythological fragments. The double label indicates that his surviving fragments blend both genres.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; identity uncertain. The label '(Hist. et Myth.)' suggests the compiler's difficulty in categorizing him.

author_bio: A little-known historian, possibly from Alexandria or Thebes, quoted in later sources. One fragment comes from a papyrus (P. Oxy. 27.2469), suggesting a lost work on local or mythological history.
author_notes: Two fragments exist, one from a papyrus. The (Hist.) distinguishes this figure from other known Aristodemoi.

author_bio: An obscure figure known only from a single fragment; otherwise unattested in historical sources.
author_notes: Name appears only in fragmentary attribution; no biographical data.

author_bio: Aristomenes was an Athenian comic poet of the 3rd century BCE, belonging to the tradition of Middle or New Comedy. Only fragments of his work survive, preserving titles that suggest mythological burlesque and everyday domestic themes.
author_notes: The name is standard but the corpus is very fragmentary.

author_bio: Ariston of Ceos was a Greek Peripatetic philosopher, successor of Lyco as head of the Lyceum. He wrote on ethics and philosophy, but only fragments survive. He is an important figure for the Peripatetic school.
author_notes: The name 'Ariston Ceus' clearly indicates Ariston of Ceos, a known historical philosopher. The fragments are authenticated.

author_bio: Ariston of Cos was a Peripatetic philosopher known only from rhetorical fragments that likely stem from a commentary or treatise on rhetoric.
author_notes: Only a single fragmentary work survives; the historical figure is barely attested beyond this corpus.

author_bio: Ariston (Apol.) is a fragmentarily preserved Greek author known only through a single surviving fragment. The parenthetical '(Apol.)' likely indicates an Apollonian or Apollodoran association, but the exact identification remains uncertain. His work is cited in later sources, suggesting possible activity in the Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The source label 'tlg1992' refers to a fragment attributed to an Ariston, possibly a commentator or historian. The '(Apol.)' disambiguation is conventional in the TLG corpus to distinguish this figure from other authors named Ariston.

author_bio: Ariston of Chios (or Ceos) was a Stoic philosopher of the 3rd century BCE, a pupil of Zeno who emphasized ethical theory and rejected dialectics and physics. His works survive only in fragments and testimonia, and he was known for his wit and sharp criticism of conventional Stoic doctrines.
author_notes: The label 'Phil.' indicates philosophical work. Using the TLG citation, the author is distinct from other figures named Ariston. Historically attested as a Stoic philosopher.

author_bio: Aristonicus was a Greek grammarian active in the late Hellenistic and early Imperial periods, known for his commentaries on Homer and for his work on the critical signs of the Alexandrian editors.
author_notes: Known only through fragments and later testimonia; the epithet 'Gramm.' indicates his profession as a grammarian.
representative_titles: De signis Iliadis | De signis Odysseae | Testimonia | Fragmentum

author_bio: Aristonicus, noted as a historian (Hist.), is known only from a single fragment; his identity and historical context are unknown.
author_notes: Label (Hist.) distinguishes him from other Aristonicus figures; only 146 words survive.

author_bio: Aristonous was a Hellenistic poet, known for a paean to Apollo and a hymn to Hestia, preserved on stone inscriptions. He may have been active at Delphi or in Athens.
author_notes: Only the two hymns survive; the attribution to a single author is secure from the inscriptions themselves.
representative_titles: Paean in Apollinem | Hymnus in Vestam

author_bio: A Greek author of uncertain identity, possibly a comic poet or philosopher, known only from a few fragments.
author_notes: Very little is known about Aristonymus; the fragments are brief and attribution may be uncertain.

author_bio: Aristophanes is the most famous Athenian comic playwright of the Old Comedy tradition. His surviving plays are unparalleled sources for political and social satire of late 5th- and early 4th-century Athens.
author_notes: Major canonical author; the parenthetical '(Comic.)' disambiguates from other persons named Aristophanes.
representative_titles: Aves | Vespae | Nubes | Ranae | Equites | Plutus | Lysistrata | Pax

author_bio: A leading Alexandrian scholar and grammarian, pupil of Zenodotus and head of the Library of Alexandria. He innovated in lexicography, literary criticism, and textual criticism; his works survive only in fragments, but his influence on later scholarship is immense.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the disambiguator 'Gramm.' distinguishes him from the comic poet.
representative_titles: Aristophanis historiae animalium epitome subjunctis Aeliani Timothei aliorumque eclogis | Fragmenta | Nomina aetatum (fragmenta) | Argumenta fabularum Aristophani tributa (fragmenta) | Ceteri Aristophanis libri (fragmenta) | Paroemiae (fragmenta) | Commentaria in Callimachi pinaces (fragmenta) | Nomina aetatum (fragmentum Parisinum)

author_bio: A leading Alexandrian scholar and lexicographer, pupil of Callimachus and Zenodotus, instrumental in the study of Greek literature and textual criticism.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; note the parenthetical (Hist.) distinguishes from the playwright Aristophanes.

author_bio: Athenian comic poet of Middle Comedy, known only from fragments.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; limited biographical data.

author_bio: Aristotle was a foundational figure in Western philosophy, a student of Plato and tutor of Alexander the Great. His works span logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics, natural sciences, and poetics.
author_notes: This entry covers the entire Aristotelian corpus transmitted in Greek, including genuine works, fragments, and spuria. Aristotle's influence on subsequent thought is immeasurable.
representative_titles: Historia animalium | Fragmenta varia | Metaphysica | Problemata | Politica | Analytica priora et posteriora | Ethica Nicomachea | Physica

author_bio: An obscure Greek comic poet known from a single fragment. Possibly active in the 4th century BCE, but virtually nothing is known of his life or work.
author_notes: The designation (Comic.) distinguishes this Aristoxenus from the Peripatetic philosopher and music theorist Aristoxenus. The single surviving fragment is minimal.

author_bio: Aristoxenus of Tarentum, a Peripatetic philosopher and music theorist, wrote foundational works on harmony and rhythm. His *Elementa harmonica* is the earliest surviving systematic treatise on Greek music theory.
author_notes: Well-attested historically; the disambiguating label '(Mus.)' distinguishes him from other Aristoxeni.
representative_titles: Elementa harmonica | Fragmenta | Elementa rhythmica (lib. 2) | Fragmentum (P. Oxy. 1.9) | Fragmenta Parisina (e cod. bibl. imp. Par. 3027) | Elementa rhythmica (lib. 1)

author_bio: Greek Stoic philosopher and doxographer from Alexandria, known for a summary of philosophical schools preserved in Stobaeus.
author_notes: Known primarily through Stobaean epitome; historicity is secure but biographical details are limited.
representative_titles: Liber de philosophorum sectis (epitome ap. Stobaeum) | Physica (fragmenta)

author_bio: Armenidas was a shadowy Greek historian or mythographer, known only from fragmentary quotations.
author_notes: Source label ambiguous; may be a lost local historian.

author_bio: Arrian of Nicomedia was a historian, philosopher, and Roman senator, best known for his Anabasis of Alexander, a major source on Alexander the Great, and for his Discourses of Epictetus.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure; extensive surviving corpus including history, philosophy, and military treatises.
representative_titles: Alexandri anabasis | Historia Indica | Fragmenta | Tactica | Cynegeticus | Historia successorum Alexandri (fragmenta ap. Photium, Bibl. cod. 92) | Bithynicorum fragmenta | Periplus ponti Euxini

author_bio: Arrianus is a Greek author of uncertain identity, distinct from the historian Arrian of Nicomedia, known only from a fragment and titles quoted by later sources.
author_notes: Likely a distinct figure from the famous Arrian; fragments suggest a grammarian or rhetorician.

author_cultural_context: Byzantine Greek monastic and literary tradition
author_bio: Arsenius (fl. 12th–13th cent.) was a Byzantine monk and author of the Apophthegmata, a collection of sayings of the Desert Fathers. His work is a key source for monastic spirituality.
author_notes: The author is known from the manuscript tradition; association with the monk Arsenius is well-established.

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to Artaxerxes, but likely a later Greek composition; the heading refers to the textual corpus itself.
author_notes: This is a corpus title occupying the author slot; authenticity disputed.

author_bio: Artemidorus (Eleg.) is an obscure Hellenistic elegist known only from a few fragmentary lines; identity and date are uncertain.
author_notes: Only 4 words survive; author label may conflate with other figures named Artemidorus.

author_bio: Author of the *Oneirocritica*, the most comprehensive surviving work on dream interpretation from antiquity.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; the epithet 'Onir.' refers to his oneirocritical work, which survives intact.

author_bio: Artemon of Pergamon was a Greek grammarian or historian, possibly active in the 1st century BCE or CE. Fragments suggest work on local history or lexicography, but his identity remains shadowy.
author_notes: The epithet 'Pergamenus' links him to Pergamon; however, his corpus is too small for confident appraisal.

author_bio: Artemon Grammaticus was a Hellenistic grammarian or scholar, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: The epithet 'Gramm.' indicates a grammarian; multiple Hellenistic scholars named Artemon exist.

author_bio: Greek historian of uncertain date, possibly active in the late Hellenistic period. Fragments preserved by later compilers.
author_notes: The label '(Hist.)' identifies this as the historian Artemon, distinct from the shadow of the same name. Transmission is fragmentary and indirect.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian and literary critic, known for his work on Homer and perhaps on the poets of Alexandria. His fragments survive in later scholia and lexica.
author_notes: Historical person attested in ancient sources, though his works are largely lost.

author_bio: Asclepiades of Samos (fl. c. 270 BCE) was a Hellenistic epigrammatist active in Alexandria, known for erotic and sympotic poems that influenced later Greek and Roman love elegy.
author_notes: The identifier '(Epigr.)' indicates the distinction from other authors named Asclepiades (e.g., the physician). His corpus consists largely of epigrams from the Greek Anthology.

author_bio: Asclepiades (Gramm. et Hist.) was a Hellenistic Greek grammarian and historian known only through fragments. His works, which survive in meager quotations, likely encompassed literary criticism and historical commentary, reflecting the scholarly traditions of the Alexandrian and Pergamene schools.
author_notes: The label 'Gramm. et Hist.' is a modern disambiguation added by the Thesaurus Linguae Graecae (TLG) to distinguish this figure from other authors named Asclepiades; the assignment of genre and historical identity is uncertain due to fragmentary evidence.

author_bio: Asclepiades the mythographer wrote a work on tragic myths, possibly used as source for later mythographers; only fragments remain.
author_notes: Labeled 'Myth.' to distinguish from other Asclepiades; identity and precise date uncertain.

author_bio: Asclepiodotus was a Greek military writer, author of a surviving manual on tactics (Tactica). His work is a key source for Hellenistic military science.
author_notes: Well-attested author with a surviving work; standard identification in military history.

author_bio: Asclepius of Tralles was a 6th-century Neoplatonic philosopher and commentator on Aristotle and Nicomachus. His works, including commentaries on the Metaphysics and the Introduction to Arithmetic, reflect the Alexandrian school of philosophy.
author_notes: Attested as a historical commentator; his works survive in substantial medieval manuscripts. The corpus is well-defined and of moderate scale.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis metaphysicorum libros A-Z commentaria | Commentaria in Nicomachi Geraseni Pythagorei introductionem arithmeticam

author_bio: Semi-legendary early Greek poet, known from a few surviving fragments of epic and elegiac verse; little certain biographical information survives.
author_notes: Attribution rests on a small body of fragments; identity and dates are uncertain. The name may refer to a single poet or be a conflation of multiple figures.

author_bio: Asopodorus is known only from a few inscribed titles; nothing else is known about this figure.
author_notes: Only 6 words survive, making the identity and historicity of Asopodorus highly uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek Peripatetic philosopher and commentator
author_bio: Aspasius was a Peripatetic philosopher of the 2nd century CE, best known for his commentary on Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; his commentary survives.

author_bio: The Assumption of Moses is a Jewish pseudepigraphon originally written in Greek or Hebrew, now known from fragments. It recounts the death of Moses and associated eschatological prophecies, reflecting Hellenistic Jewish apocalyptic traditions.
author_notes: The title occupies the author slot as a conventional cataloging label; the original author is unknown.

author_bio: Asterius was a Greek ecclesiastical writer and preacher, possibly of the 4th century, author of homilies on moral and exegetical themes.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Scr. Eccl.)' clarifies his role; some homilies survive via Symeon Metaphrastes; identity debated.

author_bio: Asterius the Sophist was an Arian Christian theologian and orator of the early 4th century. He composed commentaries on the Psalms, preserved in part through homilies and catena fragments, and was a key figure in the Arian controversy.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the label 'Scr. Eccl.' identifies him as an ecclesiastical writer. The homilies and fragments are securely attributed.
representative_titles: Commentarii in Psalmos (homiliae 31) | Fragmenta in Psalmos (in catenis)

author_bio: Astrampsychus is a legendary figure associated with a collection of oracles and sortes (lot oracles) used in Greco-Roman Egypt for divination.
author_notes: The epithet 'Magus' indicates his traditional association with magic; historicity uncertain.

author_bio: Astydamas was an Athenian tragic poet, active in the 4th century BCE. His fragments are preserved through anthologies.
author_notes: There were two tragedians named Astydamas (the Elder and the Younger); the fragments are assigned to them collectively.

author_bio: Athamas is a mythic figure from Greek mythology, son of Aeolus and king of Orchomenus, known for his tragic family story. The single surviving fragment under his name likely comes from a lost epic or genealogical poem.
author_notes: Fragment attributed to a mythic figure; genuine authorship improbable; may be a spurious or traditional attribution.

author_bio: Athanasius (sophist) was a Byzantine rhetorician and commentator on Hermogenes, known for his prolegomena to rhetorical theory.
author_notes: The label 'Soph.' indicates the sophist, distinguishing him from the bishop Athanasius.
representative_titles: Prolegomena in Hermogenis librum PERÌ STÁSEWN (epitome)

author_bio: Athanasius of Alexandria was a Church Father, bishop, and theologian. He was a central figure in the Arian controversy, a staunch defender of Nicene orthodoxy, and the author of major theological and exegetical works including the 'Life of Antony' and treatises against the Arians.
author_notes: The corpus includes authentic works, as well as some texts of disputed or spurious attribution (marked Sp. in source).
representative_titles: Expositiones in Psalmos | Orationes tres contra Arianos | Synopsis scripturae sacrae Sp. | De sancta trinitate (dialogi 1, 3, 5) Sp. | Apologia contra Arianos sive Apologia secunda | Epistulae quattuor ad Serapionem | De decretis Nicaenae synodi | Quaestiones ad Antiochum ducem Sp.

author_bio: Athanis of Syracuse (or possibly of Rhegion) was a Greek historian whose work survives only in fragments. His writings appear to have covered Sicilian history, likely from the 4th century BCE.
author_notes: Tentatively identified as Athanis of Syracuse (FGrH 562); known from citations in later authors such as Athenaeus.

author_bio: Athenaeus of Naucratis authored the Deipnosophistae, a vast compendium of learned table talk citing many earlier writers. His work is a key source for fragments of lost Greek literature and for social history.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; standard TLG source.

author_bio: Author of epigrams preserved in the Greek Anthology, not to be confused with the much later author of the Deipnosophistae. His poetry is conventional in themes.
author_notes: The suffix '(Epigr.)' distinguishes this epigrammatist from Athenaeus of Naucratis.

author_bio: Athenaeus is an author known only from a technical treatise on siege engines; his identity and dates are unclear, possibly a writer of the late Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The work 'De machinis' is extant and has been studied; however, no external evidence confirms the author's historicity.

author_bio: A 2nd-century Christian apologist and philosopher from Athens, author of the 'Legatio pro Christianis' and the treatise 'De resurrectione'. His works are among the earliest surviving examples of Christian philosophical apologetic written in Greek, addressed to the emperors Marcus Aurelius and Commodus.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; works preserved in a single manuscript tradition.
representative_titles: Legatio sive Supplicatio pro Christianis | De resurrectione

author_cultural_context: Peripatetic philosopher and possible comic playwright; known from limited testimonia and fragments
author_bio: Athenio was a Peripatetic philosopher and perhaps a comic poet active in the late Hellenistic period. Only a few fragments of his work survive, predominantly transmitted by Athenaeus, offering glimpses into philosophical and literary discussions.
author_notes: Very little is known; the TLG entry conflates possible different individuals with the same name (e.g., the philosopher vs. an alleged comic poet). The source identification rests on indirect citations.

author_bio: Athenodorus (Phil.) is a Stoic philosopher, often identified with Athenodorus Cordylion or Athenodorus of Tarsus. He was a teacher of Augustus and wrote philosophical works, now lost except for fragments.
author_notes: Several philosophers named Athenodorus exist; the '(Phil.)' distinguishes him, but specific identification is uncertain.

author_bio: Athenodorus, a tragic poet of the classical period, known only from a single title.
author_notes: Fragments are extremely limited; the 'Trag.' disambiguates the profession.

author_bio: A Greek title meaning 'Return of the Atridae', likely a fragment of a lost epic or tragic poem dealing with the return of Agamemnon and Menelaus after the Trojan War. Only a minimal fragment survives (9 words).
author_notes: The author field is a title, not a person. The fragment is of uncertain origin and date; it may belong to the Epic Cycle or a lost tragedy.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian and astronomer from Rhodes, known for a prose reworking of Aratus' Phaenomena.
author_notes: Often identified with the Attalus cited by later scholiasts; fragmentary work.

author_bio: Middle Platonist philosopher and teacher, known through fragments of his commentaries and treatises preserved by later authors.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Atticus is a Platonist philosopher; only fragments of his works survive.

author_bio: Augeas is a mythological king of Elis, famous for his stables cleansed by Heracles. In TLG, 'Augeas' collects epigraphic and literary fragments (tituli, fragmentum) that mention or are attributed to him in a legendary context.
author_notes: Attribution to Augeas as 'author' is conventional; the fragments are about or attributed to the mythical figure, not a historical writer.

author_bio: Author of a single Greek epigram preserved in a Byzantine anthology; likely a Roman with Greek literary interests, but otherwise unattested.
author_notes: The name is Latin, but the work is in Greek; the author may be a minor figure or a misidentification.

author_bio: Shadowy figure known only from a few fragments; possibly a poet of the early Archaic period. The name may be connected with myth or cult.
author_notes: Extremely sparse evidence; the author's existence and date are conjectural.

author_bio: Autocharis is known only from a single fragment, likely a papyrus. The name may indicate a female author or character, but no further biographical or literary context is available.
author_notes: The fragment is too brief to determine historicity or genre. Possibly a ghost author or an error in attribution.

author_bio: Autocrates was a Greek comic poet, likely of Middle Comedy. Only a few fragments and a title survive, and his biography is almost entirely lost.
author_notes: The '(Comic.)' disambiguator marks the comic poet. Attestation is minimal; only three works (fragmenta, titulus) survive.

author_bio: Autocrates (Hist.) is a shadowy Greek historian known only from a single fragment. The subject matter is unknown, as the fragment preserves no clear context. The label '(Hist.)' marks him as a historian.
author_notes: Only 47 words survive; biographical data, period, and region are speculative. The TLG author ID groups all fragments under this name.

author_cultural_context: Greek mathematician and astronomer; member of the Peripatetic school.
author_bio: Autolycus of Pitane was an early Greek astronomer whose surviving works 'On the Moving Sphere' and 'On Risings and Settings' are among the oldest extant Greek mathematical treatises.
author_notes: Well-attested figure with two surviving works; significant in history of astronomy.
representative_titles: De ortibus et occasibus | De sphaera quae movetur

author_bio: Axionicus was a poet of Middle or New Comedy in Athens. He is known from fragments that display the wit and social observation characteristic of the genre.
author_notes: The fragments are few and limited; the biographical tradition is sparse. The name appears in ancient catalogues of comic poets.

author_bio: Bacchylides of Ceos was a major Greek lyric poet, nephew of Simonides and contemporary of Pindar. His surviving works include epinician odes, dithyrambs, and paeans, known for their clarity and vivid narrative.
author_notes: Corpus well-established from papyri discoveries; some fragments remain dubia.
representative_titles: Epinicia | Dithyrambi | Encomia (fragmenta) | Dithyramborum fragmenta | Fragmenta dubia | Paeanes (fragmenta) | Fragmenta ex operibus incertis | Prosodia (fragmenta)

author_bio: A shadowy figure known only from a brief fragment; perhaps a Hellenistic historian or grammarian. No secure biographical information survives.
author_notes: Only one fragment (34 words) survives; identity and period are conjectural.

author_bio: Barlaam of Seminara (c. 1290–1348) was a Greek-Italian scholar and theologian, known for introducing Euclidean studies to the West and for his involvement in the Hesychast controversy. His paraphrase of Euclid reflects his mathematical interests.
author_notes: This entry refers to Barlaam of Calabria, distinct from the legendary Barlaam of the Barlaam and Josaphat tradition.
representative_titles: Paraphrasis in Euclidis elementorum librum secundum

author_bio: The Epistle of Barnabas is an early Christian Greek theological treatise, traditionally linked to Barnabas but almost certainly pseudonymous; it is transmitted as a single work.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work itself, as the true author is unknown. The attribution to Barnabas is a traditional and pseudonymous ascription.

author_bio: Basil the Great, bishop of Caesarea, was a foundational theologian of Eastern Christianity, known for monastic rules, homilies, and treatises against Arianism.
author_notes: Extensive corpus with some dubia and spuria; solid attribution for major works.
representative_titles: Epistulae | Enarratio in prophetam Isaiam Dub. | Homiliae super Psalmos | Sermones de moribus a Symeone Metaphrasta collecti | Adversus Eunomium (libri 5) | Asceticon magnum sive Quaestiones (regulae brevius tractatae) | Homiliae in hexaemeron | Regulae morales

author_bio: Basilides (possibly a Stoic philosopher or grammarian) is known from a single fragment. He may be the same as or distinct from Iamblichus's teacher Basilides, but the evidence is minimal.
author_notes: The name Basilides is common; this fragment may derive from a philosopher or a compiler, but identification is uncertain.

author_bio: A very obscure author known only from sparse fragments of uncertain provenance. The name Basilis is attested in lexicographical sources; no biographical information survives.
author_notes: Fragments are brief and lack clear context. Attribution is tentative.

author_bio: Basilius (Med. et Scr. Eccl.) is a little-known figure who combined medicine and ecclesiastical writing. The work 'De virginitate' suggests a monastic or ascetic context.
author_notes: The combination of medical and ecclesiastical interests is unusual; the author's identity and date are uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Christian ecclesiastical writer; possibly Basil of Seleucia or another homilist.
author_bio: Basilius is a late antique ecclesiastical writer credited with a corpus of homilies on feasts and saints, notably on St. Thecla; distinct from Basil of Caesarea.
author_notes: Disambiguated as ecclesiastical writer (not Basil the Great). Large corpus.
representative_titles: Sermones xli | De vita et miraculis sanctae Theclae libri ii Sp. | Homilia in sanctum pascha et in recens illuminatos | Homilia in assumptionem domini | Homilia in sanctum Andream | Homilia in sanctum pascha | Homilia in passionem domini | Homilia in pentecosten

author_bio: Bato was a comic poet of Middle or New Comedy, known from a few fragments preserved in later anthologies.
author_notes: Labeled 'Comic.' to distinguish from other Batos; fragments are few and brief.

author_bio: Bato is a historian and rhetorician known only from fragmentary citations; his works, possibly including historical and rhetorical treatises, are lost.
author_notes: The label 'Hist. et Rhet.' is a modern scholarly designation; no secure biographical information.

author_bio: A mock-heroic epic poem parodying the Iliad, describing a war between frogs and mice. It was traditionally attributed to Homer in antiquity but is now recognized as the work of an unknown poet. The title itself serves as the catalog entry.
author_notes: Author entry is the work title; the poem is anonymous. The TLG code includes two variant versions.

author_bio: A Babylonian priest of Bel and historian who wrote a Greek-language history of Babylon, the Babyloniaca.
author_notes: Surviving only in fragments and citations; variant spelling Beros(s)us reflects uncertainty in transmission.

author_bio: Bias of Priene was one of the Seven Sages of Greece, traditionally known for sayings and maxims. Only a single fragment and a testimonium survive under his name.
author_notes: Bias is a figure of legend; the surviving texts are very brief and their authenticity is uncertain.

author_bio: Bion of Phlossa (or Smyrna) was a Hellenistic bucolic poet, known for his 'Lament for Adonis' (Epitaphius Adonis) and other poems; he is often grouped with Theocritus and Moschus.
author_notes: Little is known about his life; the biographical tradition is sparse. The source ID uses a CTS URN.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Epitaphius Adonis | Epithalamium Achillis et Deidameiae Sp.

author_bio: Bion of Soli was a Greek philosopher and writer, known for his Cynic-influenced moralizing literature. He was a prolific author of diatribes and prose works that influenced later Roman satire.
author_notes: The epithet 'Solensis' identifies his origin from Soli in Cilicia. Only fragments of his works survive, but his influence is notable in later Greek and Roman literature.

author_cultural_context: Greek historian, possibly of Proconnesian origin
author_bio: Bion was a Hellenistic historian, author of a work on the history of the island of Prokonnesos. Only fragments survive, cited by later authors.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Hist.' likely distinguishes him from the homonymous philosopher. The fragments are very short and attribution uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek author identified in sources as both a mathematician and philosopher. Little is known about this figure, and it is uncertain whether the same Bion is intended in both fields or if the name groups distinct individuals. Only testimonia survive.
author_notes: The source label 'Bion (Math. et Phil.)' ambiguously combines a mathematician and a philosopher of the same name. There is no clear evidence that these represent a single historical figure. The TLG record classifies this as a single author entry.

author_bio: A Greek philosopher and writer, likely of the Cynic or Peripatetic school, known for moral diatribes and fragments.
author_notes: The qualifier 'Phil.' distinguishes this Bion from the poet Bion of Smyrna; his philosophical works survive only in fragments.

author_bio: An obscure figure known only from a single inscription; no further biographical details are available.
author_notes: The sole evidence is a very short inscription, making it difficult to assess historicity or identity.

author_bio: Biotus is known only from a single fragment; nothing can be securely asserted about his life or work.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary source; historicity unconfirmed.

author_bio: Biton is a shadowy Hellenistic author known only for a single surviving technical treatise on the construction of siege engines (katapults). His work reflects the advanced military engineering of the Hellenistic period.
author_notes: Little is known of Biton; his name and work are preserved in a single manuscript. The date and provenance are conjectural.

author_bio: Blaesus is a name associated with a single Greek poetic fragment. No biographical information survives.
author_notes: Single fragment; author's identity and period are speculative.

author_bio: Boeo (or Boio) is a semi-legendary figure, often credited as a poet or priestess in early Greek tradition. Little is known, with fragments attributed to her/him concerning mythological subjects. The historicity is uncertain, but the persona is ancient.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary references; name varies (Boeo, Boio); status as historical person impossible to verify.

author_bio: A minor Greek epigrammatist, possibly from Sidon, known from two short pieces: one epigram in the Greek Anthology and a funerary inscription. The disambiguator 'Epigr.' distinguishes him from other figures named Boethus (e.g., the sculptor and the philosopher).
author_notes: Very sparse evidence; identity and dates are uncertain. The corpus consists of only 44 words across two texts.

author_bio: Boethus (Phil.) is known only from fragments of a philosophical work. His exact identity and dates are uncertain; he may belong to the early Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Phil.)' indicates an uncertain attribution within philosophical literature; the surviving fragments (728 words) offer limited biographical or doctrinal context.

author_bio: Boeus is a name known from a single fragment, possibly from a lost Boeotian epic or local history. The figure's historicity is shadowy, and the surviving text is very brief.
author_notes: Referenced in later sources; actual identity unclear.

author_bio: Known from a single testimonium; possibly a poet or writer, but nothing else is known.
author_notes: Only a testimonium (a mention in another author) survives; the name is rare and no work fragments exist.

author_bio: Boiscus is a Greek author known only from a single surviving fragment of a work. The name is rare, and no biographical details are attested. The fragment's content may suggest a poetic or prose context.
author_notes: Single fragment survival; no independent biographical testimony.

author_bio: Bolus of Mendes was a Hellenistic writer on natural sciences and magical lore, whose works often circulated under the name of Democritus. His fragments discuss sympathetic principles and alchemical techniques.
author_notes: Fragments are attributed to Bolus but often transmitted under pseudonyms; source ID includes testimonia.
representative_titles: Physica et mystica (fort. opus Χειρόμηκτα) (sub nomine | Περὶ συμπαθειῶν καὶ ἀντιπαθειῶν (sub nomine Democriti) | Ad Leucippem (sub nomine Democriti) Sp. (e cod. Paris. B.N. gr. 2327, fol. 258r) | Fragmenta | Testimonia | Testimonium

author_bio: A shadowy early Pythagorean, possibly a son of Pythagoras or a disciple. Survives only in testimonies and a dubious fragment.
author_notes: Attributed by later sources; fragment is of uncertain authenticity.

author_bio: Collection of fictional letters attributed to Marcus Junius Brutus, likely a rhetorical or literary exercise from the early Imperial period.
author_notes: The heading is a title of a corpus, not a person; letters are pseudonymous and not by the historical Brutus.

author_bio: Bryson, a Greek philosopher known only from a single fragment. Possibly associated with the Megarian school or else a Sophist; disputed identity.
author_notes: Very little evidence; the fragment comes from a late source. May be confused with Bryson of Heraclea.

author_bio: Bucolicum is a modern editorial label for an anonymous bucolic fragment (P. Vindob. 29801). The text, titled 'Pan et Echo,' is a Greek poetic fragment of uncertain authorship, found on papyrus.
author_notes: 'Bucolicum' is not an author but a modern descriptor for an anonymous bucolic fragment. The parenthetical 'Pan et Echo' is the modern title. Use author_agent_kind=work_title.
representative_titles: Fragmentum bucolicum: Pan et Echo (P. Vindob. 29801)

author_bio: Butas is a shadowy ancient Greek author known only from a single fragment. Nothing can be stated with certainty about the author's identity or dates; the name may preserve a corrupt or obscure tradition.
author_notes: The meager attestation (14 words) makes classification extremely uncertain. The name could be a misreading or a pseudonym.

author_bio: Butherus is a scarcely attested Greek author known only from a single fragment. Nothing certain can be said about his life or works; his name appears in lexicographical or grammatical sources.
author_notes: The fragment's origin and authenticity are uncertain. The name may be a corruption or unique mention in a secondary source.

author_bio: Valentinus was a prominent Gnostic teacher and poet, active in Alexandria and later Rome. He founded the Valentinian school of Gnosticism. Only a single hymn fragment survives.
author_notes: Well-known as a heresiarch, but the surviving poetic work is very brief (34 words). Attribution of the hymn is circumstantial.

author_bio: Valerius Apsines was a Greek rhetorician, author of an *Ars Rhetorica* and a work on figured problems; active in the Athenian rhetorical tradition.
author_notes: The name Valerius may indicate Roman citizenship; works survive in part and attribution is sometimes debated.
representative_titles: Ars rhetorica | Περὶ τῶν ἐσχηματισμένων προβλημάτων

author_bio: Roman-era Greek poet who composed fables in iambic verse, known as the 'Mythiambi Aesopici'. He adapted Aesopic material for a literate audience.
author_notes: Probably named Valerius Babrius; his work survives in two main manuscript traditions.

author_bio: A collection of words or sermons on Christ from the Scriptures, possibly by a monk named Theodosius. The work is transmitted under a descriptive Latin title and the author's identity is uncertain.
author_notes: The label 'Verba in scripturis de Christo' is a descriptive title, and the manuscript notes suggest possible authorship by a monk Theodosius. Classified as 'work_title' because the author slot is filled by the textual heading, not a confirmed person.
representative_titles: Verba in scripturis de Christi natale, epiphaniis et pentecoste (fort. auctore quodam Theodosio monacho) (e codd.

author_bio: Vettius Valens was a Greek astrologer from Antioch whose Anthologiarum libri ix (Anthology) is a major source for Hellenistic astrology. He wrote in Greek despite his Roman name.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; his work survives in full.

author_bio: The Latin title 'Vita Adam et Evae' (also known as the Apocalypse of Moses) designates a pseudepigraphal work recounting the lives of Adam and Eve after the expulsion from Eden. The text is transmitted in Greek and other versions, with anonymous authorship.
author_notes: Author slot occupied by the work title 'Vita Adam Et Evae'; this is a textual corpus, not a person. The work is pseudepigraphal and attributed to no individual.
representative_titles: Vita Adam et Evae (sub titulo Apocalypsis Mosis)

author_bio: An anonymous collection of the Life and Sayings of Secundus the Silent Philosopher, a text blending philosophical biography with gnomic wisdom.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work; the text is anonymous.

author_bio: The 'Vita Sophoclis' is a biographical text about the playwright Sophocles, transmitted under the name of various scholars. Its author is unknown, and the text itself occupies the author slot in the citation system.
author_notes: The source label is a title, not a personal name; it designates the anonymous biographical work traditionally attributed to no single author.

author_bio: Vitae Aeschinis is a textual corpus consisting of biographical accounts of the orator Aeschines. The work is an anonymous compilation used as a source for ancient biographical tradition.
author_notes: The title stands in for the anonymous author; this is a conventional cataloging practice.

author_bio: The 'Vitae Aesopi' are a body of Greek prose narratives recounting the life of the legendary fabulist Aesop. Composed and redacted over many centuries (from ca. 1st century CE to Byzantine times), these biographies combine anecdotal, folkloric, and fictional elements and transmit various recensions, including the Vita G, Vita W, and the Planudean version.
author_notes: The author entry is the title of the work/collection, as it is a composite, multi-authored, and multi-recensional corpus. Not attributed to a single author.
representative_titles: Vita G (e cod. 397 Bibliothecae Pierponti Morgan) (recensio 3) | Vita W (vita Aesopi Westermanniana) (recensio 2) | Vita Pl vel Accursiana (sub auctore Maximo Planude) (recensio 1) | Vita (fort. auctore Aphthonio) | Βιβλίον μυθικὸν τοῦ Αἰσώπου, εἰκονίζον ἀπὸ τῶν ἀλόγων ζῴων

author_canonical_name: Vitae Arati et varia de Arato
author_cultural_context: Greek scholarly and biographical tradition on Aratus
author_bio: This is a composite manuscript corpus comprising prolegomena, vitae, scholia, and various treatises on Aratus's Phaenomena, compiled from multiple anonymous sources. It encompasses diverse materials once attributed to authors such as Achilles Tatius, Leontius Mechanicus, and others, but is treated as a unified textual collection.
author_notes: The source label combines multiple works formerly attributed to different authors but now viewed as anonymous compilations. It is cataloged as a work title occupying the author slot.

Paris. suppl. gr. 607A) | Excerpta varia de phaenomenis Arati | Sphaera (olim sub auctore Empedocle) | Vita Arati (= Vita 1) (olim sub auctore Achille Tatio) (e cod. Vat. gr. | Prolegomena in Aratum (= Περὶ ἐξηγήσεως) (olim sub auctore | De zodiaco (olim sub auctore Leontio Mechanico)

author_bio: Vitae Aristotelis is a modern editorial label for a collection of anonymous biographies of Aristotle, notably the Vita Marciana and the Vita vulgata. These texts are treated as a single authorial entry in the TLG.
author_notes: The entry represents a corpus of biographical texts, not a single author. 'Vitae Aristotelis' is a work‑title occupying the author slot.
representative_titles: Vita Marciana (post epitomen Ptolemaeeae vitae) (cod. Marc. gr. 257) | Vita vulgata (post epitomen Ptolemaeeae vitae)

author_bio: Collection of anonymous biographical notices (vitae) of the poet and geographer Dionysius Periegetes (2nd century CE). These texts are found in manuscripts and serve as prefatory material to his 'Periegesis'.
author_notes: The heading refers to a set of texts (Vitae), not a personal author.

author_bio: The 'Vitae Hesiodi Particula' is a brief, fragmentary ancient biography of the poet Hesiod, preserved in a single manuscript witness. It serves as a textual item occupying the author slot in the catalog.
author_notes: This entry is a fragmentary text, not a personal author. It is cataloged under the title of the work itself because its original author is unknown or the text is anonymized in transmission. The genre is biographical but the text is preserved without a named author.

author_bio: The *Vitae Homeri* (Lives of Homer) is a collective designation for a group of ancient and medieval biographies of the legendary poet Homer. These texts, often anonymous and compiled from various sources, provide legendary and scholarly accounts of Homer's life and works.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the textual corpus, not by a person. Multiple distinct vitae are included (e.g., Vita Herodotea, Sudae vita).
representative_titles: Plutarchi De Homero 2 | Vita Herodotea | Sudae vita | Plutarchi De Homero 1 | Plutarchi vita | Vita Proculea (e Chrestomathia) | Vita sexta | Vita quinta

author_bio: A corpus of biographical texts (Vitae) devoted to the poet Oppian, compiled and transmitted in Byzantine manuscripts. The lives offer varying accounts of Oppian's origin and career, blending historical tradition with hagiographic and rhetorical topoi.
author_notes: The 'author' entry is a modern cataloging construct for a collection of anonymous and attributed Lives of Oppian; no single author exists.
representative_titles: Vita Oppiani (Vita β) | Vita Oppiani Anazarbensis (e cod. phil. gr. 135 in Bibl. nat. in Vindob.) | Vita Oppiani (Vita α) | Vita Tzetziana (e codd. Laur. gr. 31.3, Ambros. gr. C 222 inf., Vat. gr. 1345)

author_display_name: Vitae Pindari Et Varia De Pindaro
author_canonical_name: Vitae Pindari et varia de Pindaro
author_date_range: compiled 9th–15th c. CE, with earlier sources
author_bio: A composite work transmitting ancient biographies and miscellaneous scholarly notes about the poet Pindar. This corpus includes materials from various sources, possibly compiled by Byzantine scholars such as Thomas Magister or Moschopoulos.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the work itself; this is a standard cataloging practice for anonymous compilations transmitted under a descriptive label.
representative_titles: Vitae Pindari et varia de Pindaro

author_bio: The Vitae Prophetarum is a collection of lives and legends of the biblical prophets, transmitted in multiple recensions under the names of Dorotheus and others. The author slot is occupied by the textual corpus itself.
author_notes: The header 'Vitae Prophetarum' functions as a title or corpus label. Traditional ascription to Dorotheus is pseudepigraphic. The TLG entry covers multiple recensions.
