author_bio: A Roman historian of the 2nd century BCE, who wrote in Greek about Roman history; fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: The author is identified as Gaius Acilius, a Roman senator and historian; works only survive in fragments.

author_bio: Gaius Asinius Quadratus was a Greek historian writing under the Roman Empire, author of a work on Parthian history and perhaps other fragments. His surviving fragments are brief.
author_notes: The name appears as 'Gaius (Asinius Quadratus)'. The parentheses disambiguate the fuller name. The fragments are preserved via Stephanus of Byzantium and other sources.

author_bio: Gaius Musonius Rufus was a Roman Stoic philosopher of the 1st century CE, whose lectures, preserved by his student Lucius, emphasize practical ethics and the equality of women.
author_notes: Display name includes 'Gaius' as a praenomen; the canonical name is Musonius Rufus.
representative_titles: Dissertationum a Lucio digestarum reliquiae | Epistulae spuriae | Fragmenta minora | Fragmentum

author_bio: Early Christian writer of uncertain identity; known for a fragmentary work discussing the canon and ecclesiastical traditions.
author_notes: The epithet '(Scr. Eccl.)' denotes an ecclesiastical writer; Gaius is a Roman name and the identification with the Roman presbyter Gaius (3rd cent. CE) is plausible but not certain.

author_cultural_context: Latin biographical and antiquarian literature (Greek works)
author_bio: Suetonius Tranquillus, the Roman biographer and antiquarian, also wrote some works in Greek, including treatises on Greek terms of abuse and on children's games, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: The Greek works are much less known than his Latin Lives; the source label 'Gaius' is the praenomen.
representative_titles: Περὶ βλασφημιῶν καὶ πόθεν ἑκάστη | Περὶ τῶν παρ’ Ἕλλησι παιδιῶν

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek physician and philosopher in the Roman Empire
author_bio: Galen of Pergamon was the most influential physician of antiquity, whose voluminous writings on medicine, anatomy, and philosophy dominated Western and Islamic medicine for over a millennium.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; extremely prolific author with many authentic and spurious works.
representative_titles: De usu partium | De methodo medendi libri xiv | De compositione medicamentorum secundum locos libri x | De simplicium medicamentorum temperamentis ac facultatibus libri xi | De compositione medicamentorum per genera libri vii | In Hippocratis aphorismos commentarii vii | De placitis Hippocratis et Platonis | De anatomicis administrationibus libri ix

author_bio: Geminus was a Greek mathematician and astronomer, author of the 'Elementa astronomiae', an influential introductory work on astronomy that survives largely intact.
author_notes: Date and place are approximate and debated; some scholars place him in the 1st cent. BCE, others slightly later.
representative_titles: Elementa astronomiae | Calendarium Sp.? | Fragmenta optica Dub.

author_cultural_context: Eastern Orthodox Christianity; patristic exegesis; Alexandrian and Antiochene synthesis
author_bio: Gennadius I was Patriarch of Constantinople (458–471) and an influential biblical exegete. He participated in the theological controversies following Chalcedon, writing commentaries on the Pauline epistles that survive only in catenae fragments. His work represents the intersection of Antiochene and Alexandrian traditions. The extant fragments are preserved in multiple chains, covering six New Testament epistles.
author_notes: Well-attested patristic figure; catena attribution is consistent. Source id via TLG.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Hebraeos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Galatas (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam i ad Corinthios (in catenis) | Fragmentum in epistulam ii ad Thessalonicenses (in catenis) | Fragmentum in epistulam ii ad Corinthios (in catenis)

author_bio: Geographica Adespota is a modern editorial label for a collection of anonymous geographic fragments in Greek, of uncertain authorship and date.
author_notes: 'Adespota' indicates that the fragments lack any clear authorial attribution; the label functions as a place-holder.

author_cultural_context: Agricultural manual, compiled under Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos
author_bio: A Byzantine compilation of agricultural lore drawing on earlier Greek and Latin sources. The text is a collective work, not attributable to a single author.
author_notes: The author field represents the title of the work; see also the Geoponica as a compilation.

author_bio: Georgius Monachus Continuatus refers to the anonymous continuation of the Chronicle of George the Monk. The continuation (redactio A) covers events up to the 10th century and is part of the Byzantine chronicle tradition.
author_notes: 'Continuatus' indicates that this is a continuation by one or more unknown authors. Treated as a collective work for cataloging.

author_bio: A little-known Byzantine author of a single hymn formerly attributed to Synesius; 'Peccator' is likely a penitential epithet, and virtually nothing else is known about him.
author_notes: The sole surviving work is a short hymn; authorship is uncertain and may be a misattribution.

author_bio: George of Pisidia (Georgius Pisides) was the most prominent Byzantine Greek poet of the early 7th century AD. He served as a deacon and possibly as a diplomat in Constantinople under Emperor Heraclius. His poetic works include panegyric epics celebrating Heraclius' campaigns (the 'Persian Expedition', the 'Bulgar War'), and theological poems such as the 'Hexaemeron'. His style influenced later Byzantine literature.
author_notes: Standard attribution is secure. This is a major Byzantine author with a well-preserved corpus, central to early Byzantine poetry.
representative_titles: De expeditione Persica | Bellum Avaricum | Heraclias | In Bonum patricium | Heracliadis iii acroaseos fragmenta | In restitutionem sanctae crucis | In Heraclium ex Africa redeuntem

author_bio: George Syncellus was a Byzantine chronicler and monk, author of the 'Ecloga chronographica', a world chronicle from Creation to Diocletian.
author_notes: Syncellus is a known historical figure; his work is preserved.

author_bio: A Byzantine historian, logothete, and diplomat. He wrote the Annales covering the period after the Fourth Crusade, and produced rhetorical, theological, and epistolary works.
author_notes: Well-attested Byzantine writer; the parenthetical '(Acropolites)' is his family name.
representative_titles: Annales | Historia in brevius redacta | Contra Latinos | Laudatio Petri et Pauli | Epitaphius in Joannem Ducam | In Gregorii Nazianzeni sententias | Epistula ad Joannem Tornicem | Epitaphius in Irenam imperatricem

author_bio: Georgius Cedrenus was a Byzantine monk and historian who compiled a world chronicle (Compendium historiarum) from creation to the late 11th century. His work is a key source for Byzantine history.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Cedrenus' distinguishes him from other Georgii; the work is a single large chronicle.

author_bio: A Byzantine grammarian and deacon active in Constantinople, Choeroboscus authored extensive commentaries on Theodosius' grammatical canons, as well as works on orthography, accentuation, and poetic tropes. His writings were foundational for later Byzantine scholarship.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Choeroboscus)' is a disambiguating epithet. Some works attributed to him are of doubtful authenticity, noted in the representative titles.
representative_titles: Prolegomena et scholia in Theodosii Alexandrini canones isagogicos de flexione verborum | Prolegomena et scholia in Theodosii Alexandrini canones isagogicos de flexione nominum | Epimerismi in Psalmos | De orthographia (epitome) (e cod. Barocc. 50) | Scholia in Hephaestionem | De spiritibus (excerpta) | Περὶ τρόπων ποιητικῶν [Dub.] | De accentibus (excerpta e Choerobosco, Aetherio, Philopono et aliis) (e codd. Urbin. 151 et Laurent. 57.34)

author_bio: A Byzantine grammarian associated with manuscript transmission of the Anacreontea, a collection of late antique lyric poetry in the style of Anacreon. Little is known of his life beyond his scribal or scholarly role.
author_notes: The label '(Gramm.)' indicates a grammarian; the identification is uncertain, and the name may be a scribal attribution. The single surviving work, the Anacreontea, is a collection of poems originally attributed to Anacreon but now considered later compositions.

author_bio: Georgius Monachus (George the Monk) was a Byzantine chronicler of the 9th century, author of a world chronicle from creation to his own day. His work was widely read and later continued.
author_notes: The appellation 'Chronogr.' denotes his chronicle; he is also known as George Hamartolus.

author_bio: Byzantine historian, philosopher, and polyhistor; author of a chronicle covering the reigns of Michael and Andronikos Palaiologos.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Georgius (Pachymeres) is the Byzantine historian George Pachymeres.
representative_titles: Συγγραφικαὶ ἱστορίαι (libri vii de Andronico Palaeologo) | Συγγραφικαὶ ἱστορίαι (libri vi de Michaele Palaeologo)

author_bio: George Sphrantzes was a Byzantine historian and diplomat; his chronicle is a key source for the fall of Constantinople in 1453.
author_notes: The 'Minus Sp.' indicates a shorter/original version of his chronicle; attribution is secure.

author_bio: A shadowy author named Glaucus, possibly a poet, grammarian, or medical writer, of whom only a few fragments survive. The name was common in antiquity, making secure identification impossible.
author_notes: The name Glaucus is common across genres; the fragments are too brief and ambiguous to assign a specific field or date. The attribution is purely conventional.

author_bio: Glaucus Rheginus is a legendary early Greek poet from Rhegium, known for a single surviving fragment. His historicity is uncertain and he exists mainly in tradition.
author_notes: Ancient sources provide little secure information; the name is associated with Rhegium.

author_bio: A collection of lexical glosses on Herodotus's Histories, preserved in two manuscript versions. The author is the work itself, recorded under its conventional title.
author_notes: The heading 'Glossae In Herodotum' is a title, not a person or collective. Author agent kind set to work_title as per instructions. Historicity status not_applicable because the agent is not a person.
representative_titles: Glossae in Herodotum (= Ἡροδότου λέξεις) | Glossae in Herodotum (= Ἡροδότου λέξεις) (litterarum ordine),

author_bio: The Gnomologium Vaticanum is a collection of moral sayings attributed to various philosophers and literary figures, compiled in the Byzantine period. It preserves many otherwise lost fragments of ancient thought.
author_notes: The text itself is the author entry; it is an anonymous compilation. The title clearly indicates a work, not a person.

author_bio: Gorgias is known only from a few fragments of a historical work. The name is common and it is unclear whether this is the same as any other Gorgias. The fragments treat historical topics, but the author's identity and date remain vague.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg2255. The qualifier '(Hist.)' indicates a historian. His work is preserved in indirect tradition. The name may coincide with other figures named Gorgias.

author_bio: Gorgias of Leontini was a celebrated Sophist and rhetorician, known for his pioneering contributions to prose style and rhetoric. He was influential in Athens and authored the 'Encomium of Helen' and other works.
author_notes: The label 'Rhet. et Soph.' clarifies his role. Gorgias is well-attested in ancient sources; the fragments and testimonia are carefully collected.

author_bio: Gorgon is likely a mythological figure or a name used in a literary context. The surviving fragments are too brief to determine if this Gorgon is a mythical being or a human author. The name evokes the Gorgon of myth, but the texts may be historical or genealogical.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg2357. The author label 'Gorgon' is ambiguous; it may refer to a mythical Gorgon or an obscure historian using the name. The fragments are too small to clarify.

author_bio: The Greek Anthology (Anthologia Graeca) is a comprehensive collection of ancient Greek epigrams and poems, compiled by Byzantine scholars, notably Constantine Cephalas in the 10th century. It includes works from the archaic through the Byzantine periods and is a cornerstone of Greek literary tradition.
author_notes: This entry is a work title: the anthology itself is the cataloged entity. The compilation involves multiple authors but is indexed under the collection's name.

author_bio: A shadowy Byzantine compiler of Greek proverbs, known under the simple name Gregorius. Several collections of paroemiae are attributed to him in different manuscripts; his identity and date remain uncertain, and the name may cover a tradition of excerptors rather than a single author.
author_notes: Disambiguation is difficult; 'Gregorius' is a common name and the works may be by more than one hand. The entry aggregates several manuscript paroemiographic collections.
representative_titles: Paroemiae (e cod. Mosq.) | Paroemiae (e cod. Leidense) | Paroemiae | Paroemiae (e cod. Vat.)

author_bio: Gregory of Nazianzus, also known as Gregory the Theologian, was a Cappadocian Father, Archbishop of Constantinople, and a prolific writer of theological orations, poetry, and letters. He is a key figure in the development of Trinitarian doctrine.
author_notes: The corpus includes genuine works and some dubious attributions (e.g., Christus patiens, possibly by Constantine Manasses).
representative_titles: Epistulae | Carmina moralia | Carmina de se ipso | Contra Julianum imperatorem 1 (orat. 4) | Funebris oratio in laudem Basilii Magni Caesareae in Cappadocia episcopi (orat. 43) | Christus patiens Dub. (fort. auctore Constantino Manasse) | Apologetica (orat. 2) | De vita sua

author_cultural_context: Christian theologian, Church Father, Bishop of Nyssa
author_bio: Gregory of Nyssa was a leading Christian theologian and Bishop of Nyssa, a key figure of the Cappadocian Fathers, known for his philosophical and mystical writings, including treatises against Eunomius, homilies on the Song of Songs, Ecclesiastes, and the Psalms, and works on the creation of humanity and the life of Moses.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with extensive surviving corpus.
representative_titles: Contra Eunomium | In Canticum canticorum (homiliae 15) | In inscriptiones Psalmorum | In Ecclesiasten (homiliae 8) | De vita Mosis | De opificio hominis | Antirrheticus adversus Apollinarium | Dialogus de anima et resurrectione

author_bio: Gregory Thaumaturgus (the Wonderworker) was a Christian bishop and theologian from Neocaesarea in Pontus. A student of Origen, he is known for his panegyric to Origen, a metaphysical paraphrase of Ecclesiastes, and canonical epistles. His hagiographical reputation as a miracle-worker shaped his posthumous tradition.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg2063. The Latinized name 'Gregorius Thaumaturgus' is standard. Some works are spurious. Biographical details are partly hagiographical.
representative_titles: In Origenem oratio panegyrica | Metaphrasis in Ecclesiasten Salamonis | In annuntiationem sanctae virginis Mariae (homiliae 1-2) Sp. | Ad Tatianum de anima per capita disputatio Sp. | Epistula canonica | Fragmenta in Jeremiam (in catenis) | Sermo in omnes sanctos Sp. | De fide capitula duodecim

author_bio: Gryllus is the name attached to a single papyrus fragment (P. Oxy. 2331). Nothing is known about this author; the name may be a pseudonym or a corruption.
author_notes: The sole surviving text (P. Oxy. 22.2331) provides no context for the author's identity. The name 'Gryllus' is otherwise unattested.

author_bio: Daimachus of Plataea was a Hellenistic historian known for works on Indian and Sicilian history. Only fragments of his writings survive, cited by later authors such as Athenaeus and Pliny.
author_notes: Multiple homonymous figures exist; the identification with Plataea is conventional but not certain.

author_bio: Daimachus of Plataea was a Hellenistic historian and writer of fragmentary works. Only a few fragments of his writings survive, and he is known primarily through citations in later authors.
author_notes: The author is historically attested but only fragments remain, with 1 work and low word count. Attributed to Plataea.

author_bio: Damascius was the last head of the Neoplatonic Academy in Athens. He wrote commentaries on Plato and a major metaphysical work, *De principiis*, as well as the biographical *Vita Isidori*, which preserves details of late antique philosophical circles.
author_notes: Attested historical figure; works survive in fragmentary and complete forms.
representative_titles: In Parmenidem | De principiis | In Phaedonem (versio 1) | Vita Isidori (ap. Sudam, Hesychium, Photium et e cod. Vat. 1950) | In Philebum | In Phaedonem (versio 2) | Vita Isidori (ap. Photium, Bibl. codd. 181, 242) | Epigramma

author_bio: Damastes of Sigeion was a Greek logographer and historian active in the 5th century BCE, known for works on genealogy and mythology, now surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Damastes is sometimes confused with other early historians; fragmentary evidence limits our knowledge of his works.

author_bio: Late antique author of an optical treatise (Optica), possibly a teacher of philosophy or mathematics. His work synthesizes earlier geometric optics.
author_notes: Scarcely attested outside the Optica; identity uncertain.

author_bio: A legendary figure associated with magical and lapidary lore, Damigeron is credited with a Greek treatise on the properties of stones, known as De lapidibus, which survives in a few manuscripts.
author_notes: The attribution to Damigeron is traditional and likely pseudepigraphic; the work is a late antique compilation.
representative_titles: De lapidibus (e codd. Vat. gr. 578 + Ambros. 95 sup.)

author_cultural_context: Spartan or Greek author known from sparse fragments.
author_bio: Damippus is a fragmentarily preserved author of unknown genre, possibly Spartan, known only from a few citations.
author_notes: 524 words; uncertain historical details.

author_bio: Damon and Phintias were a pair of Greek Pythagorean philosophers from Syracuse. The story of their mutual loyalty (legendary rather than historical) is recounted by Cicero and others. Only a single testimony survives for their biography and works.
author_notes: The entry 'Damon (et Phintias)' reflects a joint testimony; the existence of either figure is poorly attested and heavily legend-shaped. The combined single work may preserve only a brief anecdote.

author_bio: Damon the historian is a barely known Greek author, preserved only in a single fragment. Nothing certain is known of his life or works beyond the existence of this text.
author_notes: The label '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Damon from the more famous Damon the musician. The fragment is of uncertain origin.

author_bio: Damon the musician was a renowned Athenian music theorist and sophist, often associated with Pericles. He wrote on the ethical and political effects of music; his doctrines survive only in fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: The label '(Mus.)' distinguishes him from Damon the historian (tlg2273). His historical role is attested in Plato and other sources.

author_bio: Damoxenus was a poet of Middle or New Comedy whose few fragments show typical comic themes of the late 4th century BCE.
author_notes: Attested by Athenaeus and in the Suda; fragments are limited but his comic identity is established.

author_bio: A lost epic poem on the story of the Danaids, known only from a single fragment. Possibly part of the Epic Cycle or a separate local epic.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of a lost epic. The attribution is to the work itself.

author_bio: Daphitas is a figure known only from a single epigrammatic fragment. He is associated in ancient sources with a story about mocking kings and being punished—possibly a legendary or apocryphal grammarian.
author_notes: Name appears in ancient glosses and anecdotes; historicity is uncertain. The single fragment is very brief (15 words).

author_bio: David (often called David the Invincible in Armenian tradition) is a Neoplatonic commentator on Aristotle and Porphyry. His works are influential in both Greek and Armenian philosophical traditions.
author_notes: Attribution of works to David is debated; possibly a conflation of multiple individuals. Used 'person' with historical status based on scholastic tradition.
representative_titles: In Porphyrii isagogen commentarium | Prolegomena philosophiae

author_bio: De Arboribus Avibusque Fabulae is a textual title referring to a literary composition known only from a Heidelberg papyrus fragment. The original author is not identified.
author_notes: The title occupies the author slot due to the lack of a named author; the fragment is preserved as P. Heidelb. 222.

author_bio: Dei(l)ochus is a Hellenistic author of two short fragmentary works. The alternative spelling with or without 'l' reflects manuscript ambiguity; he is considered a real person of the Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The two fragments (one from each of two works) are very brief. The name variation may indicate different persons or a scribal error.

author_bio: Demades was an Athenian orator and politician active in the 4th century BCE. Known for his wit and political pragmatism, he was a leading figure in Athens after the Lamian War. Only fragments of his speeches survive, preserved through quotations by later authors.
author_notes: The TLG source ID includes a URN. Demades is a well-attested historical figure, though his oratorical corpus is fragmentary.

author_bio: Demaratus is known as a Spartan historian or genealogist, cited by later authors. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Attested only in fragments; identification and biography are uncertain.

author_bio: Demareta is known from a single inscription (titulus). The name suggests a female figure, possibly the author or dedicant of the text.
author_notes: The record consists of a single word (titulus), allowing almost no reconstruction of the author's identity or work.

author_bio: A letter attributed to Demetrius of Phalerum, a 4th-3rd c. BCE Athenian statesman and Peripatetic. The letter is spurious, likely composed in the Hellenistic or Imperial period.
author_notes: The work title occupies the author slot; the historical Demetrius did not write this letter.

author_bio: Demetrius Callatinus was a Greek historian or scholar, known only from fragments of his work. His epithet 'Callatinus' likely refers to a place or a personal characteristic, but his exact identity and output remain obscure.
author_notes: Very sparse attestation; the single work 'Fragmenta' is a modern collection of surviving citations.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek Peripatetic philosopher, statesman, and orator
author_bio: Demetrius of Phaleron was an Athenian statesman and Peripatetic philosopher. He is traditionally credited with the rhetorical treatise 'On Style' (De elocutione).
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; authorship of De elocutione is debated but traditional.

author_bio: Greek comic poet known only from fragments and titles; his work is part of the Hellenistic comic tradition.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Demetrius (Comic.) is a fragmentary comic poet; little biographical information survives.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian known only through fragments, possibly active in the late Hellenistic period. He is cited by later lexicographers and scholiasts for observations on language and style.
author_notes: The label 'Gramm.' distinguishes this Demetrius from other homonyms; the biographical information is minimal and indirect.

author_bio: A Hellenistic figure known only through fragments, possibly a historian and philosopher. His identity is unclear; the fragments may derive from one or multiple authors of the same name. The designation reflects ambiguity in the source tradition.
author_notes: The parentheses indicate a disambiguating label applied by modern scholarship; the fragments are very scanty and the person cannot be securely identified with any known historical Demetrius.

author_bio: A historian of uncertain identity known only from fragments. The label '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Demetrius from other homonymous figures. No secure biography exists.
author_notes: The TLG attests only one fragmentary work; identification with known historical persons is uncertain.

author_bio: Demetrius is a name assigned to a younger comic poet attested only by fragmentary evidence; his works survive in minimal textual remains.
author_notes: The identifier 'Junior Comic.' distinguishes this Demetrius from other individuals of the same name; attribution is uncertain and based on limited testimony.

author_bio: A Hellenistic poet and philosopher known only from a single fragment and a title. Likely distinct from Demetrius of Phalerum and other more famous Demetrii.
author_notes: The author label 'Demetrius (Poet. Phil.)' is ambiguous; it likely refers to a minor Demetrius known only from a fragment. The TLG source ID tlg1301 supports this identification as a distinct author.

author_bio: A shadowy Greek poet known only from a single fragment, possibly of Hellenistic date. The epithet 'Poeta' distinguishes him from other Demetrii in the literary tradition.
author_notes: Existence and identity are uncertain; known only from a brief fragment. The designation '(Poeta)' is a modern editorial convention to differentiate from homonyms.

author_bio: The author of the treatise 'On Style' (Peri hermeneias) and two collections of epistolary models. His identity is uncertain: he may be Demetrius of Phaleron, but the work is more likely from a later hand, and is often referred to as Pseudo-Demetrius.
author_notes: The designation (Rhet.) is conventional. The works are transmitted under the name Demetrius, but the author's historical identity is disputed. The two epistolary collections include spurious examples.
representative_titles: Formae epistolicae | Formae epistolicae (duo exempla spuria)

author_bio: Demetrius (Trag.) is a shadowy figure known only from a single fragmentary title and a list of dramatis personae. Possibly a Hellenistic or Imperial-era tragedian, but nothing certain is known about his biography.
author_notes: Extremely sparse evidence; historicity uncertain. The parenthetical 'Trag.' denotes genre, not a secure identifier.

author_bio: Demochares was an Athenian political figure, historian, and orator of the early Hellenistic period, and a nephew of Demosthenes. He was prominent in his time.
author_notes: Source label 'tlg1303' is clear for Demochares. No ambiguity; the name is a standard personal name.

author_bio: Democles (Comicus?) is a Greek comic poet known only from scattered fragments. Surviving evidence is limited to two fragments, suggesting a minor figure in Greek comedy.
author_notes: The TLG author ID assigns this name to a comic poet; almost nothing is known about him. The designation 'Comic.' is tentative.

author_bio: A shadowy figure known only from a single historical fragment, Democritus (Hist.) may have been a local historian or an author whose work survived only as a quotation. Little can be securely stated about his life.
author_notes: The disambiguator 'Hist.' separates him from the philosopher Democritus; the authorship and date of the fragment are debated.

author_bio: Democritus of Abdera was a pre-Socratic Greek philosopher who, with his teacher Leucippus, formulated a comprehensive atomic theory of the universe. He also wrote on ethics, mathematics, and natural philosophy; only fragments survive.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Phil.)' disambiguates from other figures named Democritus (e.g., a comic author). The surviving works are limited to testimonia and fragments.

author_bio: Demodocus of Leros or Cos is a minor Hellenistic poet, author of a few epigrams and fragments. His works are included in the Greek Anthology tradition, though his identity is shadowy.
author_notes: Not to be confused with the Homeric bard Demodocus. The historical Demodocus is known only through a few epigrams and citations.

author_bio: A Greek historian or mythographer from the late Classical or early Hellenistic period. He compiled local histories and antiquarian works, fragments of which survive. He is sometimes considered distinct from other authors named Demon.
author_notes: The name 'Demon' is a Greek personal name, not a demonic figure. The author is known only from fragments and testimonia; identification can be uncertain due to multiple homonymous authors.

author_bio: Demonax was a Cynic philosopher, known primarily from the biography by Lucian; his own works are lost.
author_notes: Identified as the Cynic philosopher Demonax; 'Phil.' indicates a philosopher.

author_bio: Demonax, a tragic poet, is known from minimal fragments.
author_notes: The 'Trag.' indicates the genre; extant fragments are very short.

author_bio: Demonicus is a name associated with fragmentary Greek texts, possibly a rhetorician or philosopher. The identity is uncertain, with no secure biographical details.
author_notes: Known only through a single short fragment; the name may be a misattribution or obscure historical figure.

author_bio: Demosthenes is known only from fragments of an Epicurean work; nothing certain can be said about his life or school affiliation.
author_notes: The label 'Epic.' is a modern disambiguator; the historicity of this Demosthenes is uncertain and the fragments are brief.

author_bio: Preeminent Athenian statesman and orator, known for his Philippics and other political speeches opposing Philip II of Macedon. A canon of ten Attic orators.
author_notes: (Orat.) disambiguates from other Demosthenes; canonical author with extensive extant corpus.
representative_titles: De falsa legatione | De corona | In Midiam | In Aristocratem | In Timocratem | Adversus Leptinem | Exordia | In Neaeram Sp.

author_bio: Dercyllus is a name known only from fragmentary sources, possibly a legendary or semi-legendary figure. The surviving fragments are brief and provide no secure biographical details.
author_notes: Label 'Dercyllus' appears in fragmentary tradition; likely a legendary personage. Very little evidence exists to confirm historicity.

author_bio: Dexicrates was a poet of Middle or New Comedy in Athens, known from a single fragment that survives via citation. Very little is known of his life; his name appears in ancient sources, and his work is attested only in fragments.
author_notes: Only a few testimonia and fragments survive; the identification as a historical poet is based on ancient citations.

author_bio: Dexippus was a Greek historian and philosopher, known for his commentary on Aristotle's Categories. He is likely the same as the historian Dexippus of Athens.
author_notes: Works preserved; some identification with the historian Dexippus remains debated.

author_bio: Diagoras of Melos was a Greek poet and philosopher known for his atheistic views, for which he was condemned in Athens. He was lampooned in Aristophanes' 'Clouds' and became a proverbial 'atheist.' Only a few fragments of his poetry survive.
author_notes: The TLG lists him with a single fragment (23 words); the identification of Diagoras as an atheist is based on later testimonia, and the authenticity of the fragment is debated.

author_bio: The Dialexeis, also known as the Dissoi Logoi, is a short anonymous Sophistic treatise from the late 5th or early 4th century BCE, exploring contradictory arguments on ethical and philosophical topics.
author_notes: The display name includes both the title and a parenthetical rendition; the source labels this as a work, not a person, hence classified as work_title.

author_bio: Dicaearchus of Messana was a Greek Peripatetic philosopher, historian, and geographer. A student of Aristotle, he wrote on political theory, cultural history, and the geography of the Greek world, though only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; his works survive only in fragmentary form, but his intellectual influence is documented by later ancient authors.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek tragedian or dithyrambic poet?
author_bio: Dicaeogenes is a little-known Greek poet, possibly a tragedian or dithyrambist. Only a few fragments attributed to him survive.
author_notes: Very sparse evidence; identity and genre are uncertain.

author_bio: Dictys Cretensis is the purported author of a diary of the Trojan War, likely a Hellenistic or Roman invention. The surviving Greek fragments and Latin translation represent a late antique literary fiction posing as an eyewitness account.
author_notes: The name is a pseudepigraphic attribution; the fragments may derive from a lost Greek original attributed to a fictional Dictys.

author_cultural_context: Early Christian catechetical and liturgical tradition
author_bio: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles) is an early Christian treatise containing instructions on ethical conduct, baptism, fasting, prayers, and church order. It is one of the earliest extant non‑canonical Christian texts, probably composed in Syro-Palestinian Jewish-Christian circles. It was used as a manual for catechumens and local congregations.
author_notes: The entry 'Didache' is a title occupying the author slot; the text itself is an anonymous compilation, not an individual author. One of the most important sub‑apostolic witnesses to early Christian liturgy and ethics.

author_bio: Didymarchus is a minor ancient Greek author known only through a single fragmentary title. The name suggests a possible connection to the commentator Didymus, but the evidence is too meager to establish any firm identification or literary profile.
author_notes: The author is known only from a single lemma with a word count of 4, making reliable classification difficult. The name may be a variant or corruption of another author.

author_bio: Didymus the Blind was a prominent Alexandrian theologian and commentator on the Old and New Testaments. Blind from childhood, he was a leading figure in the catechetical school of Alexandria. His extensive commentaries, preserved in part, show deep engagement with Classical and Christian traditions.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg2102. Certain works attributed to Didymus are spurious (marked Sp.). His commentaries were rediscovered in papyrus finds.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in Psalmos (e commentario altero) | Commentarii in Zacchariam | In Genesim | Commentarii in Psalmos 29-34 | De trinitate (lib. 3) Sp. | Commentarii in Psalmos 35-39 | Commentarii in Psalmos 40-44.4 | Commentarii in Psalmos 22-26.10

author_bio: Didymus Grammaticus (Didymus Chalkenteros) was the most prolific scholar of the Alexandrian tradition, nicknamed 'Bronze-Guts' for his immense output. He wrote commentaries on Demosthenes, Plato, and many other authors, surviving in papyrus fragments and later citations.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Gramm.' distinguishes him from other Didymi. His scholarly role and historical identity are well-attested.
representative_titles: In Demosthenem (P. Berol. 9780) | De dubiis apud Platonem lectionibus Sp.

author_bio: An otherwise unknown Greek author of a short technical treatise on the measurement of marble and lumber, possibly a practical manual for builders or surveyors. The name 'Didymus' is common, and this figure is distinct from the famous Alexandrian scholar.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Scriptor De Mensuris)' is a modern disambiguator. Nothing certain is known about this Didymus beyond the single work.

author_bio: A physician of unknown origin, cited by Pliny and probably active in the 4th century BCE; only a fragmentary reference survives.
author_notes: Attested only through a brief citation; historicity plausible but uncertain.

author_bio: Dieuchidas of Megara was a Greek historian of the 4th century BCE, known for his work on the history of Megara. Only fragments of his writings survive, preserved in later authors such as Pausanias and Diogenes Laertius.
author_notes: Survives only in fragmentary quotations; the historical status is secure but the biographical details are sparse.

author_bio: Dinarchus was an Athenian logographos (speechwriter) and the last of the Ten Attic Orators. His surviving speeches, notably against Demosthenes, are key sources for late classical Athenian politics.
author_notes: Attested historically; works survive fragmentarily and in partial manuscripts.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | In Demosthenem | In Aristogitonem | In Philoclem | Fragmenta incertae sedis

author_bio: A shadowy figure known only from a few fragments (possibly historical or rhetorical). The name Dinias appears in a handful of citations, but no reliable biographical information survives.
author_notes: The author is known only from fragments and a single testimonium; the identity is uncertain and may be confused with other individuals of the same name. The corpus is too small for confident attribution.

author_bio: Dinolochus is a name associated with Sicilian comedy, possibly as a son or associate of Epicharmus; extant fragments are sparse and attribution is uncertain, suggesting a pseudonymous or conventional figure in the comic tradition.
author_notes: Attested only in later sources; may be a generic name for a type of comic poet.

author_bio: A Greek historian, known for a work on Persian history (Persica). Dinon is cited by later authors and provides important source material for Achaemenid Persia.
author_notes: Fragments survive via citation. His relationship to the historian Cleitarchus is debated. Some sources confuse him with his son.

author_bio: Dio Chrysostom, a Greek orator and philosopher from Prusa (Bithynia), flourished under the Roman Empire. His extensive corpus of speeches and essays blends rhetoric, moral philosophy, and political commentary, reflecting the culture of the Second Sophistic.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the source ID is standard TLG.

author_bio: A Greek author referred to in fragments, possibly a comic poet or a medical writer. The scanty remains make a secure identification difficult.
author_notes: The name Diocles is common; without a qualifying epithet, the fragments may belong to multiple individuals. Use with caution.

author_bio: An obscure Greek writer from Aspendus, known only from a single surviving fragment. Likely active in the 4th century BCE; nothing further is securely attested.
author_notes: The name suggests a historical figure, but the fragment is too brief to confirm specific details of life or work. The source may be a later citation.

author_bio: Diodorus Sardianus was a Greek epigrammatist from Sardis, known from a few epigrams and an inscription.
author_notes: Attested by a few epigrams and a titulus; the name suggests origin in Sardis.

author_bio: Diodorus Siculus authored the universal history Bibliotheca Historica in 40 books, of which 1–5 and 11–20 survive, covering mythic times to Gallic Wars. His compilatory method preserves lost sources.
author_notes: Major historian of the late Hellenistic period; work includes substantial fragments of lost books.
representative_titles: Bibliotheca historica (lib. 1-20) | Bibliotheca historica (lib. 21-40) | Fragmenta sedis incertae

author_bio: A fragmentary comic poet of the Greek New Comedy period. Diodorus is known only from quoted fragments and a few testimonia. His plays are lost; the surviving pieces offer glimpses of plot and language.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Comic.)' indicates the comic genre. Attribution relies on fragment collections; some fragments may be uncertain.

author_bio: Diodorus is known only from a single titulus (inscription), possibly of a funerary or dedicatory nature. No other literary remains exist.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Eleg.)' likely indicates an epigram or elegiac inscription. The author may be conflated with other figures named Diodorus. Agent kind assigned as person tentatively.

author_bio: Diodorus Periegetes was a Greek author of geographical or periegetic works, known only from fragments and later references.
author_notes: The author is known only from fragments; the 'Perieg.' epithet distinguishes him from other figures named Diodorus.

author_bio: A Diodorus known only from a single surviving fragment, thought to be a philosopher. The (Phil.) disambiguation separates him from other Diodori, but no firm biographical details exist.
author_notes: Fragment likely preserved in a doxographical tradition. The identity is highly uncertain; may be confused with Diodorus Cronus or another thinker.

author_bio: A Diodorus known only from a single rhetorical fragment ('On Two Men'), likely a declamation exercise. Nothing else is known about his life or background.
author_notes: The epithet 'Rhet.' is modern and distinguishes this Diodorus from other homonyms; historicity is uncertain due to lack of biographical data.

author_bio: Diodorus is known as a Christian writer (scr. eccl.), likely active in the late antique period, perhaps writing biblical commentaries, as indicated by the fragment on Romans.
author_notes: Identifier 'tlg4134' gives 'Diodorus (Scr. Eccl.)' to distinguish this figure from other Diodori. His identity is uncertain; there is ambiguity concerning his exact authorship.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis)

author_bio: Diogenes of Babylon (also known as Diogenes Babylonius) was a Stoic philosopher and a student of Chrysippus. He later headed the Stoa and was part of the famous embassy of philosophers to Rome in 155 BCE. His works survive only in testimonia and fragments.
author_notes: The epithet 'Babylonius' refers to his origin; his works are known mainly through later doxographical sources.

author_bio: Diogenes Laertius was a biographer and doxographer of the Greek philosophers. His 'Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers' is a crucial source for the history of ancient philosophy, preserving numerous fragments and anecdotes. He also composed epigrams, some of which survive.
author_notes: The author ID urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0004 distinguishes this from other Diogeneses. The work count of 2 reflects the division into the 'Vitae philosophorum' and the 'Epigrammata' within the TLG corpus.

author_bio: Diogenes of Apollonia was a Pre-Socratic philosopher known for his materialist theory that air is the fundamental substance (archê) of all things, and for his work in natural philosophy and physiology.
author_notes: Fragmentary survival; identity and authenticity of fragments established through doxographical sources.

author_bio: Diogenes of Oenoanda was a wealthy Epicurean who commissioned a monumental inscription in the marketplace of Oenoanda, summarizing Epicurean philosophy for the public. The fragments, rediscovered in the 19th and 20th centuries, constitute one of the longest Greek philosophical inscriptions and provide a unique witness to later Epicureanism. The author is known solely from this inscriptional text.
author_notes: Attestation is entirely archaeological/epigraphic; well-documented in Lycian context.

author_bio: An obscure Greek historian known only from fragmentary references; little biographical information survives.
author_notes: The label 'Hist.' indicates a historian, but precise identity and dates are uncertain. The single fragment is very brief.

author_bio: Diogenes of Sinope (Diogenes the Cynic), the famous Cynic philosopher, also wrote tragic plays (now fragmentary); the extant fragments are attributed to him but debated in authenticity.
author_notes: The 'Phil. et Trag.' disambiguates from other Diogeneses; the attribution of the tragic fragments to Diogenes the Cynic is contested; some may be by a different Diogenes.

author_bio: Obscure Greek philosopher (or philosophical writer) known only from a single testimonium. The brief heading suggests an otherwise unattested Diogenes (not the Cynic or the doxographer).
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Phil.)' indicates classification as a philosopher but no further identification is possible. Author agent kind set to person as the heading refers to an individual, but historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Diogenes (Trag.) is a shadowy figure known from a single tragic fragment; he may be confused with Diogenes of Athens or other namesakes.
author_notes: (Trag.) indicates a tragedian; the fragment is isolated, and historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian and paroemiographer, possibly Diogenianus of Heraclea, whose collection of proverbs survives in Byzantine epitomes. His identity and dates are uncertain.
author_notes: The '(Gramm.)' disambiguates from other Diogeniani (e.g., the Peripatetic). Attribution is based on epitomes; original authorship is uncertain.
representative_titles: Paroemiae (epitome operis sub nomine Diogeniani) (e cod. Mazarinco) | Paroemiae (epitome operis sub nomine Diogeniani) (e cod. Vindob. 133)

author_bio: Diogenianus was a Greek grammarian and lexicographer of the 2nd century CE, known for his work on Homeric vocabulary and possibly for compiling a collection of proverbs. He is often distinguished from contemporaries with the same name by the epithet 'Phil.' (philosophus).
author_notes: The 'Phil.' disambiguation suggests a philosopher identity; surviving fragments limit full assessment.

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to the Cynic philosopher Diogenes of Sinope, but widely considered pseudepigraphical and later in composition. The work presents moral and satirical themes in epistolary form.
author_notes: The display name is a Latin title (Genitive + 'Epistulae'), so author_agent_kind is set to 'work_title'. The attribution is traditional and pseudonymous.

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to a certain Dio (perhaps Dion of Prusa or another Dio). The authorship is uncertain, and the heading reflects the title as the organizing principle.
author_notes: The label is a cataloging convention for a work whose author is not clearly identified. 'Dionis Epistulae' literally 'Letters of Dio' but the ascription is problematic.

author_bio: Dionysius Metathemenos (Διονύσιος Μεταθέμενος) is a poorly known Greek author, possibly a compiler or adapter, of whom only fragments survive. The epithet 'Metathemenos' may indicate a reworker or translator of source texts.
author_notes: The asterisk in the display name indicates an emendation or uncertainty; only one fragmentary work is attested.

author_bio: Hellenistic historian from Argos, known only from a single fragment preserved in later sources. Little is known of his life or works.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; the ethnicon 'Argivus' may indicate origin or association. Attribution uncertain.

author_bio: Dionysius of Chalcis is known from fragments of a local history; the dating and scope of his work remain uncertain.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary sources; likely a local historian of Chalcis.

author_bio: Dionysius Chalcus was an Athenian poet and orator, known for composing sympotic and political elegies. Few fragments survive from his work.
author_notes: Referenced in ancient sources such as Plutarch.

author_bio: Dionysius of Corinth was a 2nd-century bishop of Corinth, known from Eusebius for his letters; only a brief inscription (titulus) survives.
author_notes: Very sparse remains; the identification as the bishop is conjectural.

author_bio: Dionysius I of Syracuse (c. 432–367 BCE) was a Greek tyrant, military leader, and patron of the arts. He also wrote tragedies, but only fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; literary output fragmentary.

author_bio: Dionysius Iambus was a Hellenistic scholar and poet, associated with the Alexandrian tradition. Only a single fragment of his work survives.
author_notes: The author is known only from a fragment; the epithet 'Iambus' suggests a possible connection to iambic poetry.

author_bio: Dionysius II was the ruler of Syracuse in the 4th century BCE, known as a patron of learning and a minor literary figure. Only a tiny fragment of his work survives, a single word-count of 12, suggesting possibly a quotation or spurious attribution.
author_notes: Label 'Dionysius II' likely refers to the Syracusan tyrant, but the surviving fragment (12 words) may be a misattribution or a brief citation. Historicity is certain for the ruler, but authorship of the fragment is uncertain.

author_bio: Dionysius of Byzantium was a Greek historian and geographer of the Roman period, known for his work describing the Bosporus region. His 'Per Bosporum navigatio' is a geographical periplus.
author_notes: Well-attested author with surviving work.

author_bio: Dionysius of Halicarnassus was a Greek historian and rhetorician active in Rome during the reign of Augustus. He wrote the 'Roman Antiquities' and several critical essays on Attic orators, influential in later rhetorical theory.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; extensive corpus.
representative_titles: Antiquitates Romanae | Ars rhetorica Sp. | De Demosthenis dictione | De compositione verborum | De Thucydide | De compositione verborum (epitome) | De Lysia | De Isocrate

author_bio: Dionysius of Samos (Dionysius Samius) was a Hellenistic author who wrote on local Samian history or mythology. His fragments survive in citations by later authors.
author_notes: Known from limited fragments; commonly cited by later grammarians.

author_cultural_context: Hellenistic Greek lyric and geographic poetry
author_bio: Minor Greek poet from Scymnus of Chios, known from a single fragmentary line.
author_notes: Attribution and name form uncertain; only one line extant.

author_bio: Dionysius Scytobrachion ("Leather-arm Dionysius") is a shadowy figure credited with mythographic and historical works. His biography is heavily conflated with legend, and his fragments suggest a combinatorial approach to earlier mythological traditions.
author_notes: Ancient sources are contradictory; the name may be a literary invention or a conflation of several individuals. Fragments only.

author_bio: Dionysius Thrax (c. 170–90 BCE) was a Hellenistic grammarian from Alexandria, author of the influential *Ars grammatica* (Τέχνη γραμματική), the first systematic grammar of Greek. A student of Aristarchus, his work became foundational for Western linguistic tradition.
author_notes: Attested historical figure; authorship of the *Ars grammatica* is secure, though some later interpolations are debated.

author_bio: A comic poet from the Greek comedic tradition, known from fragments and ancient testimonia.
author_notes: The qualifier 'Comic.' distinguishes this Dionysius from others of the same name; only fragments survive.

author_bio: Obscure poet referred to as an epic writer; known only from sparse fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: Designation 'Epic.' suggests epic genre, but no secure biographical data; possibly a Hellenistic figure.

author_bio: Dionysius (Geogr.) is likely Dionysius Periegetes, author of a geographical poem describing the inhabited world. His sole surviving work, 'Descriptio Graeciae', exists in a fragmentary state.
author_notes: The identifier '(Geogr.)' aligns with the geographer Dionysius Periegetes, but a different homonym cannot be excluded.

author_bio: A Greek historian known only from fragments, possibly distinct from the more famous Dionysius of Halicarnassus. The surviving material is too sparse to reconstruct his historical work with confidence.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Hist.)' disambiguates this Dionysius from other authors of the same name. Attribution of the fragments is uncertain.

author_bio: Dionysius Periegeta is the author of a hexameter Orbis descriptio, a geographic poem describing the known world. His work influenced late antique and Byzantine geography.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Perieg.)' indicates his role as a periegete, or traveler/geographer.
representative_titles: Ixeuticon sive De aucupio (paraphrasis) (olim sub auctore Eutecnio) | Orbis descriptio | Lithiaca vel lithica (fragmenta)

author_region: Eastern Roman Empire (likely Syria or Palestine)
author_bio: Dionysius is a poorly attested ecclesiastical writer, known from a few fragments of theological or exegetical content. His identity remains obscure, possibly a monk or bishop from the late antique period.
author_notes: The 'Scr. Eccl.' label indicates a Christian author. The fragmentary state of the works and the common name make precise identification difficult.

author_bio: Dionysius the Sophist was a Greek orator and letter-writer, known from a collection of rhetorical epistles. His identity is linked to the Second Sophistic movement.
author_notes: Label 'Soph.' identifies him as a sophist; distinction from other Dionysii is not fully certain.

author_bio: An ancient Greek comic poet known only from extant fragments. The surviving works, a few fragments and a single fragmentum, indicate activity in the comic genre, likely during the Hellenistic period, though no secure dates or biographical details are recorded.
author_notes: Designated 'Comic.' to distinguish from other Diophantoi; the fragmentary nature of the corpus limits assessment of prominence.

author_bio: Diophantus (Hist.) is a Greek historian known only from a single surviving fragment. The epithet distinguishes him from the mathematician Diophantus and other namesakes. The fragment likely comes from a historical work, but no further biographical information is available.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' label indicates a historian, but the identity is highly uncertain; only one fragment survives.

author_bio: Diophantus of Alexandria was a Hellenistic mathematician whose 'Arithmetica' pioneered the study of algebraic equations and number theory. His work, comprising six surviving books of the original thirteen, introduced syncopated notation and methods for solving indeterminate equations, earning him the epithet 'father of algebra.' His treatises on polygonal numbers and various fragments further attest to his computational prowess.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Math.)' is a modern cataloging disambiguation. Diophantus's historicity is well-established through internal and external references, though the precise dates remain debated.
representative_titles: Arithmeticorum libri sex | De polygonis numeris | Fragmentum Sp. (e cod. Paris. gr. 2448) | Fragmentum Sp. (e cod. Paris. 453) | Fragmentum Sp. (e cod. Paris. suppl. gr. 387, fol. 181r)

author_bio: An obscure author known only from a single fragment; no further biographical details survive.
author_notes: TLG entry with minimal surviving text; identity and period uncertain.

author_bio: Greek physician and pharmacologist from Anazarbus, author of the influential De materia medica, a standard reference on medicinal substances for centuries.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; major work De materia medica survives in multiple recensions.
representative_titles: De materia medica | Euporista vel De simplicibus medicinis | De materia medica (recensiones e codd. Vindob. med. gr. 1 + suppl. gr. 28; Laur. 73, 41 + 73, 16 + Vind. 93) | Περὶ παιωνίας καὶ ὅσα ποιεῖν δύναται (e cod. Matrit. Bibl.

author_bio: Dioscorus was a Byzantine Greek grammarian or poet known from papyrus fragments. His works include occasional poetry and grammatical writings.
author_notes: Source ID tlg2121 collects papyrus fragments; Dioscorus is sometimes associated with Dioscorus of Aphrodito, but identification is not secure.

author_bio: A Greek physician and pharmacologist, possibly the same as Dioscorides of Anazarbus, but the attribution is uncertain. The surviving fragments discuss medicinal plants and remedies.
author_notes: The name is ambiguous: may refer to the famous Dioscorides or a different figure; fragments are sparse.

author_bio: Diotimus was an Epicurean philosopher and poet, known for epigrams and fragments. His works reflect Epicurean themes and are preserved in anthologies and inscriptions.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Epic.)' indicates an Epicurean affiliation, distinguishing him from other Diotimi. Attribution of epigrams is sometimes uncertain.

author_bio: Diotimus (Phil.) is a philosophical author cited in a few testimonia. He may be identical with Diotimus the Stoic or another figure, but the evidence is too fragmentary for secure identification.
author_notes: The name is preserved only in testimonia; the 'Phil.' disambiguator suggests a philosopher, but no works survive. The historical existence is plausible but poorly documented.

author_bio: Diotogenes is a Greek author cited in Neoplatonic sources. He may have written on Pythagorean philosophy. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragments preserved by later authors; identity and date are uncertain.

author_bio: Dioxippus is a little-known author from whose works only a few fragments survive (143 words). The name appears in Greek literary traditions, but no secure biographical details are known.
author_notes: Two fragments survive with a total word count of 143. The author is not securely identified in historical sources. Status set to 'uncertain' due to fragmentary evidence.

author_bio: Comic poet of New Comedy, originally from Sinope but active in Athens. He wrote around 100 plays; only fragments survive. Plautus adapted several of his comedies.
author_notes: The suffix '(Comic.)' distinguishes this writer of comedy from other figures named Diphilus.

author_bio: Diphilus is known from a single surviving fragment, possibly an epigram or iambic poem. The disambiguation suggests two genres: epic and iambic.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; limited evidence for authentication.

author_bio: Discipulorum Cantiuncula (Latin: 'Little Song of the Disciples') is a collective designation for a fragmentary text, possibly a choral or didactic piece, surviving on papyrus (P. Med.). The authorship is attributed to a group of disciples.
author_notes: Label 'Discipulorum Cantiuncula' is a Latin title identifying the author slot as a collective group. No individual person is known; the fragment is likely from a school or performance context.

author_bio: Dius is known only from a single fragment of an historical or antiquarian work. No biographical details survive; the name is rare.
author_notes: Single fragment (290 words). The name Dius may appear in FGrHist; the work's subject is unknown.

author_bio: Greek historian of the 3rd-2nd c. BCE, author of a lost historical work covering Hellenistic events.
author_notes: Known only from a few fragments; identification is uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek patristic florilegium (anthology) of excerpts from the Church Fathers, possibly compiled by Anastasius of Sinai or Anastasius the Apocrisiarius. It treats doctrinal questions, especially Christology.
author_notes: The source heading is the title of the anthology; authorship is attributed (tentatively) to a known figure, hence the work_title designation with the note.
representative_titles: Doctrina patrum (fort. auctore Anastasio Sinaïta vel Anastasio Apocrisiario)

author_bio: Domitius is known from philological and historical fragments. He may have been a grammarian or historian, but the surviving material is too fragmentary for a secure profile.
author_notes: The fragments are brief and without secure contextual identification.

author_bio: Dorieus is known only from a single epigram preserved in the Greek Anthology. Nothing certain is known of his life, and his identity may be confused with other figures of the same name.
author_notes: The name 'Dorieus' is ambiguous and may refer to several historical persons; the attribution of the epigram is uncertain.

author_bio: Hellenistic astrologer whose lost hexameter poem on astrology survives in fragments and through later compilations, notably by Hephaestion.
author_notes: Attribution reliant on fragments; precise dating uncertain.
representative_titles: Fragmenta Graeca | Fragmenta e Hephaestionis Ἀποτελεσματικῶν libris hausta | Fragmenta alia antiqua

author_bio: An obscure Greek historian, perhaps from Rhodes or Crete, known only from a few fragments. His work may have dealt with local Sicilian or Rhodian history; almost nothing is certain.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' in the name indicates a historian; but multiple persons named Dosiadas exist. The fragments are too sparse to confirm a single historical figure.

author_cultural_context: Greek lyric poetry, Hellenistic literary experimentation
author_bio: Dosiadas was a Greek lyric poet known for composing technopaignia and epigrams, including the pattern poem 'Altar' (Βωμός).
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Lyr.)' distinguishes from other figures named Dosiadas; his corpus is very small.

author_bio: Dositheus is a shadowy Hellenistic historian known only from a few fragments. He may have been a Jewish or Greek author writing on Jewish history; his identity is uncertain and the name is common in the period.
author_notes: Fragments only; identification is highly uncertain. The parenthetical '(Hist.)' distinguishes him from other figures named Dositheus.

author_bio: Dositheus was a grammarian who wrote a bilingual (Greek-Latin) grammar, the *Ars grammatica*, used for language instruction in Late Antiquity.
author_notes: Identification as 'Magister Gramm.' (teacher of grammar) clarifies his profession. Work partially preserved.

author_bio: Dromo is a minor Greek comic poet of the New Comedy period, known from a handful of a fragments and titles recorded in ancient catalogues.
author_notes: Attribution is based on brief citations; possibly an Alexandrian scholar's classification.

author_bio: Ducas, a Byzantine historian, wrote the *Historia Turcobyzantina*, covering the late Byzantine period and the rise of the Ottoman Empire. Probably active in the 15th century, his work is a key source for the fall of Constantinople and subsequent events.
author_notes: Author identified as the historian Ducas; no further personal details are secure.

author_bio: Duris of Samos was a Hellenistic historian and tyrant of Samos, known for his 'Macedonica' and 'History of Agathocles'. He emphasized vivid, emotional narrative and was a pupil of Theophrastus. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: Major source for Hellenistic history, but fragmentary.

author_bio: Eccelus is a shadowy Hellenistic author known only from a single fragmentary text. Nothing certain is known of his life or works.
author_notes: The name is extremely rare in extant sources; the fragment is possibly a later fabrication or misattribution.

author_bio: Echecrates is a minor figure known from a few ancient testimonia, possibly a philosopher or rhetorician of the late Classical period. No works survive; the source label likely refers to a person mentioned by other authors.
author_notes: Source (TLG 2242) provides only one work labeled 'Testimonia' (69 words). The name 'Echecrates' appears in ancient texts (e.g., Plato's Phaedo as a Pythagorean), but the referent here may be a different individual; the classification as historical is tentative.

author_bio: Echembrotus is a semi-legendary figure from the early Greek tradition, said to have been an aulos player and poet who won at the Pythian Games. Only a short fragment survives.
author_notes: Attested in a single fragment and ancient testimonies; the historicity is speculative.

author_bio: Echephylidas is a shadowy figure known only from a few fragments; no firm biographical details are available.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary sources; author's historicity cannot be confirmed.

author_bio: Ecphantides was an Athenian comic poet of the 5th century BCE, a contemporary of Cratinus. He belonged to the Old Comedy tradition, though only fragments and titles of his works survive, offering limited glimpses of his style and themes.
author_notes: Testimonia and fragments collected in PCG; known from later lexicographers and scholiasts.

author_bio: Ecphantus is a figure associated with the Pythagorean tradition, known from spurious fragments on kingship (Περὶ βασιλείας). He is often considered a legendary or pseudonymous author.
author_notes: The fragments on kingship are considered spurious; the figure belongs to the pseudo-Pythagorean tradition, with uncertain historicity.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous Greek elegiac fragments, of uncertain authorship. The label 'Adespota' indicates works without ascribed author.
author_notes: Explicitly labeled as adespota (anonymous); no author attribution available.

author_bio: Anonymous or fragmentary Greek elegiac poems whose author is unknown.
author_notes: Adespota indicates authorship unknown; fragments only.

author_bio: A Neoplatonist commentator on Aristotle and Porphyry, active in Alexandria; his commentaries survive and were influential in the transmission of Aristotelian logic.
author_notes: Formerly identified as 'David'; modern scholarship distinguishes Elias as a distinct commentator. His works are substantial, especially the commentary on the Categories.
representative_titles: Eliae (olim Davidis) in Aristotelis categorias commentarium | In Porphyrii isagogen | Commentarius in Aristotelis analytica priora

author_bio: Pre-Socratic philosopher and poet from Acragas (Agrigento) in Sicily. He developed a cosmological system based on the four roots (earth, air, fire, water) and the forces of Love and Strife, and is credited with founding the Sicilian school of rhetoric. His hexameter poem 'On Nature' and the purificatory 'Katharmoi' survive in substantial fragments.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a body of surviving fragments. The epigram is a later attribution.

author_bio: Julian (Flavius Claudius Julianus), known as Julian the Apostate, was Roman emperor from 361 to 363 CE. A prolific writer in Greek, he produced speeches, letters, and treatises advocating a revival of traditional Hellenic religion and philosophy against Christianity.
author_notes: Abundant extant corpus with secure authorship.
representative_titles: Epistulae | Περὶ τῶν τοῦ αὐτοκράτορος πράξεων ἢ περὶ βασιλείας | Ἐγκώμιον εἰς τὸν αὐτοκράτορα Κωνστάντιον | Contra Galilaeos | Πρὸς Ἡράκλειον κυνικὸν περὶ τοῦ πῶς κυνιστέον καὶ εἰ πρέπει | Misopogon (sc. Ἀντιοχικὸς ἢ Μισοπώγων) | Εἰς τὸν βασιλέα Ἥλιον πρὸς Σαλούστιον | Εὐσεβίας τῆς βασιλίδος ἐγκώμιον

author_bio: Encomium Ducis Thebaidos is a fragmentary Greek papyrus poem discovered at Oxyrhynchus, praising a military leader of the Thebaid region. Its author is unknown, and the work itself occupies the author slot in the manuscript tradition.
author_notes: The heading 'Encomium Ducis Thebaidos' is a modern editorial title for a single papyrus fragment; no real author exists. Agent kind set to work_title.

author_bio: Eparchides is a Greek author known only from a single fragment. Nothing certain about his life or work survives.
author_notes: Single fragment; identity and period are speculative.

author_bio: Ephippus was an Athenian comic poet of the Middle Comedy period, known from fragments that parody mythological themes and philosophers.
author_notes: Fragments preserved via Athenaeus; entries in the Suda confirm his existence and place in Middle Comedy.

author_bio: Greek historian from Cyme, author of a universal history in 30 books, now lost, but extensively used by later writers such as Diodorus Siculus.
author_notes: Ephorus is a well-known historian; his works survive only in fragments and testimonia.

author_cultural_context: Syriac and Greek Christian patristic literature
author_bio: Ephraem Syrus was a prominent Christian theologian, poet, and hymnographer. Though he wrote primarily in Syriac, many of his works survive in Greek translation, including ascetic sermons, prayers, and moral exhortations.
author_notes: Historical figure; the Greek corpus is extensive and includes works of uncertain authenticity.
representative_titles: Sermones paraenetici ad monachos Aegypti | De paenitentia | Ad imitationem proverbiorum | Precationes e sacris scripturis collectae, quarum pleraequae sunt Sancti Ephraim, pro iis qui uolunt suam ipsorum | Institutio ad monachos | Capita centum. Quomodo quis humilitatem sibi comparet | Sermo asceticus | In sermonem, quem dixit dominus, quod: In hoc mundo pressuram habebitis, et de perfectione hominis

author_bio: Ephraem was a Byzantine historian and poet, known for a chronicle that covers events from the creation to his own time, written in political verse.
author_notes: Chronicle is attributed to Ephraem; identity as historian and poet is secure.

author_region: Eastern Roman Empire (possibly Syria or Palestine)
author_cultural_context: Early Christian Greek hagiography and miracle literature
author_bio: A Christian writer, possibly from the Syriac tradition, author of a work on the miracle of Clement of Rome. The name 'Ephraem' is common and may be confused with Ephrem the Syrian.
author_notes: The label 'Scr. Eccl.' indicates a church writer; this Ephraem is distinct from the better-known Ephrem the Syrian. Attribution rests on a single work.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous epic Greek fragments, gathered from various sources. These are poems that cannot be assigned to a known author.
author_notes: The label 'Epica Adespota' explicitly denotes anonymous epic fragments. The parenthetical '(Ca)' likely indicates a collection or corpus designation.

author_bio: A collection of unattributed epic fragments preserved on papyri, including encomia, ethopoeiae, and historical fragments from Roman Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean.
author_notes: The label 'Epica Adespota' groups anonymous epic texts; many are fragmentary and without secure authorship.

Berol. 5003) | Encomium Heraclii ducis (PSI 3.253) | Mercurius mundi et Hermupolis magnae conditor (P. Argent. 481 [= | Encomium iambicum (P. Vindob. gr. 29788b) | Fragmentum epicum historicum (P. Argent. 480 [= P. | Exercitationes ethopoeiacae (collectio C. Graves)

author_bio: Epica Incerta is a conventional scholarly label for unidentified or fragmentary Greek epic verses, attributed as a corpus rather than to a specific poet.
author_notes: This entry represents a classification of uncertain epic fragments, not a person or known work title.

author_bio: Epicharmus was a Sicilian comedian and philosopher considered a pioneer of Attic comedy. He wrote plays in Doric Greek and also philosophic gnomic poetry; fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Core figure in Greek comedy. The tradition also includes pseudepigrapha; separate labels used for genuine and spurious fragments.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Fragmenta Epicharmi | Testimonia | Fragmenta Pseudepicharmea | Fragmentum Epicharmi

author_bio: Epicrates was a Greek comic poet of the Middle Comedy period, known from fragments preserved in Athenaeus and other sources. His plays, such as 'Ambassadors' and 'Antaeus', mock philosophers and contemporary fashions. He was active in Athens.
author_notes: The TLG entry includes two works under this name, indicating reasonable survival of fragments. The identification as a Middle Comic poet is secure.

author_bio: Epictetus was a Greek Stoic philosopher who taught in Rome and later in Nicopolis. His teachings were recorded by his student Arrian in the *Discourses* and the *Enchiridion* (Manual). He emphasized ethics, self-control, and the dichotomy of control, influencing later thinkers from Marcus Aurelius to modern philosophy.
author_notes: The works are transmitted via Arrian. The gnomologia and the letter are secondary collections. The main corpus is secure.
representative_titles: Dissertationes ab Arriano digestae | Enchiridion | Dissertationum Epictetearum sive ab Arriano sive ab aliis digestarum fragmenta | Gnomologium Epicteteum (e Stobaei libris 3-4) | Gnomologium Epicteteum (e Stobaei libris 1-2) | Arriani epistula ad Lucium Gellium

author_bio: Epicurus was the founder of Epicureanism, a Hellenistic school of philosophy that taught that pleasure (ataraxia) is the highest good and that the gods take no interest in human affairs. His surviving works include three letters and a collection of maxims.
author_notes: The corpus includes authentic letters and sayings as well as later doxographic materials; some fragments are preserved by Diogenes Laërtius.
representative_titles: Deperditorum librorum reliquiae | Epistula ad Herodotum | Epistularum fragmenta | Epistula ad Pythoclem | Ratae sententiae | Epistula ad Menoeceum | Gnomologium Vaticanum Epicureum | Incertae sedis fragmenta

author_bio: Shadowy figure known only from a few fragments; perhaps a comic poet or philosopher; identity uncertain.
author_notes: Name appears in ancient sources but with conflicting attributions; insufficient evidence for reliable historicity.

author_bio: A lost epic poem of the Theban cycle, attributed in antiquity to Homer or Antimachus, but surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: The entry refers to the epic poem 'Epigoni', not to a named author; the title occupies the author slot.

author_bio: A poorly known Greek poet cited in later sources, possibly from Old or Middle Comedy. Only fragments survive, making any biographical reconstruction tentative.
author_notes: Three surviving fragments (68 words total); identity and date are speculative.

author_cultural_context: Early Greek religious and oracular literature, Cretan myth-history
author_bio: Epimenides is a semi-legendary Cretan seer, poet, and purifier from the archaic period, credited with oracular poems and a theogony. His historicity is uncertain but he is an important figure in early Greek intellectual history.
author_notes: The figure is known mainly from later biographical tradition; his surviving fragments are likely spurious or heavily reworked.

author_bio: A set of Byzantine epimerisms (grammatical breakdowns) on Homer. The collection includes the Epimerismi Homerici and related scholia, compiled by anonymous scholars.
author_notes: The author heading is a genre descriptor. The content is compiled from multiple sources.

author_bio: Epinicus is a little-known author of two fragmentary works. The name suggests a connection to comic or sympotic poetry, but no biographical details survive. The fragments are preserved through later grammatical and lexicographical sources.
author_notes: Epinicus is known only from fragmented citations; context is insufficient to determine his genre or region with confidence.

author_bio: Bishop of Salamis (Constantia) in Cyprus. A key heresiologist, his Panarion is an extensive refutation of heresies from Simon Magus to his own day. Also wrote biblical and liturgical works.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; works transmitted in multiple manuscripts.
representative_titles: Panarion (= Adversus haereses) | Ancoratus | De mensuris et ponderibus | Testimonia ex divinis et sacris scripturis (= De divina | Homilia in divini corporis sepulturam Sp. | Anacephalaeosis Sp. | De prophetarum vita et obitu (recensio prior) Sp. | Liturgia praesanctificatorum

author_bio: This entry represents the letter from the martyrs of Lyon (Lugdunum), an early Christian account of persecution in 177 CE. The text survives in fragmentary form and is an important witness to early Christian narrative and theology.
author_notes: Author slot is a work title: the letter itself is the catalog entry, not an individual author.

author_bio: The Epistle to Diognetus is an anonymous early Christian apology defending Christianity against pagan and Jewish criticisms; notable for its literary elegance and theological depth.
author_notes: The author is unknown; the work is conventionally listed under its title. It survives in a single 13th-century manuscript. The work is often classified as a protreptic or apologetic letter.

author_display_name: Epistula Ecclesiarum Apud Lugdunum Et Viennam
author_cultural_context: Early Christian letter from the churches of Gaul
author_bio: The Epistula Ecclesiarum Apud Lugdunum Et Viennam is a letter from the Christian communities of Lyon and Vienne describing the persecution of 177 CE. It is a collective work, not attributable to a single author.
author_notes: The title functions as the author entry in the catalog; the text is a communal letter.
representative_titles: Epistula ecclesiarum apud Lugdunum et Viennam

author_bio: The 'Epitaphium Abercii' is the funerary inscription of Abercius (Avircius Marcellus), a bishop of Hieropolis in Phrygia. It is a famous early Christian inscription, composed in Greek verse, describing Abercius's travels and faith. The text itself occupies the author slot in cataloging, as the author of the epitaph is the subject.
author_notes: Cataloged under the title of the inscription because the actual author is the subject Abercius; the text is self-referential. This is a work-title entry.

author_bio: The 'Epitaphium Pectorii' is an early Christian funerary inscription in Greek, named after its first word (or the name of the deceased). It is a text, not an author, and is cataloged under its title.
author_notes: The source label is the title of an inscription; no author is known, so it is classified as 'work_title'.

author_bio: Erasistratus of Ceos was a renowned physician and pioneer of anatomy and physiology in the Hellenistic period. His experimental approach and discoveries on the circulatory and nervous systems influenced Galen. Only testimonia and fragments of his writings survive.
author_notes: The corpus consists of testimonies and fragments collected from later authors.

author_bio: Third head of the Library of Alexandria, celebrated mathematician, geographer, and poet. His Catasterismi (constellation myths) circulated under his name but later underwent epitomization.
author_notes: The Catasterismi is transmitted via epitomes; other fragments include astronomical and geographical works.
representative_titles: Catasterismi | Fragmenta | De circa exornatione stellarum et etymologia de quibus videntur | Fragmentum Eratosthenicum

author_bio: Greek grammarian and lexicographer from Byblos, known for works on word meanings; extant in later epitomes.
author_notes: Name appears with variant spelling; works survive only in fragments and epitomes; historical but details uncertain.
