author_bio: Ergias is a shadowy figure from whom only a single fragment survives, possibly related to local histories of Mytilene or the Greek East. Very little is known about his life or exact dates.
author_notes: The single fragment attribution is uncertain; the author may be a later invention or a pseudonymous citation.

author_cultural_context: Hellenistic Greek poetry, female literary tradition
author_bio: Erinna was a Hellenistic Greek poetess, famed in antiquity for her poem 'The Distaff' (Ἠλακάτη), a lament for her friend Baucis. Only fragments survive; her work shows influence of Sappho and demonstrates refined Hellenistic style.
author_notes: Historical person, though the exact dates are uncertain; ancient testimonia confirm her existence.

author_bio: A poet of Middle Comedy, known only from a few fragments and testimonia. His name and fragments suggest a comic playwright active in Athens.
author_notes: Attested in ancient lists of comic poets; very few fragments survive, making his historical profile uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian and lexicographer active in the early Roman Empire, best known for his Vocum Hippocraticarum collectio, an alphabetical glossary of Hippocratic terms. He may have been affiliated with the medical school in Rome or Alexandria.
author_notes: Erotianus is well-attested as a lexicographer; his work survives in manuscript and is cited by later medical authors.

author_cultural_context: Greco-Roman Egypt and broader Roman Empire
author_bio: A modern editorial label for a corpus of anonymous or fragmentary Greek erotic novels and novelistic fragments, including pieces like the Tefnut papyrus, Metiochus and Parthenope, and Sesonchosis. These texts survive on papyri and represent the lost or fragmentary tradition of the ancient Greek novel.
author_notes: The name 'Erotica Adespota' (Anonymous Erotic Works) is a modern scholarly construct. The fragments themselves are of widely varying authorship and date.

Oxy. 42.3010) | Fragmenta (fort. pars operis Chione vel Ninus) (P. Berol. 10535 + 21234)

author_bio: Erycius is a little‑known Greek author of a single fragment (4 words). Nothing else survives, and his identity as a person is accepted on the basis of the manuscript attestation.
author_notes: The fragment is so short that Erycius may be a phantom name from a corrupt tradition.

author_bio: An alphabetic etymological lexicon, transmitting Byzantine-era word origins. Not the work of a known individual.
author_notes: Single-work entry; author field represents the text title.

author_bio: A Byzantine etymological lexicon compiled in the 9th century. The Etymologicum Genuinum is one of the earliest and most important medieval Greek etymological dictionaries, drawing on earlier lexicographic and grammatical sources.
author_notes: The work is an anonymous compilation, conventionally cited by its title. The TLG treats it as a single author entry.
representative_titles: Etymologicum genuinum (ἀνάβλησις ‐‐ βώτορες) | Etymologicum genuinum (α ‐‐ ἁμωσγέπως) | Etymologicum genuinum (littera λ)

author_bio: The Etymologicum Gudianum is a Byzantine Greek etymological lexicon, compiled ca. 11th–12th century CE, drawing on earlier lexica and scholia. It survives in multiple manuscripts and contains glosses, etymologies, and word definitions, with additions from various periods. The work is valuable for the study of ancient and medieval Greek vocabulary and textual transmission.
author_notes: The title 'Etymologicum Gudianum' is used as the author entry, as it represents a named lexicographical work, not a person. The source label is unambiguous.
representative_titles: Etymologicum Gudianum (ζείδωρος ‐‐ ὦμαι) | Etymologicum Gudianum (ἀάλιον ‐‐ ζειαί) | Additamenta in Etymologicum Gudianum (ἀάλιον ‐‐ ζειαί) (e codd. | Additamenta in Etymologicum Gudianum

author_bio: The Etymologicum Magnum is a large Byzantine etymological encyclopedia. It is an anonymous compilation, so the title stands in as the author heading.
author_notes: Compilation of earlier lexicographic works; no single author.

author_bio: A Byzantine lexicon of etymologies, extant in several manuscripts. The title 'Etymologicum Parvum' designates the text itself; it is dependent on the 'Etymologicum Magnum' and similar compilations.
author_notes: This is a textual corpus (lexicon) occupying the author slot; no personal author is known.

author_bio: The *Etymologicum Symeonis* is a Byzantine etymological lexicon compiled in the 12th century, attributed to a certain Symeon. It draws on earlier lexica such as the *Etymologicum Genuinum* and *Etymologicum Magnum*, and survives in partial manuscripts covering two alphabetical ranges.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work itself, as the actual compiler is uncertain. The label 'Etymologicum Symeonis' is a modern scholarly convention for an anonymous compilation.
representative_titles: Etymologicum Symeonis (ἀνακωχῆς ‐‐ βώτορες) | Etymologicum Symeonis (α ‐‐ ἁμωσγέπως)

author_bio: Euagon is known only from a few fragments; his identity as a historian or poet cannot be determined.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary sources; historicity uncertain.

author_bio: Euangelus is known only from two fragments, one of which is a grammatical or lexical excerpt. His identity and floruit are obscure; he may be a minor lexicographer or grammarian.
author_notes: Two fragments totaling 176 words. The name appears in lexicographical sources; no secure biographical data exists.

author_cultural_context: Minor author known only from a single inscription
author_bio: Euanthes is a scarcely attested Greek author, known only from a single title (*Titulus*) with very few words. Nothing certain about his life or historical context survives.
author_notes: The single word count of 3 suggests a fragmentary or inscriptional source. Historicity is uncertain; the name alone may not refer to a known historical person.

author_bio: An obscure figure known only from a few epigraphic tituli; possibly a grammarian or a medical writer. The surviving 'works' are short inscriptions.
author_notes: The source label is minimal (Tituli). Evidence is very limited; historicity is assumed but uncertain.

author_bio: Euboeus is a shadowy figure known only from a few fragments and a possible title. Nothing certain survives about his life, work, or corpus; the name suggests a connection to the island of Euboea, but his status as a historical author remains uncertain.
author_notes: Source (TLG 1800) includes two works (36 words total) described as 'Fragmenta et titulus' and 'Fragmenta'. The name may be a literary pseudonym or an onomastic accident; classification as legendary reflects the lack of reliable biographical data.

author_bio: Greek philosopher of the Megarian school, known for paradoxes such as the Liar Paradox.
author_notes: Known only through fragments; historical plausibility is strong but biographical details are sparse.

author_bio: Eubulus was a prominent poet of Middle Comedy in Athens, known for his mythological burlesques and refined style. Fragments of over 50 plays survive, offering insight into comic innovation between Aristophanes and Menander.
author_notes: TLG author; fragments and titles attest to a substantial comic corpus.

author_bio: Euclid, active in Alexandria around 300 BCE, is one of the most influential mathematicians in history, best known for the Elements, as well as works on geometry, optics, and astronomy.
author_notes: The source ID uses a CTS URN; Euclid's historicity is well-established, and the corpus is substantial and broadly transmitted.
representative_titles: Elementa | Data | Optica | Fragmenta | Opticorum recensio Theonis | Elementa (recensio altera, lib. 11.36-12.17) | Phaenomena | Catoptrica (recensio Theonis?)

author_bio: Greek philosopher associated with the Megarian school, known for dialectical arguments and fragments preserved indirectly.
author_notes: Distinct from the mathematician Euclid; fragmentary corpus.

author_bio: Eudemus of Rhodes was a pupil of Aristotle and a significant figure in the early Peripatetic school; he authored works on logic, physics, and ethics, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical philosopher; fragments are collected from later commentators.

author_bio: Eudemus is a shadowy figure, possibly a Hellenistic poet of medical or philosophical epigrams, known only from a single fragment.
author_notes: The (Poet. Med.) disambiguator suggests a poet associated with medicine. The historicity is uncertain due to lack of biographical evidence.

author_bio: Eudemus is known as a rhetorical writer, possibly of the Byzantine period, author of the treatise 'On Rhetorical Expressions' (excerpts).
author_notes: The identifier 'tlg1376' includes the qualification '(Rhet.)' to distinguish him from other figures named Eudemus. His exact date is uncertain, and very little is known.

author_bio: Empress of the Eastern Roman Empire, wife of Theodosius II; known for her literary works, including Homeric centos and hagiographical poetry.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; authorship of some works (e.g., Homerocentones) is debated but attributed in the tradition.
representative_titles: De martyrio sancti Cypriani (e cod. Florent. Laurent. VII, 10) | Homerocentones (fort. conditore operis et auctore Patricio quodam episcopo)

author_cultural_context: Greek astronomy, mathematics, and Platonic Academy
author_bio: Eudoxus of Cnidus was a prominent Greek astronomer and mathematician, associated with the early Academy. He developed the theory of concentric spheres to model planetary motion and wrote works on geography and ethics.
author_notes: The (Astron.) disambiguator distinguishes this figure from other Eudoxoi (e.g., Eudoxus of Cyzicus, Eudoxus the comic poet). The surviving works are fragmentary, including the Ars astronomica preserved on papyrus.

author_bio: A comic playwright known only from the title(s) and a tiny fragment; possibly a minor figure of Attic comedy.
author_notes: Only 7 words survive; the epithet '(Comic.)' indicates a comic poet, but no further context is available.

author_bio: Eudoxus (Hist.) is a shadowy historical writer known only from a single fragment; nothing can be securely stated about his life or work.
author_notes: The disambiguator '(Hist.)' separates this author from the better-known mathematician Eudoxus of Cnidus; the fragment likely comes from a lost historical account.

author_bio: Eudromus is a scarcely attested Greek author, known from a single surviving fragment. No biographical details or secure dating are available; his work may have been historical or rhetorical in nature.
author_notes: The name appears only in fragmentary sources; the corpus is too small for confident characterization.

author_bio: Euenus of Paros was a 5th-century BCE poet and sophist, associated with the early sophists and known for elegiac poetry. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Minimal remains; authorship of fragments attributed without certainty.

author_bio: A poorly attested Greek author, known only from a single diprosis (double recension) in a Venetian manuscript. Likely a Byzantine scholar or poet, but biographical data are entirely lacking.
author_notes: Attested by a single manuscript; identity and period are conjectural.

author_bio: A writer of historical or ethnographic works, known only from a few citations; almost nothing is known of his life.
author_notes: Existence depends on fragmentary citations; historicity uncertain.

author_bio: Eumedes is an author known only from a single word (a title or inscription). Nothing else is preserved, making any biographical reconstruction impossible.
author_notes: Only one word (titulus) survives. The name appears in Greek sources, but the fragmentary nature gives no certainty about identity or historicity.

author_bio: Eumelus of Corinth was a semi-legendary early Greek epic poet, traditionally dated to the 8th century BCE. He is credited with hexameter works on Corinthian mythology, including the *Corinthiaca* and a *Titanomachy*.
author_notes: Attribution to Eumelus is traditional and likely legendary; his works survive only in fragments and testimonia, with no firm biographical evidence.
representative_titles: Corinthiaca (fragmenta) | Fragmenta epica | Fragmenta | Testimonia | Fragmentum

author_cultural_context: Greek sophist and historian of the late Roman Empire; his 'Lives of the Sophists' and historical fragments are central sources for the Neoplatonic and rhetorical culture of the 4th–5th centuries
author_bio: Eunapius of Sardis was a sophist, rhetorician, and historian. His 'Lives of the Sophists' (parallel to Philostratus) is a key source for Late Antique intellectual history; his historical continuations of Dexippus survive in fragments, covering the period 270–404 CE.
author_notes: Well-attested author with two distinct works. The 'Vitae sophistarum' is nearly complete; the historical fragments are preserved in Photius and the Suda.

author_bio: Eunicus may be an Athenian comic poet of the Old Comedy, known only from two brief fragments. His identity and dates are speculative.
author_notes: Referenced in indirect tradition; extremely fragmentary.

author_cultural_context: Greek literature (possibly comedy or minor poetry)
author_bio: An obscure Greek author known only from a single surviving fragment. Little is known of Euphanes' life; his work is preserved in later quotation.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary, only 10 words survive. Attribution as person is tentative.

author_bio: Euphantus is a little-known Greek author from the Hellenistic period, possibly a philosopher or historian. Only minute fragments survive, making his identity and output difficult to assess.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; possibly the same as Euphantus of Olynthus mentioned by Diogenes Laërtius.

author_bio: Euphorion of Chalcis was a Hellenistic poet and grammarian, known for his hexameter poems and mythological erudition. He served as librarian at Antioch.
author_notes: Often conflated with the mythological Euphorion; textual basis for fragments is secure.

author_cultural_context: Writer of Greek New Comedy; fragments preserved via Athenaeus.
author_bio: Euphro was a poet of Greek New Comedy, known from a few surviving fragments quoted mainly by the lexicographer Athenaeus. His plays are lost, but his name indicates a role in the comic tradition.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; the label 'Comicus' is implicit; 1138 words provide limited context.

author_bio: A poorly known name attached to a single Priapeian epigram. Nothing further is known about Euphronius; the attribution may be authentic or a later ascription.
author_notes: Single very short work; no external biographical testimony. The author may be real or a poetic invention.

author_bio: Eupolis was one of the major poets of Old Comedy, a contemporary and rival of Aristophanes. His work is known only through fragments.
author_notes: Identifier 'tlg0461' is standard for the comic poet Eupolis. No ambiguity; a well-attested figure.

author_bio: Euripides was a prolific Athenian tragedian of the classical period, known for his innovative dramatic structure and deep psychological characterisation. He authored numerous works including *Medea*, *Hippolytus*, and *The Bacchae*, and his plays often questioned traditional religious and social norms.
author_notes: Universally recognised as a canonical author of ancient Greek drama. Multiple surviving plays and substantial fragments securely attributed.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Orestes | Helena | Phoenisae | Ion | Iphigenia Aulidensis | Iphigenia Taurica | Hippolytus

author_bio: A shadowy figure known only as 'Euripides II' or the 'younger Euripides', possibly a homonym or relative of the famous tragedian. Only titles survive, and the attribution may be a scribal error or confusion.
author_notes: The numeral 'II' suggests a second or younger Euripides, but the evidence is negligible; only 'Tituli' (titles) are extant. It may be a ghost author or misattribution.

author_bio: The Euripidis Epistulae are a set of fictitious letters attributed to the playwright Euripides, likely composed in the Imperial period. They are part of a tradition of pseudepigraphic epistles ascribed to classical authors.
author_notes: The text is a corpus of letters, not a named author. The title serves as the author entry in the catalog.

author_bio: Euryphamus is a shadowy figure known only from a single fragment. The name may refer to a lost writer, but nothing certain is known about this person.
author_notes: Attested by a solitary fragment; the historicity and even existence of Euryphamus are uncertain.

author_bio: Eurytus is a Greek author of uncertain date, known from testimonia and a single fragment.
author_notes: Surviving only in testimonia and one fragment; no secure biographical details.

author_bio: Early Church historian and exegete, author of the 'Ecclesiastical History' and apologetical works such as the 'Praeparatio evangelica'.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Eusebius of Caesarea is a major patristic author; his works are well-preserved and influential.

author_bio: Eusebius, a minor philosophical author, is known only from fragments of a lost work. His identity and exact school affiliation remain unclear.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; unclear philosophical allegiance; TLG classification as Phil.

author_bio: An ecclesiastical writer named Eusebius, known from fragments of commentaries on Pauline epistles preserved in catenae. Possibly distinct from Eusebius of Caesarea, but the identity is unclear.
author_notes: The label 'Scr. Eccl.' distinguishes this author from the more famous Eusebius of Caesarea. Attribution to a specific historical figure is uncertain.

author_bio: Eustathius of Thessalonica was a Byzantine scholar, Archbishop of Thessalonica, and prolific commentator on Homer and other classical texts. His works are central to Byzantine philology.
author_notes: Source label 'Philol. et Scr. Eccl.' indicates dual role as philologist and ecclesiastical writer.
representative_titles: Commentarii ad Homeri Iliadem | Commentarii ad Homeri Odysseam | Commentarium in Dionysii periegetae orbis descriptionem | De capta Thessalonica

author_bio: Christian theologian and ecclesiastical writer, probably a bishop; his works include commentaries and homilies, some of doubtful attribution.
author_notes: The source label 'Scr. Eccl. et Theol.' distinguishes him from homonymous authors; some works are spurious.

Joannem Damascenum, Sacra parallela) (fragmenta) | In inscriptione titulorum (fragmenta)

author_bio: Eustochius is known only from a single fragment of uncertain origin and content. No biographical details survive, and the attribution may be erroneous or refer to an otherwise unknown author.
author_notes: The single fragment (58 words) attributed to Eustochius provides no clear context for identification. The name may be a corruption or misattribution.

author_bio: A Byzantine scholar and metropolitan of Nicaea, active in the 12th century. He wrote commentaries on Aristotle's Posterior Analytics and Nicomachean Ethics, engaging with both ancient philosophy and Christian theology.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure with substantial surviving works.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis analyticorum posteriorum librum secundum commentarium | In Aristotelis ethica Nicomachea vi commentaria | In Aristotelis ethica Nicomachea i commentaria

author_bio: A late antique grammarian or sophist known for paraphrasing the didactic poems of Nicander and Oppian. His paraphrases retain scientific content in a more accessible style.
author_notes: Biographical details are scarce but the attribution of the paraphrases is consistent in manuscripts.
representative_titles: Paraphrasis in Oppiani cynegetica (fort. auctore Eutecnio) | Paraphrasis in Nicandri theriaca | Paraphrasis in Nicandri alexipharmaca | Paraphrasis in Oppiani halieutica (fort. auctore Eutecnio)

author_bio: Euthycles was a comic playwright known only from a few fragments. The ancient testimonia are sparse; his works survive only as short citations, indicating a minor figure in the comic tradition.
author_notes: The author is known only through a handful of fragments preserved by later grammarians; historicity is plausible but thinly attested.

author_bio: Euthydemus is known only from a single fragment; it is unclear whether he is a historical author or a name attributed to a lost work.
author_notes: Very scanty evidence; no biographical information exists. Source ID tlg0897.

author_bio: Eutocius of Ascalon was a mathematician and commentator on Archimedes and Apollonius of Perga, active in Alexandria; his commentaries preserve crucial mathematical texts.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; date and works known from his prefaces and citations by later scholars.
representative_titles: Commentarii in libros de sphaera et cylindro | Commentaria in conica | Commentarius in libros de planorum aequilibriis | Commentarius in dimensionem circuli

author_bio: A Roman historian, probably of Eastern origins, who served as magister memoriae under Emperor Valens. He wrote the Breviarium ab urbe condita, a concise history of Rome. The Greek translation by Paeanius is the sole representative work in this corpus.
author_notes: The entry refers to Paeanius' Greek translation of Eutropius' Breviarium, not the original Latin work. The author is listed as Eutropius in the source tradition.

author_bio: Eutychianus is a little-known Greek author of the Imperial period, possibly a rhetorician or historian, surviving only in a single fragment. The context of his work is unclear, and he may be identical with other homonymous figures.
author_notes: Identification is tentative; may be confused with other Eutychiani. TLG identifier tlg2158.

author_cultural_context: Greek ecclesiastical historian; active as a lawyer (scholasticus) in Antioch under the Byzantine Empire.
author_bio: Evagrius Scholasticus was a Syrian Greek historian whose 'Historia ecclesiastica' covers the period from the Council of Ephesus (431) to his own time in 594. He provides invaluable evidence for church history and the reign of Justinian.
author_notes: The source label includes 'Scholasticus Scr. Eccl.' to distinguish him from Evagrius Ponticus. The author is securely identified.

author_cultural_context: Desert monasticism and patristic ascetic theology
author_bio: Evagrius Ponticus was a prominent Christian monk and theologian, a leading figure of the Origenist tradition. His works on prayer, the eight evil thoughts, and monastic practice profoundly shaped later ascetic literature, often transmitted under the name of Nilus of Ancyra.
author_notes: Some works are transmitted under the name of Nilus of Ancyra (see representative titles). The source label 'Scr. Eccl.' indicates ecclesiastical writer.
representative_titles: Scholia in Proverbia (fragmenta e catenis) | Expositio in Proverbia Salomonis | Tractatus ad Eulogium (sub nomine Nili Ancyrani) | De malignis cogitationibus (sub nomine Nili Ancyrani) | Scholia in Ecclesiasten (fragmenta e catenis) | Practicus (capita centum) | De oratione (sub nomine Nili Ancyrani) | De octo spiritibus malitiae (sub nomine Nili Ancyrani)

author_bio: The 'Egyptian Gospel' (Evangelium Aegyptium) is an apocryphal gospel fragment, probably composed in Greek in early Christian Egypt. It is known only from citations and a few surviving textual witnesses.
author_notes: This is an apocryphal gospel, not attributed to a specific author; cataloged under the work title as a textual corpus.

author_bio: The Gospel of Bartholomew is an apocryphal gospel preserved in Greek fragments. It describes post-resurrection dialogues and is part of the New Testament apocrypha. No author is known.
author_notes: The text is a work title; the author slot represents the gospel itself.

author_bio: A lost Jewish-Christian gospel known from patristic citations (especially Epiphanius). It was used by the Ebionites and likely was a redaction of the Gospel of Matthew with anti-Pauline and vegetarian tendencies.
author_notes: The author slot carries the title of the lost gospel; no personal author is attested. The fragments are reconstructed from quotations in heresiological works.

author_bio: The 'Gospel of Eve' (Evangelium Evae) is a Gnostic text known only from a few fragments cited by patristic authors, likely composed in Greek in the 2nd–3rd century CE. It reflects Gnostic speculation on the figure of Eve.
author_notes: This entry is a work title used as an author heading. No historical figure by this name exists; the text is attributed (in citations) to a traditional 'Gospel of Eve'.

author_bio: The Gospel of Mary is an apocryphal Gnostic text attributed to Mary Magdalene; the title occupies the author slot as a named textual work rather than a personal author.
author_notes: The text is a single work; the author field represents the title of the work, not a historical person.

author_bio: An apocryphal gospel attributed to the apostle Peter but composed anonymously. Survives in fragmentary form, known from a late 2nd-century CE manuscript. Associated with docetic and anti-Jewish tendencies.
author_notes: The work title occupies the author slot; the author is unknown.

author_bio: The Evangelium Philippi (Gospel of Philip) is a Gnostic Christian text. The title serves as the author heading in the catalog.
author_notes: The author field is the title of the work itself, as the text is anonymous. Attributed to Philip the Apostle by tradition, but not historically composed by him.

author_bio: A lost apocryphal gospel, known only from fragments and citations by Church Fathers, likely composed in Aramaic or Greek for a Jewish Christian audience.
author_notes: The work title serves as the author abbreviation; no single author is identifiable.

author_bio: The Evangelium Thomae (Infancy Gospel of Thomas) is an apocryphal gospel recounting miracles from the childhood of Jesus. The Greek text is transmitted under the title, not an individual author.
author_notes: Authorship is unknown; the work is attributed pseudonymously to Thomas. The heading is the title of the work.

author_bio: Ezechiel the Tragedian was a Hellenistic Jewish poet, author of the Exagoge, a Greek drama on the Exodus theme, the earliest known Jewish play.
author_notes: Known only from fragments of his play; historicity is plausible but sparse.

author_bio: Zaleucus is a legendary figure credited as the first lawgiver of the Greek city of Epizephyrian Locris in southern Italy. His laws were said to be severe, but no genuine fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: The epithet 'Nomographus' means 'lawgiver'; many of the surviving 'fragments' are likely later forgeries or confusions with other lawgivers like Charondas.

author_bio: Zeno of Elea was a pre-Socratic philosopher, famed for his paradoxes challenging motion and plurality. His works survive only in testimonia and fragments.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; the fragments and testimonia are largely preserved by Aristotle and Simplicius.

author_bio: Zeno Tarsensis was a Greek author from Tarsus, possibly a Stoic philosopher or historian, known only from a few fragments.
author_notes: Distinct from Zeno the Stoic and Zeno of Sidon; the epithet 'Tarsensis' indicates origin from Tarsus. Source ID tlg2294.

author_bio: Zeno the Historian is a fragmentarily attested Greek author; no biographical details survive, and his historical work is preserved only in a single fragmentary corpus.
author_notes: The source label '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Zeno from the philosopher; the available evidence is minimal, and the identification is entirely dependent on the fragment tradition.

author_bio: Zeno of Citium was the founder of the Stoic school of philosophy in Athens. He developed a comprehensive system of logic, physics, and ethics, arguing that virtue is the only good and that the wise person lives in accordance with nature. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Phil.)' marks Zeno as the philosopher, distinct from other figures with the same name. The two works (testimonia and fragments) are the main sources for his thought.

author_bio: Zenobius was a Greek sophist and paroemiographer of the Roman imperial period. He compiled an epitome of the proverb collections of Didymus and Lucillus of Tarrha.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; his work survives in an epitomated form.
representative_titles: Epitome collectionum Lucilli Tarrhaei et Didymi

author_bio: Zenodorus was a Hellenistic grammarian and philologist known for his work Περὶ συνηθείας, a treatise on common usage preserved in a single Paris manuscript. His precise dates are uncertain, but he likely flourished in the 3rd or 2nd century BCE.
author_notes: The name Zenodorus is well attested, but biographical details are sparse. The eponymous work is fragmentary.
representative_titles: Περὶ συνηθείας (e cod. Paris. suppl. gr. 1164 [olim cod.

author_bio: Zenodotus of Ephesus, the first librarian of the Library of Alexandria, was a pioneering Homeric scholar and founder of textual criticism. His recension of the Iliad and Odyssey shaped subsequent scholarship, though only a single fragment of his own work survives.
author_notes: The single surviving fragment attributed to Zenodotus as an author proper is likely a later grammatical excerpt. His importance far exceeds the meager direct word count.

author_bio: The *Letter of Zeno* (Ζήνωνος ἐπιστολή) is a short epistolary text attributed to the Stoic philosopher Zeno of Citium, though its authenticity is disputed. It is treated as a work-title author entry in the corpus.
author_notes: The author entry is a work title ('Zenonis Epistula'), not a person. Attribution to Zeno is uncertain; possibly a later rhetorical exercise.

author_cultural_context: Late Hellenistic historian or paradoxographer; known only through a brief citation
author_bio: Zenothemis (possibly of Cyrene) is a shadowy figure known only from a single fragment preserved by a later compiler. His work may have treated wonders or historical anecdotes of North Africa.
author_notes: The evidence is a single fragment (14 words). The identification of the author is entirely hypothetical; some scholars have suggested he is a spurious name. Attested only in the Suda or scholia.

author_bio: Joannes Zonaras was a Byzantine historian and chronicler. He authored the 'Epitome of Histories', a world chronicle from creation to 1118 CE, and also compiled a lexicon.
author_notes: Identifier 'urn:cts:greekLit:tlg3135' refers to the Byzantine historian Zonaras. The name is a personal name with a well-established identity.
representative_titles: Epitome historiarum (lib. 1-12) | Epitome historiarum (lib. 13-18) | Epitome historiarum (lib. 12, clausula varia) Dub.

author_bio: Zopyrus is a shadowy figure from ancient Greece, known only from a single surviving fragment. He may have been a historian or paradoxographer, but his identity is highly uncertain. The name is attested in various contexts, but no secure biography exists.
author_notes: The fragment attributed to Zopyrus is very brief (12 words), making it difficult to assess his genre or period. The TLG entry is speculative.

author_bio: Zosimus of Panopolis is the most important Graeco-Egyptian alchemical author, whose works blend practical metallurgy with mystical and hermetic philosophy.
author_notes: The disambiguator '(Alchem.)' separates Zosimus from the historian of the same name; his extensive corpus of 68 works survives in fragmentary form.
representative_titles: Ζωσίμου τοῦ θείου περὶ ἀρετῆς καὶ ἑρμηνείας (e cod. | Περὶ τῆς κατὰ πλάτος ἐκδόσεως τὸ ἔργον (fort. pars | Ζωσίμου περὶ ὀργάνων καὶ καμίνων γνήσια ὑπομνήματα περὶ τοῦ ω | Τὸ πρῶτον βιβλίον τῆς τελευταίας ἀποχῆς Ζωσίμου Θηβαίου | Περὶ ὀργάνων καὶ καμίνων γνήσια ὑπομνήματα περὶ τοῦ ω στοιχείου | Περὶ σώματος μαγνησίας καὶ οἰκονομίας (fort. pars operis | Περὶ τοῦ λίθου τῆς φιλοσοφίας (fort. pars operis | Ζωσίμου τοῦ θείου περὶ ἀρετῆς·περὶ συνθέσεως ὑδάτων <πρᾶξις

author_bio: Late Roman historian and author of the *Historia Nova*, a Greek history of the Roman Empire from Augustus to the sack of Rome (410 CE). A pagan, he offers a critical, non-Christian perspective on the decline of Rome.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Hist.' distinguishes him from other Zosimi. His work is a key source for late Roman history, though partly lost.

author_bio: Harmodius is a fragmentarily preserved Hellenistic historian known from sparse citations. His work covered historical or paradoxographical themes, surviving only in scattered fragments.
author_notes: The source label 'Hist.' indicates a historian. Attestation is minimal, and the exact scope of his work is unclear.

author_bio: Harmodius is a shadowy figure identified only by a title; no fragments of his work survive beyond a single word or line, and his existence as a tragedian is uncertain.
author_notes: The entry rests on a single testimonium (a title) with only two words; the label 'Trag.' may be a later scholarly conjecture.

author_bio: Harpocration was a Greek grammarian of the 2nd century CE, known for his Lexicon to the Ten Attic Orators, an important source for Attic vocabulary and realia.
author_notes: This entry corresponds to the grammarian Harpocration. His lexicon survives nearly complete and is a standard reference for Attic oratory.

author_bio: A letter attributed to an otherwise unknown Harpocration, preserved as a single text. The author slot is filled by the title of the work itself.
author_notes: The entry is a text traditionally labeled as the 'Epistula of Harpocration'; the authorship is uncertain and the name may refer to a different known Harpocration.

author_bio: Hecataeus of Miletus was a pioneering Greek logographer and geographer, active around 500 BCE. He wrote the 'Genealogies' and a 'Periegesis' (world survey), important forerunners of historiography.
author_notes: Hecataeus is well-attested through later quotations. His work survives only in fragments, but his influence on Herodotus and later writers is considerable.

author_bio: Hecataeus of Abdera was a Greek historian and ethnographer active in the early Hellenistic period. He wrote a lost work on Egypt (Aegyptiaca) and possibly on the Hyperboreans, transmitted only in fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: Distinguished from Hecataeus of Miletus (archaic historian) by the ethnic 'Abderita'; works survive only in fragments and indirect tradition.

author_bio: A rare female Greek poet, Hedyle is known from a single epigram or fragment, possibly preserved by Athenaeus. She is sometimes identified as the mother or relative of the poet Hedylus.
author_notes: Very little is known; the existence and gender are inferred from a single source. The fragment consists of 33 words.

author_bio: Hedylus was a Hellenistic epigrammatist active in the 3rd century BCE, possibly from Samos. His epigrams, preserved in the Greek Anthology, display the learned and witty style typical of the period. He also wrote literary criticism.
author_notes: The attribution is secure; fragments include epigrams and a possible titular epigram.

author_cultural_context: Greek epic poetry and parodic tradition
author_bio: Hegemon of Thasos was a Greek poet known for his parodic epic 'The Battle of the Giants' (Gigantomachia), which mixed epic style with humor. A contemporary of the Sophists, he is considered an early innovator in the genre of epic parody.
author_notes: The 'Epic.' tag marks him as a poet. The surviving fragments and titles are preserved in Athenaeus and other sources.

author_bio: Hegemon of Thasos was a Greek poet active in the late 5th century BCE, credited as an early practitioner of parody. He is best known for his mock-epic 'Gigantomachia', fragments of which survive.
author_notes: The source label 'Hegemon (Parodius)' identifies him as a parodist. Ancient testimonia (e.g., Athenaeus) confirm his historicity and provide biographical details. The surviving word count is modest but sufficient for a specialist audience.

author_bio: Hegesander was a Greek historian and paradoxographer, author of lost works on Delphic and other antiquities, preserved only in fragments.
author_notes: Distinct from other authors named Hegesander. Dates are uncertain.

author_bio: Hegesianax was a Hellenistic writer of paradoxography and other works, known only through fragments. His writings included marvels and miscellaneous observations.
author_notes: Historical but obscure; only fragments survive.

author_bio: Hegesinus is a possible author of an Atthis fragment, but the attribution is spurious and may actually belong to Callippus.
author_notes: Only 28 words; the attribution is suspect.

author_bio: Hegesippus was a poet of Athenian Middle Comedy, known only through a few fragments preserved by later authors. His work reflects the transitional style of the early 4th century BCE.
author_notes: The identifier 'Comic.' distinguishes this Hegesippus from other homonyms; surviving fragments are limited and attribution may be uncertain.

author_bio: Hegesippus was an early Christian chronicler and writer of a lost work on the apostolic tradition, cited by Eusebius and others.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Hist.)' distinguishes from other Hegesippus figures; only fragments survive.

author_bio: An early Christian writer known from Eusebius of Caesarea for producing a lost work of Church history and apologetics. Hegesippus is a key source for early Christian tradition, with only fragments surviving, often transmitted through Eusebius.
author_notes: Identification relies on patristic citations; surviving fragments are very limited. The parenthetical '(Scr. Eccl.)' indicates the ecclesiastical writer classification.

author_bio: Heliodorus, a late antique philosopher and poet associated with alchemical writings. He is known for a single didactic poem addressed to Theodosius I.
author_notes: The combined epithet '(Alchem. et Poeta)' is used to distinguish him from other Heliodori; the work is a philosophical-alchemical poem.
representative_titles: Ἡλιοδώρου φιλοσόφου πρὸς Θεοδόσιον τὸν μέγαν βασιλέα περὶ τῆς

author_bio: Greek periegetic writer known for geographical and descriptive works, author of fragmentary travel or descriptive texts.
author_notes: The source identifier 'Perieg.' distinguishes him as a periegetic author; identity as a distinct historical figure is plausible but limited to fragments.

author_bio: Greek novelist, author of the *Aethiopica* (Ethiopian Story), one of the earliest and most influential ancient Greek novels.
author_notes: Well-attested author; the *Aethiopica* survives intact; biographical details from his own work and later tradition.

author_bio: Heliodorus (Trag.) is a minor tragic poet known from a few fragments and a work titled 'Ἰταλικὰ θαύματα' ('Italian Marvels'), suggesting a blend of tragedy and paradoxography. His identity is uncertain.
author_notes: The label '(Trag.)' indicates a tragedian, but the surviving titles include a prose paradoxographical work, which may indicate a different author or genre mixing.

author_bio: Hellanicus of Lesbos was a prolific Greek logographer and mythographer of the 5th century BCE, known for his genealogical and historical works that bridged myth and early history. His fragments survive mainly from citations by later authors.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments include testimonia and papyrus evidence.

author_bio: Hellenica is a title used for anonymous Greek historical papyrus fragments, possibly part of a larger lost work. The author is unknown.
author_notes: Catalog label for anonymous historical fragments; attribution is uncertain and likely a modern editorial grouping.
representative_titles: Fragmenta (P. Oxy. 5.842) | Fragmenta Londinensia (P. Oxy. 5.842) | Fragmenta Florentina (PSI 13.1304) | Fragmentum (P. Cairo inv. 26/ 6/ 27/ 1 35) | Fragmenta Londinensia incertae sedis (P. Oxy. 5.842)

author_bio: Heniochus was a playwright of Middle or New Comedy, known only from fragmentary remains.
author_notes: Fragments attributed to Heniochus; comic poet from the transition from Middle to New Comedy.

author_bio: Hephaestion of Thebes was a Greek astrologer whose 'Apotelesmatica' is a comprehensive astrological compendium drawing on earlier sources like Ptolemy and Dorotheus. His work survives in epitomes and excerpts.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Astrol.)' distinguishes him from other Hephaestions. His work is important for the transmission of Hellenistic astrology.
representative_titles: Apotelesmatica (epitomae quattuor) | Apotelesmatica | Excerptum (e cod. Marcian. gr. 334) | Excerptum (e cod. Paris. gr. 2506) | Excerptum (e cod. Vat. gr. 1056)

author_bio: Hephaestion was a Greek grammarian of the 2nd century CE, best known for his 'Enchiridion de metris', a manual on poetic meters, and other works on metrics and poetics. His treatises influenced later Byzantine scholarship.
author_notes: Label 'Gramm.' indicates grammatical/meterial writer. Multiple works attest to his prominence in the field.
representative_titles: Enchiridion de metris | De poematis | Introductio metrica | De signis | Fragmenta Hephaestionea

author_bio: Peripatetic philosopher and historian who epitomized works of Aristotle and others; his 'Excerpta politiarum' preserves fragments of Greek constitutions.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical person; epitomator active in Alexandria.

author_bio: A minor grammarian known as Heraclides Ponticus Junior, possibly distinguished from the more famous philosopher of the same name. Surviving fragments suggest work on lexicography or commentary.
author_notes: Sparse evidence; identification uncertain between multiple homonymous authors.

author_bio: A philosopher and member of Plato's Academy, Heraclides Ponticus wrote on astronomy, ethics, and literary criticism. Only fragments of his works survive.
author_notes: The '(Phil.)' disambiguates from the historian Heraclides Ponticus. Fragments are well-collected but his biography is partly legendary.

author_bio: Heraclides (Comic.) is a comic poet known only from a few fragments. The name is common and the specific identity is unclear; he may be a minor figure of Greek New Comedy.
author_notes: Label '(Comic.)' indicates a comic poet; fragments very brief.

author_bio: Heraclides Criticus (also known as the Periegete or the Describers) was a Hellenistic author of a geographical or periegetic work describing Greece. His work is preserved in fragmentary form and was sometimes attributed to Dicaearchus or Athenaeus.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg1405. The descriptor 'Criticus Perieg.' indicates the author is a critic and periegete. Attribution to Heraclides is not entirely secure.
representative_titles: Descriptio Graeciae (sub auctore Dicaearcho vel Athenaeo)

author_date_range: uncertain, perhaps 2nd cent. CE or later
author_bio: Heraclides (Grammaticus) is the name given to a Greek grammarian, possibly of the Imperial or Byzantine period, who wrote on irregular verbs. His fragmentary works survive in papyrus and quotations.
author_notes: This author is a shadowy grammarian; the fragments include a treatise on irregular verbs and a papyrus fragment. The identification with other Heraclideis is uncertain, hence the bracketed (Gramm.) to specify the field.

author_bio: A Greek historian from Cyme, author of a lost Persian history (Persica) used by later authors.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Hist.' distinguishes him from philosophers and other Heraclidae; fragmentary.

author_bio: Heraclides (Phil.) refers to Heraclides Ponticus, a philosopher and astronomer of the Platonic Academy. He is known for his contributions to cosmology and literary dialogues, though only fragments of his works survive.
author_notes: The abbreviation '(Phil.)' distinguishes this Heraclides from other homonyms (e.g., the grammarian). The single fragment is likely from his philosophical writings.

author_bio: Heraclides (Trag.) is a tragically attested ancient Greek author known solely from a single title and 10 words. The name may refer to an otherwise unknown tragedian or could be a confusion with a different Heraclides.
author_notes: The classification is highly uncertain due to extremely limited evidence. The parenthetical 'Trag.' suggests a tragedian, but this is speculative.

author_bio: Heracliti Ephesii Epistulae is a corpus of fictional letters attributed to the philosopher Heraclitus, composed in late antiquity. The letters are not authentic but part of a pseudepigraphic tradition.
author_notes: The work is pseudepigraphic; the title functions as the author entry in the catalog.

author_bio: Heraclitus (often called the Allegorist or Heraclitus Homericus) is the author of the 'Homeric Problems' (Allegoriae), a work defending Homer against charges of impiety through allegorical interpretation. He lived likely in the 1st century CE in Alexandria.
author_notes: The display name includes '1st cent' to distinguish from the Presocratic Heraclitus. Historiographical consensus places him in the early Roman Imperial period. Confidence is medium due to the lack of precise biographical data.

author_bio: A Greek comic poet known only from a single title and a few fragments. The epithet 'Comic.' distinguishes him from the philosopher Heraclitus.
author_notes: The evidence is minimal: only one or two fragments survive. The dating and even the reality of a distinct comic poet named Heraclitus are uncertain.

author_bio: A paradoxographer known as Heraclitus, author of a surviving work On Incredible Things (De incredibilibus), collecting rationalized myths.
author_notes: Labeled 'Paradox.' to distinguish from the philosopher Heraclitus; author identity uncertain, possibly of Roman era.

author_bio: This Heraclitus, distinct from the presocratic, is a philosopher of the early Imperial period to whom a series of Stoicizing or eclectic fragments and testimonia are attributed.
author_notes: The epithet (Phil.) distinguishes this author from the presocratic Heraclitus. Attribution is conventional and may refer to a lost work.

author_bio: Possibly a Megarian historian of the 4th century BCE, known primarily from a few citations in Plutarch and others. His work may have treated local Megarian traditions.
author_notes: The name appears in sparse citations; identity and corpus are uncertain.

author_bio: A philosopher known only from scant references; possibly a student of Zeno of Citium or a member of the Dialectical school. The surviving fragments offer little biographical detail.
author_notes: The source label is a single name; evidence is minimal. Historicity is assumed but with low certainty.

author_bio: Hermaeus is an obscure name preserved in a single papyrus fragment, possibly a scholar or poet. Nothing else is known about this figure.
author_notes: Only known from a single papyrus attribution (P. Oxy. 26.2442). The historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Hermarchus was a Greek philosopher of the Epicurean school, a student and successor of Epicurus. He wrote works on philosophy, but only fragments survive.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments preserved in later authors.

author_date_range: ca. 2nd–4th century CE (texts attributed to Hermes)
author_bio: Hermes (often identified with Hermes Trismegistus) is a mythic figure in the Hermetic and alchemical traditions, to whom Greek texts on the philosopher's stone and occult arts are attributed. The surviving fragments are from Byzantine manuscripts.
author_notes: Attribution is pseudepigraphic/mythic; the name covers a corpus of alchemical and philosophical texts. The riddle on the philosopher's stone is attributed jointly to Hermes and Agathodaimon.
representative_titles: Αἴνιγμα τοῦ φιλοσοφικοῦ λίθου Ἑρμοῦ καὶ Ἀγαθοδαίμονος | Aenigma (sine titulo) (e cod. Venet. Marc. 299, fol. 95)

author_bio: Greek elegiac poet from Colophon, associated with the Alexandrian tradition; fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Minor figure; surviving fragments and testimonia allow tentative reconstruction of his work.

author_bio: Possibly a Hellenistic historian; only fragments of his work survive, making his identity and output difficult to characterize.
author_notes: The epithet '(Hist.)' suggests a historian, but the attribution is uncertain and only fragmentary evidence remains.

author_period: late 2nd c. CE / early 3rd c. CE
author_bio: An early Christian apologist known for the work Irrisio gentilium philosophorum (Mockery of the Pagan Philosophers), a satirical critique of Greek philosophical schools. Little is known of his life; he may have been active in the eastern Mediterranean region.
author_notes: The designation 'Apol.' identifies him as an apologist. The single surviving work is well-preserved and attributed to Hermias in the manuscript tradition.

author_bio: Hermias was a Hellenistic historian from Methymna, known only through fragments, possibly the author of a history of Sicily under Dionysius I or a work on Lesbian affairs.
author_notes: The tag '(Hist.)' identifies this Hermias as a historian, distinguished from other figures with the same name. Only fragments survive.

author_bio: A Hellenistic Greek poet and scholar, author of an iambic poem, known only from a single surviving fragment.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Iamb.' likely indicates iambic poetry; identity uncertain due to sparse evidence.

author_bio: Hermias was a 5th-century CE Neoplatonist philosopher from Alexandria, known for his commentary on Plato's Phaedrus. He studied under Syrianus and was a contemporary of Proclus, contributing to the late Platonic commentary tradition.
author_notes: The label 'Phil.' disambiguates Hermias the philosopher from other authors named Hermias. TLG identifier tlg2317 is used.

author_bio: Hermias is a Greek poet known only from a single fragment. His identity is uncertain beyond the name and genre attribution.
author_notes: Known from a single fragment; biographical details lacking.

author_bio: Athenian comic playwright of the late 5th century BCE, rival of Aristophanes. Only fragments of his comedies survive.
author_notes: Attested in ancient testimonia; fragments preserved in Athenaeus and other sources.

author_bio: Greek grammarian and historian, known for biographical works and commentary on ancient authors, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Distinguished from comic poet Hermippus; fragments attest to his role in the Peripatetic biographical tradition.

author_bio: Hermocles was a Greek lyric poet, known for composing Ithyphallic hymns. His work survives in a single fragment.
author_notes: Attested as a poet of Ithyphallic verses; little biographical detail survives.

author_bio: Hermodorus of Syracuse is known as a disciple and early editor of Plato's works. He is said to have brought Plato's dialogues to Sicily. His surviving fragments and testimonia concern Platonic philosophy and literary history.
author_notes: Historicity is uncertain; much of his biography depends on later tradition. The attribution 'fort. auctore Hermodoro Syracusano' indicates a tentative link to a work 'De Platone'.
representative_titles: De Platone (fort. auctore Hermodoro Syracusano) | Testimonia et fragmenta

author_bio: Hermogenes of Tarsus was a prominent rhetorician of the Second Sophistic; his works on stasis theory, invention, and style were influential in Byzantine education.
author_notes: Some works (On Invention, On Method) are considered spurious; the genuine core is secure.
representative_titles: Περὶ ἰδεῶν λόγου | Περὶ εὑρέσεως [Sp.] | Περὶ στάσεων | Περὶ μεθόδου δεινότητος [Sp.] | Progymnasmata Dub. | Testimonia

author_bio: A shadowy Greek author, known only from a single fragment preserved in later sources. Probably a paradoxographer or mythographer, but little can be securely asserted about his life or oeuvre.
author_notes: Known only from minimal testimonia; historicity and exact period remain uncertain.

author_bio: Hermonax is a shadowy figure known only from a few fragments. His identity and corpus are largely undefined; the name may refer to a poet or grammarian. No secure biographical details survive.
author_notes: The fragments attributed to Hermonax are brief and of uncertain context. The author may be confused with other homonymous figures.

author_bio: Herodas (or Herondas) was a Hellenistic poet known for his mimiambi—dramatic vignettes of everyday life in colloquial Greek. His works were rediscovered in papyrus finds.
author_notes: Well-established author with a single surviving work. Display name 'Herodas' is standard. The language is Greek.

author_cultural_context: Second Sophistic; Greek rhetoric and education under the Roman Empire
author_bio: Herodes Atticus was a wealthy Athenian sophist, philosopher, and benefactor, famous for his rhetorical skill and monumental buildings. A single dubiously attributed political treatise survives.
author_notes: The single work 'Περὶ πολιτείας' is marked as dubiously attributed. Herodes Atticus is otherwise well-attested historically and literarily.

author_bio: Herodian was a Greek historian who wrote a history of the Roman Empire from the death of Marcus Aurelius to the accession of Gordian III (180–238 CE). His work is a key source for the Severan period and the third-century crisis.
author_notes: The sole surviving work is 'History of the Empire from the Death of Marcus Aurelius' (Ab excessu divi Marci).

author_bio: Herodicus was a Classical Greek physician, often credited as a pioneer in therapeutic gymnastics and dietetics. Known for treating patients with exercise and regimen, he influenced later medical thought, notably that of Hippocrates.
author_notes: Attested in ancient medical sources; fragments only.

author_bio: Herodorus of Heraclea was a Greek grammarian and mythographer, known for his work on the Argonauts and other mythological cycles. Fragments preserve his rationalizing interpretations of myth.
author_notes: The fragments are cited by later authors; the original works are lost.

author_bio: Herodotus of Halicarnassus is the earliest Greek historian whose work survives complete. His 'Histories' narrate the Greco-Persian Wars and include extensive ethnographic and geographical digressions, earning him the title 'Father of History' (Cicero).
author_notes: No disambiguation needed; Herodotus is a singular, well-attested historical figure.

author_cultural_context: Greek mathematics, mechanics, and engineering; Roman period
author_bio: Greek mathematician and engineer active in Roman Alexandria. Known for works on geometry, pneumatics, and automata; his practical treatises influenced later technology.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; multiple treatises survive. Heron is securely placed in 1st-century CE Alexandria.
representative_titles: Geometrica | Pneumatica | Metrica | Stereometrica | Definitiones | Dioptra | De automatis | Liber geeponicus Sp.

author_bio: Archaic Greek poet, author of the *Theogony* and *Works and Days*, foundational works of Greek mythology and didactic literature.
author_notes: Historical figure despite legendary biographical details; corpus includes authentic and spurious works.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Theogonia | Opera et dies | Scutum | Fragmenta astronomica | Testimonia | Fragmentum (ap. Maximum Tyrium)

author_bio: Hestiaeus is a minor Hellenistic author known from a single fragment. His identity and precise dates are uncertain, but he is sometimes associated with literary or historical writing of the period.
author_notes: Very little is known; only one fragment survives. The name could refer to multiple individuals; the attribution is conventional.

author_bio: Hesychius of Miletus, surnamed Illustrius, was a Byzantine historian and scholar of the 6th century CE. He compiled a universal history (now mostly lost) and other works, including a patria of Constantinople and a homily; his fragments preserve important historical traditions.
author_notes: Source (TLG 2274) includes four works (13,115 words). The identifier 'Illustrius Hist.' distinguishes this Hesychius from the lexicographer Hesychius of Alexandria. The historical status is well-established through Byzantine testimonia.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Patria Constantinopoleos (= liber vi operis Historia Romana atque | Homilia in natalem Christi (fort. pars operis Historia Romana atque omnigena)

author_bio: Hesychius of Alexandria compiled one of the most important ancient Greek lexica, preserving a vast number of rare words, glosses, and citations from earlier literature.
author_notes: The 'Lexicogr.' disambiguator identifies him as a lexicographer. His lexicon survives in multiple manuscript parts.
representative_titles: Lexicon (Α ‐‐ Ο) | Lexicon (Π ‐‐ Ω) | Epistula ad Eulogium

author_bio: Hesychius of Jerusalem was a presbyter and prolific author of homilies on saints and feasts. Many works attributed to him are of disputed authenticity. He is known for his exegetical and sermon literature.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Scr. Eccl.)' distinguishes this Hesychius from the grammarian. Several homilies are dubia or spuria, indicating a fluid corpus.
representative_titles: Homilia ii in sanctum Longinum centurionem (homilia 20) Dub. | In conceptionem venerabilis praecursoris (homilia 16) | In Lazarum et in ramos palmarum (homilia 18) (olim sub nomine Titi Bostrensis) Sp. | In sanctum Stephanum (homilia 9) | In sanctum Andream (homilia 7) | Homilia i in sanctum Longinum centurionem (homilia 19) Dub. | In sanctum Procopium (homilia 14) | Homilia i de Hypapante (homilia 1)

author_bio: Syracusan philosopher of the Pythagorean school, credited with an early theory of the Earth's rotation; known only from testimonia.
author_notes: Attested by later authors; no extant works.

author_bio: Neoplatonist philosopher and commentator, author of a surviving commentary on the Pythagorean Golden Verses.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; no ambiguity.

author_region: Roman Empire (likely Greece or Asia Minor)
author_bio: Hierocles was a Stoic philosopher known for his ethical writings, preserved in fragments and in Stobaeus. His work reflects Roman-era Stoic thought.
author_notes: Attested author with substantial fragments and a known philosophical profile.
representative_titles: Fragmenta ethica (ap. Stobaeum) | Ἠθικὴ στοιχείωσις | Fragmenta (ap. Sudam)

author_bio: Hieronymus, identified as a historian (Hist.) in tradition, survives only in fragments; his identity and period are unclear.
author_notes: Distinguished from homonyms by the label (Hist.); only 1077 words survive.

author_bio: Hieronymus Philosophus, an obscure Hellenistic philosopher known only from fragments. Likely a Peripatetic or eclectic thinker, his work survives solely in later quotations.
author_notes: The identifier '(Phil.)' distinguishes this Hieronymus from homonymous figures; no secure biographical details survive.

author_bio: Hierophilus was a Byzantine physician, known for treatises on dietetics and monthly regimens. His work survives in medical manuscripts and reflects the Galenic tradition.
author_notes: The identity of Hierophilus is not widely attested in external sources; the attribution relies on the manuscript tradition. The corpus is modest but specific.
representative_titles: De nutriendi methodo | Πῶς ὀφείλει διαιτᾶσθαι ἄνθρωπος ἐφ’ ἑκάστῳ μηνί

author_bio: Hierotheus is a name attached to a Byzantine alchemical poem. The figure is likely legendary or pseudonymous, attributed to a teacher of alchemy with little historical basis.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Alchem. et Poeta)' indicates alchemical and poetic works. Historicity uncertain; possibly a traditional attribution. Source ID tlg4332.
representative_titles: Ἱεροθέου φιλοσόφου περὶ τῆς θείας καὶ ἱερᾶς τέχνης διὰ στίχων

author_bio: An alchemical author, likely pseudonymous, associated with a short Greek text on the sacred art of alchemy. The name Hierotheus may be an adopted pseudonym within alchemical tradition, and the surviving text is preserved in a single Paris manuscript.
author_notes: The parentheses and qualifier '(Alchem.)' indicate the author is distinct from other figures named Hierotheus; the work is part of the Greek alchemical corpus, and authenticity is questionable.
representative_titles: Ἱεροθέου περὶ τῆς ἱερᾶς τέχνης (e cod. Paris. B.N. gr.

author_bio: Himerius was a Greek sophist and rhetorician active in Athens. His declamations and orations, preserved partly on papyrus, exemplify the Second Sophistic style and are a key source for late antique education and rhetoric.
author_notes: Textual tradition relies on a mix of manuscript and papyrus witnesses. Some fragments are of uncertain placement.
representative_titles: Declamationes et orationes | Fragmenta ex incertis orationibus | Fragmenta (P. Oslo inv. 1478)

author_bio: Hipparchus was a foremost Greek astronomer and geographer, known for his star catalog, discovery of precession, and commentary on Aratus and Eudoxus.
author_notes: The source label uses the epithet (Astron. et Geogr.) to differentiate from other figures named Hipparchus.
representative_titles: In Arati et Eudoxi phaenomena commentariorum libri iii | Fragmenta geographica

author_bio: A minor comic poet of uncertain date, known only through sparse fragments preserved in later lexicographers, possibly belonging to Middle or New Comedy.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; historicity is uncertain due to lack of biographical testimony.

author_bio: Hipparchus (Epigr.) is a minor Hellenistic epigrammatist known only from a single fragment. Nothing certain is known of his biography; the name may be a byname of another poet.
author_notes: The label '(Epigr.)' distinguishes this Hipparchus from other homonymous figures; the single fragment is too slight for confident attribution.

author_bio: Hipparchus, a poet of parody, known from a small number of fragments preserved in later sources.
author_notes: The disambiguator 'Parodius' indicates the genre; very sparse attestation.

author_bio: Hipparchus was a Cynic philosopher, possibly a student of Diogenes or Metrocles, known only from a single surviving fragment; the exact details of his life and thought are obscure.
author_notes: The source label '(Phil.)' identifies this as the philosopher, not the astronomer; the fragment is extremely brief and biographical information is virtually nonexistent.

author_bio: An early Pythagorean philosopher, associated with the discovery of irrational numbers and mathematical theory. Legend says he was drowned for revealing a Pythagorean secret. His actual teachings are shrouded in legend.
author_notes: Historicity is mixed; tradition heavily shapes his biography. Status set to legendary due to accreted myth.

author_bio: Hippias of Elis was a Greek sophist and historian, known for his wide-ranging learning and for compiling a list of Olympic victors. His historical work survives only in fragments.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' disambiguator indicates his role as a historian; identified with the sophist Hippias of Elis.

author_bio: Hippias of Elis was a prominent Sophist known for his polymathy and rhetorical skill. He appears in Plato's dialogues, and fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Well-known figure in the Sophistic movement; historicity secure.

author_bio: The Hippiatrica is a Byzantine compilation of excerpts from earlier Greek veterinary writers on horse medicine (hippiatry), surviving in multiple recensions (Berolinensia, Cantabrigiensia, Parisina, etc.) and containing material from authors like Anatolius and Apsyrtus.
author_notes: The heading refers to the compilation itself, not a single author; the work is a corpus of excerpts from various earlier authors, compiled in the Byzantine period.
representative_titles: Hippiatrica Berolinensia | Hippiatrica Cantabrigiensia | Hippiatrica Parisina | Excerpta Lugdunensia | Additamenta Londinensia ad hippiatrica Cantabrigiensia | Appendices ad hippiatrica Berolinensia | Fragmenta Anatolii de bubus | Fragmenta Anatolii de equis

author_bio: Hippocrates of Chios, a mathematician active ca. 440-400 BCE. He compiled the first known Elements of geometry and worked on the quadrature of lunes. Only indirect testimonia survive.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Math.)' distinguishes this Hippocrates from the medical writer Hippocrates of Cos. He is historically attested by Aristotle and Eudemus.

author_date_range: 5th–4th century BCE (core works); broader corpus to 2nd century CE
author_bio: The Hippocratic Corpus is a collection of roughly 60 medical works attributed to Hippocrates of Cos. The historical Hippocrates (c. 460–370 BCE) is the founding figure, but the corpus is the product of a school and tradition spanning centuries.
author_notes: The TLG entry uses 'Hippocrates (Medicus, Corpus Hippocraticum)' to signal the collective nature. The historical Hippocrates is distinct from the corpus, but the bulk of works are anonymous/school products.
representative_titles: De mulierum affectibus i-iii | De morbis popularibus (= Epidemiae) | De morbis i-iii | De articulis | De diaeta i-iv | De semine, de natura pueri, de morbis iv | De affectionibus interioribus | Coa praesagia

author_cultural_context: Greek political philosophy and urban planning
author_bio: Hippodamus of Miletus was a Greek architect, urban planner, and political theorist of the 5th century BCE, known for his grid-plan city layout and his constitutional writings. His fragments and testimonia reflect his innovative ideas on ideal states.
author_notes: Source (TLG 1436) contains two works: 'Fragmenta Sp.' and 'Testimonia' (3,344 words). He is well-attested in Aristotle (Politics) and other ancient sources; the classification 'historical' is secure.

author_cultural_context: Christian theologian and writer; sometimes considered an anti-pope; a major figure in early Christian literature.
author_bio: Hippolytus of Rome was a prominent Christian theologian and a prolific author of exegetical, polemical, and historical works. His 'Refutatio omnium haeresium' is a key source for Gnostic teachings. He is often identified with the antipope of the same name.
author_notes: The corpus includes some spurious works (e.g., 'De consummatione mundi Sp.'), as well as genuine and dubiously attributed fragments.
representative_titles: Refutatio omnium haeresium (= Philosophumena) | Commentarium in Danielem | Chronicon | De antichristo | De benedictionibus Isaaci et Jacobi | De consummatione mundi Sp. | Contra haeresin Noeti | Fragmenta in Psalmos Sp.

author_bio: Hippon was a Presocratic philosopher, sometimes called a Pythagorean, who wrote on natural philosophy; only fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Sparse fragments; the identity as a philosopher is secure but details are uncertain.

author_bio: Hipponax was an Archaic Greek iambic poet from Ephesus, known for his satirical and obscene verse, and for his innovative metrical schemes.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments preserved in later sources.

author_bio: Hippostratus is a fragmentarily known author, possibly a historian or paradoxographer from the Hellenistic period. Only scattered fragments survive.
author_notes: The source label provides no disambiguating epithet. Attestation is minimal; genre and date are uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Greek mythology (eponymous hero of the Attic tribe Hippothoontis)
author_bio: Hippothoon is a figure from Greek mythology, an eponymous hero of the Attic tribe Hippothoontis, to whom fragmentary texts are loosely attributed.
author_notes: Fragmentary texts attributed to a mythic hero; authorship is conventional.

author_bio: Hippys is a poorly attested author, sometimes cited as a historian from Rhegium. Only a handful of fragments survive, and his very existence has been doubted by some scholars.
author_notes: The source tradition is very thin; the name may even be a ghost. Fragments do not permit a secure biographical or historical placement.

author_bio: A complex corpus of Greek texts centered on the legend of Alexander the Great, known as the 'Alexander Romance'. The tradition includes multiple recensions (A, B, E, F, Byzantine poetic) composed and adapted over many centuries.
author_notes: The heading is a scholarly designation for a multiform textual tradition, not an author. The original author (Pseudo-Callisthenes) is a conventional attribution.
representative_titles: Recensio F (cod. Flor. Laurentianus Ashburn 1444) | Recensio E (cod. Eton College 163) | Recensio Byzantina poetica (cod. Marcianus 408) | Recensio F | Recensio A sive Recensio vetusta | Recensio B | Recensio E | Recensio poetica (recensio R)

author_bio: An anonymous Greek travelogue describing the lives and miracles of the desert fathers of Egypt, composed by a monk from Palestine.
author_notes: The entry uses the work title as the author field, standard for anonymous works where the text's name serves as the heading.

author_bio: Homer, the legendary founder of Greek epic, is credited with the Iliad and Odyssey, the foundational works of Western literature. His life is clouded in tradition; multiple ancient biographies survive but are largely fictional.
author_notes: Direct traditional attribution as the author of the Iliad and Odyssey. The status 'legendary' reflects the uncertain historicity of the poet behind the epics.

author_bio: The Homeric Hymns are a collection of 34 hexameter poems addressed to various Greek gods, traditionally attributed to 'Homer' but now understood as the work of multiple anonymous poets. The corpus occupies the author slot as a named textual corpus.
author_notes: The 'author' is the corpus itself; individual hymns were composed by different poets over time.
representative_titles: In Mercurium | In Apollinem (fort. auctore Cynaetho Chio) | In Cererem | In Venerem | In Bacchum | In Pana | Fragmenta hymni in Bacchum | In Tellurem matrem omnium

author_bio: This entry covers remnant fragments of a cyclic Homeric Iliad, preserved as a small fragment. The rubric 'Homerica' designates a work title for texts attributed to the Homeric tradition but not securely ascribable to a single author.
author_notes: The label 'Homerica' is used for a fragment of the Ilias Homerica in cyclum inclusa. Not a personal author; treated as work title.

author_bio: Homer, the foundational poet of Greek literature, traditionally credited with the Iliad and Odyssey; here also associated with the comic mock-epic Margites.
author_notes: Historicity debated; attribution of Margites to Homer is ancient but uncertain; included as a single-fragment entry.

author_cultural_context: Late Antique Egypt; Greek and Egyptian priestly tradition
author_bio: An Egyptian priest or grammarian, traditionally credited with the Hieroglyphica, a compendium of symbolic interpretations of Egyptian hieroglyphs. The work survives in a Greek translation by one Philippus.
author_notes: The historical existence of Horapollo is debated, but he is treated as a real figure of Late Antiquity; the work has significant reception history.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous hymns preserved on papyri, including Christian and pagan religious poetry from the early Byzantine period.
author_notes: The label 'Hymni Anonymi' is a modern editorial grouping; the works are anonymous and transmitted under various papyrus fragments.
representative_titles: Hymni e papyris magicis collecti | Christianorum carmina | Hymnus in Dionysum (P. Ross. Georg. 1.11) | Descensus ad inferos (Brit. Mus. inv. 1192) | Naassenorum carmina | Aretalogia Sarapidis (P. Berol. 10525) | Hymnus in Isim (PSI 7.844) | Carmen mystarum (P. Argent. 1313)

author_bio: Hyperides was a leading Athenian orator and statesman, one of the ten Attic orators. His speeches, rediscovered on papyri, illuminate law, politics, and social life in 4th-century Athens.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; substantial fragments and some complete speeches survive.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Pro Euxenippo | In Demosthenem | In Athenogenem | Epitaphius | Pro Lycophrone | In Philippidem

author_bio: Hypermenes is known from a single short fragment, possibly a historiographer or mythographer.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; identity and date are entirely uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek author known only from a fragment and a title; nothing is known about his life or works beyond these remnants.
author_notes: The source data includes both a fragment and a title; the authorship is uncertain and the historicity is weakly attested.

author_bio: Hypsicles of Alexandria was a Greek mathematician and astronomer active around the 2nd century BCE. He is credited with the 'Anaphoricus' and a supplement to Euclid's Elements (Book XIV).
author_notes: Secure historical figure; works well-attested in manuscript tradition.
representative_titles: Hypsiclis liber sive elementorum liber xiv qui fertur | Anaphoricus

author_bio: A collection of anonymous iambic poems and fragments transmitted without authorial attribution, comprising both early and later pieces.
author_notes: The label 'Iambica Adespota' designates anonymous iambic fragments; no author can be assigned.

author_bio: Iambica Adespota is a scholarly label for anonymous and fragmentary iambic poetry from the Greek tradition. The designation indicates that the original author is unknown and that the works survive as unattributed remains.
author_notes: The source ID 'tlg0235' and the label 'Adespota' explicitly mark this as an anonymous collection. The parenthetical '(Ieg)' likely refers to a modern edition or classification system.

author_bio: Iamblichus (Alchem.) is a figure known only from alchemical fragments. It is uncertain whether this is the same as the Neoplatonist philosopher Iamblichus of Chalcis (c. 245–325 CE) or a different person; the designation 'Alchem.' indicates the alchemical tradition.
author_notes: The TLG distinguishes this Iamblichus from the Neoplatonist; the fragment is from a Paris alchemical manuscript. Attribution is uncertain.
representative_titles: Fragmenta alchemica (e cod. Paris. B.N. gr. 2327, fol. 266r)

author_bio: Iamblichus of Chalcis was a leading Neoplatonic philosopher, founder of the Syrian school. He synthesized Platonic, Pythagorean, and theurgical traditions in works such as 'De mysteriis' and 'De vita Pythagorica', exerting a profound influence on later pagan and Christian thought.
author_notes: The parenthetical (Phil.) in the source label disambiguates the philosopher from namesakes (e.g., Iamblichus the novelist).
representative_titles: De mysteriis | De vita Pythagorica | In Nicomachi arithmeticam introductionem | Protrepticus | De communi mathematica scientia | Theologoumena arithmeticae

author_bio: Iamblichus (Scr. Erot.) is the author of the Babyloniaca, an erotic novel preserved only in fragments. He is distinct from the Neoplatonic philosopher Iamblichus; his work belongs to the Second Sophistic period.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Scr. Erot.)' disambiguates this Iamblichus from the Neoplatonic philosopher. Only fragments (3,986 words) of the Babyloniaca survive.

author_bio: Ibycus was a Greek lyric poet from Rhegium active in the 6th century BCE, associated with the court of Polycrates of Samos. His choral poetry, known through fragments, combines erotic and mythological themes and shows broad narrative scope.
author_notes: Source (TLG 0293) includes two works labeled 'Fragmenta' (2,357 words). Ibycus is a standard author in the Greek lyric canon; his historical status is well-supported by ancient testimonia and papyrus discoveries.

author_bio: Iccus of Tarentum was a Greek athlete and Pythagorean philosopher known for his advocacy of dietary discipline and physical training. His teachings on regimen survive in fragmentary testimonies.
author_notes: Primary source for Iccus is later doxographical testimonia.

author_bio: Idaeus is an obscure Epicurean philosopher known only from a single inscribed title; no other writings or biographical details survive.
author_notes: The epithet 'Epic.' suggests Epicurean affiliation, but the author is otherwise unattested. The single surviving word (titulus) may be a funerary or dedicatory inscription rather than a literary work.

author_bio: Idaeus (Phil.) is known only from a single testimonium. The epithet 'Phil.' suggests a philosopher, but no school or doctrines are attested.
author_notes: The identification of Idaeus as a philosopher rests solely on the modern disambiguator; ancient evidence is lacking.

author_bio: Idomeneus of Lampsacus was an Epicurean philosopher and historian, known for biographical works on notable figures of the Socratic circle. His fragments preserve details on the lives of philosophers and statesmen.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; known primarily through citations in later authors such as Diogenes Laërtius.

author_bio: Ignatius Diaconus was a Byzantine deacon, hagiographer, and poet. He wrote the Vita Nicephori, a biography of Patriarch Nikephoros I of Constantinople, and various poems and letters.
author_notes: Identified as Ignatius Diaconus; the work is a key source for Byzantine iconodule history.

author_bio: Ignatius of Antioch, also known as Ignatius Theophorus, was an early Christian bishop and martyr whose seven authentic letters provide crucial evidence for early church structure and theology. A corpus of spurious and interpolated letters also circulates under his name.
author_notes: The authentic letters are securely attributed; the longer recension and additional epistles are later expansions. The corpus is carefully edited to distinguish genuine from spurious material.
representative_titles: Epistulae interpolatae et epistulae suppositiciae (recensio longior) Sp. | Epistulae vii genuinae (recensio media) | Fragmenta (e cod. Florent. Laur. 6.4) Sp.

author_bio: The Little Iliad (Ilias Parva) is a lost epic of the Trojan cycle, attributed in antiquity to Lesches of Mitylene or others. Surviving only in fragments, it narrates events after the Iliad.
author_notes: The entry is a work title (Ilias Parva). The traditional author (Lesches) is uncertain and the corpus consists of fragments.

author_bio: Iliu Persis ('The Sack of Troy') is an ancient Greek epic poem from the Epic Cycle, describing the fall of Troy. The author is traditionally assigned but unknown.
author_notes: The title stands for the work itself; the original author is uncertain. Fragments survive via later summaries.

author_bio: Ion of Chios was a versatile Greek poet and prose writer, active in the 5th century BCE. He is best known for his elegies and tragedies, and his works offer valuable insights into the intellectual and cultural life of classical Greece.
author_notes: The display name includes '(Eleg.)' to distinguish this author from the more famous Ion of Ephesus. The surviving fragments are limited but significant for the study of elegiac poetry.

author_bio: Ion of Chios was a versatile Greek poet and philosopher, active in the 5th century BCE. He wrote tragedies, dithyrambs, and philosophical works, with fragments spanning multiple genres.
author_notes: The label '(Phil. et Poeta)' indicates Ion's dual reputation as philosopher and poet. His works survive mainly in fragments.
