author_bio: Iophon was an Athenian tragic poet and son of Sophocles. He was active in the late 5th century BCE; only fragments of his plays survive.
author_notes: Biographical details preserved in ancient sources (e.g., Suda, Vita Sophoclis); only minor fragments remain.

author_bio: Irenaeus of Lyons was a Church Father and bishop known for his work 'Adversus haereses', a refutation of Gnosticism that established orthodox Christian theology.
author_notes: Works survive partially in Greek and in Latin translation; the Greek fragments are collected here.
representative_titles: Adversus haereses (libri 1-2) | Adversus haereses (liber 5) | Fragmenta deperditorum operum | Adversus haereses (liber 3) | Adversus haereses (liber 4) | Adversus haereses 5.3-13 (P. Jena) | Adversus haereses 3.9 (P. Oxy. 3.405)

author_cultural_context: Attic orator, specialist in inheritance law; student of Isocrates.
author_bio: Isaeus of Athens was a prominent logographer and teacher of Demosthenes, known for his speeches on inheritance cases, preserved in eleven complete works.
author_notes: Well-attested corpus; URN source ID.
representative_titles: De Pyrrho | De Philoctemone | De Hagnia | De Cirone | De Dicaeogene | De Menecle | De Apollodoro | De Cleonymo

author_bio: A Greek poet active in Rome, author of hymns to Isis, reflecting Hellenistic religious syncretism.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Scriptor Hymnorum' specifies his genre; sometimes identified with other Isidori.

author_bio: Isidorus is known only as a tragic poet from a scanty fragmentary record. The epithet 'Trag.' suggests a tragedian, but no substantial biographical or textual evidence survives to confirm his activity or works.
author_notes: The label 'Isidorus (Trag.)' indicates a tragedian named Isidorus. The surviving fragments are minute (29 words), making historical verification difficult. Status set to 'uncertain' due to lack of evidence.

author_bio: Isigonus is a little-known Greek writer of paradoxographical works, compiling marvels and wonders. Only fragments of his writings survive.
author_notes: The name appears only in fragmentary sources; exact period and place are uncertain.

author_date_range: Texts date from the 2nd–4th century CE
author_cultural_context: Hellenistic Egyptian alchemical and magical traditions
author_bio: Isis Prophetissa is a legendary figure, the goddess Isis cast as a prophet and teacher of alchemical secrets to her son Horus. The two surviving redactions of her teachings are important for the study of Greco-Egyptian alchemy and esoteric literature.
author_notes: This entry treats Isis as a personified author figure; the works are pseudepigraphic, attributed to the goddess. The date refers to the composition of the surviving texts, not the mythic figure.
representative_titles: Ἴσις προφῆτις τῷ υἱῷ Ὥρῳ (redactio prima) (e cod. | Ἴσις προφῆτις τῷ υἱῷ Ὥρῳ (redactio secunda) (e cod.

author_bio: Isocrates was a prominent Athenian rhetorician and one of the most influential educators of the 4th century BCE. His speeches, written for political and ceremonial occasions, advocate for pan-Hellenic unity and ethical leadership, and exemplify the elevated prose style of the Attic orators.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; extensive corpus securely attributed.
representative_titles: Antidosis (orat. 15) | Panathenaicus (orat. 12) | Panegyricus (orat. 4) | Philippus (orat. 5) | De pace (orat. 8) | Archidamus (orat. 6) | Evagoras (orat. 9) | Areopagiticus (orat. 7)

author_bio: Ister was a Hellenistic scholar and mythographer, likely associated with the Ptolemaic court. He compiled works on Attic antiquities and mythology, now lost except for fragments.
author_notes: The name Ister appears in citations by later authors; biographical details are minimal and speculative.

author_bio: Isyllus is known from an inscription (IG 4.950) containing a hymn to Asclepius. He may have been a local poet at Epidauros, but little else is known of his life.
author_notes: Attested only by a single inscription; identity as a poet is inferred.

author_bio: Byzantine physician and medical writer, author of works on uroscopy, diagnosis, and the psychic pneuma. His treatises reflect Galenic tradition.
author_notes: Well-attested Byzantine intellectual; manuscripts preserved.
representative_titles: De urinis | De diagnosi | De spiritu animali

author_bio: Joannes Antiochenus was a Byzantine chronicler active in the 7th century CE, known for a universal history from Adam to the early Byzantine period. His work survives only in fragments and is valued for its preservation of earlier sources.
author_notes: Author identified as Joannes Antiochenus; source label 'tlg4394' corresponds to a single-fragment corpus. Historicity is secure based on Byzantine historical references.

author_bio: Joannes Chortasmenus was a Byzantine scholar and teacher, known for excerpts from Aristotle's Sophistici Elenchi preserved in several manuscripts.
author_notes: The surviving work is a set of excerpts, suggesting an educational context. His full corpus may be larger but is not fully extant.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos (excerpta e codd. Bolon., B.U. gr. 3637; Lond., B.L. Harl. 5697; Flor. Laur.

author_bio: John Chrysostom (c. 349–407 CE) was Archbishop of Constantinople and a prolific preacher and exegete. His homilies on Matthew, Genesis, John, Acts, and the Pauline epistles are foundational for Eastern Christian theology and rhetoric.
author_notes: The name 'Joannes Chrysostomus' is the standard Latin/Greek form. His authentic works are well defined, though some pieces (marked 'Sp.' in the list) are spurious or dubious.
representative_titles: In Matthaeum (homiliae 1-90) | In Genesim (homiliae 1-67) | In Joannem (homiliae 1-88) | Expositiones in Psalmos | In Acta apostolorum (homiliae 1-55) | In epistulam i ad Corinthios (homiliae 1-44) | Eclogae i-xlviii ex diversis homiliis Sp. | In epistulam ad Romanos (homiliae 1-32)

author_bio: John of Epiphania was a late antique historian, known for a fragment of a work on the reigns of the Byzantine emperors Maurice and Phocas. His writing is a valuable source for the late 6th century, though only a single fragment remains.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg4392. The single fragment preserves part of his historical narrative. His identity as a historian from Epiphania is established.

author_bio: A Byzantine scholar and author of works on Roman antiquities, including 'De mensibus', 'De magistratibus populi Romani', and 'De ostentis'. His writings preserve important information on Roman calendar, offices, and omens.
author_notes: Also known as Lydus; the name Joannes Laurentius corresponds to John the Lydian.
representative_titles: De mensibus | De magistratibus populi Romani | De ostentis

author_bio: John Malalas (c. 490–570 CE) was a Byzantine chronicler from Antioch, author of the *Chronographia*, a world chronicle from Adam to the reign of Justinian. His work is a key source for late antique history.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical figure; the *Chronographia* survives in multiple manuscript traditions.
representative_titles: Chronographia | Chronographia (eclogae e cod. Paris. gr. 1336) | Chronologica (fort. auctore anonymo excerptorum chronologicorum)

author_cultural_context: Christian philosopher, theologian, and commentator on Aristotle
author_bio: John Philoponus was a prominent Christian philosopher and commentator in Alexandria, known for his critical commentaries on Aristotle, theological works, and arguments against the eternity of the world, influencing both medieval Christian and Islamic thought.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure with substantial corpus.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis physicorum libros commentaria | In Aristotelis libros de anima commentaria | In Aristotelis analytica priora commentaria | In Aristotelis analytica posteriora commentaria | De aeternitate mundi | In Aristotelis libros de generatione et corruptione commentaria | In libros de generatione animalium commentaria | In Aristotelis categorias commentarium

author_bio: John of Sardis was a Byzantine scholar and rhetorician, author of *Prolegomena* to Hermogenes’ rhetorical works. His commentary was once misattributed to George Diaereta. He represents the pedagogical tradition of late antique rhetoric.
author_notes: Attribution clarified from 'olim sub auctore Georgio Diaereta'. Works studied primarily by specialists in Byzantine rhetoric.
representative_titles: Prolegomena in Hermogenis librum PERÌ EU(RÉSEWS (olim sub auctore Georgio Diaereta)

author_bio: John Scylitzes (c. 1040–after 1101) was a Byzantine historian, author of the *Synopsis historiarum*, covering Byzantine emperors from 811 to 1057. His chronicle is a major source for medieval Byzantine history.
author_notes: Historical figure; the *Synopsis historiarum* is a well-known text. Display name uses 'Joannes Scylitzes' (standard Latinized form).

author_bio: John VI Kantakouzenos was a Byzantine emperor and prolific author. His 'Historiae' document the civil wars and political turmoil of 14th-century Byzantium from his own perspective.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a substantial surviving work.

author_bio: Joannes Anagnostes was a 15th-century Byzantine historian and poet from Thessalonica. He is best known for his account of the Ottoman siege and fall of Thessalonica in 1430, written in a rhetorical style.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Anagnostes Hist. et Poeta)' indicates the epithet 'Anagnostes' (reader) and his dual role as historian and poet.
representative_titles: De extremo Thessalonicensi excidio narratio | Pro viribus acta monodia

author_bio: Joannes Archiereus is a shadowy figure associated with alchemical writings, claiming the title 'high priest' in the tradition of sacred art. The work survives in a single Byzantine manuscript.
author_notes: Source label 'Archiereus Alchem.' identifies him as a high priest and alchemist. The work is a single alchemical treatise. Historicity is uncertain; possibly a pseudonym.
representative_titles: Ἰωάννου ἀρχιερέως τοῦ ἐν Ἐβειγίᾳ περὶ τῆς θείας τέχνης

author_bio: Byzantine Greek scholar, teacher, and translator active in Constantinople and later in Renaissance Italy. Author of prolegomena to the progymnasmata of Aphthonius.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure. The parentheses in the display name indicate the family name (Argyropulus) for disambiguation.

author_bio: Joannes Camaterus was a 12th-century Byzantine scholar and astrologer, author of an astronomical introduction and a work on the zodiac. He was active in the court of Manuel I Komnenos.
author_notes: Historical Byzantine scholar; the parentheses in the display name indicate the family name.

author_bio: Joannes Cameniates was a Byzantine official and eyewitness to the Arab sack of Thessalonica in 904 CE. His account De expugnatione Thessalonicae is a primary source for the event.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Cameniates)' disambiguates him from other Joannes.

author_bio: John Cananus was a Byzantine writer who composed an account of the Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1422, offering a vivid firsthand perspective.
author_notes: Only one work securely attributed. The epithet 'Cananus' may be a family name or toponym; little else is known about his life. The work is a key source for late Byzantine history.

author_bio: John Cinnamus (or Joannes Cinnamus) was a Byzantine historian and imperial secretary under Manuel I Komnenos. He authored a history of the reigns of John II and Manuel I, as well as an Ethopoeia.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; his historical work is a key source for 12th-century Byzantium.
representative_titles: Epitome rerum ab Joanne et Alexio Comnenis gestarum | Ethopoeia

author_bio: A Byzantine rhetorician and commentator, author of prolegomena to the progymnasmata of Aphthonius and to Hermogenes’ rhetorical works.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Doxapatres' is a surname; also known as John of Sicily in some contexts.
representative_titles: Prolegomena in Aphthonii progymnasmata | Prolegomena in Hermogenis librum PERÌ EU(RÉSEWS | Prolegomena in Hermogenis librum PERÌ STÁSEWN | Prolegomena in Hermogenis librum περὶ ἰδεῶν

author_bio: Joannes, sometimes identified with the Galenus family, was a late antique or Byzantine scholar who wrote allegorical commentaries on Hesiod's Theogony and Homer's Iliad. His works blend classical and Christian traditions.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Galenus' may indicate a family name or a place; the precise identity is uncertain but the author is historically attested.
representative_titles: Allegoriae in Hesiodi theogoniam | Allegoriae in Homeri Iliadem 4.1-4

author_bio: Joannes Grammaticus was a Byzantine scholar and poet, author of the 'Ekphrasis of the World Map' and poems in the Anacreontic tradition. His work reflects the learned and classicizing culture of the 6th-century Eastern Roman Empire.
author_notes: The label 'Gramm. et Poeta' indicates a combination of grammatical and poetic authorship.

author_bio: Joannes (Gramm. et Theol.) is a Byzantine grammarian and theologian known for polemical works against Manichaeans and monophysites. His identity is often confused with John Philoponus, but distinct attributions are maintained.
author_notes: The label 'Gramm. et Theol.' distinguishes this figure from other Johns; some works may have been misattributed.
representative_titles: Adversus Manichaeos (homilia 2) | Disputatio cum Manichaeo | Adversus Manichaeos (homilia 1) | Adversus Aphthartodocetas | Apologia concilii Chalcedonensis (excerpta Graeca) | Capitula xvii contra monophysitas | Syllogismi sanctoruum patrum | In evangelium secundum Joannem (excerpta duo) (e cod. Vallicell. E 40)

author_bio: An otherwise unknown physician named John, author of a commentary on Hippocrates' On the Nature of the Child preserved in a single manuscript. The work was attributed to 'John the Physician' and shows typical Alexandrian medical exegesis.
author_notes: The identifier '(Med.)' distinguishes this John from other homonyms. The manuscript tradition is unique, and the author's identity and date are uncertain.
representative_titles: Commentarii in Hippocratis librum de natura pueri

author_bio: John Pediasimus (active c. 1300 CE) was a Byzantine scholar and commentator. His paraphrastical scholia on Hesiod's Shield of Heracles are a key witness to the Byzantine reception of archaic Greek poetry.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Pediasimus)' distinguishes this John from others. His biographical details are not well known; he is identified primarily through his scholarly works.
representative_titles: Scholia in Hesiodi scutum (scholia paraphrastica Pediasimi et exegesis Joannis Tzetzae)

author_bio: A Byzantine scholar and protospatharius (court official), Joannes wrote an exegesis on Hesiod's Works and Days. His work reflects the continuation of classical scholarship in the Byzantine Empire.
author_notes: The full title and context suggest a Byzantine grammarian; identity is known only through his commentary on Hesiod.

author_region: Eastern Roman Empire (probably Alexandria or Constantinople)
author_bio: Joannes Rhetor, a late antique or early Byzantine scholar, composed a commentary and prolegomena on Hermogenes’ rhetorical treatise *On Ideas*. His works preserve key aspects of late Greek rhetorical theory.
author_notes: Author identifier includes disambiguating label '(Rhet.)'. Otherwise unattested; conjectural identification only.
representative_titles: Commentarium in Hermogenis librum περὶ ἰδεῶν | Prolegomena in Hermogenis librum περὶ ἰδεῶν

author_cultural_context: Late antique Greek education and compilation
author_bio: Byzantine anthologist who compiled a vast florilegium of excerpts from Greek authors. His Anthologium preserves many otherwise lost fragments.
author_notes: Name includes his epithet 'Stobaeus' to denote origin. The Anthologium is his sole known work, but it is a major compilation.

author_bio: Joannes (Theol.) is a Byzantine theological writer whose works were transmitted under the names of John of Damascus and John Syncellus. Modern scholarship assigns them to an otherwise unknown theologian.
author_notes: The corpus includes three works: two against iconoclasm (once attributed to John of Damascus) and one on sacred images. The identity of the author remains debated.
representative_titles: De sacris imaginibus contra Constantinum Cabalinum (sub nomine Joannis Syncelli) (olim sub auctore Joanne Damasce | Adversus iconoclastas (olim sub auctore Joanne Damasceno) | Narratio

author_bio: Ioannes Tzetzes was a prominent Byzantine grammarian, commentator, and poet, active in 12th-century Constantinople. His works include scholia on Aristophanes and treatises on poetic genres.
author_notes: Label 'Gramm. et Poeta' reflects his dual role; well-attested figure.
representative_titles: Prolegomena de comoedia Aristophanis | Versus de poematum generibus | De Pleiadibus (excerptum)

author_bio: Joel (or Joel the Chronographer) was a Byzantine monk and historian, author of the 'Chronographia compendiaria', a world chronicle from Adam to the capture of Constantinople in 1204.
author_notes: Known only from his sole surviving work; some scholars date him to the 13th century.

author_bio: Flavius Josephus was a Jewish historian and Roman citizen. His works, written in Greek, document Jewish history, the Jewish War, and his own role in the Roman-Jewish conflict. The Βασιλεῖαι (Kings) is one of his historical writings.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the work Βασιλεῖαι is attributed to Josephus in the TLG corpus.

author_bio: A Greek novelistic expansion of the biblical story of Joseph and Aseneth, extant in multiple recensions. The work is anonymous and attributed solely by its title.
author_notes: Author slot occupied by the title of the work itself; reflects cataloging practice where no author is known. The label 'Josephus Et Aseneth' is a conventional citation heading.

author_bio: King of Mauretania and prolific Greek author. Wrote on history, geography, and natural history; works survive in fragments.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; fragments collected in FGrHist.

author_bio: A woman named Julia to whom a few epigrams in the Greek Anthology are attributed. Her identity is obscure; she may have been a Roman matron writing in Greek or a late-antique poet.
author_notes: Very few fragments; the name 'Julia' alone does not allow precise dating or location. Attribution to a historical person is highly uncertain.

author_bio: Julianus, designated as an ecclesiastical writer, is known for a commentary on the Book of Job. His identity is otherwise poorly documented.
author_notes: The label 'Scr. Eccl.' indicates Julianus is classified as an ecclesiastical author; the commentary on Job is his sole surviving work.

author_bio: Julianus is the name associated with a brief legal text on physicians (Lex de medicis). The identity and historicity of this Julianus are unclear; he may be a legal compiler or a fictitious author.
author_notes: The label 'Scriptor Legis De Medicis' indicates this Julianus is known only for this specific legal fragment. Attribution is speculative.

author_bio: Julius, an Epicurean philosopher, is known only from a few fragments.
author_notes: The 'Epic.' likely denotes Epicurean; very little known.

author_bio: Julius Pollux (Ioulios Polydeukes) was a Greek sophist and lexicographer from Naucratis who taught at Athens. He compiled the 'Onomasticon', a comprehensive Greek thesaurus arranged by subject, which survives as a key resource for ancient vocabulary and realia.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Pollux' resolves the common name Julius. The Onomasticon is his sole extant work, firmly attributed.

author_bio: Jusjurandum Medicum (the Physician's Oath) is a Greek-language text that borrows the structure of the Hippocratic Oath and adapts it for Christian medical practice, reflecting the intersection of classical medical ethics and late antique religious values.
author_notes: The name Jusjurandum Medicum refers to a specific text used as the author heading; it is not a person or collective.

author_bio: Justin Martyr was a prominent Christian apologist and philosopher. Born in Flavia Neapolis (modern Nablus), he converted to Christianity after studying various philosophical schools. He taught in Rome and was martyred under Marcus Aurelius. His works include the 'Apologies' and the 'Dialogue with Trypho'.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with secure corpus.
representative_titles: Dialogus cum Tryphone | Apologia | Apologia secunda | Fragmenta operum deperditorum

author_bio: Known only from a single-word inscription (titulus). The name may be a personal name or a fragment. No further biography is possible.
author_notes: Single-word work; 'Caecalus' may be a proper name, a textual corruption, or an incomplete lemma.

author_bio: 'Calani Epistula' is a title occupying the author slot in the manuscript tradition. It refers to a fictional letter attributed to Calanus, the Indian philosopher who accompanied Alexander the Great. The work is a late rhetorical exercise.
author_notes: This entry represents a text, not a person. 'Calani Epistula' is the Latin title for a Greek pseudepigraphical letter.

author_bio: Callias (Comicus) is an Athenian comic poet of the 5th century BCE, known from fragments and a few titles. He may be related to the family of Callias the torch-bearer; his comedies included satirical themes. His works survive only in scattered testimonies and fragments.
author_notes: Distinguished from the wealthier Callias (Hipponicus) by the comic context. The exact number of works is uncertain; five titles are listed but many are fragments.

author_bio: Callicrates was a minor historical figure known only from a brief inscription. The attribution is epigraphic.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; the identification of the person is highly uncertain.

author_bio: A philosophical author known only from fragments; possibly a Peripatetic or Academic. His works survive in sparse quotations.
author_notes: TLG entry: fragments only; identity and philosophical allegiance uncertain.

author_bio: A designation for a later epic poet named Callimachus, distinguished from the famous Hellenistic poet. Only a single fragmentary titulus survives.
author_notes: Label 'Junior Epic.' indicates a later or lesser-known poet; very minimal surviving text.

author_cultural_context: Alexandrian poet, scholar, and librarian at the Library of Alexandria; central figure of Hellenistic poetry.
author_bio: Callimachus of Cyrene was a seminal Hellenistic poet and scholar, known for his erudite poetry, elegiac Aetia, hymns, and epigrams, and for shaping the literary canon at Alexandria.
author_notes: Major corpus; disambiguated as philologist (not historian). URN source ID.
representative_titles: Aetia | Iambi | Fragmenta et titulus | In Delum (hymn. 4) | Epigrammata | In Dianam (hymn. 3) | Fragmenta grammatica | Hecala

author_bio: Callinicus (Biogr.) was a Byzantine hagiographer, author of the 'Life of Saint Hypatius', an important source for early monastic and ecclesiastical history.
author_notes: The exact identity of Callinicus is uncertain; the label '(Biogr.)' distinguishes him from other authors of the same name.

author_bio: Callinicus (Soph.) was a sophist of the Second Sophistic, known from fragments and testimonia. He may be the same as Callinicus of Pergamum, a historian and rhetor. His works are lost except for citations.
author_notes: The TLG designates '(Soph.)' to distinguish him from other Callinici; identification with known sophists is plausible but not certain.

author_bio: Callinus of Ephesus was a Greek elegiac poet of the mid-7th century BCE, known for his martial exhortations in the face of invading Cimmerians. He is considered the earliest surviving Greek elegiac poet, and only a few fragments of his work remain.
author_notes: Historically attested poet to whom a number of fragments are securely assigned; biography is fragmentary but the historical core is accepted.

author_cultural_context: Greek philosophical or medical tradition (Pythagorean or Pre-Socratic)
author_bio: Calliphon is a name known from a handful of testimonia, possibly a Pythagorean or medical writer of the classical or hellenistic period. No works survive, and his identity remains obscure.
author_notes: Known only from testimonia (fragments in later authors); the historicity and precise context are uncertain. The name may be confused with other individuals.

author_bio: Callippus was a Greek comic poet active in the Hellenistic period. Only fragments of his work survive, cited by later lexicographers and anthologists.
author_notes: The label 'Comic.' indicates a comic poet; surviving fragments are too few for full reconstruction.

author_bio: Callippus is a minor Hellenistic historian known from a single fragment. Nothing else is known about his life or historical work.
author_notes: The '(Hist.)' label distinguishes this Callippus from other homonyms (e.g., the astronomer). The fragment is preserved only as a citation.

author_bio: Callisthenes was a Greek historian and philosopher, a relative of Aristotle who accompanied Alexander the Great. His works survive only in fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; works lost but fragments collected.

author_region: Roman Empire, perhaps Alexandria or Athens
author_bio: Callistratus (Sophista) was a Greek sophist and rhetorician, probably of the Imperial period, known for his 'Descriptions of Statues' (Ekphraseis), a collection of rhetorical descriptions of works of art. His identity is distinct from the earlier commentator on Aristophanes.
author_notes: The sophist Callistratus is known only from his surviving work. The parenthetical (Soph.) distinguishes him from other authors named Callistratus, notably the grammarian. His descriptions of statues are in the tradition of Philostratus' Imagines.

author_bio: Callistratus is a name attributed to a tragic poet known only from an 8-word fragment; virtually nothing is known of his life or work.
author_notes: The designation 'Trag.' indicates classification as a tragic poet; the sole surviving work is a single title or fragment of a titulus.

author_bio: Callixenus Atheniensis (of Athens) was a historical writer active in the 4th century BCE. His fragments, preserved through later authors, concern the history of Athens and antiquarian topics.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; the attribution to Callixenus is derived from references in later writers such as Athenaeus.

author_bio: A text bearing the Latin title 'Canon Librorum,' likely a Byzantine list of canonical books (possibly biblical or patristic). The work is a catalog heading, not an author. Its genre is stichometric or canonistic.
author_notes: The author slot is filled by a work title; the source label 'Canon Librorum' is a modern descriptor for a textual tradition, not an individual.

author_bio: Cantharus was a comic playwright from Athens, active in the late 5th or early 4th century BCE. Only fragments of his comedies survive.
author_notes: Known from fragments and titles; biographical details uncertain. Source ID tlg0428.

author_bio: A fragmentary Greek composition titled 'Cantus Lugubris' (Mournful Song), preserved on a papyrus in the British Museum. The text likely belongs to a poetic or liturgical lament tradition.
author_notes: The title is Latin, but the text is Greek; the work is anonymous and preserved only in a single fragment. Cataloged as a work title.

author_bio: Capito was a Greek author known only through fragmentary remains. His identity and background are unclear, though the name suggests a possible Roman or hellenized individual. The extant fragments offer limited insight into his literary output.
author_notes: The name Capito is attested in TLG as a minor author; no securely dated biographical information survives. The fragments are assigned to a single work.

author_bio: Carcinus the younger was a tragic poet, seemingly of Sicilian origin but active in Athens. He belonged to a family of dramatists and competed at the City Dionysia.
author_notes: Identifier 'tlg0327' labels this as 'Carcinus (Junior Trag.)'. The qualifier 'Junior Trag.' clarifies this as a distinct figure from the older Carcinus; there is some ambiguity in ancient sources.

author_bio: Carcinus (tragic poet) was an Athenian tragedian of the 5th century BCE, known for competing in city festivals. His fragments survive via later quotations.
author_notes: The label 'Trag.' disambiguates Carcinus as a tragedian. Only a single fragment remains.

author_bio: An anonymous astrological poem preserved only in a fragment, of uncertain date and authorship.
author_notes: The author field is assigned to the poem itself, as the actual author is unknown; treat as a work title.

author_bio: Carmen Naupactium ('The Naupactian Poem') is an ancient epic poem, possibly from Naupactus, dealing with local myths. The author is unknown.
author_notes: The title is used as the author entry since the poet is not named. Fragments only.

author_bio: Carmina Convivialia ('Banquet Songs') is a modern collection of Greek drinking songs and sympotic verses, transmitted anonymously and assembled from various sources.
author_notes: This is a modern editorial label for a corpus of anonymous drinking songs, not a historical author.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek folk song and popular verse, oral tradition
author_bio: Carmina Popularia is a modern collective designation for anonymous fragments of Greek popular songs, street cries, and ritual verses preserved in literary quotations. They reflect non-elite oral culture.
author_notes: The heading is a modern editorial label for a corpus of anonymous folk poetry, not an individual author or work.

author_region: unknown (possibly Egypt, given the papyrus evidence)
author_bio: Carneiscus was an Epicurean philosopher whose writings survive on papyrus from Herculaneum (P. Herc. 1027). He is known for a work titled *Philistas* and other fragments, contributing to our understanding of Epicurean circles.
author_notes: The papyrus fragments from Herculaneum provide a firm basis for historicity, though the figure is specialized and not widely known.

author_bio: Carystius of Pergamon was a Greek grammarian and historian active in the late Hellenistic period. He wrote 'On the Poets of Pergamon', a work of literary history and biography, as well as other historical commentaries (esp. 'On the Tragic Poets'). Only fragments survive, preserved by Athenaeus and other later authors.
author_notes: Distinguished from Carystius the historian by region; Pergamene origin is inferred from his interest in Pergamon.

author_bio: Greek historian and Roman senator, author of a monumental *Roman History* covering Rome from foundation to his own era, partly preserved.
author_notes: Major historical source; works survive in multiple forms including epitomes and excerpts.
representative_titles: Historiae Romanae | Historiae Romanae (Xiphilini epitome) | Historiae Romanae (versio 1 in volumine 1) | Historiae Romanae (Joannis Antiocheni excerpta e Dione derivata) | Historiae Romanae (Petri Patricii excerpta Vaticana sive Maiana) | Historiae Romanae (versio 1 in volumine 3) | Historiae Romanae (versio 2 in volumine 1) | Historiae Romanae (excerpta Salmasiana)

author_bio: Cassius, known as Iatrosophista, was a Greek physician and writer of medical problems and physical questions. His work blends Hellenistic medicine with philosophical inquiry.
author_notes: The author is well attested in the tradition of Greek medical compilations, though his exact identity is sometimes confused with other writers named Cassius.

author_cultural_context: Greek rhetoric and philosophy under the Roman Empire
author_bio: Cassius Longinus (ca. 213–273 CE) was a Greek rhetorician and philosopher active in Athens and later advisor to Queen Zenobia of Palmyra. He is known for rhetorical works and a lost treatise on the sublime, once mistakenly attributed to him.
author_notes: Author name includes parenthetical disambiguation '(Longinus)' to differentiate from the historian Cassius Dio. The attribution 'Longinus' reflects a traditional misidentification as the author of 'On the Sublime'.

author_bio: Known from fragments, possibly a minor poet; the name is rare and the precise historical context is lost.
author_notes: Two fragments survive, but no reliable biographical information is available.

author_bio: A collective textual tradition of biblical commentaries (catena) compiled by various anonymous Byzantine scholars, incorporating patristic excerpts.
author_notes: TLG author entry: 'Catenae' refers to a corpus of biblical catenae, not to a specific author. The author slot is occupied by the title of the textual tradition.
representative_titles: Catena in Acta (catena Andreae) (e cod. Oxon. coll. nov. 58) | Catena in epistulam ad Romanos (typus Monacensis) (e cod. Monac. gr. 412) | Catena in epistulam i ad Corinthios (typus Vaticanus) (e cod. Paris. gr. 227) | Catena in epistulam ad Hebraeos (catena Nicetae) (e cod. Paris. gr. 238) | Catena in Matthaeum (catena integra) (e cod. Paris. Coislin. gr. 23) | Catena in Joannem (catena integra) (e codd. Paris. Coislin. 23 + Oxon. Bodl. Auct. T.1.4) | Catena in Marcum (recensio ii) (e codd. Oxon. Bodl. Laud. 33 + Paris. Coislin. 23 + Paris. gr. 178) | Catena in Lucam (typus B) (e codd. Paris. Coislin. 23 + Oxon. Bodl.

author_bio: Cebes is the traditional author of the 'Cebetis Tabula', an allegorical dialogue describing the journey of the soul through life. The historical Cebes was a disciple of Philolaus and Socrates, but the work is likely later (perhaps 1st century CE) and pseudonymous.
author_notes: The attribution to Cebes is traditional; the text is often considered pseudonymous or of uncertain date.

author_bio: Celsus was a Middle Platonic philosopher and the author of the 'True Discourse' (Ἀληθὴς λόγος), the earliest known comprehensive criticism of Christianity, preserved mainly in Origen's reply.
author_notes: Celsus is well-known from Origen's Contra Celsum, which preserves extensive fragments of his work. Date and identity remain debated but securely within the 2nd century CE.

author_bio: Cephalion is a fragmentarily preserved author of uncertain identity; possibly a historian or mythographer.
author_notes: 3082 words survive but no clear biographical data; the name may refer to multiple individuals.

author_bio: Cephisodorus is a name associated with fragmentary Greek texts, but with insufficient evidence to determine historical or legendary status; likely a minor figure or possibly confused with other bearers of the name.
author_notes: Name appears in fragmentary sources; identification uncertain.

author_bio: A Cynic philosopher and poet from Megalopolis, known for his ethical verse fragments (papyrus finds) and political activity in the Achaean League.
author_notes: Attested by literary sources and papyri; historicity secure.

author_bio: Legendary early Greek poet, sometimes associated with the epic tradition and mentioned as a possible author of the Little Iliad or the Aethiopis. His existence and works are uncertain.
author_notes: The evidence is a single testimonium; the name appears in ancient lists of epic poets but no certain fragments survive.

author_bio: The Certamen Homeri et Hesiodi is a fictional contest narrative between the poets Homer and Hesiod, preserved in a papyrus (P. Petrie 25) and later manuscripts. The text is a literary exercise, possibly by Alcidamas or a later rhetorician, but is indexed under its title as a traditional work.
author_notes: This entry is a work title, as the text is a composite composition. The authorship is unknown or debated, and the catalog uses the work's title as the agent.
representative_titles: Certamen Homeri et Hesiodi | Certamen Homeri et Hesiodi (P. Petrie 25)

author_bio: Greek philosopher and historian known for works on Egypt and religious philosophy; fragments survive through later citations.
author_notes: The 'Hist. et Phil.' label indicates a dual role as historian and philosopher; his works are known only from fragments.

author_bio: Chaeremon was an Athenian tragic poet of the 4th century BCE, known for his refined style and fragments from plays such as 'Achilles' and 'Centaur'.
author_notes: The tag '(Trag.)' distinguishes this Chaeremon as a tragedian. His works survive only in fragments cited by later authors.

author_bio: Chaerion is known only from a single inscribed titulus of two words. Nothing certain can be said about this figure.
author_notes: Extremely sparse attestation; the name may be a personal name or part of a fragment. Source status is highly uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Peripatetic school, biography and literary criticism
author_bio: Chamaeleon of Heraclea was a Peripatetic philosopher and scholar known for works on literary biography and music, including treatises on Homer, Hesiod, and Pindar. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: Attested as a student of Aristotle; fragments preserved by later authors. Attribution of fragmenta is generally secure.

author_bio: Charax of Pergamon was a Greek historian whose fragments survive on subjects such as geography and mythology. His work is transmitted chiefly through later compilations.
author_notes: The identification with Pergamon is conventional but not absolutely secure. Surviving fragments are few.

author_bio: Chares of Mytilene (possibly) is a minor figure known from a few sententiae (maxims) and fragments. His identity is uncertain; he may be a philosopher or a poet, but his works survive only in quotations.
author_notes: Not to be confused with the historian Chares of Mytilene. The attribution of the Sententiae is insecure.

author_bio: Chariclides is known only from a single fragment, preserving no firm biographical details. The name suggests a Greek context, but the work remains too scant for confident dating or attribution.
author_notes: The name Chariclides appears in Greek literature but the author's historicity is uncertain. The fragment is too brief to establish identity or period.

author_cultural_context: Attic orator or statesman known only from fragmentary citations.
author_bio: Charinus is a little-known figure from classical Athens, attested only by a single fragment, possibly an orator or politician.
author_notes: Single fragment (21 words); identity uncertain.

author_bio: Chariton of Aphrodisias was a Greek novelist, author of the romance 'Chaereas and Callirhoe', one of the earliest surviving novels.
author_notes: Well-attested as the author of the novel; name appears in the text itself.

author_bio: Charon of Lampsacus was an early Greek historian and logographer, known for works on local and Persian history. His fragments preserve important details on archaic Greek and Near Eastern interactions.
author_notes: Likely the historian, but the name Charon is also shared with a mythical ferryman and a sculptor; context confirms the Lampsacene historian.

author_bio: Legendary lawgiver of Catana in Sicily, credited with a legal code later cited by Aristotle. Historical details are intertwined with tradition.
author_notes: Attributed as a figure of archaic legislation; surviving fragment is spurious or late.

author_bio: Chersias is a shadowy figure associated with the Epic Cycle or Boeotian poetry. A single dubious fragment, sometimes attributed to Callippus, survives.
author_notes: Attribution is highly uncertain; the surviving fragment may belong to another author. Name known only from late sources.

author_bio: A brief pseudonymous letter attributed to Chilonis, a figure from Spartan historiography, but the text is known only as a literary exercise or later composition; the author field here refers to the epistle itself.
author_notes: The label 'Chilonis Epistula' is a title for a short epistolary text; classified as work title.

author_bio: Chionides was an Athenian comic poet of the Old Comedy, traditionally considered a contemporary of Magnes. Only title and fragment survive.
author_notes: Could be confused with Chionis; fragments assigned are minimal.

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to Chionis, but likely a pseudepigraphic corpus, not an individual author.
author_notes: The display name 'Chionis Epistulae' indicates a work title occupying the author slot.

author_bio: Choerilus of Iasus is reputed to have been an epic poet who accompanied Alexander the Great and wrote a poem on his victories. Only fragments survive, and his biography is shaped by tradition.
author_notes: The historicity of Choerilus is debated; he is often treated as a legendary or semi-legendary figure due to the anecdotal nature of the sources.

author_bio: Choerilus (Epic.) was a Greek epic poet from Ionia, active in the 5th century BCE. He is known primarily through fragments of his epic works, which reflect the conventions and themes of early Greek heroic poetry.
author_notes: Author identifier includes '(Epic.)' to distinguish from other figures named Choerilus. The attested fragments are sparse and transmitted through later quotations.
representative_titles: Fragmenta epica | Fragmenta et tituli | Fragmenta dubia (fort. auctore Choerilo Iasensi) | Tituli (P. Oxy. 11.1399)

author_bio: Choerilus was a tragic poet of Athens, active in the late 6th to early 5th century BCE. He is considered one of the early figures in the development of Greek tragedy, though only fragments of his work survive. His plays dealt with mythological themes and he is sometimes noted as a predecessor to Aeschylus.
author_notes: Author ID tlg0302; assigned to tragic poet Choerilus to distinguish from other homonymous authors. Fragments only.

author_bio: A corpus of anonymous Greek choliambic verse fragments, including a piece possibly by Cercidas.
author_notes: The label 'Choliambica Adespota' is a modern scholarly designation for anonymous choliambic fragments; one fragment is attributed tentatively to Cercidas.
representative_titles: Anonymus in turpilucrum (fort. auctore Cercida) | Fragmenta choliambica

author_cultural_context: Greek rhetoric and sophistry in the Eastern Roman Empire
author_bio: A Greek sophist and rhetorician from Gaza, active in the first half of the 6th century CE. He studied under Procopius of Gaza and wrote declamations, epithalamia, and other rhetorical works, providing a glimpse into late-antique education and culture.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with surviving works.

author_cultural_context: Late antique Greek poetry / Egyptian Greek
author_bio: Christodorus of Coptos was a Greek epic poet of late antiquity. He is known for his ecphrastic poetry, including descriptions of statues in the Zeuxippus gymnasium at Constantinople. His works reflect the literary culture of the Thebaid and the imperial court of Anastasius I.
author_notes: Christodorus is attested in the Greek Anthology (Book 2) and by other sources. A fragment with known provenance exists.

author_bio: The Chronicon Paschale is an anonymous Byzantine world chronicle, composed in Greek, covering history from Adam to the early 7th century CE. Its title derives from its use of the Easter (Paschal) calendar.
author_notes: The author field here is occupied by the title of the work itself; the real author is unknown.

author_bio: Anonymous Byzantine chronological compilations preserved in manuscript form, likely produced for monastic or courtly historical reference.
author_notes: Explicitly anonymous; author label reflects the nature of the texts.
representative_titles: Chronographia brevis (e cod. Paris. suppl. gr. 67) | Chronographia brevis (e cod. Coislin. 193) | Chronographia brevis (e cod. Monac. gr. 510)

author_bio: Chrysermus was a Greek physician active in Alexandria during the late Hellenistic or early Imperial period. He is known from fragmentary references in medical doxography, possibly associated with the Herophilean or Empiricist schools.
author_notes: The fragments are preserved in later medical compilations. TLG identifier tlg2195.

author_bio: Chrysippus (Hist.) is a shadowy historian known only from a single fragment; nothing is known about his life or work.
author_notes: Only 60 words survive; the label '(Hist.)' distinguishes him from the famous Stoic philosopher; historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Third head of the Stoic school in Athens, a prolific philosopher who systematized early Stoicism. He wrote extensively on logic, physics, and ethics, and is regarded as a second founder of Stoicism. His works survive only in fragments, but they are extensive and crucial for understanding Stoic thought.
author_notes: The designation (Phil.) distinguishes him from homonyms. The fragment collection includes logical, physical, ethical, and exegetical texts, as well as poetic fragments.
representative_titles: Fragmenta logica et physica | Fragmenta moralia | Fragmenta ad singulos libros relata | Fragmenta quae ad explicationem carminum Homericorum pertinent | Fragmentum (P. Ant. 2.61) | Fragmenta poetica

author_bio: Cineas was a Thessalian diplomat and author, best known as the trusted advisor of King Pyrrhus of Epirus. His writings, now fragmentary, covered historical and perhaps rhetorical subjects.
author_notes: The identification with the historical Cineas (envoy of Pyrrhus) is widely accepted but the fragments are sparse.

author_bio: Cinesias was an Athenian dithyrambic poet active in the early 4th century BCE, known for his elaborate style and frequently mocked by comic playwrights such as Aristophanes. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Identification relies on ancient testimonia; the author heading is ambiguous due to multiple possible persons named Cinesias.

author_bio: Claudius, a Greek author of uncertain identity, is known only from a single fragment. Likely active in the 1st century CE, further biographical details are unknown.
author_notes: The name 'Claudius' is common; without further qualification, the author is virtually unattested.

author_bio: Claudius Casilon is a little-known Greek grammarian or rhetor, attested only by a single fragment preserved in a manuscript. His work appears to have focused on Attic orators.
author_notes: The author is known only from a single fragment transmitted in a codex; precise dates and biography are unknown.
representative_titles: Παρὰ τοῖς Ἀττικοῖς ῥήτορσι ζητούμενα (fragmentum) (e cod.

author_bio: Claudius Iolaus is a poorly known author of historical fragments; his identity and date are uncertain, and only a few references survive.
author_notes: Fragmentary; possibly a later grammarian or compiler.

author_bio: Ptolemy was a preeminent Greco-Roman astronomer, mathematician, and geographer whose works, especially the Syntaxis (Almagest) and Geography, shaped scientific thought for over a millennium.
author_notes: This entry represents the full corpus of Ptolemy's works transmitted in Greek, including scientific and astrological texts.
representative_titles: Syntaxis mathematica | Geographia (lib. 4-8) | Apotelesmatica (= Tetrabiblos) | Geographia (lib. 1-3) | Harmonica | Phaseis | Προχείρων κανόνων διάταξις καὶ ψηφοφορία | Hypotheses

author_cultural_context: Greek author known only from a single fragment.
author_bio: Cleaenetus is an obscure Greek writer attested solely by a 17-word fragment, possibly from a lost work of history or philosophy.
author_notes: Very short fragment; no secure biographical data.

author_bio: Cleanthes of Assos succeeded Zeno as the second head of the Stoa. He was a key figure in early Stoicism, expanding its physics and theology. His Hymn to Zeus is a celebrated philosophical poem.
author_notes: Cleanthes is a well-documented historical figure. The TLG entry includes both testimonia and fragments. Display name 'Cleanthes' is sufficient.

author_bio: Clearchus (Comicus) is a poorly attested comic poet from ancient Greece. Only two fragments of his work survive, and his exact identity is uncertain; he may be the same as Clearchus of Athens or other known figures.
author_notes: The TLG distinguishes him with 'Comic.' to separate from other authors named Clearchus; his biography is virtually unknown.

author_bio: Greek philosopher of the Peripatetic school, student of Aristotle, author of works on ethics, zoology, and paradoxa.
author_notes: The label 'Phil.' distinguishes him from other homonymous figures; authorship of some fragments is debated.

author_bio: Clement of Rome (fl. c. 96 CE) was an early Christian bishop of Rome, author of the First Epistle to the Corinthians. A large corpus of pseudepigraphical writings (Homilies, Recognitions, letters on virginity) is attributed to him, reflecting later traditions.
author_notes: Only First Clement is considered authentic; the other works (marked 'Sp.') are attributed pseudepigraphically. The author_historicity_status is historical for the person, but the corpus is mixed.
representative_titles: Homiliae Sp. | Pseudo-Clementina (epitome de gestis Petri praemetaphrastica) Sp. | Pseudo-Clementina (epitome altera auctore Symeone Metaphrasta) Sp. | Epistula i ad Corinthios | Epistula ii ad Corinthios Sp. | Epistula Clementis ad Jacobum Sp. | Epistulae de virginitate Sp. | Recognitiones (ex Eusebio) Sp.

author_bio: Clement of Alexandria was a Christian theologian and head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria, known for synthesizing Greek philosophical traditions with Christian doctrine in works such as the *Stromata* and *Paedagogus*.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with multiple extant works.
representative_titles: Stromata | Paedagogus | Protrepticus | Quis dives salvetur | Excerpta ex Theodoto | Fragmenta | Eclogae propheticae | Hymnus Christi servatoris

author_cultural_context: Pseudepigraphic epistolography; tradition of the Seven Sages
author_bio: The 'Cleobuli Epistula' is a late antique pseudepigraphic letter attributed to Cleobulus, a legendary tyrant of Lindos and one of the Seven Sages. The work is a brief, moralising piece typical of the epistolary tradition that gathered and invented correspondence of wise men. Since there is no evidence of a historical author, it is treated as a work-title attribution.
author_notes: This is a work-title entry: the author slot is occupied by the title of the piece itself. There is no identifiable person behind the pseudepigraphon.

author_cultural_context: Greek gnomic poetry and riddle tradition
author_bio: Cleobulina, daughter of Cleobulus of Lindos (one of the Seven Sages), was a poet known for composing riddles in hexameter verse. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: The corpus is very small but she is a rare attested female poet from the Archaic period.

author_cultural_context: Greek lyric poetry, Seven Sages tradition
author_bio: Cleobulus of Lindos was a tyrant and poet, counted among the Seven Sages, known for gnomic sayings and possibly epigrams.
author_notes: His historicity is mixed with legend; some attributed fragments are likely later creations.

author_bio: Cleomachus is a little-known author preserved only in a few fragments, possibly from a didactic or poetic work. No biographical details survive.
author_notes: The assigned fragments are minimal; the name may be a variant or misattribution from a larger compendium.

author_bio: A Greek Stoic astronomer, known for his treatise 'On the Circular Motion of the Celestial Bodies', which explains and critiques contemporary astronomical theories.
author_notes: Biographical details are scarce; dating is approximate based on internal references. The work survives in a single medieval manuscript tradition.

author_bio: Greek author known only from a single inscription or very brief fragment; otherwise unidentified.
author_notes: Extremely sparse attestation; may refer to multiple homonymous individuals.

author_cultural_context: Athenian politician and general during the Peloponnesian War; lampooned by Aristophanes.
author_bio: Cleon was a prominent Athenian demagogue and military leader known for his hawkish stance during the Peloponnesian War. He is portrayed negatively in contemporary comedy. Surviving fragments include inscriptions and possible remnants of his speeches.
author_notes: The attribution of the fragments 'Fragmenta et titulus' and 'Fragmentum' is uncertain; some may not derive directly from Cleon but from references to him.

author_bio: Cleonides is the supposed author of the 'Eisagogē Harmonikē' (Introduction to Harmonics), a handbook of music theory summarizing Aristoxenian doctrine. The identity and date of Cleonides are uncertain; he may have lived in the 2nd century CE or later.
author_notes: The attribution to Cleonides is conventional; his historicity is not securely established. The work is a valuable source for ancient harmonic theory.

author_cultural_context: Pseudonymous author of a metrological treatise; possibly a legendary figure associated with weights and measures.
author_bio: Cleopatra (not the famous queen) is the name attached to a Greek treatise on weights and measures, likely a pseudepigraphic or legendary attribution in the tradition of ancient technical writing.
author_notes: The source lists one work; historicity is uncertain; the name may be a legendary pseudonym for practical metrological literature.

author_bio: Cleophon was an Athenian politician and demagogue known from historical sources; a few inscriptions (tituli) survive under his name.
author_notes: The corpus consists only of a single inscription; literary fragments are not extant.

author_bio: Cleostratus of Tenedos (fl. c. 500 BCE) was an early Greek astronomer and astrologer credited with introducing the zodiac and the octaeteris cycle. Only a few testimonia and fragments survive, indicating his role in early scientific thought.
author_notes: The corpus includes testimonia, fragments, and an astrological fragment. His historicity is generally accepted, though the precise details of his contributions are debated.

author_bio: Clidemus was an early Greek historian of Athens (Atthis writer), active in the 4th century BCE. His work, now fragmentary, was an important source for local Attic history, cults, and antiquities.
author_notes: Cited by later authors such as Plutarch and Athenaeus; only fragments remain.

author_bio: Clidemus was an Athenian historian and mythographer active in the 4th century BCE. He wrote an Atthis, a local history of Athens, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: (Phil.) appended to distinguish from other Clidemi; often identified as a local historian with limited surviving fragments.

author_bio: Clinias is a shadowy figure known only from a few fragments and testimonia, plausibly a Pythagorean philosopher from the 4th or 3rd century BCE. The surviving fragments suggest involvement in Pythagorean ethics and number theory.
author_notes: The identity of Clinias is unclear; the TLG treats this as a distinct author, but the material is fragmentary and the historicity cannot be confirmed. Set to uncertain.

author_bio: Clitarchus is a poorly attested author of a collection of maxims (Sententiae). The name is sometimes confused with the more famous historian of Alexander, but the extant sententiae belong to a different tradition.
author_notes: The identification with the historian Clitarchus is not supported by the content; likely a distinct, lesser-known gnomologist.

author_bio: Clitonymus is a shadowy Hellenistic author known only from a few fragments. He may have written on historical or paradoxographical subjects.
author_notes: Very little known; identity uncertain; possibly confused with other names (e.g., Clitarchus).

author_bio: Clitophon is a poorly attested ancient Greek author, known only from one fragment (413 words). The name is known from Platonic dialogues and other literary contexts, but this author cannot be reliably identified with any known figure.
author_notes: The classification is uncertain due to minimal attestation. The name appears in Plato, but this fragment may belong to a different, otherwise unknown, Clitophon.

author_bio: Clytus is a shadowy Greek author, possibly a historian or mythographer, known only from fragments. The name may indicate a connection to Miletus, but evidence is insufficient for secure identification.
author_notes: The author is known only from fragmentary citations in later authors; date and origin are speculative.

author_bio: Colluthus of Lycopolis was a Greek epic poet of the late antique period, known for 'The Rape of Helen' (Raptio Helenae), a short hexameter poem on the abduction of Helen by Paris. He belongs to the School of Nonnus.
author_notes: Source ID uses the CTS URN format. The poet is distinct from other homonymous figures (e.g., the grammarian).

author_bio: Comarius is a shadowy figure in the Greek alchemical tradition, known from a single brief treatise on the philosopher's stone preserved in a Paris manuscript. His historicity is uncertain; he may be a legendary authority.
author_notes: The source label is a personal name but the figure is known only from alchemical pseudepigrapha; 'Comarius' may be a pseudonym or legendary person.
representative_titles: De lapide philosophorum (e cod. Paris. B.N. gr. 2327, fol. 74r)

author_cultural_context: Greek comedy (especially Middle and New Comedy)
author_bio: Comica Adespota are anonymous fragments of Greek comedy, often attributed to authors like Cratinus, Eupolis, or Aristophanes in the past, but now considered securely anonymous.
author_notes: The annotation '((Caf))' may refer to a source collection (CGF) or an earlier attribution to a specific poet; the fragments are genuinely adespota.

author_bio: A corpus of anonymous comic fragments from Greek New, Middle, and Old Comedy, including spurious, dubia, and Byzantine lexicographic excerpts.
author_notes: 'Adespota' indicates anonymous comic fragments; the label stands in for the author slot. Cgfpr= Comicorum Graecorum Fragmenta (Poetae Comici Graeci).
representative_titles: Adespota novae comoediae | Dubia | Excerpta, florilegia et sententiae | Adespota veteris comoediae | Λέξεις κωμικαί | Argumenta metrica | Adespota Doriensium comoediae | Argumenta comica

author_bio: A collection of anonymous comic fragments (adespota) from ancient Greek comedy, attributed to no known author. These fragments represent lost plays from the comic tradition.
author_notes: Explicitly anonymous heading; works are unattributed comic fragments.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous or unattributed fragments of ancient Greek comedy, spanning Old, Middle, and New Comedy. The fragments include dubious and possibly Doric pieces.
author_notes: The heading 'Comica Adespota' is a standard corpus label for anonymous comic fragments. It does not represent a single author.
representative_titles: Adespota novae comoediae | Adespota veteris comoediae | Fragmenta incertae comoediae | Adespota dubia | Fragmentum incerti poetae Dorici

author_display_name: Commentaria In Dionysii Thracis Artem Grammaticam
author_canonical_name: Commentaria In Dionysii Thracis Artem Grammaticam
author_bio: A collective corpus of Byzantine scholia and commentaries on Dionysius Thrax's Art of Grammar. The entries comprise excerpts and compilations by various authors including Choeroboscus, Heliodorus, Melampus, and others.
author_notes: The display name is a descriptive title, not a personal author. Multiple manuscripts and redactions are gathered under this heading.
representative_titles: Scholia Vaticana (partim excerpta ex Georgio Choerobosco, Georgio quodam, Porphyrio, Melampode, Stephano, Diomede | Scholia Marciana (partim excerpta ex Heliodoro, Tryphone, Diomede, Stephano, Georgio Choerobosco, Gregorio Corint | Scholia Londinensia (partim excerpta ex Heliodoro) | Commentarius (sub auctore Melampode vel Diomede) | Commentarius (sub auctore Heliodoro) | Commentariolus Byzantinus | Prolegomena Vossiana

author_region: Eastern Roman Empire (Ephesus, Chalcedon, Constantinople, Jerusalem)
author_cultural_context: Ecclesiastical councils: proceedings, canons, and doctrinal decrees in Greek
author_bio: A corpus of documents from ecumenical councils of the early Church, including the Councils of Ephesus (431), Chalcedon (451), and synods in Constantinople and Jerusalem (536).
author_notes: Corporate authorship: the proceedings and decrees are produced collectively by the councils, not by an individual. Confidence is high for the corpus as a whole.
representative_titles: Concilium universale Ephesenum anno 431 | Concilium universale Chalcedonense anno 451 | Synodus Constantinopolitana et Hierosolymitana anno 536

author_bio: Conon is a Greek mythographer and writer, known for a collection of stories (Narratives) that survive in summary form. He compiled legendary and historical accounts from earlier sources, providing a valuable source for lost Hellenistic literature.
author_notes: The identification with Conon the mythographer is typical but not without uncertainty; only fragments of his work survive.

author_bio: Constantinus is the name associated with two Byzantine works: a Chronicle (Compendium chronicum) and a biography of the poet Oppian (Vita Oppiani). The identity of the author remains uncertain.
author_notes: The name 'Constantinus' may refer to more than one author; attribution of the Vita Oppiani is debated.

author_bio: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos was a Byzantine emperor (r. 913–959) and a prolific patron of learning. He oversaw the compilation of major historical and administrative encyclopedias, including De administrando imperio, De cerimoniis, and historical excerpts, which preserve invaluable ancient sources.
author_notes: Source (TLG 3023) lists 13 works (792,264 words). The emperor's personal authorship is debated; the works are associated with his court circle. The classification 'historical' is secure.
representative_titles: De virtutibus et vitiis | De legationibus | De sententiis | De cerimoniis aulae Byzantinae (lib. 1.1-92) | De cerimoniis aulae Byzantinae (lib. 1.84-2.56) | De insidiis | De administrando imperio | De strategematibus (olim sub auctore Herone Byzantio)

author_bio: The Apostolic Constitutions is a late‑fourth‑century Christian collection of church orders, blending earlier Didascalia and Didache material into a comprehensive manual on liturgy, morals, and canon law. Though traditionally ascribed to the Apostles, it is now regarded as a compilation likely made by a Syrian compiler, possibly associated with Arian circles.
author_notes: The author entry 'Constitutiones Apostolorum' is a work title occupying the author slot in TLG; the text itself is a compilation, not an individual author. Treated as a work‑title entry.

author_bio: A fragmentary text preserved on papyrus, the Conventus Avium (Assembly of Birds) is a brief work of unknown authorship, possibly a parody or fable.
author_notes: The entry represents a text title used as a placeholder for an unknown author; the fragment is a single papyrus.

author_bio: Corinna was a Boeotian lyric poetess of the 5th century BCE, traditionally associated with Tanagra and linked to Pindar. Her surviving fragments depict a focus on local Boeotian myths and a distinctly female perspective. While her historicity is debated in some sources, the culture of attribution is consistently to an identifiable female poet. The single work comprising fragments is the only extant witness to her oeuvre.
author_notes: Standard TLG identifier; the biographical tradition is ancient, giving high confidence in her historicity despite sparse surviving text.

author_bio: Cornelius is a Greek author known from a single collection of fragments. His identity and period are obscure; the Latin name suggests a possible Roman origin or context, but the works are in Greek.
author_notes: The name 'Cornelius' is common in Roman contexts; the attribution to a Greek author may be a later identification. Fragments are poorly contextualized.

author_bio: The Corpus Hermeticum is a collection of philosophical and theological Greek writings attributed to Hermes Trismegistus, produced by a school or community in Roman Egypt.
author_notes: The label 'Corpus Hermeticum' denotes the collective work of a Hermetic tradition; individual authors are unknown.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Κλείς | Poimandres | Νοῦς πρὸς Ἑρμῆν | Περὶ νοῦ κοινοῦ πρὸς Τάτ | Πρὸς τὸν υἱὸν Τὰτ ἐν ὄρει λόγος ἀπόκρυφος, περὶ | Fragmenta varia (verba Graeca solum) | Περὶ τῆς ὑπὸ τοῦ πάθους τοῦ σώματος ἐμποδιζομένης

author_bio: Cosmas, a hieromonk (monastic priest) active in the late Byzantine period, is known for a single extant work interpreting the art of gold‑making (chrysopoeia). His commentary reflects the intersection of monastic learning with the esoteric alchemical tradition inherited from late antiquity.
author_notes: The entry 'Cosmas' is an obscure Byzantine alchemical author; biographical details are minimal and the work survives only in limited manuscript tradition. Attached to a single, short text.
representative_titles: Ἑρμηνεία τῆς ἐπιστήμης τῆς χρυσοποιίας ἱερομονάχου τοῦ Κοσμᾶ

author_bio: Cosmas Indicopleustes (India-voyager) was a 6th-century merchant, later a monk, who authored the 'Christian Topography', a work that argued against a spherical Earth and described his travels.
author_notes: Identifier 'tlg4061' is for Cosmas Indicopleustes. The name is a personal name, though 'Indicopleustes' is a descriptive epithet meaning 'India-voyager'.

author_bio: Crantor of Soli was a Greek philosopher of the Old Academy, known for his commentary on Plato's Timaeus and for works on ethics. Only fragments of his writings survive.
author_notes: Historical figure; works are fragmentary but well-attributed.

author_bio: A Macedonian historian active in the early Hellenistic period, Craterus compiled collections of decrees and historical documents. His fragments are preserved through later citation.
author_notes: Fragmentary corpus; known primarily through quotations by later historians such as Plutarch and Athenaeus.

author_bio: An early Athenian comic playwright, active in the 5th century BCE, known for pioneering elements of social satire in Old Comedy.
author_notes: Distinguished from the philosopher Crates by the parenthetical 'Comic.'; fragments survive.

author_bio: Crates (Hist.) is a poorly attested historian known only from a few fragments; his identity and period are not firmly established.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Hist.)' is a disambiguator used by TLG; the fragments may originate from a lost historical work, but author's historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Crates (Poet. Phil.) is likely Crates of Thebes, the Cynic philosopher and poet. He wrote satirical epigrams and philosophical fragments in poetic form, combining Cynic moralism with literary parody.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Poet. Phil.)' distinguishes this Crates from the comic poet Crates. The identification with the Cynic philosopher is strong.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Fragmenta et titulus | Epigrammata | Fragmentum

author_bio: A collection of letters attributed to the Cynic philosopher Crates of Thebes. These pseudepigraphical epistles reflect later Cynic tradition and moral instruction.
author_notes: The author slot represents the epistolary corpus, not a person; the heading is the title of the work.

author_bio: Crateuas was a Greek physician and botanist, active at the court of Mithridates VI of Pontus, and wrote on medicinal plants; only fragments survive.
author_notes: Known from references in Dioscorides and Pliny. The fragments in the TLG are of uncertain extent and may include spurious material.

author_bio: Cratinus was a prominent Athenian comic playwright of the 5th century BCE, a major figure of Old Comedy alongside Aristophanes. Known for his satirical style and engagement with political and literary themes, he won multiple victories at the Dionysia. Only fragments of his many plays survive.
author_notes: Well-attested ancient author; fragments collected in Kassel-Austin (PCG).

author_bio: Cratinus Junior is a poorly documented comic poet, possibly a later figure distinct from the famous Old Comic poet Cratinus; only fragments survive.
author_notes: The label 'Junior Comic' suggests a distinction from the older Cratinus, but secure identification is lacking.

author_bio: An Athenian historian known only from citations in later authors, who continued Thucydides' narrative down to 394 BCE.
author_notes: Attested by Plutarch and others; surviving only in fragments.

author_bio: Athenian philosopher, associate of Heraclitus and a teacher of Plato; known through Plato's *Cratylus* and later doxography.
author_notes: Historical person but with limited direct evidence; his doctrines are known mainly through Plato's dialogue.

author_bio: Creophylus was a legendary ancient Greek epic poet, associated with Homer and said to have been from Samos. Only a fragment remains.
author_notes: Historicity is uncertain; the figure is known mainly from ancient traditions linking him to Homer.

author_bio: Crinis is a name found in fragmentary contexts, possibly a Stoic or grammarian. Only a few fragments survive, offering little biographical certainty.
author_notes: Name appears in later sources; ambiguous identification.

author_bio: Critias was an Athenian aristocrat, philosopher, and one of the Thirty Tyrants. He wrote poetic and prose works, including tragedies and political speeches. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments of multiple works.

author_bio: Crito was a poet of Attic comedy, probably of the Middle or New Comedy period. Only a few fragments of his plays survive, leaving his oeuvre and influence enigmatic.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Comic.)' disambiguates from other figures named Crito, such as Plato's interlocutor. The few surviving fragments are known via Athenaeus and other sources.

author_bio: Crito (Hist.) is a Greek historian known only from fragments. The epithet 'Hist.' distinguishes him from other figures named Crito. The surviving fragments suggest a work of historical content, but little else is known about his life or precise period.
author_notes: The designation 'Hist.' indicates classification as a historian, but the identity is uncertain; only one fragmentary work survives.

author_cultural_context: Greek philosophy (perhaps Middle Platonism or later)
author_bio: Crito (Philosophus) is a name attached to a fragmentary text of uncertain authorship, possibly a late Greek philosopher or commentator. The extant fragment is very brief, preserved in a single source.
author_notes: The fragment is too short and too poorly contextualized to confirm the historicity of this Crito, who is distinguished from Plato's interlocutor and from the physician of the same name. The author tag (Phil.) identifies him as a philosopher.

author_bio: Critolaus was a Peripatetic philosopher and historian, known as a follower of Aristotle. He led the Lyceum in the 2nd century BCE. Only fragments of his historical works survive.
author_notes: The identifier 'Hist.' disambiguates from other homonymous figures; fragments are preserved by later authors.

author_bio: Critolaus of Phaselis was a Peripatetic philosopher and head of the Lyceum in the 2nd century BCE. He is known for his defense of Aristotelian philosophy against the Stoics and for his fragmentary ethical and rhetorical works.
author_notes: Display name 'Critolaus (Phil.)' indicates the philosopher; fragments survive only in later quotations. The source ID tlg1294 corresponds to the TLG author number.

author_bio: Crobylus is a name known only from fragments of Greek comedy; possibly a Middle or New Comic poet, but biography is entirely lost.
author_notes: Survives only as an authorial lemma in fragment collections; no historical details.

author_bio: Ctesias of Cnidus was a Greek physician and historian who served at the Persian court of Artaxerxes II. He wrote Persica and Indica, accounts of Persian and Indian history and geography, now lost except for fragments and summaries.
author_notes: Ctesias is known from references in later authors and from fragmentary papyri; his works are important for Near Eastern and Greek historiography.

author_bio: Ctesiphon is known primarily as the Athenian who proposed a crown for Demosthenes, leading to the legal case 'On the Crown.' His surviving fragments are attributed to speeches or historical records.
author_notes: Only fragmentary remains; his association with the Demosthenes affair is the main source of identification.

author_bio: Cydias is a shadowy figure from the Hellenistic period, preserved only through a single fragment. He may have been a poet or prose author, but the evidence is too meager for any detailed biography.
author_notes: The name Cydias appears rarely in ancient sources, and the single surviving fragment does not provide enough context for a secure identification or dating.

author_bio: A lost early Greek epic poem that formed part of the Epic Cycle, narrating events leading up to the Iliad. Traditionally attributed to Stasinus or Hegesias of Salamis, but the name 'Cypria' functions as a title, and the poem's authorship remains uncertain.
author_notes: Cataloged as a work title because the entry refers to the poem itself, not a historical author. Attribution ancient and uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Graeco-Egyptian magical, medical, and astrological tradition
author_bio: A compilation of lapidary, magical, and medical lore, falsely attributed to Hermes Trismegistus and others. The work itself, known under the title 'Cyranides,' occupies the author slot.
author_notes: The author heading is a text title; the compilation is anonymous and pseudepigraphic.

author_bio: Cyril of Scythopolis was a monk and hagiographer who wrote biographies of the founders of the desert monastic community in Palestine. His lives of saints are key sources for the early history of Christian monasticism.
author_notes: Display name 'Cyrillus (Phil.)' indicates the philological tradition; the author is also known as Cyril of Scythopolis. The works listed are hagiographical lives of Palestinian monks.
representative_titles: Vita Sabae | Vita Euthymii | Vita Joannis Hesychastae | Vita Cyriaci | Vita Gerasimi Sp. | Vita Theodosii | Vita Abramii | Vita Theognii

author_bio: Cyril of Jerusalem was a fourth-century Christian bishop and theologian, known for his catechetical lectures (Catecheses) delivered to baptismal candidates. His works are a key source for early Christian liturgy and doctrine in the Eastern Church.
author_notes: The label '(Scr. Eccl.)' distinguishes him from other Cyrils (e.g., Cyril of Alexandria). His historicity and works are well established. Several texts (marked 'Sp.') are of disputed authenticity.
representative_titles: Catecheses ad illuminandos 1-18 | Mystagogiae 1-5 Sp. | Catechesis ad illuminandos 2 (exemplar alterum) | Homilia in paralyticum juxta piscinam jacentem | Procatechesis | Homilia in occursum domini Sp. | Catechesis ad illuminandos 2 (recensio e cod. Paris. 409) | Epistula ad Constantium imperatorem

author_bio: Patriarch of Alexandria and Church Father, a central figure in christological controversies. He authored extensive biblical commentaries, dogmatic works, and polemics against Nestorius. His corpus is one of the largest in Greek patristic literature.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Theol.' indicates Cyril the theologian (of Alexandria), to distinguish him from Cyril of Jerusalem. The author_id aggregates works under the TLG code for Cyril of Alexandria.
representative_titles: Commentarii in Joannem | Commentarius in Isaiam prophetam | Commentarius in xii prophetas minores | De adoratione et cultu in spiritu et veritate | Glaphyra in Pentateuchum | Thesaurus de sancta consubstantiali trinitate | Epistulae paschales sive Homiliae paschales (epist. 1-30) | Expositio in Psalmos

author_bio: Author of a treatise on stasis theory, likely active in the later Greek rhetorical tradition. The name Cyrus is otherwise unattested in this context.
author_notes: Single-work author; identity uncertain. The name may be a pseudonym or a misattribution.

author_bio: A Greek author referred to simply as Quintus, whose surviving fragments amount to over 4,400 words. The Latin name suggests a possible Roman context or bilingual authorship, but his works are limited.
author_notes: The author is identified only as Quintus in the source, a Latin praenomen. The language is Greek, but the naming suggests a Roman or mixed identity; the corpus is fragmentary.

author_bio: Greek epic poet active in the late Roman period, known for his *Posthomerica*, a continuation of the *Iliad* covering the Trojan War's aftermath.
author_notes: Attested historically; his poem survives largely intact, though biographical details are scant.

author_bio: Lachares is a Greek rhetorician known only from a single rhetorical fragment preserved in a Paris manuscript.
author_notes: Very sparse evidence; date and identity are uncertain.
representative_titles: Rhetorica (fragmentum e cod. Paris. suppl. gr. 670)

author_bio: Laetus is a fragmentary author, possibly a poet or historian, of unknown date and provenance. Only a single fragment survives.
author_notes: Very brief fragment (132 words). No further biographical or contextual information available.

author_bio: Lamprocles was an Athenian dithyrambic poet active in the 5th century BCE, associated with the musical innovations of Damon. Only a few fragments of his work survive, cited in later musical and literary sources.
author_notes: Distinguished from other Lamprocles entries by the author_language; his fragments are chiefly preserved in Athenaeus and other miscellanists.

author_bio: Lamynthius is a name known only from a single inscribed titulus, with no further biographical or literary context preserved.
author_notes: The sole surviving text is a titulus of one word; it is unclear whether Lamynthius is an author, dedicant, or subject. Attribution is highly uncertain.

author_bio: Laon is an obscure author known only from fragments; the content suggests possible epic or elegiac poetry.
author_notes: Only two fragments survive; author's identity is uncertain.

author_bio: Laonicus (Laonikos Chalkokondyles) was a 15th‑century Byzantine historian, author of the 'Historiae' covering the fall of Constantinople and the rise of the Ottoman Empire. He is one of the last major Byzantine historiographers.
author_notes: Laonicus is a well‑known Byzantine historian. The single work, Historiae, is substantial.

author_bio: Lasus of Hermione, a lyric poet and musician, credited with innovations in dithyrambic poetry.
author_notes: Well-known figure in literary history, though only fragments survive.

author_bio: Leo of Byzantium (Leo Byzantius) was a historian or rhetorician, known only from fragments. He may have been active in the late classical or early Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The testimonia are scanty; his exact identity and works are unclear.

author_bio: Byzantine commentator on Aristotle, known for excerpts from commentaries on the Sophistici Elenchi and Categories, preserved in a single manuscript (Vat. gr. 244).
author_notes: Not securely dated; the name suggests a possible origin from Magnesia. The works are excerpta, not full commentaries.
