representative_titles: Commentarium in Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos (excerpta praecipue e cod. Vat. gr. 244) | Commentarium in Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos (excerpta e cod. Vat. gr. 244) | Commentarium in Aristotelis categorias (excerptum e cod. Vat. gr. 244)

author_bio: Leo Pellaeus is a name linked to a fragmentary text preserved by the Church Fathers, possibly concerning the life of Alexander or other Hellenistic topics. The biographical traditions surrounding Leo are mixed with legend, and his historicity is uncertain.
author_notes: Very little known; traditions possibly legendary. Use author_historicity_status=legendary due to uncertain historicity.

author_bio: Leo Diaconus, a Byzantine historian, wrote a history covering the reigns of Romanos II through John Tzimiskes.
author_notes: Well-attested historian; the 'Diaconus' and 'Hist.' clarify his role and work.

author_bio: An otherwise unknown historian, represented by a single fragment. The appellation 'Hist.' distinguishes him from other Leos.
author_notes: Attested solely by a fragment; no further biographical information.

author_bio: Leo the Philosopher (or Leo the Mathematician) was a Byzantine scholar and philosopher; his medical compendium and other works reflect the encyclopedic learning of the Macedonian Renaissance.
author_notes: The identification as 'Leo (Phil.)' indicates the philosophical tradition; texts are securely attributed.
representative_titles: Conspectus medicinae | De natura hominum synopsis

author_bio: Leontius (the Mechanic) wrote a commentary on the astronomical sphere of Aratus. He is a minor technical writer of the early Byzantine period, known only from this work.
author_notes: The 'Mech.' label distinguishes this Leontius from other homonymous figures (e.g., Leontius the philosopher).

author_bio: Leontius, often identified as a presbyter of Constantinople, composed a series of homilies on major feasts and on Job; several were traditionally transmitted under the name of John Chrysostom. His style reflects late antique preaching conventions.
author_notes: The corpus is defined by modern scholars; many homilies were misattributed to Chrysostom. The grouping under 'Leontius (Scr. Eccl.)' is a modern convenience.
representative_titles: In pentecosten (homilia 11) | In mesopentecosten (homilia 10) | In transfigurationem (homilia 14) (olim sub nomine Joannis Chrysostomi) | In ramos palmarum (homilia 2) | In ramos palmarum (homilia 3) (olim sub nomine Joannis Chrysostomi) | In pentecosten (homilia 13) (olim sub nomine Joannis Chrysostomi) | In Job (homilia 4) | In sanctam parasceven (homilia 7)

author_bio: An obscure name, possibly a Roman or Greek author, surviving only in a single fragment of uncertain provenance.
author_notes: Only known from one fragment; identity and period are speculative; may be a misattribution.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian and rhetorician, author of a work on figures of speech (De figuris). The epithet 'Gramm.' distinguishes him from the earlier sophist Lesbonax.
author_notes: The grammatical treatise is extant; the author is often conflated with Lesbonax of Mytilene, but the 'Gramm.' label clarifies the distinction.

author_bio: Lesbonax was a Greek rhetorician and sophist, author of model speeches and rhetorical exercises, active in early Imperial times.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Rhet.)' disambiguates from other figures named Lesbonax.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek literature (possible comedy or epigram)
author_bio: Leschides is known from a single inscribed title, providing no further biographical or literary context. The name suggests a Greek author, but no works beyond the one titulus survive.
author_notes: Only a single word (titulus) is recorded; the author's status as a historical person is uncertain due to lack of evidence.

author_bio: Leucippus of Abdera (or Miletus) is regarded as the founder of atomism, the theory that reality consists of indivisible atoms moving in a void. He is known only through testimonia and fragments, as his works are lost.
author_notes: Leucippus is often conflated with Democritus; his historicity is not seriously doubted but his biography is obscure, with few surviving fragments. Confused with Democritus in some sources.

author_bio: Leuco is a minor figure in the Greek lyric tradition, known only from a single fragment and sparse testimonia. Nothing certain is known of his life or date; the name may reflect a semi-legendary poet of the Archaic period.
author_notes: Source (TLG 0470) assigns only three works consisting of a single fragment (35 words). The name 'Leuco' is attested in ancient sources but without reliable biographical detail; the classification as legendary is based on the absence of secure historical context.

author_bio: This entry comprises several anonymous lexica devoted to the vocabulary of Gregory of Nazianzus's writings. Compiled in the Byzantine period, these lexica (alphabetical, verse-indexed, and oration-specific) served as study aids for the Cappadocian Father's works.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the collected lexica; these are anonymous compilations, not the work of a single author. The cultural context is Byzantine patristic scholarship.
representative_titles: Lexicon in carmina Gregorii Nazianzeni (ordine alphabetico) (e cod. Paris. Coislin. 394) | Lexicon in carmina Gregorii Nazianzeni (= Lexicon Casinense) (e | Lexicon in carmina Gregorii Nazianzeni (ordine versuum) | Lexicon in orationes Gregorii Nazianzeni (= Λέξεις ἐκ τοῦ

author_bio: The Lexica Segueriana are a collection of Byzantine Greek lexica and grammatical treatises, preserved in a manuscript from the library of the Seguier family.
author_notes: Not a personal author but a collective title for a manuscript compilation; agent kind is 'work_title'.
representative_titles: Collectio verborum utilium e differentibus rhetoribus et sapientibus multis | Glossae rhetoricae (e cod. Coislin. 345) | De syntacticis (e cod. Coislin. 345) | Anonymus antatticista (e cod. Coislin. 345) | Collectio verborum utilium e differentibus rhetoribus et sapientibus | Δικῶν ὀνόματα (e cod. Coislin. 345)

author_bio: Lexica Synonymica is not an author but a collective title for a group of Byzantine lexicographical works that compile and distinguish synonyms in Greek. The three surviving excerpts—the Excerptum Casanatense, De differentia vocabulorum, and Differentiae verborum—are anonymous or pseudepigraphic, traditionally grouped under this heading.
author_notes: The TLG entry uses 'Lexica Synonymica' as an author surrogate. The three works are distinct but thematically related; no personal author is known.
representative_titles: Excerptum Casanatense sive Ecloga DIAFÓRWN LÉCEWN (e cod. bibl. Casanatense 264) | De differentia vocabulorum (= Περὶ διαφορᾶς λέξεων) (e cod. | Differentiae verborum (e cod. Paris. suppl. gr. 1238 servante litteram A tantum)

author_bio: Scholarly designation for a collection of Byzantine lexicographical works on syntax, surviving in multiple manuscripts. Not a personal author.
author_notes: This is a manuscript-based grouping of syntactic lexica, not an individual author.

author_bio: An anonymous Byzantine lexicon beginning with the word αἱμωδεῖν, containing lexical explanations and grammatical notes.
author_notes: The author slot is filled by the title of the lexicon; the real compiler is unknown.
representative_titles: Lexicon αἱμωδεῖν (= Lexicon anepigraphum quod incipit a voce

author_bio: A Byzantine lexicon of grammatical art, preserved in manuscript Coislin. 345, compiled from earlier grammatical sources.
author_notes: Author slot occupied by the title of the work itself, representing an anonymous compilation.

author_bio: A Byzantine lexicographic work on Attic names, traditionally attributed to various hands. The lexicon preserves otherwise lost Attic terminology.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by a title, not a person. The attribution is uncertain; it may be a compilation.
representative_titles: De Atticis nominibus (= Περὶ Ἀττικῶν ὀνομάτων) (sub nomine

author_bio: An anonymous Byzantine lexicon preserved on Patmos, containing glosses on Demosthenes and other classical authors. It is a compilation of earlier lexicographical sources.
author_notes: The label 'Lexicon Patmense' is a modern title for a manuscript compilation; no authorial figure is attached.
representative_titles: Lexicon Patmense (= Λέξεις μεθ’ ἱστοριῶν ἐκ τῶν Δημοσθένους

author_bio: An anonymous Byzantine lexicon of rhetorical terms preserved in a Cambridge manuscript, compiled from earlier sources.
author_notes: The entry is a titled work occupying the author slot; the actual compiler is unknown.
representative_titles: Lexicon rhetoricum Cantabrigiense (e cod. Cantabr. Univ. D d 4,63 in marginibus)

author_bio: An anonymous Greek lexicon transmitted in codex Sabbaiticus 137. Compiled in a Palestinian monastic environment, drawing on earlier scholia and glossaries.
author_notes: The title of the work occupies the author slot; no named compiler is known.

author_bio: The Lexicon Vindobonense is a medieval Greek lexicon preserved in a Vienna manuscript (cod. phil. gr. Vindob. 169). It is attributed to Andreas Lopadiotes, but the work itself functions as the author entry in modern catalogs due to its textual nature as a compilation. It contains glosses and lexical entries for classical and biblical Greek.
author_notes: The entry is treated as a work title because the text itself (the lexicon) is the cataloged entity, not a person. The attribution to Andreas Lopadiotes is traditional but secondary for indexing purposes.
representative_titles: Lexicon Vindobonense (auctore Andrea Lopadiota) (e cod. phil. gr. Vindob. 169)

author_cultural_context: Greek rhetoric and education, Late Roman Empire
author_bio: Libanius was a prominent Greek rhetorician and teacher of rhetoric in Antioch during the 4th century CE. A prolific author, he produced orations, letters, declamations, and progymnasmata, and his works are a major source for the social and political history of the later Roman Empire.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with extensive surviving corpus (784,597 words). No ambiguity in identification.
representative_titles: Orationes 1-64 | Epistulae 1-1544 | Declamationes 1-51 | Progymnasmata | Argumenta orationum Demosthenicarum | Epistularum Basilii et Libanii quod fertur commercium | Characteres epistolici Sp. | Fragmenta de declamationibus

author_bio: The Liber Eldad et Modad is a short fragmentary apocryphal text attributed to the biblical figures Eldad and Medad. It survives only in a Greek fragment and is not attributed to a named author.
author_notes: The title occupies the author slot; the text is a pseudepigraphic fragment, author unknown.

author_bio: The 'Liber Enoch' (Book of Enoch) is a Jewish apocalyptic text traditionally attributed to the patriarch Enoch. The Greek version, surviving in fragments and a later translation (including a recension preserved by the chronographer Syncellus), played a significant role in early Christian and Jewish mystical traditions. It describes Enoch's heavenly journeys and visions of the end times.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the work, as the text is pseudepigraphical. The Greek translation is a secondary but important witness to the original Aramaic or Hebrew text.
representative_titles: Apocalypsis Enochi | Apocalypsis Enochi (recensio ap. Syncellum)

author_cultural_context: Jewish or Christian apocryphal text; Jannes and Jambres were the names of the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses.
author_bio: The 'Liber Jannes et Jambres' is a fragmentary apocryphal book that recounts the legend of the two magicians Jannes and Jambres, who feature in Jewish and Christian traditions as opponents of Moses. The small surviving fragment suggests a once-popular narrative.
author_notes: The source label uses the title as the author; the actual author is unknown, and the work is only attested by this tiny fragment.

author_bio: The *Liber Jubilaeorum* is a Jewish pseudepigraphal work, also known as the *Book of Jubilees*, which retells biblical history from creation to the Exodus, organized into jubilee cycles.
author_notes: Attributed as a work-title author since no single author is traditionally assigned; fragmentary Greek version preserved.

author_bio: A Greek lyric or dramatic poet known from two very brief fragments, one of which may be an inscription. Almost nothing is known about his life or works.
author_notes: The fragments attributed to Licymnius are minimal and possibly of uncertain authenticity. The source likely includes a titulus (inscription).

author_bio: Limenius was an Athenian poet-musician, known for composing the Second Delphic Hymn (paean) and prosodion to Apollo, inscribed at Delphi.
author_notes: Known from the Delphic inscription; historicity is likely but details are sparse.
representative_titles: Paean Delphicus ii et prosodium in Apollinem

author_bio: Lobo is a shadowy figure known from fragments of epigrams about early poets and a possible title. The name is rare, and no secure biographical details survive.
author_notes: The name Lobo appears in fragmentary sources; the reference to epigrams on poets before the Alexandrian period suggests an antiquarian interest.
representative_titles: Fragmenta et titulus | Epigrammata in poetas ante Alexandrinorum aetatem condita

author_region: Roman Empire (probably Greece or Asia Minor)
author_bio: Lollianus is the author of the 'Phoenician Tales' (Φοινικικά), a fragmentary Greek novel known from papyrus finds at Oxyrhynchus and Cologne. He is one of the earliest prose fiction writers of the imperial period.
author_notes: The author's name is preserved on papyrus but little else is known. The attribution is secure for the fragments listed. Word count and work count moderate for a fragmentary author.

author_bio: The name Longinus is traditionally attached to the treatise On the Sublime (De sublimitate), a foundational work of Greek literary criticism. The true author is unknown; the attribution to Cassius Longinus (3rd c. CE) is often questioned.
author_notes: The work is universally important but the author's identity is a long-standing debate; sometimes labeled Pseudo-Longinus.

author_bio: Longus is the author of the ancient Greek novel Daphnis et Chloe, set on the island of Lesbos. Little is known of his life, but the work is a classic of pastoral romance.
author_notes: Biographical details are uncertain; name may be a pseudonym. The work is securely attributed.

author_cultural_context: Byzantine and post-Byzantine scholarship, copying and lexicography
author_bio: Lucas Apostolus (also known as Lucas Notaras?) was a Byzantine scholar and scribe active in the late 15th century, known for his work on a recipe for salt preparation (Σκευασία ἁλατίου) among other short texts. His output reflects the practical and learned interests of the early post-Byzantine period.
author_notes: Attested as a historical figure, but the single surviving work is very brief and minor; the identification with Lucas Notaras is conjectural.

author_cultural_context: Greek rhetorical culture and satire in the Roman Empire
author_bio: Lucian of Samosata was a prolific Greek satirist and rhetorician of the 2nd century CE, a key figure of the Second Sophistic. His works—dialogues, essays, and satires—offer witty critiques of philosophy, religion, and literature, with influential masterpieces such as True History and Dialogues of the Gods.
author_notes: Source (CTS urn) includes 71 works (286,586 words). Lucian's authorship is well-attested; the corpus includes some spuria, but the majority is genuine. The historical status is secure.
representative_titles: Hermotimus | Dialogi mortuorum | Verae historiae | Toxaris vel amicitia | Dialogi meretricii | Dialogi deorum | Quomodo historia conscribenda sit | De mercede conductis potentium familiaribus

author_bio: Lucius is a little-known Greek author of uncertain date and origin, preserved only in fragments. The work attributed to him may be a rhetorical or philosophical text, but the limited evidence makes firm conclusions difficult.
author_notes: The name 'Lucius' is very common, and without a distinguishing epithet or more context, the identity of this author remains uncertain. The single fragmentary text does not allow for secure attribution.

author_bio: This Latin name in a Greek context likely refers to Lucius Annaeus Cornutus, a Stoic philosopher and grammarian of the 1st century CE, tutor and friend of the poet Persius. The work 'De natura deorum' is a Greek treatise on the nature of the gods, possibly an epitome or a distinct work.
author_notes: The author entry 'Lucius Annaeus' is ambiguous: it could be Cornutus, but the single work 'De natura deorum' is also transmitted under other names. Attribution is tentative.

author_bio: Lucius Licinius is a Roman author who wrote in Greek, known only from a single fragment. His identity and work are obscure.
author_notes: Very little is known; the fragment may be from a historical or rhetorical work.

author_bio: Lyceas is known only from a single fragmentary inscription (titulus). The identity and historicity of this figure are highly uncertain; the name may belong to an obscure author or an otherwise unattested individual.
author_notes: The entry is based on a single word-count 2 titulus. Insufficient evidence to determine historicity; status marked as uncertain.

author_bio: Lyceas of Naucratis is known only from a single fragment; the ethnic indicates his origin, but no further biographical details survive.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary source; historicity and dating uncertain.

author_bio: Lycon was a Peripatetic philosopher who succeeded Straton as head of the Lyceum in the 3rd century BCE. Known more for his oratorical skill and lifestyle than for philosophical innovations, his work survives only in testimonia.
author_notes: Reconstructed from later biographical sources such as Diogenes Laertius.

author_bio: Lycon of Alexandria Troas is known only from fragments. The epithet 'Alexandrinus Troadis' indicates his origin from Alexandria Troas in the Troad. His identity is otherwise obscure.
author_notes: TLG records a single fragmentary work; no other testimony exists.

author_bio: Lycophron the Sophist was a Greek rhetorician and sophist, known for his work on style and argumentation, surviving only in testimonia and fragments.
author_notes: Distinguished from the poet Lycophron of Chalcis; very limited evidence for his life and work.

author_bio: Lycophron was a Hellenistic Greek poet and grammarian, active in Alexandria under Ptolemy II. He is best known for the dark, obscure poem 'Alexandra,' attributed to him, a dramatic monologue of prophetic riddles. His other tragedies are lost.
author_notes: Historical figure. Though the 'Alexandra' is attributed to him, some scholars doubt the ascription; attribute held as standard.

author_bio: Lycophronides is an obscure Greek lyric poet known only from a single fragment; nothing certain is known about his life or date.
author_notes: 48 words survive; identity and historicity speculative.

author_bio: Lycurgus was a prominent Athenian orator and statesman of the late 4th century BCE, known for his financial reforms and prosecution of political opponents. His sole surviving complete speech, Against Leocrates, demonstrates his vigorous, patriotic style.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; canon of Attic orators.

author_bio: An obscure author known only from two fragments; possibly a medical writer, but identification remains speculative.
author_notes: Multiple persons named Lycus exist; this author's identity is not securely determined.

author_bio: Lynceus was a comic playwright of the Middle Comedy period, known for a few fragments. He is sometimes identified with the gastronomical writer Lynceus of Samos, but the comic is likely a different figure.
author_notes: Attribution is uncertain due to confusion with other authors of the same name; fragments are minimal.

author_cultural_context: Corpus of anonymous Greek lyric fragments, probably from Hellenistic or Roman-era collections.
author_bio: This entry groups anonymous Greek lyric fragments whose authorial attribution is lost; the heading 'Lyrica Adespota' is a modern editorial label for such material.
author_notes: The '((Ca))' in the label is a cataloging marker indicating the source collection; the author_display_name is an anonymous editorial heading.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous lyric fragments (adespota) transmitted under the label 'Pmg' (Poetae Melici Graeci). These fragments belong to no known author and are attributed to various lost lyric traditions.
author_notes: The '((Pmg))' notation refers to the edition (Poetae Melici Graeci). The material is anonymous and heterogeneous.

author_bio: This is a collection of anonymous lyric fragments (adespota), including songs and poems of uncertain authorship from archaic and classical Greece. They are attributed to no single author, representing a collective of unattributed lyrical works.
author_notes: Adespota (without an author); collection of fragments of varying provenance and date; used for convenience in catalog.

author_bio: Lysanias is a poorly known Greek author of uncertain date, surviving only in a single fragment (140 words). The subject suggests grammatical or historical writing.
author_notes: Virtually nothing is known; the fragment may come from a larger work of grammar or lexicography.

author_bio: One of the ten Attic orators, known for lucid, forceful speeches for Athenian litigants. A metic, he wrote over 200 speeches, of which 34 survive.
author_notes: Well-documented life; some speeches are spurious but included in the corpus.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | In Agoratum | In Eratosthenem | Epitaphius Sp. | Ὑπὲρ τῶν Ἀριστοφάνους χρημάτων, πρὸς τὸ δημόσιον | In Andocidem Sp. | De caede Eratosthenis | In Alcibiadem 1

author_bio: Lysimachus is a fragmentary author, possibly a mythographer or historian from Alexandria. Only fragments survive, covering various historical and mythological topics. His identity and period are uncertain.
author_notes: There are multiple figures named Lysimachus; this entry likely refers to an obscure scholar or poet known only from citations. The assignation to Alexandria is tentative.

author_bio: Lysippus is a name associated with comic fragments, possibly of the Middle or New Comedy. The attribution to a specific author remains uncertain due to sparse evidence.
author_notes: Fragmentary and of contested attribution.

author_bio: Lysis was a Greek philosopher of the Pythagorean school, born in Tarentum. He is known for a letter to Hipparchus and for transmitting Pythagorean teachings. He is a minor but authentic figure in the Pythagorean tradition.
author_notes: The corpus includes a letter and testimonia; the attribution is traditional and generally accepted, though the letter may be a later composition.

author_bio: Macarius is the name associated with a collection of Greek proverbs (Paroemiae). The author's identity and date are uncertain, but the compilation likely dates to the Byzantine period and draws on earlier sources such as the Suda.
author_notes: The name 'Macarius' is a conventional attribution for this proverb collection; the actual compiler may be unknown.

author_bio: Macedonius (the name is fragmentary: 'Mace(donius)') is known for composing a paean to Apollo and Asclepius, preserved in inscription (IG 3.1.171b).
author_notes: The name is restored as Macedonius; only a fragment of the name survives. Attribution depends on the inscription's reading.

author_bio: Greek comic poet of the New Comedy, known for witty anecdotes and plays, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Fragments preserve anecdotes; historical existence is secure.

author_bio: Maeandrius is a fragmentary Greek author, possibly a historian or mythographer. Little is known about his life or the content of his lost work.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; identity and dating are uncertain.

author_bio: The Magica grouping comprises a corpus of Greek magical papyri, ostraca, and tablets from Roman Egypt, containing spells, hymns, and ritual instructions. This is a modern editorial classification, not an ancient author.
author_notes: Used as a conventional author label for collections of Greek magical texts; not an individual or collective author.
representative_titles: Papyri magicae | Papyri magicae (fragmenta Christiana) | Papyri magicae (ostraca) | Papyri magicae (tabulae)

author_bio: Magnes was an early Athenian comic poet, said to have won victories in the City Dionysia; only a few fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Knowledge of Magnes relies on later testimonia and fragments; his historicity is accepted but details are legendary.

author_bio: A Greek author known from a single fragment (721 words). The name is common; the subject matter suggests a rhetorical or medical context.
author_notes: The identity of this Magnus is unclear; he could be the physician Magnus of Ephesus or a rhetorician. The corpus provides no further distinguishing information.

author_bio: Maiistas is the name associated with an aretalogy (a text praising a deity) inscribed on stone (IG 11.4.1299), possibly from Delos. The name appears to be the author or composer of the dedicatory or cultic text. Little is otherwise known.
author_notes: Attested only by a single inscription; the identity and background of Maiistas are otherwise unknown.

author_bio: Mamercus was a Greek poet of the 4th century BCE, known from a single epigram. His identity is obscure; he may have been a Siceliot writer active in the Hellenistic period.
author_notes: Attested only by TLG; the name Mamercus is rare and may be a variant of Mamercus or Mamertinus. The epigram is short, and no other works survive.

author_bio: The astrological poem *Apotelesmatica* is transmitted under the name Manetho, deliberately referencing the famous Egyptian priest and historian. The work is a Hellenistic-style didactic poem on astrology, composed over several centuries by different hands.
author_notes: The attribution is pseudonymous; no historical astrologer by this name is known. The text is composite, with material from different periods.

author_bio: Manetho was an Egyptian priest and historian of the early Ptolemaic period, whose Aegyptiaca (now fragmentary) provided the basis for the traditional dynastic chronology of Egypt.
author_notes: Distinguished from an astrological writer of the same name by '(Hist.)'. Fragmentary but highly important source for Egyptian history.

author_bio: The Mantissa Proverbiorum is a Byzantine collection of proverbs, serving as a supplement to earlier proverb collections. It is a compilation text, often attributed to a later compiler but treated as a title in catalog indexing. The work preserves many ancient Greek sayings.
author_notes: This entry is a work title: the text itself is the cataloged entity. 'Mantissa' means 'addition' or 'supplement', indicating its derivative nature.

author_bio: A Byzantine author known for poetic works, possibly Manuel Philes or another Manuel. Two surviving works: Carmina and Carmina inedita.
author_notes: Likely identifiable as a known Byzantine author; further disambiguation may be needed.

author_bio: Marcellinus is a Byzantine biographer known for his Life of Thucydides, a key source on the historian's life and the transmission of his work.
author_notes: Identity uncertain; possibly the grammarian Marcellinus (5th-6th cent. CE) or a later compiler. Single-work author with limited biographical evidence.

author_bio: Marcellinus is a late antique medical author known for a treatise on pulses (De pulsibus). He may be a Byzantine physician active in the sixth century.
author_notes: The numeral 'I' distinguishes this Marcellinus from other authors of the same name; his life dates are not securely attested.

author_bio: Marcellus is a little-known historian of the Roman Imperial period, the author of a now-fragmentary historical work. His identity and the precise subject of his history remain obscure.
author_notes: The label 'Hist.' distinguishes this author from others named Marcellus. Only fragments survive, and the historical context is uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Greek medical poet or didactic writer; part of the tradition of versification of medical knowledge (e.g., Nicander, Andromachus)
author_bio: Marcellus (possibly of Side) is known only for a didactic poem on fish and their medicinal properties, preserved in fragmentary form. The work combines natural history with medical pharmacology, typical of the Hellenistic-and-Roman-period 'iatrosophist' tradition.
author_notes: The 'Poet. Med.' label designates a medical poet. The single fragment (626 words) is cited by later medical writers (e.g., Oribasius). The name 'Marcellus' is common; identification with known authors is uncertain.

author_bio: Marcellus was a Christian theologian active in the 4th century, known for his opposition to Arianism and his works on the Incarnation. His writings survive in fragments and a few longer texts, exhibiting a distinctive anti-Arian Christology.
author_notes: The 'Theol.' tag differentiates this Marcellus from other homonyms. Some works are debated in authenticity.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | De incarnatione et contra Arianos | Expositio fidei

author_bio: A Greek letter attributed to the emperor Marcus Aurelius, allegedly sent to the Roman Senate defending the Christians. The work is generally considered a late forgery from the early Christian period.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the title of the work itself; the letter is pseudepigraphical and not actually by Marcus Aurelius.
representative_titles: Epistula ad senatum, qua testatur Christianos victoriae causam fuisse

author_bio: Marcianus was a Greek geographer and epitomator, who compiled summaries of the works of Menippus and Artemidorus, and wrote a periplus of the outer sea.
author_notes: Marcianus is a known figure in ancient geography; his epitomes preserve earlier works.
representative_titles: Periplus maris exteri | Menippi periplus maris interni (epitome Marciani) | Artemidori geographia (epitome Marciani)

author_bio: Marcus Diaconus was a Christian deacon and hagiographer from Gaza, best known for his biography of Porphyry of Gaza, a vivid account of the Christianization of the city in the early 5th century.
author_notes: Identified as the deacon Marcus; full name sometimes given as 'Marcus Diaconus'.

author_bio: Marcus Antonius (Mark Antony) was a Roman politician and general. Surviving Greek fragments attributed to him include declamations and a physiognomic fragment, likely rhetorical exercises.
author_notes: Attribution of rhetorical fragments is uncertain; may be later declamations under his name.

author_bio: Marcus Aurelius was Roman emperor from 161 to 180 CE and a Stoic philosopher. His sole surviving work, the 'Meditations' (Τὰ εἰς ἑαυτόν), is a series of personal reflections on duty, virtue, and the nature of the universe, written in Greek during his military campaigns.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure. Source ID urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0562 is canonical in the TLG corpus. Single work has high word count and cultural significance.

author_bio: Roman senator and grammarian, leading orator of the Second Sophistic, tutor to Marcus Aurelius, and author of letters and rhetorical works in Greek.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Marcus Cornelius is the Greek-writing Roman orator Fronto, known for his correspondence with Marcus Aurelius.
representative_titles: Additamentum epistularum variarum acephalum | Ad Marcum Caesarem et invicem (lib. 1) | Ad Marcum Caesarem et invicem (lib. 2) | Ad amicos (lib. 1) | Epigrammata

author_bio: Neoplatonist philosopher and mathematician, student of Proclus. He wrote a commentary on Euclid's Data and a Life of Proclus.
author_notes: Possibly the same as Marinus of Neapolis; known from biographical and scholarly sources. Attribution to this work is secure.

author_display_name: Martyrium Agapae, Irenae, Chionae Et Sodalium
author_canonical_name: Martyrium Agapae, Irenae, Chionae et sodalium
author_bio: An anonymous hagiographic account of the martyrdom of Agapa, Irena, and Chiona, three Christian sisters, and their companions, under the Emperor Diocletian. The work is a conventional martyr act.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work; the true author is unknown.
representative_titles: Martyrium Agapae, Irenae, Chionae et sodalium

author_bio: An anonymous Greek hagiographical text recounting the martyrdom of Saint Conon, a Christian martyr venerated in Anatolia. The work belongs to the genre of martyr acts and is transmitted in medieval manuscript collections.
author_notes: Cataloged as a work title; no individual author is known or claimed for this text.

author_bio: The Martyrium Dasii is an early Christian martyrdom account describing the trial and execution of Dasius, a Roman soldier. The text is a hagiographical work from late antiquity, likely composed in Greek in the eastern Roman provinces. It provides insight into Christian persecution narratives.
author_notes: This entry is a work title because the text itself is the cataloged entity, not a personal author. The author is anonymous.

author_bio: A composite Christian apocalyptic text, preserved in Greek fragments, describing the martyrdom and heavenly ascent of the prophet Isaiah. The work is a title, not a person.
author_notes: This entry represents the text itself as the author. Author_agent_kind set to work_title as per instructions.

author_bio: The Martyrium Ignatii is a hagiographic text recounting the martyrdom of Ignatius of Antioch. Two recensions (Roman and Antiochene) survive.
author_notes: The source label uses the title of the work as the author heading. The text is anonymous.
representative_titles: Martyrium Ignatii Antiocheni (martyrium Romanum) | Martyrium Ignatii Antiocheni (martyrium Antiochenum)

author_bio: A martyrdom account of Marinus, transmitted as an anonymous text; no author attribution is known.
author_notes: The work title occupies the author slot; the text is anonymous.

author_bio: The Martyrium Pionii is an early Christian account of the martyrdom of Pionius of Smyrna. The text itself, not an author, is entered as the heading, typical for anonymous hagiographic works.
author_notes: The title of the work serves as the author entry in this case; no specific writer is known.

author_bio: The Martyrdom of Polycarp is a foundational text of Christian hagiography, recounting the arrest, trial, and execution of Bishop Polycarp of Smyrna. It is one of the earliest and most historically reliable martyrdom accounts, written by the church of Smyrna.
author_notes: This entry represents the text itself, not a specific author; it is attributed to the church of Smyrna as a collective. The URN indicates it is a work-level identifier.
representative_titles: Epistula ecclesiae Smyrnensis de martyrio sancti Polycarpi

author_bio: The title 'Martyrium Potamiaenae et Basilidis' stands for a hagiographic text recounting the martyrdom of Potamiaena and Basilides. The actual author is unknown.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the text title. Author is anonymous.

author_bio: The 'Martyrium Ptolemaei et Lucii' is an early Christian martyr text recounting the trials of Ptolemaeus and Lucius, probably redacted in Greek in the Roman period.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work, not a personal author; the text is anonymous and transmitted as part of martyrdom collections.

author_bio: Matron is known as the author of a comic parody of epic, the Convivium Atticum (Attic Dinner), which survives in fragmentary form. The work humorously imitates Homeric style to describe a lavish banquet, reflecting the literary culture of late 4th-century Athens.
author_notes: The identity of Matron is sometimes debated, but the Convivium Atticum is securely attributed to him in ancient sources.

author_cultural_context: Apocryphal Christian text attributed to the apostle Matthew, containing traditions about the lives of saints and the Virgin Mary.
author_bio: The 'Matthiae Traditiones' is a late antique apocryphal work preserved in Greek fragments, attributed to the apostle Matthew. It contains legends and sayings about Mary and the early church, reflecting Syrian or Byzantine Christian piety.
author_notes: The source label is a title masquerading as an author; the actual author is unknown. The work is transmitted under Matthew's name, but the attribution is pseudonymous.

author_bio: Prominent Byzantine theologian, monk, and ascetic writer. One of the most influential patristic authors, known for his defence of Dyothelite Christology. Left a large corpus including exegetical, ascetic, and dogmatic works.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure. The catalog entry includes multiple major works; representative titles listed reflect the range. Author agent kind set to person, historicity historical.
representative_titles: Quaestiones ad Thalassium | Quaestiones et dubia | Capita de caritate | Scholia in Ecclesiasten (in catenis: catena trium patrum) | Mystagogia | Liber asceticus | Expositio orationis dominicae | Expositio in Psalmum LIX

author_bio: An author named Maximus known for astrological works, including 'On Beginnings' and an epitome of the same. The precise identity and dating are unclear, but the works reflect late antique or Byzantine astrological practice.
author_notes: The epithet 'Astrol.' distinguishes this Maximus from others. The surviving works (two) are substantial enough to suggest a real practitioner, but no external biographical confirmation is available.

author_bio: Maximus (Rhet.) is the supposed author of a rhetorical treatise on insoluble contradictions, possibly Maximus of Byzantium. His identity and date remain uncertain, with activity likely in the 2nd-4th century CE.
author_notes: Attribution is tentative; parenthetical 'Rhet.' indicates rhetorical genre. Uncertainty about which Maximus is intended.
representative_titles: Περὶ τῶν ἀλύτων ἀντιθέσεων (fort. auctore Maximo Byzantio),

author_bio: Maximus of Tyre (often referred to as Maximus Sophista) was a Greek philosopher and rhetorician of the Second Sophistic. His *Dialexeis* (orations) blend Platonic philosophy with rhetorical exposition, addressing ethical and theological topics.
author_notes: Work fully extant; author is well-known within Second Sophistic literature.

author_bio: An author identified only as Maximus in theological contexts, known from a single fragment on matter. The epithet "Theol." suggests a theological or patristic writer, but his identity remains uncertain due to lack of biographical evidence.
author_notes: The source label 'Maximus (Theol.)' indicates a theological author named Maximus, but the precise identity is unclear. The fragment 'De materia' is the only surviving work, making confident classification difficult.

author_region: India (diplomatic mission to the court of Chandragupta Maurya)
author_cultural_context: Greek ethnographer; served as Seleucid ambassador to the Mauryan empire.
author_bio: Megasthenes was a Greek diplomat and historian whose 'Indica' described in detail the geography, society, and religion of India under the Mauryan empire. Though lost, his work survives in citations by later authors.
author_notes: The fragments are well-attributed despite the loss of the original work. The source label is unambiguous.

author_bio: Megillus is known only from a single, very brief fragment. The name is Greek, but no biographical information can be reliably recovered.
author_notes: The author is attested by one fragment of 69 words; nothing else is known.

author_bio: Legendary seer of Greek mythology, to whom several works on divination from bodily twitches and moles were attributed. These texts likely date from the Hellenistic or Roman period.
author_notes: Attributed to the mythic Melampus; the surviving treatises are pseudepigraphic and likely compiled in late antiquity.
representative_titles: Περὶ παλμῶν μαντικὴ πρὸς Πτολεμαῖον βασιλέα (versio A), | Περὶ παλμῶν τί σημαίνουσιν ἐν ἑκάστῳ μέρει (versio P), | Περὶ παλμῶν ἀρχομένου ἀπὸ κεφαλῆς ἕως ποδῶν (e cod. | Περὶ ἐλαιῶν τοῦ σώματος πρὸς Πτολεμαῖον βασιλέα

author_bio: Melanippides was a Greek lyric poet active in the 5th century BCE, noted for his innovations in dithyrambic poetry. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Author identified as a dithyrambic poet; fragmentary preservation limits knowledge.

author_bio: Melanthius is a shadowy figure credited with fragments of elegy and tragedy. The assignment to both genres may indicate a homonym or confusion in ancient sources; only a few lines survive.
author_notes: The double genre label 'Eleg. et Trag.' raises the possibility that two different authors named Melanthius have been conflated.

author_bio: Melanthius the historian is a fragmentary Greek author known from a few surviving quotations. He likely wrote on Athenian history or antiquities, possibly in the 4th or 3rd century BCE.
author_notes: The designation '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Melanthius from other homonymous figures. The attribution is based on sparse fragments.

author_bio: Melanthius was a Greek tragic poet of the 5th century BCE, known only from a single surviving fragment and references in later sources. He was active in Athens and is sometimes associated with the circle of Euripides.
author_notes: Virtually no works survive; the fragment may be dubiously attributed. The label 'Trag.' indicates a tragic poet, but the evidence is minimal.

author_bio: Meletius was a late antique or Byzantine author of works on human nature, combining medical (Galenic) and philosophical (Aristotelian) traditions. His treatise De natura hominis is a notable compilation.
author_notes: Identity is uncertain; possibly a physician or monk. The longer work may have been compiled from earlier sources.
representative_titles: De natura hominis | Hypothesis ad opus De natura hominis (e cod. Barocciano 131)

author_bio: Athenian citizen known as one of the prosecutors of Socrates; a single epigrammatic titulus survives.
author_notes: Surviving text minimal; identification with the historical prosecutor likely but not certain.

author_bio: Melinno is the author of a hymn to Rome in the style of Sappho, preserved in a single manuscript. Her name may be a pseudonym, and her dating is uncertain. The poem is important as a female voice in Imperial Greek literature.
author_notes: The poem 'Eis Rhomen' is the only extant work; the author's historicity is uncertain, but she is treated as a person.

author_bio: Melissa is a mythical figure associated with a fragmentary letter to Cleareta; the name means 'bee' and may refer to a priestess or nymph.
author_notes: The attribution to a person named Melissa is dubious; the fragment may be a fictional letter or later forgery.

author_bio: Melisseus is a mythological figure, a Cretan king or god associated with honey and bees; his name appears in a single fragmentary text.
author_notes: Attested only in a single fragment; status as a real author is legendary or mythic. Source ID tlg2282.

author_bio: Melissus of Samos, a 5th-century BCE Greek philosopher of the Eleatic school, disciple of Parmenides. His surviving fragments and testimonia argue for the unity and immutability of being.
author_notes: Melissus is a well-attested historical figure. The textual corpus consists of fragments and testimonia preserving his philosophical arguments.

author_bio: Bishop of Sardis and a prominent Christian apologist; his Homily on the Passover is a key early Christian text, preserved in papyri.
author_notes: The 'Apol.' label stands for 'Apologist.' A well-attested historical figure with multiple extant works.
representative_titles: De pascha (P. Beatty 8 + P. Bodmer 13 + P. Oxy. 13.1600) | Fragmenta | Fragmentum (P. Bodmer 12)

author_bio: Melito is a shadowy figure known only from a single title; whether a historical tragedian of the classical period is uncertain.
author_notes: Attested only by a bare title; no fragments securely assigned. The label 'Trag.' may indicate a conventional generic attribution.

author_bio: Memnon of Heraclea was a Greek historian who wrote a local history of Heraclea Pontica. His work is known chiefly through the epitome preserved by Photius.
author_notes: Identification depends on the epitome by Photius; original work lost.

author_bio: Menander was the foremost playwright of Athenian New Comedy, known for his witty dialogue and intricate plots. His work survives in papyrus fragments and has been increasingly reconstructed.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; corpus includes many fragments and several near-complete plays.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Dyscolus | Samia | Sententiae e codicibus Byzantinis | Sententiae | Epitrepontes | Aspis | Periciromene

author_bio: Menander the Historian was a Hellenistic Greek historian known only from fragments; his works are lost apart from a limited number of surviving excerpts.
author_notes: The source distinguishes 'Menander (Hist.)' from the famous comic playwright; the fragments are sparse and the historical context is largely reconstructed.

author_bio: Menander Protector was a Byzantine historian and official, author of a history covering the late 6th century CE, preserved only in fragments in later compilations.
author_notes: Important source for late Roman/early Byzantine diplomacy; the dubium fragment is of uncertain authenticity.
representative_titles: De legationibus Romanorum ad gentes (fragmenta ap. Constantinum Porphyrogenitum, De legationibus) | De legationibus gentium ad Romanos (fragmenta ap. Constantinum Porphyrogenitum, De legationibus) | De sententiis (ap. Constantinum Porphyrogenitum, De sententiis) | Fragmentum (e cod. Paris. gr. 1140A) [Dub.]

author_bio: Menander Rhetor (also known as Menander of Laodicea) was a Greek rhetorician and author of treatises on epideictic oratory. His works, notably *Peri epideiktikon* and *Diairesis ton epideiktikon*, were influential in late antique rhetorical education.
author_notes: The attributions of the two major treatises in the TLG corpus are secure, though the *Diairesis* was sometimes attributed to Genethlius in older scholarship.
representative_titles: Περὶ ἐπιδεικτικῶν | Διαίρεσις τῶν ἐπιδεικτικῶν (olim sub auctore Genethlio)

author_cultural_context: Byzantine gnomologia, Menandrian and Philistionic traditions
author_bio: A Byzantine collection of sententiae attributed to Menander and Philistion, compiling moral and proverbial sayings in two traditions.
author_notes: The entry is the title of a textual compilation, not an author; the attribution to Menander and Philistion is traditional and derivative.
representative_titles: Comparatio Menandri et Philistionis | Sententiae Menandri et Philistionis

author_bio: Menecles was a poet or scholar known only from fragments. The TLG record includes a single work, 'Fragmenta', totaling over 700 words, but no specific titles. He may have been a writer of elegies or epigrams, but his exact identity and output remain uncertain.
author_notes: The name Menecles is common; this entry may conflate several individuals. The lack of a clear title or genre makes classification difficult.

author_bio: Menecrates is a little-known historian, possibly from the Hellenistic period, known only from a few fragments. His identity and exact period are uncertain.
author_notes: The heading 'Historicus' attempts to distinguish him from other Menecrates; attestation is too sparse for a confident historicity assessment.

author_bio: A Greek scholar from Nysa in Caria, known from a single fragment; possibly a historian or grammarian.
author_notes: The fragmentary evidence does not allow secure dating or identification of his field of learning.

author_bio: Menecrates of Olynthus was a minor Greek author known only from a single surviving fragment. His work, if any, has been almost entirely lost, and little can be said about his life or literary output.
author_notes: The author is known only from a single fragment; all biographical details are inferred from the ethnic label 'Olynthius'. The source is ambiguous and the corpus is minimal.

author_bio: A Greek historian or antiquarian from Xanthus in Lycia, known for a work on local mythology and history. The exact scope and genre of his writing remain unclear due to the fragmentary preservation.
author_notes: The epithet 'Xanthius' links Menecrates to Xanthus. The fragments are very limited, making detailed reconstruction difficult.

author_bio: Menecrates of unknown comic tradition, represented by a few fragments and a title; likely a minor or otherwise unknown comic poet.
author_notes: Cited as (Comic.) to distinguish from other homonyms; only 19 words survive.

author_bio: Hellenistic historian whose fragmentary works, quoted by later authors, dealt with local history and mythology. His precise identity remains uncertain due to the commonness of the name.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Menecrates from homonyms. Attestation is limited to fragments; historical status is probable but not certain.

author_bio: Menecrates was a physician, likely of the 1st century BCE/CE, known from a single brief letter (Epistula Sp.) dealing with medical advice. He is a minor figure in ancient medicine.
author_notes: Label 'Med.' identifies him as a medical writer; only one small work survives.

author_bio: Phillis is a figure from Greek mythology, often connected with the story of Demophon and the naming of the Phyllis plant. The fragments attributed to this name likely belong to a Hellenistic or later literary tradition.
author_notes: The name conflates mythic and literary traditions; actual authorship is unknown.

author_bio: A Greek author known only from a single fragment and a title. The name is common; no further biographical details are available.
author_notes: Only 14 words survive; the identity of this Menelaus is unclear and could refer to any of several homonymous authors.

author_bio: Menesthenes is an obscure Greek author, known from a single fragment that may come from a historical or paradoxographical work. No further details about his life or oeuvre are preserved.
author_notes: The name is rare and potentially confused with other individuals; the fragmentary evidence limits reliable attribution.

author_bio: Menestor of Sybaris was a pre-Socratic Greek natural philosopher and botanist, among the earliest to write a specialized work on plants (likely 'On Agriculture'). Only fragments and testimonia survive, quoted by Theophrastus. He seems to have studied plant physiology and the effects of climate on vegetation.
author_notes: Menestor is known primarily from Theophrastus. The attribution to Sybaris is plausible but not absolutely certain.

author_bio: Menetor is known solely from a single fragment; his identity and period are unknown, but he is likely a minor grammarian or writer.
author_notes: Only one fragment is attributed; may be a ghost name or an alternative spelling.

author_cultural_context: Cynic philosopher and satirist; his prose-and-verse style was fleetingly influential, though his comic works survive only in titles and fragments
author_bio: Cynic satirist from Gadara who created a distinctive blend of prose and verse (Menippean satire). His works, now lost except for a few titles, influenced later Roman satirists such as Varro and Lucian.
author_notes: The 'Comic.' label refers to the comic/ satirical genre. The two surviving titles are known from ancient citations; exact wording may be uncertain. Menippus himself is historically attested, but his literary corpus is extremely fragmentary.

author_bio: Menippus of Pergamon (or Menippus Geographus) was a Hellenistic geographer known from a few fragments describing coastal features. He is sometimes identified with a Periegetes who wrote on the coasts of the Mediterranean.
author_notes: The identifier '(Geogr.)' distinguishes this person from a homonymous philosopher. The fragments are few and the attribution is based on later citations. Identity uncertain.

author_bio: Menippus of Gadara was a Cynic philosopher and satirist of the 3rd century BCE, credited with influencing the genre of Menippean satire; only a single spurious epistle survives by this name.
author_notes: The source label '(Phil.)' indicates the Menippus known as a philosopher, but the single epistle is considered spurious; the identification is philologically standard.

author_bio: Menodotus was a Greek physician of the Empiricist school, active in the Hellenistic period. He wrote medical and philosophical works, now lost; only fragments and testimonia survive, preserved by later authors such as Galen.
author_notes: The attribution is based on fragments in later medical writers. The name Menodotus is also borne by other figures (e.g., a historian), but the medical context is best supported.

author_bio: Author of a single fragment preserved in later tradition; little is known about this figure, and the name may be a textual corruption or a pseudonym.
author_notes: Only a single fragment is extant; the name Menophilus is attested as a person but without sufficient biographical context.

author_bio: Menyllus is a little-known author from whom only one fragment survives, probably a Hellenistic prose writer or historian. Almost nothing is known of his life or work.
author_notes: The name is poorly attested; the fragment may represent the only evidence for this author. Likely a minor historian or grammarian.

author_bio: Meropis is a fragmentary epic or mythological work, transmitted on papyrus. The name is taken from the text, not a known author.
author_notes: Catalog label derived from the title of the work; no author indicated.

author_bio: Greek lyric poet and composer, active under the Roman emperor Hadrian; fragments and epigrams survive.
author_notes: Known from a small corpus; TLG source ID confirms limited surviving works.

author_bio: Metagenes is a Greek author known from fragments; possibly an Old or Middle Comedy poet active in Athens. His works survive only in citation.
author_notes: Possibly identical with the comic poet Metagenes; fragments are sparse and context-dependent.

author_bio: Metopus is a fragmentarily known author, possibly a historian or paradoxographer from the Hellenistic period. Only a small number of fragments survive, leaving the nature of his work unclear.
author_notes: The source label provides no disambiguating epithet. Attestation is minimal; genre and date are uncertain.

author_bio: Metrodorus (Hist.) is a minor Hellenistic historian known only from fragments preserved by later authors. His historical work, likely covering events of the late Hellenistic period, survives in a single fragmentary text.
author_notes: The label 'Hist.' indicates a historical writer, but no biographical details survive; the fragmentary state of the work limits confidence.

author_bio: Metrodorus of Lampsacus was a prominent Epicurean philosopher, a close disciple of Epicurus, and regarded as one of the major figures of the school alongside Hermarchus. He authored numerous works on philosophy, but only fragments and testimonia survive. His thinking centered on atomistic materialism and the pursuit of pleasure or avoidance of pain (ataraxia).
author_notes: The qualifier '(Major Phil.)' is used to disambiguate from other figures named Metrodorus. The historical identification is secure; known through Diogenes Laërtius and other doxographical sources.

author_bio: Metrodorus of Lampsacus was an Epicurean philosopher, a close friend and disciple of Epicurus. He wrote extensively on Epicurean doctrine, especially against the Academy. His works survive only in testimonia and fragments, but he was a central figure in the early Epicurean school.
author_notes: The epithet 'Phil.' distinguishes this Metrodorus from the historian and other namesakes. His fragments are collected in Usener's 'Epicurea' and in later editions.

author_bio: An author known only from a single fragment; possibly a monastic or ecclesiastical figure.
author_notes: TLG entry: only 50 words survive; identity and period uncertain.

author_bio: Michael Attaliates was an 11th-century Byzantine historian and judge, author of a 'History' covering events from 1034 to 1079. His work is a key source for the period.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure. The single work, 'Historia', is substantial.

author_bio: Michael Apostolius (also called Michael Apostolius of Constantinople) was a 15th-century Byzantine Greek scholar and paroemiographer. He compiled a collection of proverbs, the 'Collectio paroemiarum', drawing on ancient sources.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure of the early Renaissance. Single extant work.

author_bio: Michael Critobulus was a Byzantine historian and official who wrote a history of the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople. His work is a key source for the end of the Byzantine Empire, written from within the Ottoman court in Greek.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Critobulus)' is a conventional disambiguation from other Michaels. The author is securely historical.
representative_titles: Historiae | Epistula ad Mechemet II | Precatio | Poema

author_bio: Michael Glycas was a 12th-century Byzantine historian, theologian, and poet, known for his chronicle 'Annales' and various theological works.
author_notes: Glycas is a well-attested Byzantine author; the parentheses in the display name indicate disambiguation from other Michaels.

author_bio: Michael of Ephesus was a Byzantine commentator on Aristotle, part of the 11th–12th century Aristotelian revival. He produced numerous commentaries on Aristotelian works, many of which survive.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Phil.)' indicates philosopher; his commentaries were sometimes misattributed to Alexander of Aphrodisias.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos commentarius (= PseudoAlexander 1) | In ethica Nicomachea ix-x commentaria | In parva naturalia commentaria | In libros de partibus animalium commentaria | In librum quintum ethicorum Nicomacheorum commentarium | In librum de animalium incessu commentarium | In libros de animalium motione commentarium | In Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos commentarius (selecta) (=

author_bio: Michael Psellus was a leading Byzantine intellectual of the 11th century, known for his Chronographia, philosophical works, and wide-ranging learning.
author_notes: Parentheses disambiguate from other Michaels; Psellus is a well-documented historical figure.
representative_titles: Theologica | Opuscula logica, physica, allegorica, alia | Poemata | Chronographia | Opuscula psychologica, theologica, daemonologica | Oratoria minora | Orationes forenses et acta | Orationes panegyricae

author_bio: A figure known only from a single surviving fragment; possibly a grammarian or poet, but the evidence is too scant for a secure profile.
author_notes: Only one fragment; the name is common, making identification problematic.

author_bio: Mimnermus was an early Greek elegiac poet from Ionia, celebrated for his poems on love, youth, and the fleeting nature of life. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Key figure in the development of Greek elegy; historical existence is secure.

author_bio: A minor Greek tragic poet known only from a single fragment, possibly a different figure from the elegiac poet Mimnermus of Colophon. The parenthetical 'Trag.' distinguishes him as a tragedian.
author_notes: Very sparse evidence; the fragment is of uncertain attribution. The name may refer to a otherwise unknown playwright, or could be a confusion with the famous elegiac poet.

author_bio: Greek rhetorician, author of a work on rhetorical arguments (Περὶ ἐπιχειρημάτων). Likely active in the Roman Imperial period.
author_notes: The name is rare; the author is known only through this treatise, which survives in fragmentary form.

author_bio: Minyas is a mythic figure associated with the early Greek epic poem of the same name, known only from fragments and later references. The title character likely belonged to the Boeotian Minyan lineage, but the author of the poem is traditionally regarded as a legendary person.
author_notes: Author name likely derived from the poem's title; no historical person is attested; tradition is uncertain.

author_bio: A single letter attributed to Mithridates VI of Pontus, preserved in historical sources. The work occupies the author slot in the catalog because the author is the title itself, representing the letter of a king.
author_notes: Author field used for the title 'Mithridatis Epistula'; the agent is the text, not a person.

author_bio: Mnaseas of Patara (or possibly elsewhere) was a Hellenistic mythographer and geographer, known for a collection of myths and a work on oracles. Only fragments survive.
author_notes: Mnaseas is cited by later authors; his exact identity and dates are uncertain due to fragmentary evidence.

author_bio: Mnesimachus was a poet of Middle Comedy, known only from fragmentary remains of two plays. His work is quoted for culinary and social details characteristic of the genre.
author_notes: Only fragmentary evidence; date and region approximate; comic genre attribution from TLG.

author_bio: Hellenistic historian known only from a single fragment. The label (Hist.) distinguishes him from a comic poet of the same name.
author_notes: Fragmentary; existence inferred from citation. The '(Hist.)' disambiguation suggests uncertainty about identity.

author_bio: Moderatus was a Greek author, possibly a philosopher or scholar, of whom only fragments survive.
author_notes: Surviving only in fragments; identity and dates are uncertain.

author_bio: Moeris is the compiler of a lexicon of Attic Greek words, often cited as Moeris Atticista. The work lists Attic versus Hellenistic usage and is an important resource for the study of Atticism.
author_notes: The identity and date are uncertain; some scholars place him in the 2nd century, others later. The lexicon is the sole surviving work.

author_bio: A female Greek poet from Byzantium, mentioned by Eusebius and included in the canon of nine lyric poets by some critics; a few epigrams and a fragment of a poem survive.
author_notes: Also known as 'Moero' or 'Myro'; the sparse surviving fragments are in the Greek Anthology and elsewhere.

author_bio: Molpis was a Spartan writer of antiquarian and historical works, known only from a few fragments. He likely compiled information on Spartan institutions and rituals.
author_notes: Very little is known about Molpis; the name may refer to more than one person. The fragments are minimal.

author_bio: Monimus was a Cynic philosopher from Syracuse, a pupil of Diogenes of Sinope. Known for his radical asceticism and witty sayings, he survives only in scattered fragments preserved by later doxographers. His philosophy emphasized self-sufficiency and rejection of conventional values.
author_notes: The TLG lists Monimus with a single fragment (28 words); his biography is reconstructed from later sources such as Diogenes Laërtius, and the attribution of the fragment is uncertain.

author_bio: Monodia is a fragmentary Greek poem found on papyrus (P. Ryl. 1.15), likely a mime or solo song featuring a woman lamenting a gladiator or lover, attributed to a work rather than a named author.
author_notes: The name 'Monodia' likely refers to the genre or title of the work, not a person. Classified as work_title.

author_bio: Montanus was the founder of the Montanist movement, a prophetic and ascetic Christian sect. His oracles, preserved only in quotations by opponents, exhibit a charismatic and apocalyptic tone.
author_notes: No original works survive; all fragments come from patristic polemics. The attribution of specific oracles to Montanus himself rather than to his followers (e.g., Priscilla, Maximilla) is debated.

author_bio: Morsimus was a minor Athenian tragic poet of the 5th cent. BCE, satirized by Aristophanes; only a single word (9 letters) survives from his work.
author_notes: Attested in ancient sources (e.g., Aristophanes, scholia), but his surviving corpus is negligible. The historicity is plausible.

author_cultural_context: Hellenistic Greek bucolic and didactic poetry
author_bio: Moschion was a Hellenistic Greek poet and playwright, known from fragmentary remains on mythological and philosophical themes. His work survives only in quotations by later writers, indicating a modest but recognized place in Alexandrian literary circles.
author_notes: The work count is 1 (fragments only). The attribution is secure from ancient citations but the corpus is too small to confirm prominence. No independent manuscript tradition survives.

author_bio: Moschus of Syracuse was a Hellenistic bucolic poet, known for his erotic and mythological hexameter poetry. His most famous work is 'Europa', an epyllion that influenced later Latin poetry.
author_notes: The corpus includes some spurious works (e.g., 'Megara' and 'Epitaphius Bionis'), but 'Europa' is securely attributed. Moschus is considered a major figure in Hellenistic poetry.
representative_titles: Europa | Epitaphius Bionis Sp. | Megara Sp. | Epigrammata | Fragmenta | Eros drapeta

author_bio: Moses, the seminal prophet and lawgiver of the Hebrew Bible, appears in Greek-language pseudepigraphic and magical texts. The two extant Byzantine works attributed to him are a moralizing poem (Εὐποία καὶ… ) and a 'diplosis' (exegetical or ritual text) reflecting later Jewish-Christian syncretism.
author_notes: These texts are pseudepigraphically attributed to Moses; he is classified as a mythic figure. The TLG headword refers to the biblical figure, not a historical Greek author.
representative_titles: Εὐποία καὶ εὐτυχία τοῦ κτισαμένου καὶ ἐπιτυχία καμάτου καὶ | Μωσέως δίπλωσις (e cod. Venet. Marc. 299, fol. 185r)

author_cultural_context: Legendary pre-Homeric singer, associated with Orpheus and the Eleusinian Mysteries; a mythical culture-hero of poetry
author_bio: In Greek mythology, Musaeus is a legendary poet and seer, son (or pupil) of Orpheus, credited with oracular and didactic poems, especially on eschatology. His name was attached to lost works in antiquity.
author_notes: The 'Epic.' label indicates classification as an epic poet, though no genuine epic poem survives; only 'Tituli' (the titles of works attributed to him) remain. All surviving ascriptions are late and legendary; the historicity status is set to mythic.

author_region: Eastern Roman Empire (probably Constantinople or Alexandria)
author_bio: Musaeus (Grammaticus) is the author of the epyllion 'Hero and Leander', a late antique Greek poem in 343 hexameters. He is often associated with the school of Nonnus of Panopolis.
author_notes: Historical figure; single celebrated work.

author_cultural_context: Hellenistic / Imperial Greek philosophy and scholarship
author_bio: A Greek scholar and writer on philosophical and doxographical subjects, known only through fragments and testimonia. His works, including a treatise on the Nile and a doxography of the Seven Sages, are quoted by later authors such as Diogenes Laertius and Stobaeus.
author_notes: The disambiguator 'Phil.' distinguishes this Musaeus from the mythical poet Musaeus. Identity and dates are uncertain; fragments collected by Müller and others.

author_bio: Myia is a legendary female philosopher, said to have been a student of Pythagoras and possibly his daughter; her surviving letter is a later pseudepigraph.
author_notes: Myia appears in later Pythagorean traditions; the letter attributed to her is likely a Hellenistic or Imperial forgery.

author_bio: Myron of Eleutherae was a renowned Greek sculptor of the mid-5th century BCE, famous for his bronze works such as the Discobolus, now known only through Roman copies and literary testimonia.
author_notes: The TLG fragment entry reflects the survival of only literary testimonia and no surviving continuous text; the author is well-attested historically but no verbatim works survive.

author_bio: Myronianus is known only from a single fragment or citation; no secure biographical or historical details survive.
author_notes: Attested only in fragmentary sources; author's historicity cannot be confirmed.

author_bio: Greek historian from Lesbos, known for a work on the history of Lesbos (Lesbiaca), surviving in fragments.
author_notes: Little is known beyond fragmentary citations; may be confused with other homonyms.

author_bio: Myrtilus is a mythic figure of Greek myth, the charioteer of Oenomaus, whose name is attached to a few fragments from lost Greek poetry. The attribution is traditional and not historically grounded.
author_notes: Multiple fragments attributed but all derive from mythographic tradition; no historical person is known.

author_bio: Naumachius is a shadowy figure, known from a single hexameter fragment on naval warfare or a didactic poem. Almost nothing is known of his life.
author_notes: Only one fragment survives; attribution is tenuous.

author_bio: Nausicrates is a scarcely attested Hellenistic Greek author known only from fragmentary remains. The meager evidence does not support a confident reconstruction of his life or works.
author_notes: The author is known only from brief fragments; the label 'Nausicrates' may conflate multiple individuals or be a textual ghost.

author_bio: Nausiphanes of Teos was a Greek philosopher of the Democritean school, known as a teacher of Epicurus. He wrote on physics and rhetoric, but only fragments and testimonia survive. His relationship to Epicurus was contentious, as Epicurus reportedly rejected his influence.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg2334. The corpus contains fragments and testimonia. His dates are approximate, known from biographical sources.

author_bio: Nautarum Cantiunculae ('Sailors' Songs') is a modern editorial title for two short anonymous poems preserved on papyrus, possibly sung by sailors.
author_notes: The source label is a modern descriptive title for anonyma. Not the work of a known author.
representative_titles: Ad Rhodios ventos (P. Oxy. 11.1383) | Fragmentum (P. Oxy. 3.425)

author_bio: Neanthes of Cyzicus was a Hellenistic historian and paradoxographer. He wrote works on local history and wonders, and his fragments contribute to the study of Hellenistic antiquarian literature.
author_notes: Fragments survive; attribution is reasonably secure.

author_bio: Nemesius, bishop of Emesa (modern Homs), was a Christian philosopher who wrote De natura hominis, a work on human nature drawing from Greek philosophy and Christian doctrine.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; a bishop and author of an influential treatise.

author_bio: Neophron was a Greek tragic poet, said to have been a predecessor of Euripides. Only small fragments survive, largely from his play 'Medea', which may have influenced Euripides' version.
author_notes: Neophron's historicity is accepted, but the extent of his influence on Euripides' 'Medea' is debated. Fragments are preserved through later citations.

author_bio: A Hellenistic scholar and critic, possibly from Paros, known for work on poetry and poetics. He is cited by later authors like Philodemus, but only fragments survive.
author_notes: The name is common; the identity is uncertain, and the fragments may not all belong to the same person.

author_bio: Nepualius wrote a work 'On Sympathy and Antipathy' (Περὶ τῶν κατὰ ἀντιπάθειαν καὶ συμπάθειαν), a typical subject for ancient natural philosophy. His dates and life are unknown; only this fragment survives.
author_notes: Single work of 757 words. The title indicates a treatise on natural sympathies and antipathies. Nepualius is otherwise unattested.
representative_titles: Περὶ τῶν κατὰ ἀντιπάθειαν καὶ συμπάθειαν

author_bio: Nessas is a minor ancient Greek author known only from fragments and testimonia; the available corpus is extremely limited, and no firm biographical details survive.
author_notes: The source label provides only a name with scant associated texts; the existence and works of this author are attested solely through fragmentary evidence.

author_bio: The New Testament is a collection of 27 texts, including Gospels, Acts, Epistles, and Revelation, composed in Koine Greek. It is the foundational scripture of Christianity and a central corpus of ancient world literature.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the work itself, not a specific person. This is a corpus filing label.
representative_titles: Evangelium secundum Lucam | Acta apostolorum | Evangelium secundum Matthaeum | Evangelium secundum Joannem | Evangelium secundum Marcum | Apocalypsis Joannis | Epistula Pauli ad Romanos | Epistula Pauli ad Corinthios i

author_bio: Nicaenetus is a Hellenistic Greek poet known for fragmentary epigrams and poetic works. His surviving verses exhibit the learned and refined style typical of the Alexandrian literary tradition.
author_notes: Attribution is traditional; only fragments survive, making biography uncertain. The name is rare and not widely attested externally.

author_bio: Nicander was a Hellenistic Greek poet, active at the court of Attalus III of Pergamon, best known for his didactic hexameter poems on toxicology (Theriaca, Alexipharmaca).
author_notes: The (Epic.) disambiguator marks him as an epic (hexameter) poet. His exact dates are uncertain, but he is generally placed in the 2nd cent. BCE.

author_bio: A grammarian named Nicander, known solely from fragments. The disambiguation '(Gramm.)' distinguishes him from the poet Nicander of Colophon. No secure dates or biography survive.
author_notes: The label '(Gramm.)' indicates this is a different Nicander (a grammarian) from the well-known poet. Attribution is highly uncertain.

author_bio: Nicander (Hist.) is a fragmentary Greek historian, distinguished by the parenthetical '(Hist.)' from the more famous poet Nicander of Colophon. Only a few fragments of his historical work survive, indicating lost writings on antiquarian or local history.
author_notes: The identifier '(Hist.)' is a modern editorial disambiguation. The fragments are cited in later authors, but little is known about his life or exact dates.

author_bio: Nicanor is a grammarian known from fragments on Homeric and other topics. His identity is sometimes confused with other homonyms.
author_notes: Fragments only; possibly multiple grammarians named Nicanor.

author_bio: Nikephoros Blemmydes was a Byzantine scholar and monk. He wrote on theology, philosophy, and alchemy. The two works attributed here concern chrysopoeia (gold-making) and liturgical construction.
author_notes: The attribution of the alchemical works is debated; the source record includes 'fort. auctore Nicephoro' indicating uncertainty.
representative_titles: Νικηφόρου τοῦ Βλεμμύδου περὶ τῆς χρυσοποιίας (fort. | Ἅπερ χρῄζει ἡ παροῦσα κατασκευή (fort. auctore Nicephoro

author_bio: Nicephorus Bryennius (c. 1062–1137 CE) was a Byzantine historian, statesman, and general. He wrote the 'Historiae', a history of the House of Komnenos covering the period 1070–1079, continuing the work of his father-in-law Alexios I.
author_notes: Well-documented Byzantine historian; author_historicity_status=historical.

author_bio: Nicephorus Gregoras (ca. 1295–1360 CE) was a Byzantine historian, theologian, philosopher, and polymath. He composed a monumental 'Roman History' (Historia Romana) covering events from 1204 to 1359 CE, as well as numerous rhetorical, philosophical, and theological works. He was a key figure in the Byzantine intellectual circles and a participant in the Hesychast controversy.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a substantial surviving corpus. The TLG entry includes his major historical work.

author_bio: Nicephorus I was Patriarch of Constantinople (806–815) and a prominent theologian and historian. His works include a refutation of the Iconoclast synod of 815 and a historical breviarium covering the late 6th to early 7th centuries.
author_notes: The corpus includes a doubtful work (Chronographia brevis) in two recensions; the breviarium is securely attributed.
representative_titles: Refutatio et eversio definitionis synodalis anni 815 | Breviarium historicum de rebus gestis post imperium Mauricii (e cod. Vat. gr. 977) | Chronographia brevis Dub. (recensiones duae)

author_bio: Nicephorus II Phocas was Byzantine emperor from 963 to 969, a successful general. The treatise 'De velitatione bellica' is attributed to him, though likely compiled from his military instructions. It is a key text on Byzantine skirmishing tactics.
author_notes: The attribution of 'De velitatione bellica' to Nicephorus is traditional and likely reflects his patronage and military experience rather than personal authorship.
representative_titles: De velitatione bellica (sub nomine Nicephori II Phocae)

author_bio: Known only from a single inscription or titulus; possibly an Athenian citizen. No literary fragments survive beyond this epigraphic record.
author_notes: The source label is based on a single word (Titulus). The name is common; identification is uncertain.

author_bio: Nicetas Choniates was a Byzantine Greek historian and civil servant, best known for his 'History' (Chronicle Narrative), which covers the period from 1118 to 1207, including the fall of Constantinople to the Fourth Crusade. His work is a key source for the Byzantine world in the Komnenian and Angeloi periods.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure; his major work survives intact.

author_bio: The so-called 'Letter of Nicias' is a brief epistolary text attributed to Nicias, but the author slot here represents the text itself rather than a historical person.
author_notes: The heading 'Niciae Epistula' is a title occupying the author position; the actual author is unknown or the letter is a literary exercise.

author_bio: Nicias (Epigr.) was a Greek epigrammatist, possibly from Miletus, active in the early Hellenistic period. A few of his epigrams survive in the Greek Anthology, showing typical erotic and dedicatory themes.
author_notes: The tag '(Epigr.)' distinguishes him from the physician Nicias. Attribution to a single author is uncertain due to scanty remains.

author_bio: Nicias (Hist.) is a historian known only from a single fragment. The title of his work is lost, and his identity is unclear; he may be a minor Hellenistic or Imperial-era author.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; the '(Hist.)' disambiguator suggests a historical writer, but no further biographical details survive.

author_bio: Nico is a little-known author of comic or satyric fragments; possibly a poet of Middle or New Comedy. Only 37 words survive.
author_notes: Name may be a short form; genre and dates are entirely conjectural.

author_bio: Nicochares was a poet of Old Comedy in Athens, known only from fragments preserved in later lexicographers and scholiasts. His plays contributed to the robust tradition of political and social satire.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; identification relies on citations in later sources.

author_bio: Nicocles is known only from fragments; possibly a rhetorician or historian, but his identity remains uncertain due to sparse testimony.
