author_notes: The TLG identifier tlg1534 assigns a single fragmentary work; the name may refer to more than one figure, and the corpus is too small for firm classification.

author_bio: An ancient Greek author known only through fragmentary remains; details of his life and works are lost.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; nothing certain about his identity or date. Possibly a historian or poet.

author_bio: Nicolaus I Mysticus was Patriarch of Constantinople and a key figure in Byzantine ecclesiastical and political life. His letters and tracts address theological and diplomatic issues of the early 10th century.
author_notes: The name 'Nicolaus I' refers to the patriarch; the source label 'I' helps distinguish him from later homonyms. His corpus is extensive and well-preserved.

author_bio: Nicolaus Comicus was a poet of New Comedy, known from a few surviving fragments and titles. He is cited by Athenaeus and possibly other sources, but remains a minor figure.
author_notes: The source label 'Comic.' identifies him as a comic poet. Fragments are sparse, and his dating is approximate based on stylistic and citation patterns.

author_bio: Nicolaus (Hist.) is a historian known only through fragmentary remains. The name may refer to a Greek historian of uncertain period, possibly Nicolaus of Damascus, but this attribution is not secure.
author_notes: The source label 'Nicolaus (Hist.)' suggests a historical writer; fragmentary evidence makes precise identification difficult.

author_bio: A late antique rhetorician and sophist, known for his Progymnasmata, a rhetorical textbook used in Byzantine education.
author_notes: Well-attested author of rhetorical works; the label 'Rhet. et Soph.' clarifies his profession.
representative_titles: Progymnasmata | Progymnasmata (capita περὶ συγκρίσεως, περὶ ἠθοποιίας, περὶ | Progymnasmata (caput περὶ συγκρίσεως) (e cod. Brit. Mus. addit.

author_bio: A Greek mathematician and philosopher from Alexandria, known for his work on arithmetic and music theory. He authored the *Introduction to Arithmetic* and other Neopythagorean writings. Only a few fragments survive of his lost works.
author_notes: Attested as historical; fragments are collected under the TLG code. The identification with the mathematician Nicomachus of Gerasa is uncertain; 'Alexandrinus' may imply a different Nicomachus.

author_bio: Nicomachus was a Greek comic playwright active in the late Classical to early Hellenistic period, known from only a handful of fragments. He composed in the tradition of Middle Comedy, with titles and themes traceable through later lexicographers.
author_notes: Identification based on fragmentary evidence; the (Comic.) disambiguation distinguishes him from other Nicomachi. Attestations rely on indirect transmission.

author_bio: A mathematician and philosopher from Gerasa, known for his works on arithmetic and music theory, including the influential *Introductio arithmetica*.
author_notes: Clearly identified historical figure; works transmitted under his name.
representative_titles: Introductio arithmetica | Theologoumena arithmeticae | Harmonicum enchiridion | Excerpta

author_bio: An obscure tragic poet known only from a few titles preserved in ancient sources. The biographical tradition is likely legend-based.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Trag.)' distinguishes this Nicomachus from other figures. Only one title fragment (3 words) survives; historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Nicophon was an Athenian comic poet of the late Old Comedy. He competed with Aristophanes and others, but only a few fragments of his plays survive.
author_notes: Biographical data is scanty; fragments are preserved through indirect tradition.

author_bio: Nicostratus was an Athenian comic poet of Middle Comedy, known from several fragments and at least one title. His works were transmitted through Athenaeus and other sources.
author_notes: The multiple fragments and title suggest a modestly productive poet. Disambiguation from Nicostratus the rhetorician is sometimes necessary.

author_bio: Legendary founder of Nineveh and husband of Semiramis; the eponymous hero of a lost Greek novel (Ninus Romance) from the Roman period, fragments of which survive on papyrus.
author_notes: The author heading corresponds to the protagonist of a novel, not a real author. Author agent kind set to person because the catalog treats Ninus as the authorial attribution (the work is titled after him).
representative_titles: Ninus (fragmenta AB) (P. Berol. 6926) | Ninus (fragmentum C) (PSI 13.1305) | Ninus (fragmentum D) (P. Gen. 85)

author_bio: A Byzantine diplomat and historian who traveled to Ethiopia and South Arabia on diplomatic missions under Justinian I. His historical work, known only in fragments preserved by Photius, provides valuable geographic and ethnographic details.
author_notes: The identification of Nonnosus as author of these fragments is based on Photius' Bibliotheca; the fragmentary nature and limited attestation warrant modest confidence.

author_cultural_context: Late antique Greek epic and Christian poetry
author_bio: Nonnus of Panopolis was a major Greek poet of the 5th century CE, author of the vast epic 'Dionysiaca' and a verse paraphrase of the Gospel of John. His work bridges pagan and Christian traditions.
author_notes: The source includes three works; the attribution of the 'Paraphrasis' to a second Nonnus is debated but the corpus is treated as unitary here.
representative_titles: Dionysiaca | Paraphrasis sancti evangelii Joannei (fort. auctore Nonno alio) | Epigramma

author_bio: The Nostoi is a lost Greek epic poem from the Trojan cycle, narrating the returns of the Greek heroes after the fall of Troy. It survives only in fragments and is attributed to various poets by ancient sources, but the title itself functions as the author entry.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of a lost epic work. The label 'Nostoi' is used in catalogs to represent the anonymous epic tradition. No individual author is named.

author_bio: Middle Platonist philosopher from Apamea, known for his synthesis of Platonic, Pythagorean and Oriental ideas; influential on later Neoplatonism.
author_notes: Extant only in fragments; well-known among specialists for his philosophical influence.

author_bio: Numenius, author of a didactic poem on farming or fishing, known only from fragments and titles. Active in the 2nd century CE, he is a minor figure in the tradition of Greek technical verse.
author_notes: The label 'Poet. Didac.' indicates the author is known only as a didactic poet; very fragmentary.

author_bio: Greek historian from Heraclea Pontica, author of a local history and other works. His fragments, preserved by later authors, provide valuable information on the history of the Black Sea region and the Diadochi.
author_notes: Known from fragments and indirect tradition; the name is well-attested in ancient sources.

author_bio: Nymphodorus is a fragmentarily preserved author of uncertain genre, possibly historical or philosophical. Only one work, surviving in fragments, is attributed to him.
author_notes: Identity and oeuvre unclear; author may be conflated with others of the same name.

author_bio: Xanthus of Lydia was a Lydian historian who wrote a history of Lydia in Greek. He is often considered a predecessor to Herodotus. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Multiple figures named Xanthus exist; this entry likely refers to the historian Xanthus of Lydia. The two listed works may be fragments from the same or different authors.

author_bio: Xenagoras was a Hellenistic Greek historian and paradoxographer, known from fragments that treat mythological and geographical marvels. He may have been active in the 3rd or 2nd century BCE and is cited by later authors for curious lore.
author_notes: Fragments preserved in scholia and later compilations. TLG identifier tlg1752.

author_bio: Xenarchus was a playwright of Middle or New Comedy, known only from fragments. He may have been active in Athens during the late 4th to early 3rd centuries BCE.
author_notes: Fragments only; little biographical data survives. Identification is tentative.

author_bio: Figure known only from a single testimonium (likely in Diogenes Laertius or similar). Possibly a Corinthian sophist or philosopher mentioned in passing. No works survive.
author_notes: Single testimonium; the historicity is uncertain but the name appears in a philosophical context.

author_bio: An obscure author known only from quoted fragments of uncertain origin. The name Xenion appears in grammatical or lexicographical sources; the identity and period remain indeterminate.
author_notes: Fragments are brief and lack context; the authorship attribution may be conventional.

author_bio: Xeno is a fragmentary Greek author of uncertain affiliation, possibly a philosopher. Only a single tiny fragment (22 words) survives, offering no secure identification.
author_notes: Extreme scarcity of evidence; the name may refer to more than one figure.

author_bio: Athenian tragic poet, recorded as a competitor in the Dionysia. Only a single fragment remains.
author_notes: Attested as a historical figure in didascalic records, but the sole extant fragment preserves little of his work.

author_bio: Xenocrates of Chalcedon was a Greek philosopher and the third head (scholarch) of Plato's Academy, succeeding Speusippus. He wrote extensively on cosmology, metaphysics, and ethics, but only testimonia and fragments survive. His thought influenced Middle Platonism.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg0634. The corpus includes only testimonia, doctrine, and fragments. His life dates are well-established.

author_bio: Xenomedes of Ceos was a Greek mythographer and historian known for local Cean traditions. His work survives only in fragments quoted by later authors.
author_notes: Attribution secure but fragmentary survival limits biographical detail.

author_bio: Pre-Socratic philosopher, poet and critic of traditional mythology from Colophon. He founded the Eleatic school and wrote elegiac poems and skeptical philosophical works.
author_notes: Well-attested figure with substantial fragments; one of the earliest Greek philosophers.
representative_titles: Testimonia | Fragmenta | Fragmenta (Silli et De natura) | Epigramma | Testimonium

author_bio: Xenophilus was a philosopher of the Pythagorean school, active in the early Hellenistic period. He is known from testimonia in later writers, such as Diogenes Laërtius, and may have authored works on harmony or music.
author_notes: Survives only through secondary testimonia; no direct fragments of his works are preserved.

author_bio: Xenophon was an Athenian historian, philosopher, and soldier, a student of Socrates. His works cover history, biography, and Socratic dialogues, and are key sources for 4th-century BCE Greek and Persian history.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure in the Greek literary canon.
representative_titles: Cyropaedia | Hellenica | Anabasis | Memorabilia | Oeconomicus | Symposium | Cynegeticus | Agesilaus

author_bio: Xenophon of Ephesus is the author of the Greek novel *Ephesiaca* (The Ephesian Tale), a romance recounting the adventures and trials of the lovers Habrocomes and Anthia.
author_notes: Well-attested author with a near-complete extant novel; minor debate on dating but generally placed in the imperial period.

author_bio: A collection of letters traditionally attributed to Xenophon, transmitted as a textual corpus under his name.
author_notes: This entry is a named text occupying the author slot; 'Xenophontis Epistulae' refers to a corpus of letters, not an individual author.

author_bio: Xuthus is known only from a single testimonium. The name is rare; it is unclear whether this is a historical philosopher or a literary persona.
author_notes: The single attestation provides no secure context for identification.

author_bio: Ocellus (also Ocellus Lucanus) is a shadowy figure of the Pythagorean tradition, to whom was attributed a philosophical work On the Nature of the Universe. Likely a pseudepigraphic author, his texts are part of the pseudopythagorean corpus.
author_notes: The name Ocellus is associated with pseudopythagorean literature; historicity uncertain but treated as a legendary figure.
representative_titles: De universi natura Sp. | Testimonia | Fragmenta Sp.

author_bio: A lost epic poem, possibly attributed to Creophylus of Samos, narrating the capture of Oechalia by Heracles. Only a few fragments survive.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work. The attribution to Creophylus is ancient but uncertain.

author_bio: Oecumenius was a Greek Christian exegete active in the 6th century CE, known for a commentary on the Apocalypse and numerous fragmentary catena comments on Pauline epistles.
author_notes: The author is a well-known patristic commentator, but the precise details of his life remain uncertain; the corpus relies heavily on catena traditions.
representative_titles: Commentarius in Apocalypsin | Fragmenta in epistulam i ad Corinthios (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Hebraeos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Ephesios (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Colossenses (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam i ad Timotheum (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Galatas (in catenis)

author_bio: A lost archaic epic poem of the Theban cycle recounting the story of Oedipus; sometimes attributed to Cinaethon of Sparta. Only a single fragment survives.
author_notes: The author slot is a work title: the epic title Oedipodea; the attribute to Cinaethon is uncertain and fragmentary.

author_bio: Known only from a single-word inscription (titulus); no biographical details survive.
author_notes: TLG entry: the entire corpus is a one-word titulus; identity and period uncertain.

author_bio: Oenomaus was a Cynic philosopher from Gadara, active in the early 2nd century CE. Known for his fierce criticism of oracles and divination, his fragments survive primarily through Eusebius' Praeparatio Evangelica.
author_notes: Author identified as a historical Cynic philosopher; fragments preserved indirectly.

author_bio: Oenopides of Chios was an early Greek astronomer and mathematician who worked on the calculation of the Great Year and formulated geometrical theorems.
author_notes: The testimonia are fragmentary; known mainly through later doxographers.

author_bio: The Old Testament is a canonical collection of Jewish scriptures, transmitted in Greek via the Septuagint. It comprises texts from various periods and authors. In the corpus, it is treated as a single author entry for the entire collection.
author_notes: The heading 'Old Testament' is used as a collective title for the Septuagint corpus. Multiple authors and periods are involved.
representative_titles: Psalmi | Genesis | Ezechiel | Jeremias | Isaias | Numeri | Exodus | Deuteronomium

author_bio: Olympiodorus the Alchemist was a late antique commentator on alchemical texts, associated with the Alexandrian school. His work on Zosimus and the Hermetic tradition survives in fragmentary form, reflecting a fusion of Greek philosophy and practical alchemy.
author_notes: The identification with the philosopher Olympiodorus is uncertain; the label '(Alchem.)' distinguishes him as an alchemical writer. The manuscript tradition is complex.
representative_titles: Εἰς τὸ κατ’ ἐνέργειαν ζωσίμου ὅσα ἀπὸ Ἑρμοῦ καὶ τῶν | De arte sacra (appendices e codd. Venet. Marc. 299 + Paris. B.N. gr. 2250)

author_bio: Olympiodorus the Deacon was a 6th-century CE Greek Christian theologian and exegete, known for commentaries on Job, Ecclesiastes, and Jeremiah, among other biblical books, and for a fragment against Severus of Antioch.
author_notes: The source label 'Diaconus Scr. Eccl.' identifies him as a deacon and ecclesiastical writer; his works survive largely in catena and fragmentary form.
representative_titles: Commentarii in Job | Commentarii in Ecclesiasten | Commentarii in Jeremiam (in catenis) | Commentarii in Lamentationes (in catenis) | Commentarii in Baruch (in catenis) | Commentarii in Jeremiae epistulam | Fragmentum in illud: Gaudete in illa die Sp. | Contra Severum Antiochenum (fragmentum ap. Pseudo-Anastasium Sinaitam, Contra monophysitas)

author_bio: Olympiodorus the Younger was a Neoplatonic philosopher and commentator active in Alexandria in the 6th century CE. He wrote commentaries on Plato (Alcibiades, Gorgias, Phaedo) and Aristotle (Categories, Meteora), as well as a 'Prolegomena' to logic. His works are a key source for the later Platonic tradition.
author_notes: The epithet 'Phil.' distinguishes him from Olympiodorus the historian. He is a central figure in the Alexandrian Neoplatonic school.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis meteora commentaria | In Platonis Gorgiam commentaria | In Platonis Alcibiadem commentarii | In Aristotelis categorias commentarium | In Platonis Phaedonem commentaria | Prolegomena | Scholia in Aristotelis librum de interpretatione (e cod. Vat. Urbin. gr. 35)

author_cultural_context: Greek literature under the Roman Empire
author_bio: Onasander is a Greek military writer and philosopher, best known for his treatise the Strategicus, a practical manual on generalship and military command. His work is addressed to Roman officials and reflects the synthesis of Greek tactical thought with Roman imperial needs.
author_notes: Onasander is a well-attested historical figure with a surviving complete work of substantial length.

author_bio: Onatas is an obscure figure known only from a few fragments, possibly a philosopher or a poet; his identity and works are uncertain.
author_notes: The name Onatas is rare; the fragments preserved may be spurious or misattributed. The period and region are unknown.

author_bio: Ophelio is known from a few fragments of two works. The name is rare and no ancient testimonies survive to establish his identity.
author_notes: The content of the fragments does not allow for secure period or genre placement.

author_cultural_context: Imperial Greek didactic poetry; natural history
author_bio: Oppian of Anazarbus (or Corycus) is the author of the 'Halieutica', a didactic poem on fishing and marine life. He is distinguished from a later 'Oppian of Apamea' who wrote the 'Cynegetica'. The Halieutica is dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius and reflects an encyclopedic interest in ichthyology, combining poetic tradition with scientific observation and moralizing commentary.
author_notes: Distinct from Oppian of Apamea; biographical details are uncertain but the poem's dedication provides chronological anchor. The source id uses CTS URN.

author_bio: A Greek poet from Apamea in Syria, author of the Cynegetica, a didactic poem on hunting; distinct from Oppian of Corycus (author of Halieutica).
author_notes: Well-attested author; the Cynegetica is his principal work; some confusion with Oppian of Corycus exists in scholarly tradition.

author_bio: The Chaldean Oracles are a collection of hexameter verses attributed to Julian the Theurgist, combining Platonic philosophy with eastern mysticism. They were highly influential in Neoplatonic thought.
author_notes: The entry is for the text itself, not a person. The display name 'Oracula Chaldaica' is a title occupying the author slot. The note '(olim sub auctore Juliano Theurgo)' clarifies former attribution.

author_cultural_context: Greek and Roman (Jewish and Christian apocalyptic)
author_bio: The 'Oracula Sibyllina' are a collection of pseudo-prophetic hexameter poems, composed by Jewish and Christian authors under the guise of the Sibyl.
author_notes: The author field represents the title of the corpus; no single historical author exists, the work is a composite of multiple hands.

author_bio: A collection of Greek apocalyptic oracles attributed to the Tiburtine Sibyl. The text circulated as a corpus; no authorial persona is intended.
author_notes: Author_agent_kind=work_title as the entry is a named textual corpus, not a person.

author_bio: A lost Greek Jewish work purporting to be a speech by the patriarch Joseph. Only a few fragments survive, possibly from a pseudepigraphon or exegetical composition. It is sometimes associated with the 'Exagoge' of Ezekiel the Tragedian.
author_notes: The author slot is the title of a fragmentary text; there is no named author. The fragments are preserved in later Christian and Jewish sources, and the dating is speculative.

author_bio: This entry represents the work 'Oratio Manassis', a rhetorical or liturgical text attributed to or associated with a figure named Manasses. It is cataloged as a work-title entry because the author slot is occupied by the name of the text itself, reflecting cataloging practice for anonymous or uncertain attributions.
author_notes: Catalog entry uses 'Oratio Manassis' as the author heading; likely a work title by convention, as the actual authorship is unclear.

author_bio: Oribasius was a Greek physician and medical writer, court physician to Emperor Julian the Apostate; his encyclopedic 'Collectiones medicae' compiled earlier medical knowledge.
author_notes: Major medical corpus; some works survive only in epitomes or fragments.
representative_titles: Collectiones medicae (lib. 1-16, 24-25, 43-50) | Synopsis ad Eustathium filium | Libri ad Eunapium (lib. 1-4) | Eclogae medicamentorum | Collectiones medicae (libri incerti) | Testimonia

author_bio: Origen of Alexandria, one of the most influential Christian theologians and biblical exegetes of the early Church. His voluminous corpus includes commentaries, homilies, and the apologetic Contra Celsum, shaping Greek patristic thought.
author_notes: Origen is a well-attested historical figure and a central author in Christian literature. Many works survive in Greek, through catenae, and in Latin translation.
representative_titles: Contra Celsum | Selecta in Psalmos Dub. (fragmenta e catenis) | Commentarii in evangelium Joannis (lib. 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 10, 13) | Commentarium in evangelium Matthaei (lib. 12-17) | Fragmenta in Psalmos 1-150 Dub. | Philocalia sive Ecloga de operibus Origenis a Basilio et Gregorio Nazianzeno facta (cap. 1-27) | Commentarii in evangelium Joannis (lib. 19, 20, 28, 32) | In Jeremiam (homiliae 12-20)

author_cultural_context: Late antique grammar and lexicography, compilation
author_bio: Orion of Thebes (or Alexandria) was a Greek grammarian and anthologist, known for an etymological lexicon and an anthology. The several excerpta in TLG reflect his influence on later Byzantine lexica.
author_notes: Attribution of all excerpts to a single Orion is debated; some may represent different compilers. TLG aggregates them under one ID.
representative_titles: Etymologicum | Etymologicum (excerpta e cod. Darmstadino 2773) | Anthologion | Etymologicum (excerpta e cod. regio Paris. 2630) | Etymologicum (excerpta e cod. regio 2610) | Etymologicum (excerpta e cod. Vat. gr. 1456)

author_bio: A corpus of poems, hymns, and theological works attributed to the legendary poet Orpheus, forming a significant body of Orphic literature. These texts, composed over a millennium, articulate Orphic cosmology, eschatology, and ritual practice, and include the Orphic Argonautica, the Orphic Hymns, and the Derveni Papyrus commentary.
author_notes: The label 'Orphica' is a modern scholarly construct grouping diverse works under a tradition rather than a single author. The sources are heterogeneous; author_agent_kind is 'tradition' because the works are ascribed to the Orphic tradition, not to an individual.
representative_titles: Argonautica | Hymni | Lithica | Lithica kerygmata | Fragmenta | Fragmenta (P. Derveni) | Testimonia | Fragmentum astrologicum

author_bio: Orus was a Greek grammarian and lexicographer, author of a collection of Attic words and orthographical works, cited by later Byzantine lexica.
author_notes: The display name 'Orus' is used here; fragments are scattered across Zonaras and other sources.
representative_titles: Vocum Atticarum collectio (fragmenta ap. alios auctores et in aliis Ori scriptis) | Vocum Atticarum collectio (fragmenta ap. Zonarae lexicon) | Orthographia (περὶ τῆς <ι> ἀνεκφωνήτου) (olim sub titulo

author_bio: Ostanes is a legendary figure associated with the origins of alchemy in the Hellenistic period, often described as a Persian sage or magus. Works attributed to him blend philosophy and practical alchemy.
author_notes: Historicity is uncertain; Ostanes appears in alchemical literature as an authoritative name, likely pseudonymous or traditional.
representative_titles: Ὀστάνου φιλοσόφου πρὸς Πετάσιον περὶ τῆς ἱερᾶς ταύτης καὶ

author_bio: A corpus of six fragmentary paeans (cult hymns) attributed to the collective genre tradition rather than a single author. The texts include hymns to Apollo, Asclepius, and historical figures such as Titus Quinctius Flamininus and Seleucus, reflecting Hellenistic court and civic piety.
author_notes: The display name 'Paeanes' refers to a grouping of anonymous hymn texts, not an individual author. The source ID and tradition treat this as a genre-based corpus.
representative_titles: Paean Delphicus i in Apollinem | Paean Erythraeus ad urbem Dium repertus | Paean Erythraeus in Aesculapium | Fragmentum Erythraeum paeanis in Apollinem | Fragmentum paeanis in Titum Flamininum | Fragmentum Erythraeum paeanis in Seleucum

author_bio: Paeon is a shadowy figure known only from a single fragment. No biographical details survive, and the identification remains speculative.
author_notes: Only one fragment is attributed to Paeon; the name is rare and the work count is minimal.

author_bio: Palaephatus was a Greek mythographer active in the late 4th or early 3rd century BCE, known for rationalizing myths in his work 'On Incredible Tales' (De incredibilibus). His approach interpreted mythical events as misunderstandings of natural phenomena or historical facts.
author_notes: The name Palaephatus may be a pseudonym meaning 'ancient speaker'; his historicity is assumed but not firmly attested.

author_bio: Palladius was a Byzantine physician and medical commentator, active in the late antique period. He is best known for his commentaries on Hippocratic works, particularly the sixth book of Epidemics, and for a treatise on fevers.
author_notes: Author disambiguated by period and subject. The epithet 'Med.' indicates medical authorship.
representative_titles: Commentarii in Hippocratis librum sextum de morbis popularibus | Scholia in Hippocratis de fracturis | Synopsis de febribus | Περὶ βρώσεως καὶ πόσεως

author_bio: Christian bishop and monastic historian, known for the 'Lausiac History', a foundational work on early monasticism. Also authored a dialogue on John Chrysostom and other ascetic texts.
author_notes: Palladius of Helenopolis is a well-attested historical figure. The 'Scr. Eccl.' descriptor confirms his role as an ecclesiastical writer.
representative_titles: Dialogus de vita Joannis Chrysostomi | Historia Lausiaca (recensio G) | De gentibus Indiae et Bragmanibus Sp. | Prooemium ad historiam Lausiacam Sp. | Epistula ad Lausum

author_bio: Pamphila of Epidaurus was a Greek historian and author of a miscellany (Historical Commentaries), now fragmentary, known through Photius and other excerptors; she is one of the few female prose authors from antiquity.
author_notes: Known mainly from Photius (Bibliotheca cod. 175); her work survives only in fragments; uncertainty about her exact dates and scope of work.

author_bio: Pamphilus (Poeta) is a poet known from a single fragment. The content and genre are undetermined, and no biographical details survive.
author_notes: Extremely obscure; the label '(Poeta)' is a generic disambiguator, but the fragment may be of dubious attribution.

author_bio: Pamphilus (Trag.) is a little-known tragedian, surviving only through a single title or fragment. His name may be a generic attribution.
author_notes: The label 'Trag.' specifies his genre; his historicity is doubtful.

author_bio: Pamprepius of Panopolis was a poet and grammarian active in Athens and Constantinople; fragments preserved on papyrus reflect epic and encomiastic poetry.
author_notes: Fragments from P. Vindob. 29788 AC; associated with the late antique pagan revival.

author_bio: Panarces is known only from a few fragments; almost nothing is known about his life or work, and even his name may be corrupt or misattributed.
author_notes: Only 57 words survive; the identity of Panarces is uncertain and the attribution may be dubious.

author_bio: Pancrates was a Greek epic poet active in Alexandria, known from a few fragments, possibly the author of a poem on the Nile or on Hadrian's Antinous.
author_notes: The label '(Epic.)' denotes this Pancrates as an epic poet. Attribution is uncertain due to sparse testimony.

author_bio: Pancrates was a Greek epigrammatist from the Roman period, possibly active in Alexandria; a few epigrams and fragments survive.
author_notes: The (Epigr.) disambiguator marks him as an epigrammatist. His identity is sometimes confused with other authors named Pancrates.

author_bio: Panteleius is known only from a single fragment, possibly from a didactic or philosophical poem. His identity and period are obscure.
author_notes: The source is a single fragment; author's dates and provenance are unknown.

author_bio: Panyassis of Halicarnassus was an epic poet active in the early 5th century BCE, author of the Heracleia and possibly the Ionica; his works survive only in fragments, and he is known as a relative of Herodotus.
author_notes: Well-attested in ancient sources; fragments collected from citations.

author_cultural_context: Apostolic Fathers; early Christian traditions about Gospel origins
author_bio: Papias was an early Christian bishop and writer, traditionally associated with the Apostle John. His work 'Expositions of the Sayings of the Lord' survives only in fragments cited by Irenaeus and Eusebius. He is a crucial witness to early oral traditions about the composition of the Gospels and holds enduring significance for New Testament source criticism.
author_notes: Well-established figure in early Christianity; fragments transmitted via major patristic authors. Historical identity is secure though the work is lost.

author_bio: A figure associated with alchemical writings, known from a single manuscript (Ven. Marc. 299). The name Pappus ('teacher' or 'grandfather') may be a nickname or a separate person from the mathematician Pappus of Alexandria.
author_notes: The label '(Alchem.)' distinguishes this Pappus from the mathematician, but identity and historicity are uncertain.
representative_titles: Πάππου φιλοσόφου <ὅρκος> (e cod. Venet. Marc. 299, fol.

author_bio: Pappus of Alexandria was a major late antique mathematician and commentator, active in the 4th century CE. His Synagoge (Collection) is a key source for Greek geometry, and he also wrote commentaries on Ptolemy.
author_notes: Pappus is a well-documented figure in the history of mathematics. The label 'Math.' distinguishes him from other individuals named Pappus.
representative_titles: Synagoge | Commentaria in Ptolemaei syntaxin mathematicam 5-6

author_bio: This is a martyrdom account of Carpus, Papylus, and Agathonice, dating from the early Christian period. The text is anonymous and transmitted under the title listing the saints.
author_notes: The author entry is the title of the text; no named author.
representative_titles: Martyrium sanctorum Carpi, Papyli et Agathonicae

author_bio: Anonymous compiler of a collection of wonders about waters, known from a single manuscript in Florence. The name 'Paradoxographus Florentinus' is a modern label for the unknown author of this paradoxographical text.
author_notes: The author is not a historical person but a modern construct for a fragmentary text. The label is used in TLG to identify the source; no ancient name is associated.

author_bio: The Paradoxographus Palatinus is the conventional name for the anonymous author of a collection of marvels, preserved in the Palatine Anthology. The name is a modern editorial construct.
author_notes: The name 'Palatinus' refers to the manuscript (Palatine). The actual author is unknown; the label is used by scholars for convenience.

author_bio: The Paradoxographus Vaticanus is the conventional name given to an anonymous author of a collection of miraculous and marvelous tales (paradoxography), preserved in a Vatican manuscript. The work compiles wonders from natural history and myth, drawing on earlier sources. The author's name is a modern label.
author_notes: Padre ID tlg0582. The name 'Paradoxographus Vaticanus' is a scholarly designation for the anonymous compiler. The text is a single work, 'Admiranda' (Wonders). The date and provenance are uncertain.

author_bio: The Paraleipomena Jeremiou (Latin: 'Things Omitted from Jeremiah') is a pseudepigraphal text concerning the prophet Jeremiah. It survives in Greek and other versions, with no known author.
author_notes: Author slot is occupied by the work title 'Paraleipomena Jeremiou'. The text is anonymous, attributed to a tradition rather than a specific person.

author_bio: Paramonus is known only from a single inscription (a titulus); no further biographical details are recoverable.
author_notes: The entry consists of a single word in a single inscription. The attribution is tentative; the name could be a personal name or part of a formula.

author_bio: This entry represents a set of paraphrases of Dionysius Periegetes' geographical poem, produced by anonymous scholiasts. The title occupies the author slot in the TLG corpus.
author_notes: Not an individual author; corpus of paraphrases; author_agent_kind=work_title per instructions.

author_bio: Parmenides of Elea (fl. c. 480 BCE) was a foundational Pre-Socratic philosopher, known for his hexameter poem on Being and the Way of Truth. His ontology profoundly shaped subsequent Greek metaphysics.
author_notes: Parmenides is a central figure of early Greek philosophy; the testimonia and fragmenta corpus is well-established. His historicity is unquestioned.

author_bio: Parmeno was a minor Hellenistic poet or grammarian known from a handful of fragments; his work survives only in citations by later authors.
author_notes: The fragments are extremely brief; the attribution is uncertain and relies on late testimonia.

author_bio: Parm(en)iscus is a Graeco‑Egyptian grammarian or philosopher known from a single testimonium. The variant spelling suggests textual fluidity, but a historical person is likely.
author_notes: The name appears with or without the medial vowel; the testimonium is too short to confirm authorship of any lost work.

author_bio: An anonymous collection of fragmentary Greek parodic poetry from uncertain periods and authors.
author_notes: 'Parodica Anonyma' is a modern editorial label for a corpus of anonymous fragments; no individual author attribution possible.

author_bio: Paron is a Greek author known only from a single testimonium. Nothing certain can be stated about his life or work.
author_notes: The testimonium provides no further context; Paron may be an otherwise unknown grammarian or poet.

author_bio: Parrhasius of Ephesus was a renowned Greek painter active in the late 5th and early 4th centuries BCE, known for his competition with Zeuxis and his treatise on painting; surviving only as epigrams.
author_notes: Author name is that of a painter known from literary sources; the works attributed are epigrams, likely later literary exercises or attributions.

author_bio: Parthax is known only from a single fragment. The name may be a corruption or a pseudonym. Nothing certain is known about the author or the work's origin.
author_notes: Only a fragment (81 words) survives. Author identity is uncertain; possibly a ghost name.

author_bio: Parthenius of Nicaea was a Greek poet and grammarian who was brought to Rome as a prisoner and became a teacher of Vergil. His collection 'Narrationes amatoriae' ('Erotic Stories') survives, providing sources for later Latin poets.
author_notes: Source ID uses the CTS URN format.
representative_titles: Narrationes amatoriae | Fragmenta et tituli | Poesis reliquiae

author_bio: The Passion of Perpetua and Felicity, an early Christian martyrological narrative describing the imprisonment and execution of Perpetua, Felicity, and their companions in Carthage, survives in both Latin and Greek versions; the Greek version is a translation of the Latin original.
author_notes: The author slot is filled by the title of the work; traditionally attributed to Perpetua herself in part, but overall authorship is uncertain; the Greek version is a translation.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous texts describing the monuments, history, and topography of Constantinople, compiled from earlier sources. The corpus includes the 'Parastaseis Syntomoi Chronikai' and narratives about Hagia Sophia and the Apostoleion.
author_notes: The display name 'Patria Constantinopoleos' is a generic title for the corpus of city descriptions. No single author can be identified.
representative_titles: Παραστάσεις σύντομοι χρονικαί | Διήγησις περὶ τῆς Ἁγίας Σοφίας | De sepulcris imperatorum quae sunt in templo sanctorum apostolorum | Διήγησις περὶ τῆς Ἁγίας Σοφίας (variae lectiones)

author_bio: Patrocles the historian is known only from fragments, possibly dealing with local history or geography. His works are lost except for scanty testimonia.
author_notes: The designation 'Hist.' suggests a historian; only fragmentary remains. His identity and dates are uncertain.

author_bio: Patrocles (Trag.) is a tragic poet known only from a single tiny fragment; virtually nothing is known about his life or work.
author_notes: Label '(Trag.)' indicates a tragic poet; extremely fragmentary.

author_bio: Paulus Alexandrinus was a Greco-Egyptian astrologer active in late 4th-century Alexandria. His two main works, the Elementa apotelesmatica and the Anacephalaeosis, are key texts in Hellenistic astrology.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Astrol.)' disambiguates from other writers named Paulus.

author_bio: Paul of Aegina (Medicus) was a Greek (Byzantine) physician active in Alexandria in the 7th century CE. His medical compendium (Epitomae medicae libri septem) became a standard reference in both Greek and Arabic medicine.
author_notes: The '(Med.)' disambiguator indicates the physician. Historical figure with surviving substantial work.

author_bio: Paulus Silentiarius was a Byzantine court poet and official who served under Emperor Justinian. He is celebrated for his ekphrastic hexameter poems describing Hagia Sophia and its ambo, which blend classical literary form with Christian architectural description.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Silentiarius Poeta' clarifies his role as a poet and court official. His connection to Justinian's building projects is historically attested.

author_cultural_context: Fictional epistolography, imperial Greek rhetorical exercise
author_bio: This entry collects a pair of fictional letters attributed to Pausanias of Sparta and the Persian king Xerxes, likely composed as a rhetorical exercise in the imperial period. The letters reflect stereotyped depictions of Spartan and Persian character and are not authentic historical documents.
author_notes: The author heading is a descriptive name for a pair of letters; the entry is a work‑title, not an individual author. The dating of composition is uncertain but is likely an early imperial rhetorical production.

author_bio: Pausanias the Atticist was a lexicographer who compiled a collection of Attic Greek words and phrases, preserving knowledge of classical usage.
author_notes: Distinct from the travel writer Pausanias; the epithet 'Attic.' indicates specialization in Attic lexicography. Only one known work attributed to him.

author_bio: A historian named Pausanias, distinct from the travel writer, known from fragments; possibly the author of a work on Attica or the like.
author_notes: Very little survives; the label 'Hist.' distinguishes this Pausanias from the geographer.

author_bio: Pausanias wrote the 'Description of Greece,' a ten-book travelogue and guide to the sites, myths, and monuments of mainland Greece. His work is a crucial source for Greek topography and art history.
author_notes: The 'Perieg.' disambiguator stands for 'periegete' (travel writer). Widely studied and cited.

author_bio: Peirazomene is the title of a fragmentary literary work preserved on papyrus (Brit. Mus. inv. 2208). The name, meaning 'the tempted woman' or 'the test', likely designates a lost drama or poetic composition. Only a single fragment survives, offering minimal context for genre or authorship.
author_notes: The name appears as a lemma in papyrological records, likely representing the title of a work rather than a personal author. The entry is classified under author_agent_kind=work_title as it refers to a textual entity occupying the author slot.

author_bio: Pelagius is the name attached to a Byzantine alchemical treatise Πελαγίου φιλοσόφου περὶ τῆς θείας ταύτης καὶ ἱερᾶς τέχνης. The author is otherwise unknown and may be a pseudonym invoking an earlier authority.
author_notes: The name may be pseudonymous; the treatise survives in a single manuscript tradition.
representative_titles: Πελαγίου φιλοσόφου περὶ τῆς θείας ταύτης καὶ ἱερᾶς τέχνης

author_bio: A figure known only from a few fragments, possibly a historian or paradoxographer. Nothing certain is known about his life or dates.
author_notes: Virtually nothing is known about Pempelus. The fragments are too sparse for secure attribution or contextualization.

author_bio: Periander was a tyrant of Corinth and one of the Seven Sages of Greece. Known for his wise governance and patronage of the arts, he is credited with various epigrams and philosophical sayings, though many attributed works are likely spurious. His rule marked a period of prosperity and cultural advancement.
author_notes: The single spurious epistle attributed to him is of uncertain origin; included due to traditional attribution in the corpus.

author_bio: Perictione is a figure associated with early Pythagorean or Platonic circles, known through fragmentary philosophical writings attributed to her. Her precise identity and historical role remain debated, but she is often linked to the intellectual milieu of Classical Athens.
author_notes: The attribution is based on sparse testimonia; the fragments are brief and may be pseudonymous.

author_bio: The Periplus of Hanno is a Greek translation of a Punic account of a Carthaginian exploration along the West African coast. The text describes the founding of colonies and encounters with local peoples.
author_notes: The title stands for the author slot; the original Punic text is lost, and the Greek version is the only surviving witness.

author_bio: The Periplus Maris Erythraei is a 1st-century CE Greek text describing navigation and trade routes in the Red Sea and Indian Ocean. It is transmitted anonymously, with a spurious attribution to Arrian.
author_notes: Textual corpus in author slot; transmitted anonymously. Use author_agent_kind=work_title.
representative_titles: Anonymi (Arriani, ut fertur) periplus maris Erythraei

author_bio: A Greek periplus (coastal navigation guide) describing the shores and ports of the Mediterranean Sea. The text, also known as the Stadiasmus Maris Magni, is an anonymous compilation of sailing distances and landmarks.
author_notes: The work is anonymous and conventionally referred to by its title. The source ID reflects the text as an author entry in the TLG.

author_bio: A periplus (coastal navigation guide) of the Black Sea, traditionally attributed to Arrian but of uncertain authorship. This entry treats the work itself as the authorial heading.
author_notes: Attributed to Arrian in some manuscripts but the heading 'Anonymi (Arriani, ut fertur) periplus ponti Euxini' indicates uncertain authorship. Work title used.
representative_titles: Anonymi (Arriani, ut fertur) periplus ponti Euxini

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek Stoic philosopher, pupil of Zeno
author_bio: Persaeus of Citium was a Stoic philosopher and a pupil of Zeno. He wrote philosophical works but only fragments survive.
author_notes: Known from ancient testimonia; fragmentary survival.

author_bio: Persinus is a scarcely attested Greek author known only from a single two-word title. Nothing is known of his life, works, or historical context.
author_notes: The name appears only in a single fragmentary source; identification and historicity are uncertain.

author_bio: Petron is a Greek author known only from a testimonium. The name likely refers to a poet or writer of uncertain date and origin.
author_notes: Known only from a single testimonium; no secure biographical information.

author_bio: Christian author known from canonical epistles and a Paschal treatise; possibly a bishop.
author_notes: TLG entry; identity uncertain, possibly Peter I of Alexandria or another Peter.
representative_titles: Epistula canonica (e Περὶ μετανοίας) (canones 1 14), | De paschate ad Tricentium (fragmentum)

author_bio: Phaedimus (possibly an epic poet) is known only from a single five-word fragment. Nothing else is recorded about his life or works; his identification rests on a conjectural attribution.
author_notes: The identifier '(Epic.)' suggests an epic poet, but the evidence is extremely minimal. The very low word count (5) indicates a highly uncertain attribution.

author_bio: Phaedimus is a shadowy figure to whom a few Greek epigrams are attributed; his dating and identity remain uncertain.
author_notes: The abbreviation 'Epigr.' indicates his known genre; only fragments survive.

author_cultural_context: Peripatetic philosopher and historian; pupil of Aristotle.
author_bio: Phaenias of Eresus was a Peripatetic philosopher and historian known for his biographical and historical works, preserved only in fragments.
author_notes: Fragmentary corpus; source ID tlg1578 from TLG.

author_bio: Phaestus is a little-known Greek author, possibly of the Classical period, of whom only a fragment survives. The name suggests a connection to the Cretan city of Phaistos, but direct evidence of origin or dates is lacking.
author_notes: The author is known only from a single fragment preserved in later sources; identification and dating are uncertain.

author_bio: Phalaridis Epistulae is a collection of letters attributed to the tyrant Phalaris of Acragas, but widely regarded as a later rhetorical forgery.
author_notes: The letters are pseudepigraphic; 'Phalaris' here denotes the corpus, not a person.

author_bio: Phanocles was a Hellenistic poet known for a poem on the loves of famous figures; his work survives only in fragments, and his life is obscure.
author_notes: The fragments are brief and the biographical tradition is thin; may be a pseudonym or legendary figure.

author_bio: Athenian historian and antiquarian, author of a lost work on Athenian history and cults, quoted by later writers.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments and testimonia; uncertain dates and scope of work.

author_bio: Phanodicus is a shadowy figure known only from fragments of his work, likely a Hellenistic writer. The surviving fragments are scant, and his identity as a historical person is uncertain.
author_notes: Attested only through fragmentary evidence; the name suggests a Greek author of uncertain date and origin.

author_bio: Pherecrates was a Greek comic playwright of the Old Comedy tradition, active in Athens during the 5th century BCE. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Well-known among scholars of Greek comedy; fragments are cited by later authors.

author_bio: Pherecydes of Athens (or of Leros) was a mythographer and historian, author of a Genealogia (or Histories) in ten books, a major source for later mythographers; surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Distinguished from Pherecydes of Syros by the '(Hist.)' label; his fragments are collected in Jacoby FGrHist; some uncertainty about his place of origin and the title of his work.

author_bio: Pherecydes of Syros is a semi-legendary figure active in the early Greek philosophical and mythological traditions, reputedly a teacher of Pythagoras and author of a cosmogonic work. His surviving fragments blend mythic narrative with nascent philosophical speculation.
author_notes: The identifier 'Myth. et Phil.' indicates his dual attribution to mythology and philosophy; his historicity is uncertain and based on later testimonia.

author_bio: A pseudepigraphic letter attributed to the pre-Socratic philosopher Pherecydes of Syros. The letter is a literary fiction, likely part of the epistolary tradition of philosophical forgeries.
author_notes: The attribution to Pherecydes is spurious. The text is known only from a single medieval manuscript. Date and origin are uncertain.

author_bio: Pherenicus is known only from a single fragment; possibly a poet or grammarian. Nothing certain can be affirmed about his life or period.
author_notes: The existence and identity of Pherenicus are inferred solely from a fragment (30 words); he may be a phantom name.

author_bio: Phileas was a bishop of Thmuis in Egypt and a martyr. His sole surviving work is an epistle to his flock, preserved in Eusebius's *Historia Ecclesiastica*.
author_notes: The letter is cited by Eusebius; the attribution to Phileas is secure.

author_bio: Philemon was a prominent comic poet of New Comedy, rival of Menander. His plays were popular in antiquity; only fragments and a few titles survive.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical figure; works survive only in fragments. The parenthetical '(Comic.)' disambiguates from other authors named Philemon.

author_bio: Philemon Junior was a comic poet of the Hellenistic period, likely a younger relative of the more famous New Comedy poet Philemon. His work survives only in fragments, with two works attested.
author_notes: Labeled 'Philemon (Junior Comic.)' indicates a distinct author from Philemon Senior. Attribution is consistent in ancient sources but remains uncertain due to fragmentary evidence.

author_bio: Philetaerus was an Athenian comic poet of Middle Comedy, known for his plays on mythological themes and domestic life. He was a contemporary of Antiphanes and Alexis.
author_notes: Fragments of his comedies survive, but the exact number of works is uncertain. The display name may also refer to a different Philetaerus, but the genre context supports the comic poet identification.

author_bio: Philetas of Cos was a Hellenistic poet and grammarian, renowned as a predecessor of the Alexandrian poets and tutor to Ptolemy II Philadelphus. His poetry survives only in fragments, but he had a major influence on Callimachus and Theocritus.
author_notes: Philetas is securely attested as a historical figure; fragments include poetic and grammatical works.

author_bio: Philiades is otherwise unattested as an author; the name appears in a single brief fragment of uncertain provenance.
author_notes: The 12-word fragment attributed to Philiades provides no further context; it may be a misattribution or a name derived from a source text.

author_bio: A shadowy Hellenistic poet, said to have been a tragic or lyric writer from Corcyra, of whom only a few fragments survive.
author_notes: The existence and works of Philicus are poorly attested; the fragments are minimal and may be misattributed.

author_bio: Philinus was a Greek scholar or physician, likely associated with the medical or historical traditions. Fragments of his work survive, suggesting activity in the Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The name Philinus is common; the fragments may belong to a medical or historical writer, but exact identification is uncertain.

author_bio: Philippides was an Athenian comic playwright of Middle Comedy, active in the late 4th and early 3rd centuries BCE. Only fragments of his plays survive, revealing a blend of social and political satire.
author_notes: Identified as a comic poet; fragments are cited by later lexicographers and scholiasts.

author_bio: Philip II of Macedon, father of Alexander the Great, was a king of Macedon and a major figure in the political unification of Greece. Under his name a corpus of letters (Epistulae) is transmitted, though of uncertain authenticity.
author_notes: The letters attributed to Philip II are preserved in the historical tradition. Attribution is conventional; authenticity of individual letters debated.

author_bio: A Greek comic poet known only from a single surviving fragment (4 words) and possibly a second attribution; identity date and place unknown.
author_notes: Attested from only a tiny fragment; the label '(Comic.)' distinguishes him from other Philippoi; work count is 2 but likely only one genuine fragment.

author_bio: Philippus (Hist.) is a Greek historian known only from fragments. The epithet 'Hist.' distinguishes him from other authors named Philippus.
author_notes: Fragmentary survival; identity and precise historical context uncertain.

author_bio: Philiscus was a comic poet active in the early Hellenistic period, known only through fragmentary remains. His works, preserved in a few citations and papyrus scraps, indicate a place in the tradition of New Comedy, though the specific details of his career and corpus remain largely obscure.
author_notes: The label 'Comic.' disambiguates this figure from other authors named Philiscus. Attribution rests on ancient testimonia and a handful of fragments.

author_bio: Philiscus was a Greek rhetorician known only from a single surviving fragment. His exact dates and works are unclear, but he is placed in the rhetorical tradition.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; the epithet '(Rhet.)' identifies him as a rhetorician, but little else is known.

author_bio: Philiscus (Trag.) is a shadowy Hellenistic tragic poet, known only from a single fragment. Multiple figures named Philiscus are known; this one may be identical with Philiscus of Abydus or another homonym.
author_notes: The label '(Trag.)' distinguishes him from namesakes in Middle Comedy or rhetoric; the evidence is too meager to confirm his existence as a separate author.

author_bio: Philistus of Syracuse was a Greek historian who wrote a history of Sicily, focusing on the tyranny of Dionysius I and II. His work survives only in fragments, cited by later authors such as Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch.
author_notes: Authorship of fragments is often uncertain; some attributions may be disputed.

author_bio: Menecrates, identified as a poet-philosopher, is known only from a single fragment and a title. Nothing certain is recorded about his life or school.
author_notes: The epithet 'Poet. Phil.' may be a modern scholarly designation; the author is otherwise unattested.

author_bio: Philo of Alexandria (Philo Judaeus) was a Jewish philosopher and exegete who blended Greek philosophy with biblical interpretation. His extensive corpus is foundational for Hellenistic Jewish thought.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; major author in Greek Jewish literature.
representative_titles: De specialibus legibus (lib. i-iv) | Legum allegoriarum libri i-iii | De vita Mosis (lib. i-ii) | De somniis (lib. i-ii) | Legatio ad Gaium | Quis rerum divinarum heres sit | De mutatione nominum | De opificio mundi

author_bio: A little-known Epicurean philosopher, possibly of Jewish origin ('Judaeus'). His fragments, cited by later authors, suggest engagement with Epicurean doctrines. The epithet 'Senior' may distinguish him from the more famous Philo of Alexandria.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Judaeus Senior Epic.)' signals a tentative identification. The fragments are sparse and the figure is poorly attested.

author_bio: Philo of Byzantium was a mechanical engineer and writer on military technology. His Belopoeica (on artillery) and Parasceuastica et poliorcetica (on fortifications and siegecraft) are key sources for Hellenistic poliorcetics.
author_notes: Designation 'Mech.' distinguishes this Philo from others of the same name; his works are well-preserved and influential in the history of technology.

author_bio: A medical writer known only from a single fragment. The designation 'Med.' distinguishes this Philo from other figures of the same name. No biographical details survive.
author_notes: TLG lists a single fragment; author identification is speculative, hence low confidence.

author_bio: Hellenistic writer of paradoxography, known for a work on the seven wonders of the world. The epithet 'Paradox.' distinguishes him from other authors named Philo.
author_notes: The designation 'Paradox.' indicates a specialized paradoxographer; little is known about this author beyond the transmitted work.

author_bio: Philo (Poeta) is a shadowy figure known only from a single surviving fragment. The epithet 'Poeta' suggests a poetic composition, but no further biographical details are recoverable.
author_notes: The designation 'Poeta' is a modern disambiguating label; no ancient testimonies confirm this specific Philo as a poet.

author_bio: Philochorus was a prominent Athenian historian and atthidographer active in the 4th–3rd centuries BCE. He authored a celebrated local history of Athens, clearly demonstrating his role as a scholar of Attic antiquity.
author_notes: Source label 'tlg0583' is a standard TLG identifier for Philochorus. The name is clearly a personal name; no ambiguity.

author_bio: Philocles was a poet of Greek Middle Comedy, known from a single surviving title. His work reflects the transitional style between Old and New Comedy, blending mythological burlesque with social satire. Only fragments and testimonia preserve his legacy.
author_notes: The 'Comic.' disambiguates this Philocles from other authors of the same name. Attribution is based on sparse ancient references; the single extant title 'Titulus' is likely a generic label.

author_bio: Athenian tragic poet of the 5th century BCE, nephew of Aeschylus. He competed successfully against Sophocles and Euripides but only a few fragments of his plays survive.
author_notes: Minor tragic poet; fragments are very sparse and nearly all without dramatic context.

author_bio: A poet known for composing a paean to Dionysus, preserved in inscriptions. His historicity is uncertain; he may be a semi-legendary figure from the early Hellenistic or late Classical period.
author_notes: The surviving paean fragments are attributed to Philodamus, but biographical details are lacking; status as legendary reflects uncertainty.
representative_titles: Paean in Dionysum | Paean in Dionysum (fragmenta incerti ordinis)

author_bio: Philodemus of Gadara was an Epicurean philosopher and poet whose works, preserved among the Herculaneum papyri, treat ethics, rhetoric, and the history of philosophical schools.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; many surviving fragmentary works from the Herculaneum library.

author_bio: The Philogelos ('Laughter-Lover') is a late antique collection of jokes, traditionally attributed to Hierocles and Philagrius but anonymous in authorship.
author_notes: Author slot filled by the title of the work; multiple attributions exist.
representative_titles: Philogelos sive Facetiae (sub auctoribus Hierocle et Philagrio)

author_bio: Philolaus was a Presocratic philosopher and a prominent Pythagorean. He is among the first to commit Pythagorean teachings to writing. Fragments of his work, especially on number theory and cosmology, are preserved by later authors.
author_notes: The corpus includes testimonia, genuine fragments, and spurious ones; attribution of individual fragments can be debated.

author_bio: Philonides is a fragmentarily preserved Hellenistic author, known only from citations and indirect tradition.
author_notes: Multiple homonyms exist; this entry likely refers to a minor historian or grammarian.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous alchemical or philosophical fragments, preserved under the heading 'Philosophus Anonymus.' The author is unknown, and the works are grouped by convention.
author_notes: Label assigned by modern editors; actual author(s) unknown. See corpus for details.

author_cultural_context: Christian theological-philosophical tradition; texts likely from a single author or school under a conventional label.
author_bio: The name 'Philosophus Christianus' is a modern editorial label used for a collection of Greek theological and philosophical texts, possibly from the same author or tradition, addressing cosmology and exegesis.
author_notes: The label is a conventional cataloging term; the texts are grouped under this heading in manuscript tradition; no historical person identified.
