author_bio: Philostephanus is known only from a single fragment of a comic play and is classified among the writers of Greek Middle or New Comedy. His work survives in transmission via later lexicographers.
author_notes: The label '(Comic.)' in the source indicates this author is a comic poet, but evidence is minimal; only 54 words survive.

author_bio: Philostephanus of Cyrene was a Hellenistic historian and scholar known for geographical and mythological writings. His fragments treat topics in paradoxography and local history.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' disambiguator indicates his role as a historian. Survives only in fragments cited by later authors.

author_bio: A Greek ecclesiastical historian of the Arian tradition. His Church History, now lost except for extensive fragments preserved by Photius and others, covers the period from the Council of Nicaea (325) to the early 5th century from a pro-Arian perspective.
author_notes: The fragments are preserved through indirect tradition; three separate fragment groups are listed. Philostorgius is a key source for non-Nicene Christianity.
representative_titles: Historia ecclesiastica (fragmenta ap. Photium) | Historia ecclesiastica (fragmenta e passione Artemii) | Historia ecclesiastica (fragmenta e vita Constantini) (cod. Angelic. 22)

author_cultural_context: Second Sophistic, Greek rhetorical and literary culture
author_bio: A member of the Philostratus family of sophists and writers, Philostratus Minor composed a series of ekphraseis (Imagines) describing paintings, continuing the work of his more famous namesake, Philostratus Major.
author_notes: Distinguished from Philostratus Major by the epithet 'Minor'. Attested as a historical figure.

author_bio: Philostratus Sophista (Flavius Philostratus) was a prominent Greek sophist of the Second Sophistic, active in Athens and Rome. He wrote a series of works, including the 'Imagines' (Eikones), which describe a gallery of paintings, and the 'Life of Apollonius of Tyana'.
author_notes: Philostratus the Sophist is well-attested as an Athenian sophist. The entry records only the 'Imagines' among his works, but this is a major text of the Second Sophistic.

author_bio: Philostratus the Athenian, a prominent sophist and biographer, authored the 'Life of Apollonius of Tyana' and 'Lives of the Sophists', central works of the Second Sophistic.
author_notes: The URN identifies the Philostratus author group; this entry refers to the well-known Athenian sophist, not the younger Philostratus of Lemnos.
representative_titles: Vita Apollonii | Vitae sophistarum | Heroicus | De gymnastica | Epistulae et dialexeis | Nero | Epigramma

author_bio: Philoxenus, likely a grammarian or lexicographer, produced work known primarily through fragments. The surviving corpus may include lexicographical or literary material, though specifics are unclear.
author_notes: The identification is uncertain; multiple homonymous authors exist.

author_bio: A celebrated dithyrambic poet from the island of Cythera, active in Athens and later at the court of the Macedonian king Archelaus. He was known for his innovative and vivid musical style. Only fragments and titles of his works survive.
author_notes: The ethnonym 'Cytherius' distinguishes him from other poets named Philoxenus (e.g., Philoxenus of Leucas). His fragments are collected in PMG.

author_bio: Philoxenus of Leucas was a celebrated dithyrambic poet of the late 5th and early 4th centuries BCE, known for his innovative musical style and colorful mythological narratives. His works survive only in fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical poet in ancient sources (e.g., Aristophanes, Plutarch). The fragments are few but securely attributed.

author_bio: Philumenus was a Greek medical writer who authored a surviving treatise on venomous animals and their remedies. The work reflects practical pharmacology and natural history knowledge of the Roman era.
author_notes: Attested in medical tradition; his work survives mostly in a single manuscript.

author_bio: Philyllius was an Athenian comic poet active in the transition from Old to Middle Comedy. Known from several fragments and a title, his plays dealt with mythological burlesque and everyday life.
author_notes: Fragments preserved by Athenaeus and other lexicographers. The exact number of plays is uncertain; the title may be a variant reading.

author_bio: Phintys was a female Pythagorean philosopher from Sparta. She wrote on the proper conduct of women, fragments of which survive, reflecting the Pythagorean tradition of ethical instruction.
author_notes: Attested in a few fragments; her identity as a historical Pythagorean woman is plausible but not exhaustively documented.

author_bio: Phocylides of Miletus was an archaic Greek gnomic poet, known for his moralizing hexameter verses; a later pseudepigraphic work (Pseudo-Phocylides) was also attributed to him.
author_notes: The genuine fragments are distinguished from the Pseudo-Phocylidean poem by modern scholars; the 'Sententiae' may refer to the pseudepigraphic work; overall his corpus is small but well-known.

author_bio: Phoebammon is a name attached to a rhetorical treatise on figures; the author may be a legendary or purely nominal figure within the Greek rhetorical tradition.
author_notes: The work 'De figuris' is questionably attributed; the note 'fort. auctore Phoebammone alio' indicates uncertainty about which Phoebammon (if any) wrote it—possibly distinct from the known 6th-century Egyptian monk.

author_bio: Phoenicides was a Hellenistic comic poet, probably active in Athens or Alexandria. Only fragments of his work survive, cited by later authors.
author_notes: Surviving fragments and titles are few; authorship of some fragments may be uncertain.

author_bio: Phoenix of Colophon was a Hellenistic poet of iambic verse, known for a few fragments of satirical or moralizing poetry. His life and dates are poorly documented.
author_notes: Phoenix is sometimes conflated with other figures of the same name; the attribution rests on a small body of fragmentary iambic poems.

author_bio: Phormis was a Sicilian comic playwright of the 5th century BCE, known for his works in the Attic tradition of comedy. His fragments suggest involvement in the development of comic drama.
author_notes: Very little is known about Phormis; only scant fragments survive.

author_cultural_context: Archaic Greek epic, lost epic tradition
author_bio: The Phoronis is a lost archaic Greek epic poem concerning the mythological figure Phoroneus and early Argive history, existing now only in fragments. It is attributed to an unknown author and is known through later quotations and summaries.
author_notes: The catalog heading 'Phoronis' refers to the lost epic poem itself, not to an individual author. The author is unknown.

author_bio: Photius was a Byzantine scholar and Patriarch of Constantinople. His Bibliotheca summarizes and excerpts hundreds of classical texts, and his Lexicon is a major source for ancient Greek vocabulary.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure. Attribution of catena fragments is sometimes debated but generally accepted.
representative_titles: Bibliotheca | Lexicon (Ε ‐‐ Ω) | Lexicon (Α ‐‐ Δ) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis) | Commentarii in Matthaeum (in catenis) | Commentarii in Joannem (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam I ad Corinthios (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ii ad Corinthios (in catenis)

author_bio: Phrynichus II was an Athenian comic poet of the Old Comedy, contemporary with Aristophanes, known only from title fragments and brief testimonia.
author_notes: Very fragmentary survival; almost nothing known of his life beyond testimonia.

author_bio: Atticist grammarian and lexicographer, author of the *Praeparatio Sophistica* and *Eclogae*. He compiled word-lists and usage guides to promote Attic Greek purity during the Second Sophistic.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Attic.' designates Phrynichus the Atticist, distinguishing him from the comic poet Phrynichus. Multiple manuscript families of his works survive.
representative_titles: Praeparatio sophistica (epitome) | Eclogae | Praeparatio sophistica (fragmenta) | Eclogae (familia q) | Eclogae (familia T)

author_bio: Athenian comic poet of Old Comedy, contemporary with Aristophanes; known from fragments and ancient references.
author_notes: Distinct from the tragic poet Phrynichus; his comedies survive only in fragments.

author_bio: An early Athenian tragedian, known as a predecessor of Aeschylus. He reportedly introduced the first female characters into tragedy and was celebrated for his lyrical style. Only a few fragments survive of his plays, such as the Capture of Miletus and the Phoenician Women.
author_notes: The label '(Trag.)' distinguishes him from other literary figures named Phrynichus. The surviving fragments preserve only about 200 words, so reconstruction of his work is highly speculative.

author_bio: A Greek historian whose work (the 'Histories' in 28 books, covering the period 272–220 BCE) survives in fragments. He was a supporter of the democratic and anti-Macedonian side, and his vivid style was criticized by Polybius for sensationalism.
author_notes: Attested by several ancient authors. The fragments come mainly from a papyrus and citations in Athenaeus and others.

author_bio: Physiologus is the title of an anonymous Greek work that became the foundation of the medieval bestiary tradition. It compiles moralized descriptions of animals, stones, and plants from both natural history and biblical exegesis. The text is not attributed to a named author.
author_notes: The entry is the work itself, not a person. Multiple recensions exist, with the first redaction being the oldest. Author_agent_kind=work_title.

author_bio: Pigres is known only from a single fragment, possibly a Carian or Greek poet. The attribution is tenuous; the fragment may be a quoted verse from a lost work.
author_notes: The only fragment is a single line or short citation; the existence of a historical writer named Pigres is uncertain.

author_bio: One of the greatest Greek lyric poets, Pindar composed epinician odes celebrating victors in the Panhellenic games. His works survive in four books of odes and numerous fragments. His style is renowned for its intricate metre and elevated diction.
author_notes: Well attested historical figure; core author of the Greek canon.

author_bio: Pisander of Camirus is a semi-legendary epic poet credited with an Heraclea (epic on Heracles) and possibly a few epigrams. His historicity is uncertain, but he is known from later testimonia and a few fragments.
author_notes: Tradition associates him with early epic; the fragments are extremely brief.

author_bio: Pisander of Laranda was a Greek epic poet of the Roman period, known for his mythological poem Heroikai Theogamiai (Divine Marriages of Heroes). Only fragments survive, but he contributed to the epic tradition of Late Antiquity.
author_notes: Attested in the Suda and other lexicographical sources; very fragmentary.

author_bio: A pseudepigraphic letter attributed (in the manuscript tradition) to the Athenian tyrant Peisistratus. The text belongs to the genre of historical epistolography, likely composed as a rhetorical exercise.
author_notes: The heading 'Pisistrati Epistula' indicates a text occupying the author slot; the name refers to the work itself, not a person.

author_bio: A short epistolary text attributed to Pittacus of Mytilene, one of the Seven Sages. The authenticity of the letter is doubtful; it likely belongs to the later epistolographic tradition.
author_notes: The author heading is the title of the work itself, as the text is transmitted as a letter in the name of Pittacus.

author_cultural_context: Greek lyric poetry and the Seven Sages
author_bio: Pittacus of Mytilene was a statesman and one of the Seven Sages of Greece. A few poetic fragments and an epigram survive, reflecting his role as a wise lawgiver and poet.
author_notes: The attribution of the fragment and epigram is traditional.

author_bio: Plato Comicus was an Athenian comic poet of the Old and Middle Comedy, active in the late 5th to early 4th centuries BCE. He wrote approximately 30 plays, but only fragments survive. His works satirized contemporaries, including sophists and politicians.
author_notes: Distinguished from the philosopher Plato by the disambiguation '(Comic.)'. Fragments collected in PCG.

author_bio: Plato was a foundational philosopher of the Western tradition, student of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle. He authored numerous dialogues exploring ethics, metaphysics, epistemology, and politics, and founded the Academy in Athens.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the parenthetical '(Phil.)' disambiguates from other authors named Plato.
representative_titles: Leges | Respublica | Gorgias | Timaeus | Theaetetus | Phaedo | Cratylus | Philebus

author_bio: Platonius is the name given to a Greek grammarian or scholiast, possibly of the Late Antique or Byzantine period, who wrote fragments on Greek comedy, including discussions of the poets and dramatic conventions.
author_notes: Platonius is a shadowy figure known only from fragments on comedy. His identity is uncertain; the name may even be a misnomer. The fragments contribute to the study of ancient comic theory.

author_bio: Plotinus was the founder of Neoplatonism, a philosopher whose *Enneads*, edited by Porphyry, represent a synthesis of Platonic, Aristotelian, and Stoic thought. He taught in Rome and profoundly influenced later Western philosophy.
author_notes: The single work count refers to the unified corpus of the Enneads as transmitted in the TLG. The historicity of Plotinus is beyond doubt.

author_bio: Prolific Greek essayist and biographer, known for his Parallel Lives and Moralia. A priest at Delphi, he synthesized Greek and Roman cultural traditions.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure; extensive manuscript tradition.
representative_titles: Quaestiones convivales (612c-748d) | Fragmenta | Pompeius | Alexander | Aetia Romana et Graeca (263d-304f) | Antonius | Cato Minor | Apophthegmata Laconica Sp.? (208b-242d)

author_bio: Polemaeus is known only from a short epigraphic fragment ('Tituli'), with no secure biographical details.
author_notes: Epigraphic attribution; minimal textual evidence for individuation.

author_bio: Polemon of Ilium, also known as Polemon Periegetes, was a Greek historian and geographer whose works, including a periegesis of Athens, survive in fragments.
author_notes: Not to be confused with other Polemons; (Periegetes) is a common disambiguator.

author_bio: Poliochus is an obscure comic poet surviving only in two brief fragments. Nothing further is known of his life or works.
author_notes: Very sparse attestation; genre inferred from TLG classification.

author_bio: Polyaenus was a Macedonian rhetorician and military writer, author of the Strategemata, a collection of historical military stratagems dedicated to Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus. His work preserves valuable tactical anecdotes.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the Strategemata is extant, along with some fragments and testimonia.

author_bio: Greek historian whose 'Histories' document the rise of Rome in the Mediterranean. His work provides key evidence for the period 264–146 BCE.
author_notes: TLG identifier indicates Hist. disambiguation from other Polybii.
representative_titles: Historiae | Fragmenta ex incertis libris | Testimonium

author_bio: Polybius the Rhetorician is a Greek author known for works on rhetorical figures and solecisms, surviving in fragments.
author_notes: Distinct from the historian Polybius; works are technical rhetorical treatises.
representative_titles: De barbarismo et soloecismo | Fragmenta de figuris

author_bio: Polycarp of Smyrna was a bishop and early Christian martyr, a disciple of the Apostle John and teacher of Irenaeus. His sole surviving writing, the Epistle to the Philippians, is a key text of the Apostolic Fathers, addressing ethics, church order, and doctrine.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a secure corpus, though the letter to the Philippians includes some later interpolations or compilations debated by scholars.

author_bio: Hellenistic Greek author known only from fragments. No biographical details survive beyond the name.
author_notes: Attested solely via fragments; no secure biographical data.

author_bio: A Hellenistic historian from Argos, known from a few fragments concerning Argive and Greek historical traditions. He may also have written on the foundation of cities. Very little is known about his life or work.
author_notes: The testimonia are few and sometimes confused with other Polycliti (e.g., the sculptor). The fragments are cited in later sources such as Pliny and the Suda.

author_cultural_context: Greek literary culture under the Roman Empire; known as a historian of disputed reliability, often associated with the Second Sophistic rhetorical tradition
author_bio: Greek historian, likely active in the 2nd century CE, known only from fragments of a lost work preserved by later authors. His historical writings covered mythical and early Greek traditions, but his reliability was questioned in antiquity.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' label indicates a historian; the TLG source is a single fragment, leaving his identity and output almost entirely inferred. Attestations in later authors (e.g., Pausanias) suggest a real but minor figure.

author_bio: Polycrates was a bishop of Ephesus and a prominent figure in the Quartodeciman controversy. His sole surviving fragment is from a synodical letter defending the Asian practice of celebrating Easter on 14 Nisan.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Scr. Eccl.)' designates him as a church writer. Only a fragment of his letter survives, cited by Eusebius.

author_cultural_context: Greek author, perhaps a historian or grammarian
author_bio: Polycritus is a poorly known Greek author, surviving only in a single titulus (inscription or title), with no substantial textual remains.
author_notes: The sole word count of 1 suggests the record is based on a citation or title fragment rather than continuous text.

author_bio: Polyidus was a Greek poet, perhaps a dithyrambic composer from the Classical period. Only a single fragment of his work survives.
author_notes: The identity is uncertain; possibly the same as the sophist or tragic poet mentioned by later sources.

author_bio: Polyphrasmon was an ancient Greek tragic poet, a son of the renowned tragedian Phrynichus, but only fragmentary titles survive. He is a minor figure in the history of Attic tragedy.
author_notes: The source record is limited to a single title with very few words. The existence and identity rely on ancient testimonia.

author_bio: Polystratus was an Epicurean philosopher of the 3rd century BCE, known from papyrus fragments found at Herculaneum (P. Herc.). He wrote against irrational contempt ('Περὶ ἀλόγου καταφρονήσεως') and other works.
author_notes: Author identified as Polystratus; source label 'tlg1629' includes two works from Herculaneum papyri. Historicity is well established through Epicurean school tradition.
representative_titles: Περὶ ἀλόγου καταφρονήσεως (P. Herc. 336/ 1150) | Fragmenta (P. Herc. 346)

author_bio: Polyzelus is a shadowy figure known only from a few fragments. Possibly a lost epic or lyric poet, his existence is attested only through indirect sources and quotations.
author_notes: Identifier 'tlg0499' is for 'Polyzelus'. The identity is very uncertain; the fragments are meager, and the name may be that of a little-known person with legendary accretions.

author_cultural_context: Roman statesman of Greek origin; writer of minor historical/antiquarian fragments; possibly related to the poet Pompeius Macer of Mytilene
author_bio: Pompeius Macer was a Roman knight of Greek descent, possibly from Mytilene, who served under Augustus. He wrote historical works (possibly on the Trojan War or Roman antiquities) now lost except for minimal fragments.
author_notes: The fragment (40 words) is preserved in Pliny the Elder or other Latin sources. There is confusion with multiple individuals named Macer; this entry likely represents a Greek-writing author.

author_cultural_context: Neoplatonic philosophy, Homeric scholarship, music theory, logic
author_bio: Porphyry of Tyre was a prolific Neoplatonic philosopher, student of Plotinus, and editor of the Enneads. He wrote influential commentaries on Homer, Aristotle, and Ptolemy, as well as treatises on ethics, abstinence, and music. His works shaped late antique and medieval thought.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a large and varied corpus. The author ID includes some works of uncertain attribution or fragmentary transmission, but the core corpus is secure.
representative_titles: Quaestionum Homericarum ad Iliadem pertinentium reliquiae | Εἰς τὰ ἁρμονικὰ Πτολεμαίου ὑπόμνημα | De abstinentia | In Aristotelis categorias expositio per interrogationem et responsionem | Quaestionum Homericarum ad Odysseam pertinentium reliquiae | Quaestionum Homericarum liber i (recensio V) | Zetemata codicis Vaticani | Quaestionum Homericarum liber i (recensio Χ)

author_bio: Posidippus of Cassandreia was a poet of New Comedy in Athens. Fragments of his plays survive in papyri and later citations, showing a taste for domestic and romantic plots.
author_notes: Distinguished from the epigrammatist Posidippus of Pella by the label 'Comic.'; the source ID is standard.

author_bio: A Hellenistic epigrammatist active at the Ptolemaic court in Alexandria, known from the Milan Papyrus and other sources; not to be confused with the comic poet of the same name.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Epigr.' distinguishes this author from the Middle Comedy poet Posidippus. The Milan papyrus (P.Mil.Vogl. VIII 309) added many new epigrams to the corpus.

author_bio: Posidonius, labeled 'Epic.' (Epicurean?), is a little-known figure who may have been a philosopher or poet. The preserved work is a single titulus—possibly a dedication or epitaph—and no further writings or biographical details survive.
author_notes: The abbreviation 'Epic.' is ambiguous; it could indicate Epicurean affiliation or some other descriptor. The one-word work 'Titulus' suggests an epigraphic source.

author_bio: Greek Stoic philosopher, geographer, and historian from Apamea; active in Rhodes. His historical works survive in fragments cited by later authors.
author_notes: The '(Hist.)' disambiguation indicates the historian identified separately from the philosopher; fragments collected in FGrHist.

author_bio: Posidonius of Apameia was a major Stoic philosopher, historian, geographer, and polymath. His works survive mainly in quotations and summaries.
author_notes: (Phil.) distinguishes the philosopher Posidonius from other namesakes; his influence is vast.

author_bio: Minor Greek poet known only from a few fragments and a title. Likely active during the Hellenistic period, but no biographical details survive.
author_notes: Very sparse attestation; name appears only in fragmentary sources. Author agent kind set to person based on traditional biographical heading, but historicity uncertain.

author_bio: Potamon of Alexandria was a Greek philosopher of the 1st century BCE who founded an eclectic school combining elements of Platonism, Stoicism, and Peripatetic thought. Only a few fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Historically attested by Diogenes Laërtius and other sources, but the surviving fragments are very brief (42 words). Set to historical with medium confidence.

author_bio: A short didactic text, possibly medical or proverbial, transmitted under this Latin title. Not attributed to a known author.
author_notes: The Latin title 'Praecepta Salubria' suggests a Western transmission; the original Greek context is unclear.

author_cultural_context: Title of a mime prelude, possibly from Roman Egypt.
author_bio: Praelusio Mimi is the title of a fragmentary mime prologue preserved on papyrus (P. Giss. 1.1), not attributed to a named author.
author_notes: The heading refers to the work title, not a personal author.

author_bio: Early Greek poet credited with the invention of the satyr play, active in the late 6th or early 5th cent. BCE. Only a few fragments survive; the biographical tradition is likely shaped by legend.
author_notes: Pratinas' historicity is secure, but many biographical details are uncertain due to limited evidence. He is a significant but fragmentary figure in drama history.

author_bio: Praxagoras of Cos was an ancient Greek physician known for his work on anatomy, particularly the distinction between veins and arteries. He was a forerunner of the study of the pulse.
author_notes: Known only from fragments and later references; his exact dates are uncertain.

author_bio: A Greek lyric poetess from Sicyon, active in the mid-fifth century BCE. She was known for her hymns, dithyrambs, and drinking songs, though only fragments survive.
author_notes: Her authorship is attested by ancient sources, but the surviving corpus is very small.

author_bio: Praxiphanes was a Peripatetic philosopher and grammarian active in the Hellenistic period, known for his work on poetics and literary criticism. Surviving fragments reflect his scholarly interests.
author_notes: Known from ancient testimonia and fragments; his historicity is secure.

author_bio: Praxiteles was one of the most celebrated sculptors of Classical Athens, known for works like the Aphrodite of Knidos. A single epigram is attributed to him.
author_notes: The epigram may be a later attribution; the name is best known for sculpture rather than poetry.

author_bio: Priscianus Lydus was a 6th-century Byzantine Neoplatonist philosopher and commentator, known for his paraphrase of Theophrastus.
author_notes: Active in the Neoplatonic school of Athens; sometimes identified with Priscian of Lydia.

author_bio: Priscus, known from a single title, was likely a Greek epic poet of Late Antiquity. No text survives beyond the title.
author_notes: Only a title recorded; epic genre from TLG; historicity uncertain but traditionally considered historical.

author_bio: Priscus is a historian and rhetorician known from fragmentary and testimonial sources; his works survive only in quotations and references, covering historical or rhetorical topics in the Greek Imperial period.
author_notes: The 'Hist. et Rhet.' disambiguator indicates historical and rhetorical authorship, but the identity remains uncertain; source likely a Byzantine compendium.

author_bio: Proclus was the head of the Platonic Academy in Athens and a major Neoplatonist philosopher. He wrote extensive commentaries on Plato and Euclid, as well as original systematic works.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure; highly influential in later philosophy and theology. URN source.
representative_titles: In Platonis Timaeum commentaria | In Platonis Parmenidem | In Platonis rem publicam commentarii | Theologia Platonica (lib. 1-5) | In primum Euclidis elementorum librum commentarii | In Platonis Alcibiadem i | In Platonis Cratylum commentaria | Institutio theologica

author_bio: Procopius of Caesarea was a prominent late antique historian and adviser to the general Belisarius. He authored the major works *De Bellis*, *De Aedificiis*, and the scandalous *Historia Arcana* (Secret History), providing essential insight into the reign of Justinian I.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Hist.)' disambiguates from late alchemical and other homonyms. The works are secure attributions.
representative_titles: De bellis | De aedificiis (lib. 1-6) | Historia arcana (= Anecdota)

author_bio: Procopius of Gaza was a Christian rhetorician and exegete active in the early 6th century. He composed commentaries on biblical books (Isaiah, Ecclesiastes, Canticle) and authored rhetorical exercises (Declamationes), letters, and a Horologium. His work reflects the fusion of classical rhetorical training and Christian scholarship in late antique Gaza.
author_notes: The TLG identifier clarifies the homonym: this is Procopius the rhetorician and ecclesiastical writer, not the historian.
representative_titles: Commentarii in Isaiam | Catena in Canticum canticorum | Epistulae 1-166 | Catena in Ecclesiasten (e cod. Marc. gr. 22) | Declamationes | Descriptio imaginis | Horologium | Refutatio Procli (fragmentum e cod. Vat. gr. 1096)

author_bio: Greek Sophist and teacher, known for linguistic theory and moral fables; works survive only in fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; fragmentary corpus.

author_bio: A corpus of Byzantine scholarly prolegomena and treatises on Greek comedy, including works on meter and theatrical practice. The entry represents the corpus, not a single author.
author_notes: Author_agent_kind=work_title because the label is a title of a textual corpus. Covers 18 works, some by different anonymous hands.
representative_titles: De comoedia (Anonymus Crameri ii) | De comoedia (Anonymus Crameri i) | ἐκ τοῦ Ἡφαιστίωνος ἐπιτομὴ τῶν ἐννέα μέτρων | De comoedia | Tractatus Coislinianus (e cod. Paris. Coislin. 120) | De Hellenismo et Atticismo | De scoliis | De choro

author_bio: Promathidas was a Greek historian or mythographer, known from fragments and a possibly spurious titulus.
author_notes: Source identification uncertain; 'Titulus' may refer to an inscription or a spurious work.

author_bio: Promathion is a shadowy figure, possibly a historian or mythographer, known from a single fragment. Nothing certain is known of his life or work.
author_notes: Attested only in one fragment; authorship and historicity are uncertain. Source ID tlg2548.

author_bio: Pronomus was a celebrated aulete (flute-player) and composer from Thebes, credited with innovations in aulos technique and music for dramatic performances.
author_notes: Modern designation based on ancient testimony; single epigraphic or indirect source.

author_bio: Prorus is known solely from a single short inscription or title (three words). No further biographical or historical details survive.
author_notes: The extremely sparse attestation makes confident classification impossible; the name may be a variant or misreading.

author_bio: A pre-Socratic philosopher and leading Sophist, known for his relativistic epistemology and maxim 'Man is the measure of all things.'
author_notes: Surviving only in testimonia and fragments; no complete works extant.

author_bio: Protagorides is a lost Greek author known only from fragments preserved in later sources. He may have written on history or paradoxography.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; identity and period are uncertain. Label used for a fragmentary author.

author_bio: The Protevangelium Jacobi is an apocryphal gospel attributed to James, traditionally dated to the mid-2nd century CE. It focuses on the birth and early life of Mary, and the birth of Jesus, offering an influential narrative that shaped later Marian doctrine.
author_notes: The author is a text title occupying the author slot, not a person. The work is widely known and cited in early Christian literature.

author_bio: An obscure author of whom only fragments remain. The name Proxenus occurs in various contexts; it is unclear if the same person is meant. Possibly a historian or mythographer.
author_notes: The fragments are short and lack clear generic or biographical context. Multiple different figures named Proxenus exist, causing ambiguity.

author_bio: A shadowy figure known only from a single epigram attributed to him in the Greek Anthology, possibly a pseudepigraphon mimicking the name of the famous tragedian Agathon.
author_notes: Very brief epigram; authorship uncertain; likely a fictional attribution or misattribution to the historical Agathon.

author_bio: Pseudonymous author of Neopythagorean philosophical fragments attributed to the historical Archytas of Tarentum. Likely composed in the Hellenistic or Roman period.
author_notes: Well-known pseudepigraphon. Author agent kind set to person because the heading refers to a pseudonymous figure; historicity status set to pseudonymous accordingly.

author_bio: The label 'Pseudo-Auctores Hellenistae' is a modern scholarly designation for a group of anonymous or pseudonymous Hellenistic authors whose fragments are collected under this heading. These texts are of uncertain authorship but are associated with Hellenistic literary production.
author_notes: The source label 'Pseudo-Auctores Hellenistae' indicates a modern grouping; individual authors are unknown or uncertain. The collective is used for catalog organization.

author_bio: Pseudo-Codinus is the conventional name for the anonymous author or compiler of several late Byzantine texts, including the 'Patria Constantinopoleos' and 'De officiis'. These works describe the history, monuments, and court rituals of Constantinople. The name 'Codinus' is a pseudonym from a manuscript attribution.
author_notes: The label 'Pseudo-Codinus' indicates that the works were once attributed to a certain Codinus, but this attribution is now considered false. The real identity is unknown.
representative_titles: Patria Constantinopoleos | De officiis (= officia palatii Constantinopoleos) | De annis ab orbe condito | Patria Constantinopoleos (ordo et tituli paragraphorum, quem sequitur recensio C, ejusque additamenta) | Patria Constantinopoleos (variae lectiones) | Patria Constantinopoleos (ordo et tituli paragraphorum, quem sequitur | De officiis (= officia palatii Constantinopoleos) (e codd Vat. gr. 162 + | De officiis (= officia magnae ecclesiae) (auctore Andrea Darmario)

author_bio: Pseudonymous author to whom an epigram is attributed in the Demosthenic corpus. Not a historical person but a later attribution exploiting the authority of the orator Demosthenes.
author_notes: Single epigram of 12 words; the attribution is clearly pseudepigraphal.

author_bio: The author writing under the name of Dionysius the Areopagite (Paul's convert) produced a corpus of mystical and hierarchical theology deeply influential in both Eastern and Western Christianity. The identity remains unknown.
author_notes: Universally recognized as pseudonymous, with plausible dating to the late 5th–early 6th century. The corpus is a unified theological project.
representative_titles: De divinis nominibus | De ecclesiastica hierarchia | De caelesti hierarchia | Epistulae | De mystica theologia

author_bio: Author of medical and pharmacological works falsely attributed to the famous physician Dioscorides; the corpus includes treatises on venomous animals, venoms, and stones.
author_notes: The 'Pseudo-' prefix indicates pseudonymous attribution; works are spurious but part of the Dioscoridean tradition.
representative_titles: De iis, quae virus ejaculantur, animalibus libellus, in quo et de rabioso | De venenis eorumque praecautione et medicatione (= | De lapidibus

author_cultural_context: Hellenistic medical writers under Galen's name
author_bio: Pseudo-Galen is a conventional label for a collection of works wrongly attributed to Galen of Pergamon, composed by various unnamed medical writers between the 2nd and 4th centuries CE, covering pharmacology, medical definitions, and philosophical compilations.
author_notes: Conventional label for works transmitted under Galen's name but not by him.
representative_titles: De remediis parabilibus libri iii | Introductio seu medicus | De optima secta ad Thrasybulum liber | Definitiones medicae | De historia philosophica | Ad Gaurum quomodo animetur fetus | De fasciis liber | De affectuum renibus insidentium dignotione et curatione liber

author_bio: A collection of letters falsely attributed to Heraclitus, reflecting Cynic and Stoic popular moralizing. The corpus is a pseudepigraphic work that uses Heraclitus' name to lend authority to its ethical teachings.
author_notes: The author heading refers to the work itself (a pseudepigraphic letter collection), not a historical person. This is a text-based filing label.

author_cultural_context: Hippocratic tradition, medical and astrological texts
author_bio: A group of anonymous medical writers whose works circulated under the name of Hippocrates, covering dietetics, pharmacology, and astrological medicine.
author_notes: Pseudonymous corpus attributed to Hippocrates; multiple authors unknown.
representative_titles: Περὶ διαφορᾶς τροφῶν πρὸς Πτολεμαῖον | Ἑρμηνεία περὶ ἐνεργῶν λίθων | Epistula ad Ptolemaeum regem de hominis fabrica | Epistula ad Ptolemaeum regem | Περὶ διαφόρων καὶ παντοίων τροφῶν

author_bio: A corpus of theological and polemical works traditionally attributed to Justin Martyr but now considered spurious. The author(s) remain anonymous, writing in Greek under Justin's name.
author_notes: The designation 'Pseudo-Justinus' indicates that the works are not by Justin Martyr but by later, unknown Christian writers. Source ID includes multiple works under the same URN.
representative_titles: Quaestiones et responsiones ad orthodoxos | Confutatio dogmatum quorundam Aristotelicorum | Quaestiones Christianorum ad gentiles | Cohortatio ad gentiles | Expositio rectae fidei | Quaestiones gentilium ad Christianos | Epistula ad Zenam et Serenum | De resurrectione

author_bio: 'Pseudo-Lucian' designates the hypothetical author(s) of several works transmitted under the name of Lucian of Samosata but considered inauthentic. The corpus spans various genres and dates.
author_notes: The source label uses the conventional designation for spurious Lucianic works; the author is not a single historical person but a modern editorial construct.
representative_titles: Asinus | Amores | Demosthenis encomium | Charidemus | Philopatris | Cynicus | Epigrammata | Ocypus

author_cultural_context: Early Christian monasticism, Greek patristic literature
author_bio: Pseudo-Macarius is the conventional name for the anonymous author of a large corpus of Greek homilies and spiritual writings traditionally attributed to Macarius of Egypt. The works are central to the development of early Byzantine ascetic theology.
author_notes: The name Pseudo-Macarius is a scholarly designation; the works are pseudonymously attributed to the historical Macarius of Egypt. The exact identity is uncertain.
representative_titles: Sermones 64 (collectio B) | Homiliae spirituales 50 (collectio H) | Sermones 1-22, 24-27 | Epistula magna | Homiliae 7 (collectio HA) | Apophthegmata | Opusculum 1 (= De custodia cordis), MPG 34: 821 841. | Sermo 23 (recensio completa)

author_bio: Author of the military manual *Strategicon*, traditionally attributed to the Byzantine emperor Maurice (582-602 CE) but likely composed under his name by Urbicius or another tactician. A key late antique tactical manual.
author_notes: Label 'Pseudo-Mauricius' indicates attribution is pseudonymous or falsely ascribed to Emperor Maurice; the *Strategicon* is a major Byzantine military treatise.
representative_titles: Strategicon (sub nomine Mauricii Imperatoris vel Urbicii)

author_bio: An unknown Christian author of mythological scholia, falsely attributed to Nonnus of Panopolis. The work explains pagan myths from a Christian perspective, likely for educational purposes.
author_notes: The attribution to Nonnus is spurious; the author's true identity is unknown. The text survives in multiple manuscripts.

author_cultural_context: Greek (with Jewish or Hellenistic Jewish influence)
author_bio: The so-called Pseudo-Phocylides is the anonymous author of a Greek gnomic poem in hexameters, the Sententiae, which was falsely attributed to the 6th-century BCE poet Phocylides. The work combines ethical precepts from Greek and Jewish traditions, probably written by a Hellenistic Jewish or early Christian author.
author_notes: The pseudonymity is widely accepted. The Gnom. abbreviation distinguishes this corpus from the genuine Phocylides. The work is important for the study of Hellenistic-Jewish literature.

author_bio: A label for several works transmitted under Plutarch's name but not by him; the Placita philosophorum, De musica, and De fluviis are compilations by unknown authors.
author_notes: Pseudo-Plutarch covers multiple anonymous texts; source ID reflects the corpus of wrongly attributed works.
representative_titles: Placita philosophorum (874d-911c) | De musica (1131b-1147a) | De fluviis

author_bio: The text attributed to Pseudo‑Polemon is a Greek physiognomic manual falsely ascribed to the famous 2nd‑century Sophist Polemon of Laodicea. It offers a system for interpreting character from bodily features and draws on earlier physiognomic traditions. The identity of the actual author remains unknown.
author_notes: The 'Pseudo‑' designation clearly marks the work as pseudepigraphic. The real author is unknown; the name is retained as the TLG entry heading.

author_bio: Unknown author of astrological works falsely attributed to Claudius Ptolemy, including the Centiloquium.
author_notes: Explicitly pseudonymous attribution; no historical person identified.

author_bio: Anonymous Greek author of a geographical poem in iambic trimeters, traditionally but falsely attributed to Scymnus of Chios.
author_notes: The label 'Pseudo-' indicates a false attribution; the true identity is unknown.
representative_titles: Ad Nicomedem regem, vv. 1-980 (sub titulo Orbis descriptio) | Ad Nicomedem regem, vv. 722-1026

author_bio: Pseudo-Sphrantzes is the conventional name for the author of the 'Chronicon Maius', a work attributed to the 15th-century historian George Sphrantzes but likely expanded or forged by Macarius Melissenus in the 16th century. The text is a key source for the fall of Constantinople but is mixed with later interpolations.
author_notes: The 'Pseudo-' prefix indicates the attribution is pseudonymous; the work is considered a composite of genuine Sphrantzes material and later expansions by Macarius Melissenus.
representative_titles: Chronicon sive Maius (partim sub auctore Macario Melisseno) | Chronicon sive Maius (additamenta Macarii Melisseni + lectiones variae)

author_bio: Pseudo-Symeon is the conventional name for the anonymous author of a Byzantine chronicle transmitted under the name of Symeon, but likely written by a different, unknown compiler active in the 10th century.
author_notes: Name assigned by modern scholars; author is a pseudepigraphic figure.
representative_titles: Chronographia (partim edita e cod. Paris. gr. 1712)

author_bio: The conventional name for the anonymous compiler of a large Greek lexicon, falsely attributed to the Byzantine historian and theologian Johannes Zonaras (12th c. CE). The lexicon draws on earlier sources such as the Suda and Cyrillus.
author_notes: The prefix 'Pseudo-' indicates a false attribution. The work is a compilation, not by a single identifiable author. The lexicon is an important witness to Byzantine lexicography.

author_display_name: Ptolemaei II Philadelphi Et Eleazari Epistulae
author_canonical_name: Ptolemaei II Philadelphi Et Eleazari Epistulae
author_bio: A collection of purported letters between Ptolemy II Philadelphus and the Jewish high priest Eleazar, transmitted in Greek. The correspondence is part of the legendary narrative surrounding the Septuagint translation.
author_notes: The author slot contains the title of the correspondence, not an individual. The letters are generally considered pseudepigraphic.

author_bio: Ptolemy III Euergetes I was the third pharaoh of the Ptolemaic dynasty. He is credited with a single extant epigram, reflecting the literary interests of Hellenistic rulers.
author_notes: Attribution rests on a single epigram; likely authentic as a royal composition.

author_bio: Ptolemy IV Philopator (reigned 221–204 BCE) was the fourth Pharaoh of the Ptolemaic dynasty. A single word attributed to him survives (perhaps an inscription or titulus), representing his only literary or epigraphic record.
author_notes: This entry likely refers to an inscription or a single-word fragment attributed to the king. The work count and word count are minimal.

author_bio: Ptolemy VIII Euergetes II (Physcon) was a Hellenistic king of Egypt from the Ptolemaic dynasty. He was a patron of learning and himself wrote historical or scientific works, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Only fragments attributed to him remain, and the extent of his authorial role is debated. The fragments may include memoirs or historical notes.

author_bio: A prominent Valentinian Gnostic teacher; his 'Letter to Flora' is a key source for Gnostic theology, preserved by Epiphanius.
author_notes: The 'Gnost.' label identifies him as a Gnostic author. A well-known figure in Gnostic studies; his letter is a substantial extant work.

author_bio: Alexandrian grammarian who compiled lexical works on word differences; surviving texts are a composite of later spuria.
author_notes: The label 'Gramm.' distinguishes him from homonymous authors; works transmitted with spurious additions.
representative_titles: De differentia vocabulorum (= Περὶ διαφορᾶς λέξεων) [Sp.] (e | De differentia vocabulorum (= Περὶ διαφορᾶς λέξεων κατὰ

author_bio: Ptolemaeus (Hist.) is a Greek historian known only from surviving fragments attributed to a work of historical character. The epithet distinguishes him from the many other Greek authors named Ptolemy. His exact dates and regional background remain unknown.
author_notes: The 'Hist.' label indicates that this Ptolemaeus is a historian, distinguished from others with the same name; only fragments remain.

author_bio: Ptolemais is a little-known author of a fragmentary work on music theory. The name may refer to a city (Ptolemais) or a person, but the surviving fragments suggest a connection to the Peripatetic or Pythagorean tradition.
author_notes: The identity is contested; the author may be a person from the city or the city itself used as a label. The music fragments are the only evidence.

author_bio: Publius is a name appearing in Greek fragments, possibly representing a Greek author bearing a Latin name, or a Roman writing in Greek. Little can be said with certainty about identity or works.
author_notes: Uncertain historicity; name may be a Latin praenomen; fragments insufficient for firm attribution.

author_bio: A Roman Greek author, possibly a grammarian or paradoxographer, known from the collection On Marvels and from fragments on various wonders. The name may refer to a writer of the Second Sophistic period, perhaps identical with a known sophist.
author_notes: The identity is uncertain, as several persons with the name Publius Aelius are known from inscriptions. The fragments are transmitted in miscellanies and paradoxographical collections.

author_bio: Publius Herennius is a known Roman author writing in Greek, from whom a single fragmentary work survives. He may have been active in the intellectual circles of the Second Sophistic.
author_notes: Only one work is ascribed to him; the true extent of his oeuvre and influence remains uncertain due to sparse evidence.

author_bio: A shadowy figure known only from a single fragment; possibly a lost author or a misattribution.
author_notes: The entry is a hapax in the corpus with no secure biographical data; the fragment may belong to a known author under a variant name.

author_bio: A shadowy figure to whom a single fragment is attributed; possibly a historian or geographer, known only from a brief citation.
author_notes: Extremely scant attestation; likely a minor or obscure author; source ID confirms single fragment of 100 words.

author_date_range: c. 319–272 BCE (life of Pyrrhus of Epirus)
author_cultural_context: Greek historical and military writing; the fragments may relate to Pyrrhus' own memoirs or treatises
author_bio: Greek king of Epirus and general; a small number of fragmentary texts are attributed to him, possibly from his lost memoirs or military writings.
author_notes: The attribution is uncertain; the fragments may be from a work by Pyrrhus himself or from a later source. The historical figure is well-known, but direct authorship is debated.

author_bio: Pythaenetus is known only from a few fragments preserved in later authors. The subject matter of the fragments suggests possible interest in natural philosophy or paradoxography, but his identity and dates remain obscure.
author_notes: No securely datable context. TLG classification may group testimonia with fragmenta. Historicity uncertain due to lack of external biographical attestation.

author_bio: Pythagoras of Samos was a pre-Socratic philosopher and mathematician whose life and teachings were soon enveloped in legend. The corpus attributed to him is largely spurious or later Pythagorean, but his historical influence on Greek thought is profound.
author_notes: Attribution of many works is uncertain; the historical figure is shadowed by legend.
representative_titles: Testimonia | Fragmenta astrologica | Fragmenta | Carmen aureum | Epigramma

author_bio: Group of philosophers and writers associated with the Pythagorean tradition, whose testimonies and fragments survive in later compilations.
author_notes: The name 'Pythagoristae' refers collectively to followers of Pythagoras; the agent is treated as a school rather than an individual.

author_bio: Pytheas of Massalia was a Greek explorer and geographer who circumnavigated Britain and journeyed to the Arctic. His work 'On the Ocean' survives only in fragments and quotations by later authors.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; fragments preserved by Strabo, Pliny, and others.

author_bio: Pythermus is a barely attested author known from a single fragment. The historicity and period are uncertain; he may be a minor historian or a mythical figure.
author_notes: The source has only 113 words; the name is otherwise unknown in literary tradition. The nature of the fragment is unclear.

author_bio: Pythocles was a pupil of Epicurus, known from Epicurean sources and fragments. He is the addressee of Epicurus's letter on astronomy (Pythocles' letter), and may have written works on Epicurean topics, now lost.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; attestation is primarily through Diogenes Laertius and papyri. TLG identifier tlg2560.

author_cultural_context: Greek dramatist (comic poet) and later diplomat; known for the satyr play 'Agen'.
author_bio: Python of Catana (or Byzantium) was a Greek comic and tragic poet active in the late 4th century BCE. He is best known for a fragment of a satyr play mocking the Athenian politician Demades. Only one fragment survives.
author_notes: The identity of 'Python' is ambiguous; there may be confusion with the more famous Python of Byzantium.

author_bio: A corpus of anonymous Greek rhetorical treatises and handbooks compiled from various periods, including progymnasmata, stylistic guides, and commentaries on the parts of speech. These texts, transmitted without authors' names, reflect the evolution of rhetorical education in the Greek tradition.
author_notes: The heading 'Rhetorica Anonyma' is a modern cataloging label for anonymous rhetorical works; no single author exists. The 22 works span a wide chronological range, with some previously misattributed to figures like John Doxapatres.
representative_titles: Progymnasmata | Epitome artis rhetoricae | Περὶ τῶν τοῦ λόγου σχημάτων | Expositio artis rhetoricae | Περὶ τῶν ὀκτὼ μερῶν τοῦ ῥητορικοῦ λόγου (e cod. Paris. | Περὶ τῶν τεσσάρων μερῶν τοῦ τελείου λόγου (e cod. Paris. | Prolegomena in artem rhetoricam (olim sub auctore Joanne Doxapatre) | Περὶ τῶν σχημάτων τοῦ λόγου

author_bio: Greek poet and grammarian, known for epic and epigrammatic works; fragments preserved in anthologies and testimonia.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Rhianus is a poet of the Hellenistic period, with only fragmentary remains.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Epigrammata | Testimonia | Fragmentum et titulus

author_bio: Rhinthon was a Hellenistic dramatist from Tarentum in Magna Graecia, known for creating hilarotragoidiai, tragic burlesques that parodied classical tragedy. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: TLG entry for Rhinthon indicates fragmentary preservation; biographical details derive from later lexicographers.

author_bio: Romanus Melodus is the foremost early Byzantine hymnographer, credited with composing numerous kontakia; his works are central to the Greek liturgical tradition.
author_notes: Attribution of some cantica is debated; the Akathistos Hymn is dubiously assigned.
representative_titles: Cantica | Cantica dubia | Cantica genuina | Ἀκάθιστος ὕμνος [Dub.]

author_bio: Greek physician active in the late 1st to early 2nd century CE, known for anatomical and urological treatises. His works survive mainly in fragments and later compilations.
author_notes: The label '(Med.)' disambiguates from other authors named Rufus. Attested as a historical figure.
representative_titles: De renum et vesicae morbis | De corporis humani appellationibus | Quaestiones medicinales | De satyriasmo et gonorrhoea | De partibus corporis humani | Synopsis de pulsibus | De ossibus

author_bio: Greek rhetorician known as Rufus the sophist. Author of an 'Ars Rhetorica', likely active in the early Roman imperial period.
author_notes: The label '(Soph.)' distinguishes this figure from other Rufi. Identity and dates are not precisely known, but the rhetorical manual is extant.

author_bio: John of Damascus (Saint John Damascene) was a prominent Byzantine theologian, hymnographer, and apologist. His works include theological treatises, homilies, and the influential 'Fount of Wisdom'.
author_notes: Source URN includes multiple works; some are dubia (e.g., 'Vita Barlaam et Joasaph' is considered spurious). Author status as saint and Church Father is securely attested.
representative_titles: Sacra parallela (recensiones secundum alphabeti litteras dispositae, quae tres libros conflant) (fragmenta e cod. | Commentarii in epistulas Pauli Dub. | Vita Barlaam et Joasaph Sp. | Expositio fidei | Orationes de imaginibus tres | Dialectica sive Capita philosophica (recensio fusior) | Dialectica sive Capita philosophica (recensio brevior) | Contra Manichaeos

author_cultural_context: Christian ecclesiastical historian writing in Greek; his work continues Eusebius' chronicle and covers the 4th–early 5th centuries
author_bio: Salaminius Hermias Sozomenus (often called Sozomen) was a Christian historian who composed a multi-volume 'Ecclesiastical History' covering the period from Constantine to Theodosius II. His work draws heavily on earlier sources, providing a narrative from a pro-Orthodox perspective.
author_notes: Well-attested author; the single surviving work is substantial. The given name 'Salaminius' is rarely used in modern references; 'Sozomenus' is far more common.

author_bio: Greek Neoplatonist philosopher, author of the concise theological treatise 'On the Gods and the Cosmos'.
author_notes: Sometimes identified with the homonymous friend of Emperor Julian.

author_bio: Salmanas is a poorly known author of a methodological work on the production of spherical hail. The name and text suggest a possible astrological or scientific context, but little is known about the person.
author_notes: The sole work 'Μέθοδος...' is attributed to Salmanas but the author's historicity is uncertain; may be a pseudonymous or late attribution.
representative_titles: Μέθοδος δῑ ἧς ἀποτελεῖται ἡ σφαιροειδὴς χάλαζα κατασκευασθεῖσα

author_bio: Sannyrion was a Greek comic poet of the late 5th century BCE, associated with Old Comedy. Only fragmentary titles and lines survive from his work, along with a few testimonia.
author_notes: Sannyrion is known from the comic tradition; fragments are sparse but he is attested as a contemporary of Aristophanes. The entry includes both fragments and a title.

author_bio: This entry groups fragments attributed to both Sappho and Alcaeus, the two great lyric poets of Lesbos. The combined corpus reflects the shared transmission and manuscript tradition of their poetry.
author_notes: The source label 'et Alcaeus' indicates a composite grouping of fragments attributed to both Sappho and Alcaeus, treated here as a collective author entry for cataloguing purposes.

author_bio: Sappho was a lyric poet from the island of Lesbos, widely regarded as one of the greatest poets of antiquity for her emotional, personal verse, mostly surviving in fragments.
author_notes: The (Lyr.) disambiguator indicates the author is treated as a lyric poet in the TLG corpus.

author_bio: Famous lyric poet from Lesbos, noted for her personal and emotional poetry. Most of her work survives in fragments.
author_notes: The label 'Sapphus' is a variant genitive form; the canonical name is Sappho.

author_bio: Hellenistic biographer, known for a life of Euripides surviving on papyrus. Active in the Peripatetic tradition, his work combines anecdotal detail with literary criticism.
author_notes: The suffix '(Biogr.)' distinguishes this Satyrus from the comic poet of the same name.

author_bio: Satyrus was a Hellenistic Greek biographer and historian known for his biographical works, particularly on the lives of philosophers and kings. Only fragments survive, including papyrus remains from Oxyrhynchus.
author_notes: Parenthetical '(Hist.)' distinguishes this Satyrus from a known comic poet. The identification is secure.

author_bio: Greek historian, possibly from Mytilene, known from a few fragments of his historical works.
author_notes: Scanty fragments; the name may be confused with other authors. Historical existence is probable but poorly documented.

author_bio: A corpus of scholia (marginal and interlinear annotations) on the works of the Greek rhetor Aelius Aristides. These scholia were compiled over centuries by multiple anonymous commentators and are a key source for the reception of Aristides.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of a text (a collection of scholia). No single individual is responsible; it is a cumulative work of many scholiasts.

author_bio: A corpus of ancient and medieval scholia (commentaries and marginal notes) on the speeches of the Athenian orator Aeschines. The scholia are anonymous compilations drawing on earlier grammatical and rhetorical scholarship.
author_notes: The entry represents a corpus of scholia, not a single author. The material is attributed to multiple anonymous scholars over time.

author_bio: The Scholia in Aeschylum are a corpus of marginal and interlinear notes on the plays of Aeschylus, compiled by various Byzantine scholars over centuries, including Thomas Magister and Demetrius Triclinius.
author_notes: This entry represents the collective body of scholia on Aeschylus, not a single author; using work_title as agent kind.

author_bio: A corpus of ancient and medieval scholia (exegetical notes) on the Argonautica of Apollonius of Rhodes. These annotations draw on earlier commentaries and glossaries, representing a tradition of Homeric and Hellenistic scholarship rather than a single author.
author_notes: The heading 'Scholia In Apollonium Rhodium' refers to a collective body of scholia, not a named individual. authorship is attributed to a tradition of grammarians and scholars.
representative_titles: Scholia in Apollonii Rhodii Argonautica (scholia vetera)

author_cultural_context: Greek scholiastic tradition on Aratus' Phaenomena
author_bio: A corpus of ancient and medieval commentaries and marginal notes on Aratus' didactic poem Phaenomena, compiled by multiple anonymous scholars over centuries.
author_notes: The author field is a modern editorial label for a body of scholia; no single author exists.
representative_titles: Scholia in Aratum (scholia vetera) | Scholia in Aratum (scholia vetera) (e codd. Palat. gr. 40 (P) + Paris | Scholia in Aratum (scholia vetera) (e cod. Perusino A 35) | Scholia in Aratum (scholia vetera) (P. Berol. 5865) | Scholia in Aratum (scholia vetera) (e cod. Paris gr. 2728)

author_bio: The scholia on Aristophanes are a corpus of marginal annotations, commentaries, and critical notes on the comedies of Aristophanes. They were compiled by multiple anonymous scholars over centuries, including figures like John Tzetzes, Thomas Magister, and Demetrius Triclinius.
author_notes: The author field is occupied by the collective title of the scholia corpus, not a single author. The individual scholiasts (Tzetzes, Moschopulus, etc.) are subsumed under this heading.
representative_titles: Scholia in nubes (scholia anonyma recentiora) | Scholia in equites (scholia vetera et recentiora Triclinii) | Scholia in plutum (scholia vetera et fort. recentiora sub auctore Moschopulo) | Commentarium in nubes (scholia recentiora Tzetzae) | Scholia in vespas (scholia vetera, recentiora Tricliniana et Aldina) | Scholia in nubes (scholia vetera) | Scholia in pacem (scholia vetera et recentiora Triclinii) | Scholia in nubes (scholia recentiora Eustathii, Thomae Magistri et Triclinii)

author_bio: A collective body of ancient and medieval annotations on Aristotle's works, compiled by multiple unknown scholars over centuries.
author_notes: The author field represents the scholia corpus itself, not a person or group; classification as a work title.
representative_titles: Scholia in Aristotelis ethica Nicomachea (scholia vetera et recentiora) (e cod. Paris. gr. 1854) | Scholia in Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos (scholia recentiora) (e cod. Vat. Urb. gr. 35) | Scholia in Aristotelis sophisticos elenchos (scholia recentiora)

author_bio: The Scholia in Callimachum is a corpus of ancient commentaries and notes on the works of the poet Callimachus. These scholia, preserved in papyri and medieval manuscripts, represent a tradition of philological scholarship from the Hellenistic and Roman periods.
author_notes: This is not an individual author but a collection of scholia attributed to multiple scholars; it functions as a work title in the author slot.

author_bio: A collection of scholia (marginal annotations) on the works of Clement of Alexandria, partially attributed to Arethas of Caesarea. Illuminates textual reception in Byzantium.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the scholia. The attribution to Arethas is partial and uncertain.
representative_titles: Scholia in protrepticum et paedagogum (scholia recentiora partim sub auctore Aretha)

author_date_range: compiled 9th–14th c. CE, with earlier scholiastic layers
author_bio: A collection of ancient and medieval scholia (marginal annotations) on the speeches of Demosthenes. The corpus includes scholia vetera attributed in part to Ulpianus of Emesa (4th c. CE) and later additions by Byzantine commentators. This anonymous compilation serves as a key resource for Demosthenic studies.
author_notes: The author field reflects the title of the work; the compilation is a collective scholarly product. The attribution to Ulpianus (mentioned in parentheses) is traditional and uncertain.
representative_titles: Scholia in Demosthenem (scholia vetera) (fort. auctore Ulpiano)

author_bio: This corpus consists of scholia (annotations) on the work of Dionysius of Byzantium, a Byzantine geographical text. The scholia were compiled by anonymous commentators over several centuries.
author_notes: The author slot is filled by the title of the work itself, as the text is a composite of anonymous scholarly annotations.
representative_titles: Scholia in Dionysii Byzantii per Bosporum navigationem (scholia recentiora)

author_bio: The 'Scholia in Dionysium Periegetam' is a corpus of scholia (annotations) on the 'Periegesis of the Known World' by Dionysius Periegetes. These scholia were compiled over time, often attributed to Demetrius of Lampsacus in older scholarship, but are now considered anonymous. They provide grammatical, historical, and geographical commentary.
author_notes: This is a work title occupying the author slot; the scholia are a compilation, not a single author. The olim attribution to Demetrius Lampsacenus is rejected in current scholarship.
representative_titles: Scholia in Dionysii periegetae orbis descriptionem (scholia vetera) (olim sub auctore Demetrio Lampsaceno) | Scholia in Dionysii periegetae orbis descriptionem (scholia vetera)

author_bio: Scholia or marginal annotations on the Arithmetica of Diophantus, composed by anonymous Byzantine scholars. These scholia reflect later mathematical commentary and are attributed as a corpus.
author_notes: The author slot represents the scholastic corpus, not an individual writer.
representative_titles: Scholia in Diophanti arithmetica (scholia recentiora e codd. Matr. Bibl. Nat. 4678; Vat. gr. 191 et 304)

author_bio: A collection of ancient and medieval scholia (explanatory notes) on the works of Euclid, compiled over centuries by various commentators.
author_notes: The entry represents the corpus of scholia on Euclid, not a single author; it is cataloged as a textual corpus taking the author slot.

author_bio: The Scholia in Euripidem comprise a corpus of marginal annotations, glosses, and critical notes on the plays of Euripides, compiled by various anonymous scholars from the Hellenistic period through the Byzantine era. The corpus includes both vetera and select later scholia.
author_notes: This entry represents a collective textual corpus conventionally attributed to 'Scholia in Euripidem' in TLG; it is not an individual author but a work title.
representative_titles: Scholia in Euripidem (scholia vetera) | Scholia in Euripidem (cod. Hierosolymitanus patriarchalis 36) | Glossae in Euripidis Troiades (scholia vetera) | Glossae codicis Vaticani 909 in Euripidis Rhesum (scholia vetera) | Glossae codicis C in Euripidis Rhesum (scholia vetera)

author_cultural_context: Greek scholiastic tradition on Hesiod's Works and Days and Theogony
author_bio: A substantial collection of ancient and medieval commentaries, glosses, and notes on Hesiod's major poems, produced by a succession of scholars including Proclus, Moschopulus, Tzetzes, and others.
author_notes: The author field is a modern label for a composite scholiastic corpus; individual named contributors are present, but the corpus is treated as a single editorial entity.
representative_titles: Scholia in opera et dies (scholia vetera partim Procli et recentiora partim Moschopuli, Tzetzae et Joannis Galeni | Scholia in opera et dies (scholia vetera) | Scholia in theogoniam (scholia vetera) | Glossae in theogoniam

author_bio: The corpus of ancient and medieval scholia (marginal commentaries) on the Iliad and Odyssey, representing a layered tradition of exegesis from the Alexandrian scholars through to the Byzantine period.
author_notes: The author heading is a collective corpus title. The scholia are a compilation by multiple anonymous commentators.
representative_titles: Scholia in Iliadem (scholia vetera) | Scholia in Odysseam (scholia vetera) | Scholia in Iliadem (scholia vetera) (= D scholia) | Scholia in Iliadem (scholia vetera et recentiora e cod. Genevensi gr. 44) | Scholia in Iliadem (scholia recentiora Theodori Meliteniotis) (e cod. Genevensi gr. 44) | Scholia in Odysseam 1.1-309 (scholia vetera) | Hypotheses in Iliadem (e D scholiis) | Inscriptiones et hypothesis in Odysseam

author_bio: A collection of scholia (ancient and medieval notes) on Iamblichus' commentary on Nicomachus' Introduction to Arithmetic, compiled from various sources.
author_notes: This is a modern title for a corpus of anonymous scholia; no personal author exists.
representative_titles: Scholia in librum Iamblichi in Nicomachi arithmeticam introductionem (scholia vetera et recentiora)

author_bio: A collection of ancient and Byzantine scholia (marginal annotations) on the works of the Attic orator Isocrates. The scholia preserve exegetical and grammatical notes from earlier commentators.
author_notes: The source label refers to a corpus of scholia, not to a named author. Hence classified as work_title.

author_bio: A collection of ancient and Byzantine scholia (commentaries) on the works of Lucian of Samosata, including the so-called scholia vetera and the later scholia by Arethas of Caesarea. These marginal annotations preserve valuable interpretive and textual traditions.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by a named textual corpus; the works are scholia, not an individual author. The source label 'Scholia In Lucianum' is a modern catalog heading.
representative_titles: Scholia in Lucianum (scholia vetera et recentiora Arethae) | Scholia in Pseudo-Luciani Halcyonem (scholia vetera)

author_bio: A substantial corpus of ancient and Byzantine scholia on Lycophron's Alexandra, including contributions from Isaac and John Tzetzes, reflecting cumulative exegetical traditions.
author_notes: The author display name refers to the collection of scholia, not a person. Partially attributed to known Byzantine scholars but recorded under the work title.
representative_titles: Scholia in Lycophronem (scholia vetera et recentiora partim Isaac et Joannis Tzetzae)

author_bio: This entry represents a collection of scholia (marginal annotations) on works of Maximus the Confessor, compiled by anonymous Byzantine scholars. The text is a corpus of exegetical commentary.
author_notes: Heading is a textual corpus, not a person; use author_agent_kind=work_title.

author_bio: A corpus of annotations and glosses on the poems of Nicander, compiled over several centuries by anonymous scholiasts.
author_notes: Title used as author entry; not a person but a collection of scholia.
representative_titles: Scholia et glossae in Nicandri theriaca (scholia vetera et recentiora) | Scholia et glossae in Nicandri alexipharmaca (scholia vetera et recentiora)

author_bio: The scholia to Oppian are annotations and glosses on the Halieutica and Cynegetica of Oppian. They represent a collective tradition of scholarly commentary, not an individual author.
author_notes: The display name is a work title: 'Scholia In Oppianum'. This is a corpus of exegetical material, not an author.
representative_titles: Scholia et glossae in halieutica (scholia vetera et recentiora) | Scholia et glossae in cynegetica (scholia vetera et recentiora)

author_bio: Scholia in Pausaniam is a collection of ancient and Byzantine commentaries on Pausanias' Description of Greece. The attribution to Agathias is uncertain and likely represents a later ascription in a manuscript.
author_notes: The scholia are transmitted under the name Agathias but this is considered spurious; the work is treated as an anonymous scholion.
representative_titles: Scholia in Pausaniae periegesin (scholia vetera sub auctore Agathia)

author_cultural_context: Greek scholiastic tradition on Pindar's odes
author_bio: A large and layered corpus of exegetical notes, glosses, and commentaries on Pindar's epinician and other poems, compiled over many centuries by multiple anonymous and named scholars.
author_notes: The author field indicates a corpus of scholia; individual contributors include Didymus, Aristarchus, and later Byzantine scholars, but the corpus is treated as a collective work.
representative_titles: Scholia in Pindarum (scholia vetera) | Scholia et glossae in Olympia et Pythia (scholia recentiora Triclinii, Thomae Magistri, Moschopuli, Germani) (col | Scholia in Pindarum (scholia vetera et recentiora partim Thomae Magistri et Alexandri Phortii) (e cod. Patm.) | Scholia in Pythia v-vii (scholia vetera et recentiora Thomae Magistri et Triclinii) (e cod. Flor.) | Scholia in Nemea et Isthmia (scholia vetera et recentiora partim Thomae Magistri et Triclinii) | Capitula ad praefationem scholiorum ad Pindari carmina | Scholia in paeanes (scholia vetera) (P. Oxy. 5.841)

author_bio: The Scholia in Platonem are ancient and medieval annotations on the dialogues of Plato. They include the scholia vetera from late antiquity and the Byzantine scholia of Arethas, forming a major source for Platonic textual criticism.
author_notes: This is a collective corpus of scholia, not an individual author. The entry represents the entire body of scholia.
representative_titles: Scholia in Platonem (scholia vetera) | Scholia in Platonem (scholia recentiora Arethae)

author_bio: This entry represents the corpus of scholia and glosses on the plays of Sophocles, compiled and annotated by multiple anonymous commentators over several centuries.
author_notes: Catalog entry for a named textual corpus; author slot attributed to the work itself.
representative_titles: Scholia in Sophoclem (scholia vetera) | Scholia in Sophoclis Oedipum tyrannum (scholia recentiora) | Scholia in Sophoclis Ajacem (scholia vetera) | Scholia et glossae in Sophoclis Ajacem (glossae et scholia recentiora) | Scholia in Sophoclis Oedipum Coloneum (scholia vetera) | Argumenta et excerpta interpretationum allegoricarum in Sophoclis Ajacem

author_bio: The scholia vetera on Theocritus are a collection of ancient and medieval annotations, explanations, and critical notes on the Idylls. Compiled over centuries, they represent a composite scholarly tradition rather than a single author.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title 'Scholia In Theocritum', representing a collective scholarly work. The scholia are attributed to various commentators including Theon, but the compiled corpus is anonymous.

author_bio: A corpus of scholia (commentary notes) on the progymnasmata of Aelius Theon, the Greek rhetorician. The scholia represent later pedagogical annotations, likely compiled in the Byzantine period.
author_notes: This is a collective commentary tradition, not a personal author; cataloged as a work title occupying the author slot.

author_bio: A corpus of ancient and medieval annotations on the text of Thucydides, compiled over centuries by various scholars. The scholia include textual criticism, historical notes, and grammatical explanations.
author_notes: The label 'Scholia in Thucydidem' is a conventional title for a collective body of commentary, not an individual author. The corpus includes scholia vetera and recentiora.
representative_titles: Scholia in Thucydidem (scholia vetera et recentiora)

author_bio: A collection of ancient and medieval annotations on the works of Xenophon, preserving exegetical and textual observations from multiple hands.
author_notes: The author display name is a title referring to the corpus of scholia, not to a person. Standard catalog heading for scholia corpora.

author_bio: Sciras is a shadowy figure in early Greek poetry, sometimes linked to the genre of elegy or archaic epic. His existence and works are known only through fragments and later testimony.
author_notes: The historical reality of Sciras is uncertain; ancient testimonia are scarce and contradictory. Some sources treat him as a semi-legendary poet.

author_bio: Sclerias is a scarcely known figure, possibly a poet or prose writer, whose existence is inferred from a handful of fragments. Nothing certain is known about his life or works.
author_notes: The name Sclerias appears in fragmentary sources and may alternatively be a misreading or a variant of another name. The attribution is highly uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Greek lyric poetry; school or sympotic songs; acrostic tradition
author_bio: The 'Scolia Alphabetica' is a modern editorial title for a corpus of Greek alphabetic acrostic songs (scolia) transmitted on papyri. These are anonymous sympotic texts, arranged alphabetically, used in educational or convivial settings. The author slot here represents a collection title, not any individual writer. The surviving fragments are from P. Oxy. 1795 and P. Oxy. 15.
author_notes: This is a work-title entry: the designation 'Scolia Alphabetica' is a modern editorial construct. No author is known; the texts are fragmentary and anonymous.

author_bio: A conventional title for a Byzantine historical text on Emperor Leo V the Armenian, preserved in two manuscripts; the author's name is not preserved.
author_notes: The agent heading is a descriptive title for an anonymous historical work; actual author unknown.
representative_titles: De Leone Armenio (e cod. Paris. gr. 1711) | De Leone Armenio (e cod. Vat. gr. 2014)

author_date_range: 6th-5th century BCE (traditional) / text ca. 4th century BCE
author_bio: Traditionally a 6th-5th c. BCE explorer from Caryanda, credited with a periplus of the Mediterranean and Black Sea. The surviving Periplus Scylacis is a later (4th c. BCE) compilation attributed to him.
author_notes: The historical Scylax is shadowy; the Periplus is a composite work. The author slot gathers testimonia and fragments under the traditional name.

author_bio: The anonymous continuator of John Scylitzes' chronicle, extending the narrative into the Komnenian period.
author_notes: The label designates a textual continuation, not a single scribe; authorship is composite.

author_period: Late Hellenistic or early Imperial period
author_bio: Scythinus was a Greek epigrammatist, known from a single surviving poem in the Greek Anthology. His work reflects the learned and allusive style of the later epigram tradition, often dealing with mythological themes.
author_notes: The 'Epigr.' tag indicates his genre. Only one epigram (AP 9.516) is securely attributed; others may be lost. The name might be a pseudonym.

author_bio: Scythinus of Teos was a Hellenistic poet and philosopher, known for works blending poetry with philosophical themes, possibly linked to the Peripatetic school.
author_notes: Fragments and testimonia survive; his identification as a philosopher-poet is plausible but not certain.

author_bio: Seleucus was a Hellenistic Greek poet known only from a single fragment of a work titled Ἱλαρὰ ᾄσματα (Cheerful Songs). Nothing further is securely attested about his life or poetic circle.
author_notes: Attested by a single fragment of 17 words; author identity is uncertain and may be confused with other homonymous figures.

author_bio: Semonides of Amorgos was an archaic Greek iambic poet, best known for his satirical poem on women and for his role in the development of the iambic genre alongside Archilochus.
author_notes: Biographical details are sparse and partly reconstructed from later sources. The extant corpus comprises fragments and testimonia. His work survives mainly through quotations by later authors.

author_bio: A shadowy figure, possibly a local historian or antiquarian from Delos, known only from a handful of fragments preserved by later authors such as Athenaeus. He wrote on Delian antiquities.
author_notes: The identity of this Semus is uncertain; he may be the same as Semus of Delos, but the source is ambiguous. Attribution of the fragments is speculative.

author_bio: A group designation for several Alexandrian scholars or critics (possibly from the Library of Alexandria) who produced scholarly comments on classical texts.
author_notes: Collective label for a group of scholars; not an individual.

author_cultural_context: Early Christian tradition and oral teaching
author_bio: 'Seniores Apud Irenaeum' refers to unnamed early Christian elders (presbyters) whose traditions are quoted by Irenaeus of Lyons. They represent a collective oral source for early theological and exegetical material.
author_notes: This is a modern scholarly label for the anonymous traditions cited by Irenaeus. The agent is not a single person but a collective group.

author_bio: A collection of moral maxims attributed to the Pythagorean school, perhaps compiled by Demophilus. The text is part of the corpus of Pythagorean pseudepigrapha and widely cited in later Greek anthologies.
