author_notes: The author slot is used for the collection title; attribution to Demophilus is conjectural.
representative_titles: Sententiae Pythagoreorum (fort. auctore vel collectore Demophilo) | Sententiae Pythagoreorum

author_bio: A collection of moral sayings attributed to Sextus, likely of Pythagorean or Christian provenance. The work itself functions as the author entry in the source.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work; the actual author(s) are unknown. The attribution to 'Sextus' is traditional and may be pseudonymous.

author_bio: A canonical group of seven early Greek sages (including Thales, Solon, and others). Sayings and anecdotes attributed to them circulated widely and were collected in various anthologies.
author_notes: The heading 'Septem Sapientes' denotes a collective attribution for a body of wisdom literature. Individual sages are historical or semi-legendary, but the grouping as an author is conventional.
representative_titles: Apophthegmata (ap. auctores diversos) | Apophthegmata (ex collectione Demetrii Phalerei) (ap. Stobaeum) | Sententiae | Testimonia | Praecepta (sub auctore Sosiade) (ap. Stobaeum)

author_bio: Serapion was an ecclesiastical writer known from fragmentary sources, possibly associated with the monastic or theological circles of late antique Egypt. The surviving texts are brief and do not permit a full reconstruction of his work.
author_notes: The label 'Scr. Eccl.' identifies Serapion as an ecclesiastical writer; only a short fragment survives, and distinguishing him from other Serapions is difficult.

author_bio: A tragic poet known only from a single fragment. Nothing certain survives about his life or date; the attribution rests on a citation in later sources.
author_notes: The label '(Trag.)' indicates the genre; the author may be a historical person but is virtually unattested.

author_bio: Serenus was a Greek mathematician known for works on conic sections: De sectione coni and De sectione cylindri. He likely lived in Roman Egypt.
author_notes: Mathematical authorship is secure; geographical context is inferred.
representative_titles: De sectione coni | De sectione cylindri

author_bio: Severianus was a Christian exegete, possibly a bishop or presbyter, whose works survive mostly in catenae fragments and under other authors' names (e.g., John Chrysostom).
author_notes: Many works attributed to Severianus are spurious or misattributed in the manuscript tradition.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in epistulam i ad Corinthios (in catenis) | In Job (sermones 1-4) (olim sub auctore Joanne Chrysostomo) | De caeco nato (olim sub auctore Joanne Chrysostomo) | De caeco et Zacchaeo (olim sub auctore Joanne Chrysostomo) | Fragmenta in epistulam ii ad Corinthios (in catenis) | In illud: Quando ipsi subiciet omnia (olim sub auctore Joanne Chrysostomo) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Colossenses (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis)

author_bio: Severus was a Byzantine physician (iatrosophista) known for a treatise on clysters. The work is addressed to a certain Timotheus.
author_notes: Disambiguated by 'Iatrosophista Med.' Identified as a medical author from late antiquity.
representative_titles: De instrumentis infusoriis seu clysteribus ad Timotheum

author_bio: Severus, a Platonist philosopher of unknown exact dates, is known only through a single fragment from a work On the Soul (Περὶ ψυχῆς). He appears to belong to the tradition of Middle Platonism.
author_notes: The designation '(Phil.)' indicates attribution to a philosopher; limited evidence makes precise dating difficult.

author_bio: A Greek sophist known for a collection of rhetorical exercises (narrationes and ethopoeiae), typical of late antique school training in composition.
author_notes: Severus is a shadowy figure; the works survive in a few manuscripts and are often grouped with other late sophists.

author_cultural_context: Christian chronography and encyclopedic writing; early Christian Greek literature
author_bio: Christian chronographer and historian, author of the Cesti (encyclopedic miscellany) and letters. Known for his work on chronology and his correspondence with Origen.
author_notes: Securely identified as Sextus Julius Africanus; historical figure with a clear corpus of fragments and some extant letters.
representative_titles: Cesti (fragmenta) | Epistula ad Aristidem | Epistula ad Origenem

author_cultural_context: Pyrrhonian Skepticism; Greek medicine and philosophy
author_bio: Sextus Empiricus is the most extensive surviving source for Pyrrhonian Skepticism. His two major works, 'Outlines of Pyrrhonism' and 'Against the Mathematicians', systematically articulate skeptical arguments across all fields of knowledge.
author_notes: Sextus Empiricus is a well-attested historical figure. The corpus is substantial and foundational for the study of ancient Skepticism.

author_bio: Silenus was a Hellenistic historian from Sicily, known as a source for later authors such as Diodorus Siculus. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: The source label 'Silenus (Hist.)' distinguishes this historian from mythical figures of the same name; the fragments are few and brief.

author_bio: Silenus (Trag.) is a name appearing in the TLG corpus attributed to a possible tragic poet, perhaps a confused reference or misattribution. Only a title survives, with no extant fragments beyond the lemma.
author_notes: The designation '(Trag.)' indicates tradition as a tragedian; but the evidence is minimal. Possibly a doublet or error with the satyr Silenus.

author_cultural_context: Hellenistic poet and grammarian; associated with the literary circle of the Museum at Alexandria.
author_bio: Simias of Rhodes was a Hellenistic poet and grammarian known for his experimental poems, including the famous 'technopaegnia' in the shape of an egg and an axe. He also wrote epigrams and grammatical works.
author_notes: The identity is well-established, though the canonical name may be confused with other persons named Simias.

author_bio: Simon of Athens was a Greek author known for treatises on horses, including 'De forma et delectu equorum'. His work survives in fragments and influenced later hippiatric writings.
author_notes: Specific identity uncertain; sometimes identified with Simon the Hippiatrist; fragments from technical tradition.
representative_titles: De forma et delectu equorum | Fragmenta

author_bio: An obscure epic poet, possibly earlier than the well-known Simonides of Ceos. Only a few inscribed tituli survive.
author_notes: The suffix '(Epic.)' distinguishes this Simonides from the famous lyric poet. The evidence is minimal and the attribution uncertain.

author_bio: Simonides of Ceos was a lyric and elegiac poet, known for his choral poetry and epigrams. He was a major figure of the Archaic and early Classical periods.
author_notes: Identifier 'tlg0261' includes '(Lyr.)' to distinguish him from other Simonides. This is the famous lyric poet and not the historian or other homonyms.

author_region: Cilicia, then Athens, then Sassanid Persia
author_bio: Simplicius of Cilicia was a leading Neoplatonist philosopher and commentator on Aristotle. He studied under Ammonius in Alexandria and later in Athens before the closure of the Academy. His commentaries on Aristotle's *Physics*, *De Caelo*, *Categories*, and *De Anima* are among the most important sources for ancient philosophy. The *De Anima* commentary may be by Priscian of Lydia.
author_notes: The note 'Sp. (fort. auctore Prisciano Lydo)' on the *De Anima* commentary indicates a debated attribution. Main corpus is securely Simplicius.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis physicorum libros commentaria | In Aristotelis quattuor libros de caelo commentaria | In Aristotelis categorias commentarium | In Aristotelis libros de anima commentaria Sp.? (fort. auctore Prisciano Lydo) | Commentarius in Epicteti enchiridion | De caelo i (interpretatio Graeca ex Kc)

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek author (possibly a poet or grammarian)
author_bio: Simus is a name known only from testimonia, possibly a Greek poet or grammarian, but without any surviving works or secure biographical details.
author_notes: Only testimonia survive; no direct textual fragments.

author_bio: A comic poet known only from a single fragment. The name Simylus is otherwise unattested in Greek comedy, and the attribution remains uncertain.
author_notes: The source label 'Simylus (Comic.)' suggests a comic poet, but only 4 words survive across two fragments. The identification is tenuous and may conflate different individuals or be a misattribution.

author_bio: Simylus is a shadowy Hellenistic poet of elegiac verse, known only from a handful of fragments. His identity and dates are uncertain, and no biographical details survive.
author_notes: The label '(Eleg.)' disambiguates this author from other figures named Simylus. Attribution is fragmentary and derivative.

author_bio: Simylus is a likely Hellenistic philosopher or writer, known only from fragmentary citations. His identity remains obscure.
author_notes: (Phil.) may connect him with the Peripatetic or Middle Platonic tradition; attribution is uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Unknown (likely a name from an inscription or a brief subscript)
author_bio: Sminthes is known only from a single surviving word (a title or subscript) attached to a fragment. Nothing can be securely determined about the identity, period, or context of this figure; the name itself may be a personal name, a place‑name, or an epithet.
author_notes: The entry is based on a single word. There is insufficient evidence to classify the agent or the nature of the text. The historicity and identity are wholly uncertain.

author_bio: An obscure historian or mythographer from Argos known only from fragments. The identity and dates are uncertain; possibly the same as Socrates of Argos mentioned by Plutarch.
author_notes: Label 'Argivus' indicates origin; historical existence is plausible but poorly attested.

author_bio: A Hellenistic historian from Argos, known for a lost work on local Argive history cited by later grammarians.
author_notes: Distinguished from the philosopher Socrates by the parenthetical 'Hist.'; very few fragments survive.

author_bio: The famous Athenian philosopher, central figure in Platonic dialogues; known through the works of his students, though his own writings (if any) do not survive. The 'Phil.' label distinguishes him from the church historian of the same name.
author_notes: This entry likely collects fragments attributed to Socrates himself, though his thought is primarily known through Plato and Xenophon. The 'Phil.' label disambiguates from later figures.

author_bio: A lay Christian historian who wrote a continuation of Eusebius's Church History, covering the period from 305 to 439 CE, with a balanced perspective on ecclesiastical disputes.
author_notes: Commonly called Socrates Scholasticus; his work is a key source for 4th and 5th century church history.

author_bio: A collection of pseudepigraphical letters attributed to Socrates and his circle. These letters were composed to illustrate Socratic philosophy and were not written by the historical figures they name.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the collection; no single person authored the corpus.

author_bio: A collection of letters (Epistulae) attributed to Socrates and his circle. These are generally considered spurious, likely composed as rhetorical exercises or novels.
author_notes: The heading designates a corpus of letters, not a specific author; attribution to Socrates is pseudepigraphic.

author_bio: A shadowy Greek author known only from a single fragment; identity and date are not securely established.
author_notes: Single fragment (word count 13); no external attestation; author may be an otherwise unknown person or a misattribution.

author_bio: Sogenes is known only from an inscription, likely a dedicatory or sepulchral text. No further biographical details survive.
author_notes: Only known from a single word in a titulus; identity and historicity are unclear.

author_bio: Solon was an Athenian statesman, lawgiver, and poet. His surviving fragments include political and elegiac poetry.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments preserved in later sources.

author_bio: The *Epistulae Solonis* (Letters of Solon) are a collection of letters attributed to the legendary Athenian lawgiver Solon. They are widely regarded as later forgeries or rhetorical exercises, not authentic correspondence from the archaic period.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work. The letters are pseudepigraphic; the actual author(s) are unknown. The text is studied as part of ancient epistolary fiction.

author_bio: Sopater of Paphos was a Hellenistic comic playwright known for his work in 'Middle Comedy' or early 'New Comedy'. He is principally preserved only in fragments, with his surviving work consisting of a small number of quoted lines. His comedies often engaged with mythological burlesque and contemporary social satire.
author_notes: The label '(Comic.)' clarifies the genre and distinguishes from other bearers of the name. Attribution is secure but surviving fragments are minimal.

author_bio: A late antique rhetorician and commentator, active probably in the 4th or 5th century CE. He wrote works on rhetoric, including a division of rhetorical questions (Diairesis zetematon) and scholia on Hermogenes and Aristides.
author_notes: The identity of this Sopater is uncertain: he may be distinct from other homonymous figures (e.g., the Neoplatonist Sopater of Apamea). The corpus combines rhetorical and philosophical strands.
representative_titles: Διαίρεσις ζητημάτων | Scholia ad Hermogenis status seu artem rhetoricam | Prolegomena in Aristidem

author_display_name: Sopatri Et Marcellini Scholia Ad Hermogenis Status Syriani
author_canonical_name: Sopatri et Marcellini Scholia ad Hermogenis Status Syriani
author_bio: A composite commentary on Hermogenes' treatise on stases, attributed collectively to Sopater and Marcellinus, with Syriani material. Represents a scholarly tradition rather than a single author.
author_notes: The author field combines multiple named scholiasts; the attribution is a collective editorial construct.
representative_titles: Scholia ad Hermogenis librum PERÌ STÁSEWN

author_bio: Sophaenetus is a little-known Greek author, possibly a historian or military writer from the classical period, whose fragments suggest an account related to the Persian Wars or earlier Greek history.
author_notes: Only one work attested; identification uncertain; likely a minor historian known only from citations.

author_bio: Sophilus was a poet of Middle Comedy, known only from fragments and titles. His works survive in scanty remains, indicating a minor figure in the comic tradition.
author_notes: Known only from fragments; little biographical information survives. Source ID tlg0502.

author_bio: Sophocles the Younger was possibly a grandson of the great tragedian, and himself a playwright, but only a single fragment of his work survives.
author_notes: The identification as 'Junior' distinguishes him from the famous Sophocles; very little known.

author_bio: One of the three great Athenian tragedians, Sophocles wrote over 120 plays, of which seven survive complete. He is celebrated for his development of dramatic structure and his profound exploration of human fate, justice, and moral agency.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Trag.' distinguishes this entry as the tragedian from other homonymous authors in the corpus.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Oedipus Coloneus | Oedipus tyrannus | Philoctetes | Electra | Ajax | Antigone | Trachiniae

author_bio: Sophonias was a Byzantine scholar and commentator who produced a paraphrastic commentary on Aristotle's De anima. His work, typical of the Palaiologan Renaissance, renders Aristotle's text in accessible Greek with explanatory notes.
author_notes: Often identified as a monk or teacher; his exact identity remains debated, but the commentary is securely attributed.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis libros de anima paraphrasis

author_bio: Sophron of Syracuse was a writer of mimes (realistic dramatic scenes in prose), whose work influenced Plato and later Hellenistic literature. Only fragments remain.
author_notes: Well-known in antiquity; corpus largely lost.

author_bio: Sophronius of Jerusalem was a Byzantine monk and patriarch of Jerusalem. He is known for his theological writings, hagiographies, and liturgical poetry, and for his role in the defense of Chalcedonian Orthodoxy against Monothelitism.
author_notes: The work attributed here is a grammatical excerpt from Joannes Charax, likely preserving material from Sophronius's scholarly activity.
representative_titles: Excerpta ex Joannis Characis commentariis in Theodosii Alexandrini canones

author_bio: A Greek physician from Ephesus, active in Rome during the 1st–2nd centuries CE, renowned for his gynecological and medical treatises.
author_notes: Soranus is a well-attested medical writer; his works survive in substantial manuscripts.
representative_titles: Gynaeciorum libri iv | De fasciis | De signis fracturarum | Vita Hippocratis

author_bio: Sosibius was a Spartan historian and grammarian, known for works on Spartan customs and chronology. His fragments survive from later authors citing his research.
author_notes: Two works are listed: fragmenta and a papyrus fragment. The identity is uncertain but generally accepted as a Hellenistic scholar.

author_bio: A minor comic poet of New Comedy, known only from a few fragments preserved in Athenaeus and other sources; the name may appear in lists of comic victors, but biographical details are lacking.
author_notes: Distinguished from the historian Sosicrates (Peripatetic); fragments are very short.

author_bio: Hellenistic historian known from fragments; likely author of a work on philosophical successions (Διαδοχαί) used by Diogenes Laërtius. Possibly from Rhodes.
author_notes: Mostly lost; fragments preserved in later doxographic sources. The (Hist.) disambiguator indicates the historian Sosicrates distinct from the comic poet of the same name.

author_bio: Sosigenes is poorly known; only one testimonium (testimonium) survives. He may have been a scholar or writer active in the late Hellenistic period.
author_notes: The name occurs in a single testimonium; no further biographical or textual context is preserved.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek comic poet or author
author_bio: Sosipater is a poorly known figure from Greek comedy, possibly of the Hellenistic or Roman era, of whom only a fragment remains.
author_notes: Name known from a single fragment; historicity not secure.

author_bio: Sosiphanes is a poorly attested figure, possibly a poet of the Hellenistic period. Only a few fragments survive, and both his identity and oeuvre are uncertain.
author_notes: The name appears in fragmentary sources; no secure biographical information exists.

author_bio: Sositheus was a Hellenistic poet known for his tragic and dramatic works, of which only fragmentary evidence survives. He is associated with the Alexandrian literary tradition.
author_notes: Author identified only through fragments; little biographical information exists.

author_bio: Obscure Greek author known only from fragments. The name Sosthenes is attested for several individuals, but this corpus entry likely refers to a minor or unidentified writer.
author_notes: Ambiguous heading; could refer to multiple homonymous persons. Author agent kind set to person based on standard catalog practice, but historicity is uncertain due to lack of detail.

author_bio: Sostratus the grammarian is a figure known from fragments and an inscription. He likely worked on Homeric or other literary commentary, characteristic of Hellenistic philology.
author_notes: The epithet '(Gramm.)' specifies his field; the fragments and titulus give scanty but reliable attestation.

author_bio: Greek poet known only from a single titulus (inscription or epigram). No secure biographical information.
author_notes: Attested by a single seven-word work. The label 'Poeta' indicates an ancient not modern classification. Historicity uncertain.

author_bio: 'Sotadea' refers to a collection of fragmentary poems in the Sotadean metre, named after the Hellenistic poet Sotades. The corpus is a genre-based attribution rather than a named author, comprising verses preserved in quotations.
author_notes: The entry 'Sotadea' is a textual category, not a person. It collects anonymous fragments in Sotadean metre, akin to a generic corpus. The actual poet Sotades is a historical figure, but this corpus is assigned a separate author entry for cataloging convenience.

author_bio: Comic playwright of Middle or New Comedy known only from fragments; possibly distinct from Sotades the iambic poet.
author_notes: The label 'Comic.' distinguishes him from other Sotades figures; authorship attribution is fragmentary and uncertain.

author_bio: Sotades of Maroneia was an Alexandrian Greek poet active under Ptolemy II Philadelphus, known for composing ribald and satirical poems in the 'Sotadean' meter (a form of ionic verse). He was reportedly executed for lampooning the king. Only a few small fragments of his work survive, preserving his reputation for audacious social and political commentary.
author_notes: The label '(Iamb.)' identifies his iambic/satirical poetic genre. His historical existence and circumstances are reasonably certain, though fragmented.

author_bio: Sotion of Alexandria was a Hellenistic biographer and doxographer, known primarily for his 'Successions of the Philosophers', a lost work that traced the lineages of Greek philosophical schools. His work was a key source for later compilers like Diogenes Laertius.
author_notes: The author is known only from fragments and testimonia; exact dating is uncertain.

author_bio: Speusippus was a Greek philosopher and a nephew of Plato, whom he succeeded as head of the Platonic Academy (scholarch) from 348/7 to 339 BCE. He wrote on philosophy, mathematics, and ethics, but only fragments of his works survive. He is known for developing Platonic thought in a more systematic and Pythagorean direction.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments collected and studied in modern scholarship.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Epistula ad Philippum regem Sp. | Epistulae Sp. | Epigramma

author_bio: Sphaerus of Borysthenes was a Stoic philosopher and student of Zeno and Cleanthes, active in Alexandria and Sparta; known for political and ethical writings, now lost.
author_notes: Fragments only; minor Stoic figure.

author_bio: A shadowy figure to whom a tiny number of very short inscriptions or fragments are attributed. The name may be a variant spelling or a misattribution.
author_notes: Only 4 words survive; the identity is almost entirely obscure. It may refer to a sculptor, poet, or another type of author — no secure context.

author_bio: Staphylus is a fragmentarily preserved author, perhaps a historian or mythographer writing on the history of Cyprus or the Greek islands. He is known only from citations in later lexicographers and scholiasts.
author_notes: The name is known exclusively via sparse citations; his identity and works remain largely uncertain.

author_bio: Stephanus Byzantius, or Stephen of Byzantium, was a Greek grammarian and geographer of the 6th century CE, author of the 'Ethnica', a comprehensive geographical lexicon (now surviving only in an epitome), and an epigram.
author_notes: Well-attested Byzantine scholar. The 'Ethnica' survives only in a later epitome, but is a key source for ancient geography and toponyms. The epigram is an occasional piece.

author_bio: Stephanus of Alexandria (also known as Stephanus the Alchemist) was a late antique philosopher and teacher active in Alexandria, possibly under the reign of Heraclius. He is credited with alchemical works and commentaries, including 'On the Great and Sacred Art', a key text in Byzantine alchemy.
author_notes: Distinguished from other authors named Stephanus by the qualifier '(Alchem.)'. His identity is partially mingled with legendary alchemical figures; the attribution of the text may be pseudepigraphic.
representative_titles: De magna et sacra arte (sub nomine Stephani Alexandrini philosophi)

author_bio: Athenian comic poet of the Middle or New Comedy, known only from a few fragments preserved by Athenaeus and other sources.
author_notes: Very little biographical data; fragments are brief and often cited without context.

author_bio: Stephanus (Grammaticus) is a Byzantine commentator on rhetoric, known primarily for his commentary on Aristotle's Rhetoric. He may have been active in the late Byzantine period.
author_notes: The disambiguator 'Gramm.' distinguishes him from others named Stephanus; his exact dates and identity are uncertain.

author_bio: Stephanus of Athens (or Alexandria) was a physician and philosopher active in the 6th–7th centuries CE, known for commentaries on Hippocrates and Galen. He represents the late Alexandrian medical tradition with Platonic philosophical influences.
author_notes: The label 'Med. et Phil.' disambiguates from other Stephani. TLG identifier tlg0724; work count includes multiple scholia and commentaries.
representative_titles: Scholia in Hippocratis prognosticon | Commentarii in priorem Galeni librum therapeuticum ad Glauconem | Scholia in Hippocratis de fracturis | Collyrium ophthalmicum (olim sub auctore Stephano Archiatro)

author_bio: Stephanus (Med.) was a Byzantine physician and commentator best known for his commentary on Magnus Sophistes' work on urines. His writings reflect the medical tradition of late antiquity, blending Galenic theory with practical diagnostics.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Med.)' is a modern disambiguation to distinguish this Stephanus from other authors of the same name, such as Stephanus of Byzantium. The exact identity and dates are uncertain.
representative_titles: In Magni sophistae librum de urinis

author_bio: Stephanus was a Neoplatonic commentator on Aristotle, likely active in Alexandria. His commentary on Aristotle's De Interpretatione survives, reflecting teaching practices of the late Alexandrian school.
author_notes: Identity uncertain; possibly Stephanus of Alexandria; philosophical tradition.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis librum de interpretatione commentarium

author_bio: Stesichorus was a celebrated lyric poet from Himera in Sicily, known for his narrative choral poems on epic themes; ancient sources attribute a richly biographical yet partly legendary life, including the tale of his blinding and recovery.
author_notes: Widely cited in antiquity; surviving fragments are collected and assigned to named poems.

author_bio: A possible second Stesichorus, distinct from the famous lyric poet of Himera, but only known from a single-word title fragment. The existence of a second poet of this name is speculative.
author_notes: The label 'Stesichorus II' is a modern designation for a fragment possibly from a different poet. The evidence is extremely thin.

author_bio: A little-known author preserved only in a single fragment; perhaps a mythographer or poet.
author_notes: No external biographical references; the fragment is all that survives.

author_bio: Stesimbrotus of Thasos was a 5th-century BCE historian and rhapsode known for his work on Homer and his biographical/political writings about Athenian statesmen. His fragments offer a glimpse into early historical and literary criticism.
author_notes: Attested in ancient sources (e.g., Plutarch, Athenaeus) as a historical figure with a specific intellectual profile. The fragments are few but well-identified.

author_bio: A shadowy figure from the early Greek lyric tradition, Sthenelus is known only from two fragments, possibly from choral lyric or drama. He is often linked to the Sicilian or Italiote Greek literary milieu.
author_notes: The historicity of Sthenelus is uncertain; ancient sources are scant and contradictory. The fragments preserved are extremely brief.

author_bio: Sthenidas is a Greek author of whom only a fragment survives. His identity and dates are uncertain.
author_notes: Only a single fragment is extant; no biographical details are known.

author_bio: Strabo of Amaseia was a Greek geographer and historian whose monumental Geographica in 17 books surveys the known world of the Roman Empire. He also wrote a lost historical work. His text is a cornerstone of classical geography.
author_notes: Stable attribution; unique author with a well-preserved corpus.

author_bio: Straton was a Greek comic poet of the Middle or New Comedy period, known only from fragments. His works are cited by later lexicographers and indicate a minor but recognized role in the comic tradition.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Comic.)' distinguishes him from other authors named Straton; his corpus is fragmentary.

author_bio: Straton of Lampsacus, known as Straton the Physicist, was the third head of the Peripatetic school after Theophrastus. He was noted for his naturalistic explanations of physical phenomena and his rejection of teleology, influencing later Epicurean thought.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Phil.)' indicates a philosopher, distinguishing him from other figures named Straton. The surviving fragments are primarily from his physical and philosophical works.

author_bio: Stratonicus was a celebrated musician and kithara player, known for his wit and sharp tongue. He is mentioned in several ancient sources, but his own literary output is limited to a single fragment, possibly an epigram or musical notation.
author_notes: Stratonicus is better known as a historical figure than as an author; the fragment is of uncertain genre.

author_bio: Strattis was an Athenian comic poet active in the late 5th and early 4th centuries BCE. He wrote plays in the tradition of Old Comedy, with a particular penchant for mythological parody and political satire. Only fragments of his work survive.
author_notes: Strattis is known from fragments and testimonia. The five works attributed to him are all fragmentary, often cited in later lexicographers.

author_bio: The Suda is a massive Byzantine encyclopedic lexicon, compiled circa the 10th century. It contains historical, literary, and lexical entries from antiquity and the Byzantine period. Its compiler attribution is debated and likely collective, but the name functions as a work title.
author_notes: The name 'Suda' is treated as a work title in TLG; it is not a person nor a real collective authorship, despite traditional scholarly usage.

author_cultural_context: Early Greek comedy and iambic poetry
author_bio: Legendary figure credited as an early originator of Greek comedy in Megara and Athens. Only a few fragments survive, and his historical existence is uncertain.
author_notes: Attribution based on ancient testimonia; no securely contemporary sources. The name may be a literary invention or reflect a later tradition.

author_bio: Symeon Logothetes (also known as Symeon Magister) was a Byzantine chronicler active probably in the 10th century, author of a universal chronicle often attributed also to Leo Grammaticus or Theodosius Melissenus.
author_notes: The transmission of the Chronicon is complex, with multiple redactions and attributions; Symeon Logothetes is the hypothesized original author but his historicity is not entirely secure.
representative_titles: Chronicon (sub nomine Leonis Grammatici vel Theodosii Melisseni vel Julii Pollucis) (redactio A + B operis sub t

author_bio: Byzantine hagiographer and logothete; known for his systematic collection and rewriting (metaphrasis) of saints' lives active under the Macedonian dynasty.
author_notes: Major Byzantine hagiographer; his corpus is extensive, but this entry reflects a single work. Well attested historically.

author_bio: A pseudonymous alchemical author using the name of Synesius of Cyrene. The surviving text is a cosmological/alchemical treatise addressed to Dioscorus.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Alchem.)' distinguishes this alchemical writer from the philosopher Synesius of Cyrene. Attribution is uncertain; likely a later pseudonymous work.
representative_titles: Συνεσίου φιλοσόφου πρὸς Διόσκορον εἰς τὴν βίβλον Δημοκρίτου, ὡς

author_bio: A Greek philosopher, orator, and bishop from Cyrene, student of Hypatia, and later bishop of Ptolemais. His extensive writings, including letters, hymns, and treatises, blend Neoplatonic philosophy with Christian thought and offer insight into the intellectual and religious life of the late 4th–early 5th century.
author_notes: The label '(Phil.)' distinguishes Synesius from other bearers of the name; his works are well-attested and include both philosophical and rhetorical texts.
representative_titles: Epistulae | Aegyptii sive de providentia | Oratio de regno | Dion | De insomniis | Calvitii encomium | Hymni | Catastases

author_bio: Syrianus was a prominent Neoplatonic philosopher and commentator, head of the Platonic Academy in Athens before Proclus. His commentaries on Aristotle's Metaphysics and on Hermogenes treatises were influential in the later Neoplatonic curriculum, synthesizing earlier Platonic traditions with Aristotelian logic.
author_notes: Well‑attested historical figure, an important link in Neoplatonic transmission. Multiple substantial works survive. The spurious Praefatio is noted in representative titles as possibly by another author.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis metaphysica commentaria | Commentarium in Hermogenis librum PERÌ STÁSEWN | Commentarium in Hermogenis librum περὶ ἰδεῶν | Praefatio in Hermogenis librum περὶ ἰδεῶν [Sp.] (fort. auctore

author_bio: Tatianus was a Christian apologist and theologian of Syrian origin, a student of Justin Martyr, best known for his Oratio ad Graecos and the Diatesseron gospel harmony.
author_notes: Well-attested figure in early Christian literature; also associated with encratism.

author_bio: Reputed Pythagorean philosopher and son of Pythagoras, known from late biographical traditions. Only a single fragmentary text survives under his name, likely a pseudepigraphical composition.
author_notes: Telauges is a shadowy figure known only from doxographical references; the attributed text is of uncertain authenticity.

author_bio: Teleclides was an Athenian comic poet of the 5th century BCE, active in Old Comedy. Only fragments of his plays survive, offering glimpses of his satirical style.
author_notes: The name Teleclides is securely attested in ancient sources, though biographical details are scant. The fragmentary nature of the corpus limits assessment.

author_bio: Telegonia is a lost epic poem of the Greek Epic Cycle, attributed to Eugammon of Cyrene but surviving only in fragments. The title occupies the author slot as a placeholder for the anonymous textual tradition.
author_notes: The entry uses the poem's title as the author heading; the actual authorship is traditionally assigned to Eugammon, but the corpus labels it by work title.

author_bio: Telephus is a name associated with a Greek grammarian or lexicographer, possibly of the 2nd century CE, but the evidence is extremely limited. The surviving fragments are attributed to him in later sources, but his identity and work remain obscure.
author_notes: The attribution is uncertain; the name may refer to more than one figure. No secure biographical data exist.

author_bio: Teles (c. 235 BCE) was a Cynic philosopher whose surviving fragments, preserved mainly by Stobaeus, develop themes of self-sufficiency, exile, and the comparison of poverty and wealth. He is a key source for later Cynic and Stoic moral thought.
author_notes: The name Teles is securely attested as a historical Cynic, but only fragmentary evidence survives; works are transmitted primarily through Stobaeus and occasional papyri.
representative_titles: Περὶ αὐταρκείας | Περὶ φυγῆς | Περὶ συγκρίσεως πενίας καὶ πλούτου (ap. Stobaeum) | Περὶ ἀπαθείας | Περὶ τοῦ δοκεῖν καὶ τοῦ εἶναι | Περὶ τοῦ μὴ εἶναι τέλος ἡδονήν | Περὶ περιστάσεων

author_bio: Telesilla was a Greek lyric poetess from Argos, renowned for leading a defense of the city against Sparta, though only fragments of her poetry survive.
author_notes: Historical figure but with very fragmentary survival of works.

author_bio: Telestes was a Greek lyric poet and dithyrambist, active in the late Classical period. Known from fragmentary remains and an ancient titulus, he contributed to the development of the dithyramb.
author_notes: Identity as a dithyrambic poet is secure, but biographical details are sparse. The two works preserved are fragmentary.

author_bio: Terpander of Lesbos was a legendary poet and musician credited in antiquity with innovations in Greek music, including the seven-stringed lyre and the development of the nome. His historicity is uncertain, but he was a foundational figure in the Spartan musical tradition.
author_notes: The surviving fragments are extremely brief and their authenticity is debated. The attribution '(fort. auctore Terpandro)' in representative titles reflects uncertainty.
representative_titles: Fragmenta (fort. auctore Terpandro) | Fragmentum (fort. auctore Terpandro)

author_bio: A pseudepigraphical work containing the farewell speeches of the twelve sons of Jacob. The text functions as the author entry in the source.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title; the actual author(s) are unknown and the work is a composite of multiple traditions.

author_bio: Testamentum Abrahae is a pseudepigraphic apocalyptic text recounting Abraham's journey through heaven and his death, transmitted in multiple recensions and often attributed to a patriarchal or angelic speaker.
author_notes: The work is a title occupying the author slot; it is not ascribed to a historical author. Two recensions (A and B) are attested.
representative_titles: Testamentum Abrahae (recensio A) (olim partim sub auctore Hesychio | Testamentum Abrahae (recensio B)

author_bio: The Testament of Job is a Jewish pseudepigraphon, expanding the biblical story of Job. It survives in Greek manuscripts and is considered part of the Old Testament pseudepigrapha, with no single known author.
author_notes: Author field occupied by the work title; attribution is to the text itself, not a person or collective.

author_bio: The 'Testament of Solomon' is a pseudepigraphical Greek work, attributed to King Solomon, detailing his control over demons through a magical ring. It survives in multiple recensions and reflects syncretistic magical and religious traditions.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the title of the work itself, as no individual author is identifiable; multiple recensions exist.
representative_titles: Testamentum Salomonis (recensiones A et B) (mss. HILPQ) | Narratio de propheta et sapientissimo rege Salomone (cod. Monasterii sancti Saba 290) | Vita Salomonis (cod. 132 Monasterii sancti Dionysii in Monte Atho) | Testamentum Salomonis (recensio C) (mss. VWSTU) | Conspectus titulorum | Testamentum Salomonis (recensio C, I.1 3) (mss. VW) | Sigilla anuli Salomonis | Testamentum Salomonis (recensio A) (ms. L)

author_bio: The Testamentum of the Forty Martyrs is a hagiographical text recounting the martyrdom of forty Christian soldiers in Sebaste. The work is anonymous and transmitted under its titular label.
author_notes: The author field is filled by the title of the work; no known author attributed.

author_bio: Astrological writer whose fragments survive in later compilations; authorship likely pseudonymous under the name of the legendary Babylonian sage Teucer.
author_notes: TLG disambiguation: Astrol. marks the astrological corpus; historicity uncertain.
representative_titles: De duodecim signis (Rhetorii Aegyptii excerpta) (e cod. Berol. 173) | De sideribus consurgentibus (fragmenta) (e cod. Danensi 2)

author_bio: Teucer (historian) is a little-known Hellenistic historian, possibly from Cyprus, whose fragments survive via later citations.
author_notes: The label 'Hist.' distinguishes him from the mythical archer. Attestation is scanty.

author_bio: Thales of Miletus was a pre-Socratic philosopher, mathematician, and astronomer, traditionally regarded as the first philosopher in the Greek tradition. He is known for his work on geometry and natural philosophy, though his own writings survive only in fragments and testimonia.
author_notes: The name is securely historical, though many details of his life and thought are preserved in later doxographical sources.

author_bio: Thallus was a Greek historian or chronographer active in the Roman period, known from fragments cited by later Christian authors such as Eusebius. He may have written a world chronicle.
author_notes: Fragments are preserved in other authors; his identity and precise work remain uncertain. Source ID tlg1706.

author_bio: Hellenistic historian known only from a fragment; details of his life and work are lost.
author_notes: Only one fragment survives; uncertain identity.

author_bio: Theagenes, designated a philosopher, survives only in testimonia. His philosophical affiliation and dates are unknown, but the name may point to a minor figure in the Platonic or Neoplatonic tradition.
author_notes: Theagenes (Phil.) is attested solely through ancient citations; no work survives intact.

author_bio: Theages is a shadowy figure cited as a Pythagorean or Socratic author; only fragments survive, and his historicity is uncertain.
author_notes: Fragmentary; possibly a later fabrication or misattribution.

author_bio: Reputed Pythagorean philosopher and wife of Pythagoras, or possibly a later figure; surviving fragments deal with ethics and women's conduct.
author_notes: Historicity uncertain; multiple women named Theano appear in Pythagorean tradition. Attributed fragments may be pseudonymous or later compositions.

author_bio: Thearidas is a fragmentary Greek author of whom nothing certain is known beyond the preservation of a single brief text.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; identity and genre are impossible to ascertain.

author_bio: The 'Thebaïs' is a lost epic poem of the Theban cycle, attributed in antiquity to Homer or other early poets, but now treated as a text without a named author. The surviving fragments recount the war of the Seven against Thebes.
author_notes: The entry refers to the epic title, not a person; author slot uses work title.

author_bio: Themison is a poorly attested figure known only from a single surviving fragment. The name appears in classical sources, but his identity and corpus remain minimal, suggesting a minor or lost author.
author_notes: Only a brief fragment (34 words) survives under this name. Historical status is plausible but uncertain due to lack of biographical data.

author_bio: Themistius was a statesman, philosopher, and orator in the Roman Empire. He is celebrated for his paraphrases of Aristotle and for his political speeches, which illuminate the intellectual and political life of the 4th century.
author_notes: The corpus includes both genuine and spurious works, including the paraphrasis on Aristotle's Analytics and commentaries. Themistius was a key figure in the Neoplatonic tradition.
representative_titles: In Aristotelis physica paraphrasis | Quae fertur in Aristotelis analyticorum priorum librum i paraphrasis | In Aristotelis libros de anima paraphrasis | Analyticorum posteriorum paraphrasis | (Sophoniae) in parva naturalia commentarium | Βασανιστὴς ἢ φιλόσοφος | <Ὑπὲρ τοῦ λέγειν ἢ πῶς τῷ φιλοσόφῳ λεκτέον> | Ἐρωτικὸς ἢ περὶ κάλλους βασιλικοῦ

author_bio: The 'Epistles of Themistocles' is a collection of fictional letters attributed to the Athenian statesman Themistocles. Composed long after his death, these epistles form part of the Greek epistolary tradition, blending historical scenarios with rhetorical invention. They are valuable as sources of rhetorical education and political fiction in the Roman Imperial period.
author_notes: Attribution to the historical Themistocles is pseudepigraphical. The work is cataloged under the title because the true author is unknown. author_agent_kind set to 'work_title' as the text itself serves as the author entry.

author_bio: Theocles is a shadowy figure known only from a single fragment of *Ithyphalli* (a form of phallic hymn). Nothing else is securely attested about his life or authorship.
author_notes: Name appears only in fragmentary transmission; possibly legendary or erroneously attributed.

author_bio: Greek poet from Sicily, active in Alexandria under Ptolemy II. He established the bucolic genre with his Idylls, blending pastoral life with literary sophistication.
author_notes: The qualifier 'Bucol.' distinguishes him from the historian Theocritus; his authorship of the Syrinx is debated.

author_bio: A sophist named Theocritus, possibly of the Imperial period, known only from a brief fragment.
author_notes: Distinct from the bucolic poet Theocritus; the label 'Soph.' indicates a rhetorical context.

author_bio: Theodectas of Phaselis was a Greek rhetorician and tragic poet, a pupil of Isocrates. He wrote for the stage and compiled rhetorical handbooks; only fragments remain.
author_notes: Known primarily through ancient testimonia; fragments of his tragedies and rhetorical work survive.

author_bio: Bishop of Cyrrhus and influential theologian of the Antiochene school. His extensive corpus includes biblical commentaries, a church history, and apologetic works, making him a major patristic author.
author_notes: Theodoretus is a well-attested historical figure and a key author in Greek patristic literature.
representative_titles: Interpretatio in Psalmos | Interpretatio in xiv epistulas sancti Pauli | Commentaria in Isaiam | Interpretatio in Ezechielem | Interpretatio in xii prophetas minores | Quaestiones in Octateuchum | Historia ecclesiastica | Graecarum affectionum curatio

author_bio: Hellenistic epigrammatist from Syracuse, whose work appears in the Greek Anthology. His poetry shows the influence of Callimachus and is characterized by learned mythological references.
author_notes: The corpus includes epigrams and a few inscribed fragments. Possibly identified with Theodoridas of Syracuse mentioned in literary sources.

author_bio: Theodorides is a name known only from a few inscribed tituli, suggesting an individual who was commemorated in epigram or poetic dedication. No works beyond these brief inscriptions survive, leaving his identity and literary significance unclear.
author_notes: The source label 'Tituli' indicates that the record is based on inscriptions. The name Theodorides is not otherwise attested in literary sources.

author_bio: Theodorus of Colophon is a shadowy figure, known only from a single inscription (a titulus) that provides his name and city. No literary or philosophical works survive, and his exact contribution to Greek culture remains unknown.
author_notes: The only evidence for Theodorus is the inscription cited as 'Titulus' in the TLG record; no other testimonia are known.

author_bio: Theodore the Studite was a Byzantine monk, theologian, and abbot of the Monastery of Stoudios in Constantinople. A leading figure in the iconodule resistance during the Iconoclasm, he left a substantial corpus of letters, poems, and homilies.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure with extensive surviving works.
representative_titles: Epistulae | Iambi de variis argumentis | Homilia in nativitatem Mariae (olim sub auctore Joanne Damasceno)

author_bio: Theodorus is the name associated with a collection of Greek letters, possibly from late antiquity. The identity of the author is uncertain; the corpus may be pseudonymous or compiled.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Epist.)' indicates authorship of epistles. Authorial identity unclear; possibly a conventional attribution. Source ID tlg3158.

author_bio: Uncertain figure associated with fragments of a commentary on Matthew preserved in catenae. The double name suggests possible confusion with other Theodori.
author_notes: The label 'Heracleensis vel Theodorus' indicates editorial uncertainty: possibly a Theodorus of Heraclea, or a different Theodorus. Attribution is tentative.

author_bio: Theodorus of Cyrene was a Greek mathematician of the 4th century BCE, known for contributions to irrational numbers and mentioned by Plato; surviving only in testimonia.
author_notes: The 'Math.' disambiguator indicates mathematical works; the identity as Theodorus of Cyrene is inferred, but the source label remains ambiguous.

author_bio: Theodorus is a poet known only from a single inscribed title or epigram of very few words. His identity and period are entirely uncertain.
author_notes: The label 'Poeta' identifies his genre, but with only 2 words surviving, the attribution is tenuous and likely refers to a minor figure.

author_bio: Byzantine poet and writer active in the Komnenian period, known for occasional poetry and court rhetoric.
author_notes: Name 'Prodromus' may be a nickname; identification with Theodore Prodromus is consistent but not fully secure.
representative_titles: Epithalamium fortunatissimis caesaris filiis | Epitaphius in Theodoram nurum Bryennii

author_bio: Theodorus (the ecclesiastical writer) preserved fragments of exegetical works on the Gospels of John and Matthew, which survive in catenae. Little else is known of his life.
author_notes: The designation 'Scr. Eccl.' distinguishes this Theodorus from other homonyms; fragments only.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in Joannem (in catenis) | Fragmenta in Matthaeum (in catenis)

author_bio: Theodorus Scutariota was a Byzantine historian of the late Byzantine period. He authored Additamenta (additions) to the history of George Akropolites, covering events of the 13th century. His work provides valuable details on the Nicaean Empire and early Palaiologan period.
author_notes: The author's identity and corpus are known from manuscript attribution.

author_cultural_context: Christian theologian; known from exegetical fragments preserved in catenae.
author_bio: Theodorus (Theol.) is a source of biblical exegetical fragments preserved in Greek catenae on the Pauline epistles and the Gospel of Matthew. His identity is uncertain but reflects late antique Christian scholarship.
author_notes: The source label '(Theol.)' distinguishes this Theodorus from others; only fragments survive; dating and identity are approximate.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in epistulam ad Romanos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in Matthaeum (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam i ad Corinthios (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ad Hebraeos (in catenis) | Fragmenta in epistulam ii ad Corinthios (in catenis)

author_bio: A tragic poet known only from a few testimonia and fragments; the attribution to a specific Theodorus is uncertain.
author_notes: The identifier 'Trag.' indicates the genre, but the author's identity is unclear; only a few words survive.

author_bio: Theodosius was a Greek astronomer and mathematician from Bithynia. He authored works on spherical geometry, including the 'Sphaerica', which influenced later astronomy. His works survive and are significant for the history of mathematics.
author_notes: The identifier '(Astron. et Math.)' distinguishes this Theodosius from others. The corpus is substantial and reliably attributed.
representative_titles: Sphaerica | De diebus et noctibus | De habitationibus

author_bio: Byzantine deacon and poet, known for his poem On the Capture of Crete, recounting the Byzantine reconquest of the island in 961 CE. His work offers insight into 10th-century Byzantine historical poetry and court culture.
author_notes: The designation 'Diaconus Hist. et Poeta' indicates a didactic and historical poetic role; his single known work survives in one manuscript tradition.

author_bio: Theodosius (Grammaticus) is a Greek grammarian, often identified with Theodosius of Alexandria or a later Byzantine grammarian. His extant works include grammatical canons and treatises, some of which are spurious or attributed to other authors.
author_notes: Multiple works under this name; some may be by Theodorus Prodromus or different Theodosii.
representative_titles: Περὶ γραμματικῆς [Sp.] (fort. auctore Theodoro Prodromo) | Canones isagogici de flexione verborum | Canones isagogici de flexione nominum | De dialectis Sp. (fort. auctore Theodosio alio vel anonymo) | Περὶ κλίσεως τῶν εἰς <ων> βαρυτόνων (e cod. Haun. 1965), | Epitome catholicae Herodiani Sp. (fort. auctore quodam Theodosio Byzantio) | Περὶ κλίσεως τῶν εἰς <ων> ὀξυτόνων (e cod. Haun. 1965), | Περὶ τόνου [Sp.] (fort. auctore Theodoro Prodromo)

author_bio: Theodotus is a Greek author of whom only fragments survive; not securely identifiable with any known figure. Possibly a grammarian or poet.
author_notes: Name common in Greek antiquity; no distinguishing epithet or secure context. Source ID tlg1720.

author_bio: Theognetus is a fragmentary comic poet known from a few titles and sparse fragments. His exact date and location are unknown; he may belong to New Comedy or a later phase.
author_notes: The source label includes 'Fragmenta | Titulus', indicating both fragments and a title. He is cited in Athenaeus and other sources, but very little is known about his life.

author_bio: Theognis of Megara is a Greek elegiac poet of the Archaic period, whose corpus of gnomic and sympotic verse reflects aristocratic values and political turmoil. Theognidean poetry survives through a collection of couplets and fragments that blend personal reflection with social commentary.
author_notes: The label 'Eleg.' distinguishes him from other figures named Theognis; the corpus includes both authentic and later accretions.
representative_titles: Elegiae | Fragmenta dubia | Fragmenta sedis incertae | Epigrammata

author_bio: An otherwise obscure historian named Theognis, known only from a single fragment, possibly of a local history.
author_notes: Listed as 'Hist.' (historian) to distinguish from the elegiac poet Theognis; extant fragment is minimal.

author_bio: Theognis (Trag.) is a name appearing in ancient testimonia for a tragic poet, but almost nothing is known of his life or works; the single surviving fragment of two words provides no context.
author_notes: The designation (Trag.) distinguishes this figure from the elegiac poet Theognis. The historicity is uncertain; the name may be a ghost or a confusion.

author_bio: Theognostus was a Byzantine grammarian known for his Orthography (Canones), a didactic work on spelling and accentuation, important for the study of Greek.
author_notes: Sometimes confused with other Theognosti; the work is securely attributed to the 9th c. grammarian.

author_bio: Theolytus is a shadowy figure of early Greek poetry, mentioned by later sources as an early author of a poem on the birth of Apollo or similar mythological themes. His historicity is uncertain; he may be a legendary name attached to early hexameter tradition.
author_notes: Survives in a single fragment (21 words). Attestation is late and indirect. TLG identifier tlg1723.

author_bio: Theon of Alexandria was a Greek grammarian and commentator. He is known for scholia on Pindar and other works. The epithet 'Gramm.' distinguishes him from homonyms.
author_notes: Disambiguated by 'Gramm.' The fragments and the Pindar commentary survive in papyri.
representative_titles: Fragmenta | Commentarium de Pythiis xii Pindari (P. Oxy. 31.2536) | Testimonia

author_bio: Theon of Alexandria (c. 335–405 CE) was a Greek mathematician and astronomer, father of Hypatia. He wrote commentaries on Ptolemy's Almagest and the Handy Tables, and is noted for his editorial work on Euclid's Elements.
author_notes: The label 'Math.' disambiguates from other Theons. TLG identifier tlg2033; work count includes commentaries, testimonia, and epigrams.
representative_titles: Commentaria in Ptolemaei syntaxin mathematicam i-iv | Ὑπόμνημα εἰς τοὺς προχείρους Πτολεμαίου κανόνας | Εἰς τοὺς προχείρους κανόνας (commentarium parvum) | Testimonia | Epigrammata

author_bio: Theon (Phil.) is a philosopher, likely Theon of Smyrna, a Platonist and mathematician known for his work 'De utilitate mathematicae', which discusses the use of mathematics in philosophy and astronomy.
author_notes: The identifier '(Phil.)' points to a philosopher; Theon of Smyrna is the most likely candidate, but several homonyms exist.

author_bio: An otherwise unknown author named Theophanes, represented only by fragmentary texts. It is unclear whether this refers to a single figure or conflates multiple persons.
author_notes: Fragmentary entries; insufficient evidence to determine historicity or identity. Multiple homonymous Theophanes exist, making attribution uncertain.

author_bio: Theophanes Confessor was a Byzantine monk and chronicler, best known for his *Chronographia*, a world chronicle covering events from 284 to 813 CE. He is venerated as a saint in the Eastern Orthodox Church.
author_notes: The epithet 'Confessor' reflects his religious status; his work is a key source for early Byzantine history.

author_bio: Theophanes Continuatus is the conventional Latin title given to a Greek chronicle that continues the work of Theophanes Confessor, covering events from 813 to 961 CE. Its authorship is collective and official.
author_notes: The label 'Theophanes Continuatus' refers to the text itself, not a single author; it is a work title used as an author heading in catalogues.

author_bio: Theophilus was the bishop of Antioch and a prominent Christian apologist of the later 2nd century CE. His only surviving work, the 'Ad Autolycum', defends Christianity against pagan criticisms.
author_notes: The label 'Apol.' distinguishes this Theophilus from others. The authorship of 'Ad Autolycum' is secure and well-attested.

author_bio: Theophilus (Comic.) is a comic playwright known only from fragments. The precise date and identity are uncertain, but he is considered a minor figure of Greek comedy.
author_notes: Label '(Comic.)' indicates a comic poet; only fragments survive.

author_bio: Theophilus is a shadowy historical figure known from a single fragmentary reference. The abbreviation 'Hist.' suggests a historian, but no securely attributable works survive.
author_notes: The label 'Hist.' may denote a historian, but the source is too fragmentary to confirm an identity.

author_bio: Theophilus Protospatharius was a Byzantine physician and medical writer, known for his treatise on fevers. The double name indicates a combined attribution with Stephanus, possibly a collaborator or later redactor.
author_notes: The attribution 'Protospatharius et Stephanus' suggests multiple authorship or a composite tradition.

author_bio: Theophilus Protospatharius was a Byzantine physician and medical writer, author of a major anatomical treatise *De corporis humani fabrica* and works on pulse, urine, and excrement. His title 'Protospatharius' indicates a high court office.
author_notes: The attribution is secure; the corpus includes several medical texts.
representative_titles: De corporis humani fabrica libri quinque | De pulsibus | De urinis | De excrementis

author_bio: This entry represents a collaborative or compilative work attributed to multiple figures, including Theophilus Protospatharius, Damascius, and Stephanus, all likely medical commentators from the late antique/Byzantine period. The commentary on Hippocratic aphorisms reflects a tradition of scholarly synthesis.
author_notes: The compound author label groups three names, suggesting a collective or composite authorship rather than a single person. The attribution is traditional and may reflect a compilation by later Byzantine editors.

author_bio: Theophilus, an ecclesiastical writer, authored fragments on the Gospels of Matthew and John preserved in catenae.
author_notes: The 'Scr. Eccl.' denotes ecclesiastical writer; fragments only.
representative_titles: Fragmenta in Matthaeum (in catenis) | Fragmenta in Joannem (in catenis)

author_bio: The figure known as 'Theophrastus the Alchemist and Poet' wrote a didactic poem on the divine art of alchemy, falsely attributed to the famous Peripatetic philosopher Theophrastus. The work is a product of late antique alchemical literature, using the authority of a classical name.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Alchem. et Poeta)' disambiguates from Theophrastus of Eresus. The attribution is pseudonymous; the actual author is unknown. The work title is given in Greek.
representative_titles: Θεοφράστου φιλοσόφου περὶ τῆς θείας τέχνης διὰ στίχων ἰάμβων

author_bio: Theophrastus was a Greek philosopher and naturalist, a student of Aristotle and his successor as head of the Lyceum. He authored foundational works in botany (Historia plantarum) and philosophy, and his Characters influenced later literature. His scientific and philosophical writings survive in substantial fragments.
author_notes: The '(Phil.)' disambiguator indicates the philosopher (as opposed to other Theophrastus entries). Identified as a historical person with firm biographical evidence.
representative_titles: Historia plantarum | De causis plantarum (lib. 2-6) | Fragmenta | De causis plantarum (lib. 1) | De sensu et sensibilibus (= fr. 1, Wimmer) | Characteres | De igne (= fr. 3, Wimmer) | Physicorum opiniones

author_bio: Theophylactus Simocatta was a Byzantine historian and author of a history of the reign of Emperor Maurice (582–602). He also wrote a collection of fictional letters and a physical treatise. His works are important for late Roman and early Byzantine history.
author_notes: Well-documented figure; his career and authorship are securely established.
representative_titles: Historiae | Epistulae | Quaestiones physicae | De vitae termino

author_bio: Theopompus was an Athenian comic poet of the Old and early Middle Comedy, active in the late 5th and early 4th centuries BCE. He was a contemporary of Aristophanes and known for his inventive plots and social satire.
author_notes: The '(Comic.)' disambiguates this Theopompus from the historian Theopompus of Chios. Fragments and titles of several comedies survive, offering insights into the development of Greek comedy.

author_bio: Theopompus (Epic.) is a shadowy figure known only from a few fragments of epic poetry. The label distinguishes him from the historian Theopompus, but his historicity is uncertain and he may be a legendary name.
author_notes: The identifier 'Epic.' distinguishes this figure from the historian Theopompus; very little is known.

author_bio: A major Greek historian and rhetorician, pupil of Isocrates, known for his Philippica and Hellenica; his works survive only in fragments, but he was a critical influence on later historiography.
author_notes: Standard TLG identifier; fragments and testimonia extensively studied.

author_bio: Theotimus is known from a small set of fragmentary works. The name suggests a possible philosophical or literary context, but no secure historical or biographical details survive.
author_notes: Only fragmentary evidence; the name is relatively common in Greek literature, but this specific figure cannot be securely identified with any known historical person.

author_bio: Theseus is the legendary king of Athens, celebrated for his exploits including the slaying of the Minotaur and the unification of Attica. Fragments attributed to him likely derive from lost epic or tragic sources.
author_notes: Attribution to Theseus as author is clearly legendary/spurious; the work likely belongs to a later mythographer. The authority is of interest for the reception of myth.

author_bio: Ancient Greek dramatic poet credited as the first actor in Greek tragedy. His fragmentary works survive only in quotations and testimonia.
author_notes: TLG author entry: Thespis is a legendary figure in the history of Greek drama; surviving fragments are few and mostly dubious.

author_bio: Thessalus is a shadowy figure associated with a collection of herbal works attributed to a legendary physician. The texts, transmitted under his name, blend medical and magical lore and survive in multiple medieval manuscripts, suggesting a complex tradition of attribution.
author_notes: The name 'Thessalus' may refer to a legendary or semi-legendary author; the corpus consists of five works on herbal virtues, likely pseudepigrapha. The agent kind is person, but historicity is legendary due to lack of secure biographical evidence.
representative_titles: De virtutibus herbarum (e cod. Paris. gr. 2502 + Vindob. med. gr. 23) | De virtutibus herbarum (e cod. Monac. 542) | De virtutibus herbarum (e cod. Matrit. Bibl. Nat. 4631 olim 110) | De virtutibus herbarum (e codd. Paris. gr. 2502 + Vindob. med. gr. 23 + | De virtutibus herbarum (e codd. Paris. gr. 2256 + Venet. App. cl. V 13 + Mosq. 415)

author_bio: Theudotus is known from a single inscription (titulus) comprising two words. Nothing else is recorded about his life or works; he may have been a minor poet or dedicant.
author_notes: The author heading represents a specific individual attested by an inscribed text; the corpus is minimal.

author_bio: Thomas Magister was a Byzantine monk and scholar, known for his Atticist lexicon 'Ecloga nominum et verborum Atticorum', a key work in the revival of Attic Greek.
author_notes: The name 'Magister' is a Latinized title indicating his role as a teacher.
representative_titles: Ecloga nominum et verborum Atticorum | Poemata de Arato Dub.

author_bio: Thrasyalces is a scarcely known figure from the early Hellenistic period, surviving only through a few testimonia. His exact field (philosophy, medicine, or rhetoric) remains unclear.
author_notes: Very obscure; only testimonia survive; possibly a philosopher or physician.

author_bio: The Thrasybuli Epistula is a letter transmitted under the name of the Athenian general Thrasybulus, likely a later composition.
author_notes: Source label uses the title as author heading. Authenticity is highly doubtful; may be a Hellenistic or Imperial forgery.

author_bio: Thrasyllus was a grammarian and philosopher active in Alexandria and later in Rome under Tiberius. He is best known for his edition and arrangement of Plato's dialogues, though his own written output survives only in fragments.
author_notes: Attribution is based on later testimonia; no complete work survives. The fragments are brief and scattered.

author_bio: Thrasymachus of Chalcedon was a prominent Sophist and rhetorician of the 5th century BCE, known for his contributions to rhetorical theory and his role in Plato's Republic. Only fragments and testimonia survive.
author_notes: Thrasymachus is a well-known figure from Platonic dialogues and later rhetorical sources; the surviving fragments and testimonia are collected.

author_bio: Thucydides, the Athenian historian, wrote the History of the Peloponnesian War, a foundational work of analytical historiography. His narrative style and inquiry into political and military causation have defined the Western historical tradition.
author_notes: The undoubted historical figure; standard TLG source.

author_bio: Thugenides was a Hellenistic comic poet, known only from fragmentary remains. His work likely belongs to New Comedy.
author_notes: Only fragments survive, some possibly misattributed.

author_bio: Tiberius is known for his rhetorical treatise 'De figuris Demosthenicis', which analyzes the figures of speech used by Demosthenes. Little else is known about his life.
author_notes: The name Tiberius alone is ambiguous; the author may be a rhetorician of the Augustan period, possibly identical with or distinct from the emperor Tiberius. The TLG assigns this tag to the work, not the emperor.

author_bio: Timachidas was a Hellenistic Greek author known primarily from fragments and inscriptions (tituli). His works survive only in scant quotations and epigraphic sources.
author_notes: Author identity is uncertain; source ID tlg1732 represents a fragmentary corpus of uncertain attribution. No secure biographical details are available.

author_bio: Timaeus Praxidas is known from a single fragment; the name may indicate a genealogical or local historian, but no secure identification is possible.
author_notes: Attested only in fragment; double name suggests possible patronymic/ethnic; historicity uncertain.

author_bio: Timaeus of Tauromenium was a Greek historian active in the late 4th and early 3rd centuries BCE. Exiled from Sicily, he wrote a massive 'History' of Sicily and the western Greeks down to 264 BCE, and a work on the Pyrrhic War. His work survives only in fragments, but he was a major source for later historians like Diodorus Siculus and Polybius.
author_notes: The epithet 'Hist.' distinguishes Timaeus the historian from other Timaeus figures (e.g., the philosopher). His fragments are collected in FGrHist 566.

author_bio: A Greek philosopher known from a handful of fragments and a spurious title. The designation 'Phil.' indicates his philosophical activity, possibly within the Platonic or Pythagorean tradition. The testimonia and fragments offer only glimpses of his thought; the title 'Fragmenta et titulus Sp.' suggests a contested attribution.
author_notes: The tag 'Sp.' in the representative titles indicates a spurious or uncertain attribution. The author's identity is ambiguous; the name Timaeus is well-known from Plato's dialogue, but this figure is distinct.

author_bio: Late antique sophist and grammarian known for a Platonic lexicon preserved in a single manuscript (Coislin. 345). The work collects and explains words from Plato's dialogues.
author_notes: The parenthetical '(Sophista Gramm.)' indicates the label used in TLG to distinguish this Timaeus from other homonyms. His historicity is plausible but not fully certain.

author_bio: Greek historian of uncertain origin; wrote works on Hellenistic dynasties, surviving only in fragments.
author_notes: Identification is uncertain; may be confused with other homonymous figures.

author_bio: Timagetus is an obscure ancient author, known only from fragments of prose. His historicity is uncertain and he may be a legendary or misattributed name.
author_notes: Very little is known about this author; the name appears in fragmentary sources.

author_bio: Timagoras is a shadowy figure of Greek literature, known only through a handful of fragments. Nothing certain can be said about his life or output.
author_notes: Only fragments survive; name may refer to more than one person.

author_bio: Timesitheus is known only from a few Greek inscriptions (tituli), likely a Roman military or administrative figure under the Severan dynasty. The epigraphic record provides minimal biographical data.
author_notes: The TLG includes Timesitheus as an author based on epigraphic texts (tituli) of uncertain authorship. He may be a historical individual who composed the inscribed texts, but the attribution is conventional. Word count is extremely low.

author_bio: Timocles was a poet of Middle Comedy in Athens, known from fragmentary remains. His plays survive only in quotations by later authors, offering glimpses into the comedic and social themes of the period.
author_notes: The label 'Comic.' disambiguates from other authors named Timocles. Surviving fragments are sparse, so the biography is reconstructed from contextual evidence.

author_bio: Greek tragic poet of the 4th century BCE, known from a single fragment. The epithet 'Trag.' distinguishes him from the comic poet of the same name.
author_notes: Only one fragment (34 words) survives; the attribution to tragedy is based on the source label. Little else can be confirmed.

author_cultural_context: Greek lyric poetry, symposium, Persian Wars
author_bio: Greek lyric poet and composer of skolia, known for his bitter rivalry with Themistocles. Fragments survive of his invective and sympotic verse.
author_notes: Historically attested figure; fragmentary corpus.

author_bio: Hellenistic historian known only from a surviving fragment.
author_notes: Only one fragment survives; little is known of this historian.

author_bio: A known only from a single fragment; likely a rhetorician or sophist, but no biographical details survive.
author_notes: Very meager remains; identification as a rhetor is based on the label in the source.

author_bio: Timon of Phlius was a Greek philosopher and poet, a follower of Pyrrho, whose satirical 'Silloi' lampooned dogmatic philosophers. Fragments preserve his skeptical verse.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical figure in the Pyrrhonian tradition.

author_bio: Timonax is a Greek author of whom only fragments survive; his identity and date are unknown.
author_notes: Only fragments are extant; no biographical information available.

author_bio: Timonides is a fragmentary Greek author known only from a single surviving fragment. Neither his identity nor his literary output can be securely characterized.
author_notes: The source provides no indication of genre or date; name may be confused with other individuals.

author_bio: Timostratus is an obscure author known from a few fragments and titles; he may be a comic or epic poet, but nothing certain is known.
author_notes: Only three works are attested, comprising two fragments and some titles; the name is too sparse for confident period or genre assignment.

author_bio: Timotheus is a known comic poet from the classical period, whose work survives only in fragments. Though little is known of his life, his fragments attest to his participation in the comic tradition.
author_notes: The label 'Comic.' in TLG identifies Timotheus as a comic poet; attribution is secure but biographical details are sparse.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian known from a few excerpts on animal lore and syntax, preserved in Byzantine manuscripts.
author_notes: The parenthetical 'Gramm.' distinguishes this grammarian from the more famous poet Timotheus of Miletus.
representative_titles: Excerpta ex libris de animalibus (e cod. Paris. gr. 2422) | Κανόνες καθολικοὶ περὶ συντάξεως (e cod. Coislin. 387)

author_bio: Timotheus (the historian) wrote historical fragments, possibly of local or chronicle nature. Little is known of his life or specific works.
author_notes: The designation 'Hist.' distinguishes this Timotheus from other homonyms; surviving fragments are minimal.

author_bio: A celebrated poet and musician from Miletus, known for his dithyrambic and lyric poetry. He is associated with the 'New Music' movement and is attested as active in Athens and Macedonia. The surviving fragments and titles provide insight into his innovative style.
author_notes: The designation 'Lyr.' distinguishes him from other authors named Timotheus. Historical attestations include references in Aristotle and later grammarians.

author_bio: Timotheus is a tragedian of the Greek Hellenistic period, known only from an inscription or citation recording titles of his plays; no literary fragments survive.
author_notes: The '(Trag.)' disambiguator distinguishes this Timotheus from the lyric poet; evidence is minimal.

author_cultural_context: Ancient Greek (possibly author of an inscription)
author_bio: Timoxenus is an obscure Greek author known only from a single surviving inscription (titulus). Almost nothing is known of his life or works.
author_notes: Extremely sparse evidence; only a single word count fragment.

author_cultural_context: Lost epic poem of the Greek Epic Cycle; attributed to Eumelus of Corinth or, later, to Arctinus of Miletus.
author_bio: The 'Titanomachia' is a lost archaic Greek epic that narrated the war between the Titans and the Olympian gods. It formed part of the Epic Cycle. Only fragmentary quotations and a few lines survive.
author_notes: The source label uses the title of the poem as the author entry; the real author is unknown, though ancient attribution points to Eumelus or Arctinus.

author_bio: Tragica Adespota is a conventional label for anonymous fragments of Greek tragedy whose authorship cannot be assigned to a known playwright. The corpus preserves lines from lost plays, offering valuable evidence for the tragic genre outside the canonical three.
author_notes: Source (TLG 1738) lists three works containing 17,954 words of anonymous tragic fragments. The heading 'Tragica Adespota' is standard for this type of material; it does not represent a person or group but rather a corpus of unattributed texts.

author_bio: Troilus was a Late Antique Greek rhetorician and grammarian, known for his prolegomena to Hermogenes' art of rhetoric.
author_notes: Sometimes conflated with other homonyms; the epithet 'Grammaticus' is implied.
representative_titles: Prolegomena in Hermogenis artem rhetoricam | Epigramma (auctore Troilo Grammatico)

author_bio: Trophonius is a mythological figure, a hero and oracle-giver in Boeotian mythology. A text on rhetoric is attributed to him, likely a spurious ascription reflecting his legendary status.
author_notes: Attribution to the hero Trophonius is clearly legendary; the work is likely pseudepigraphal or misattributed.

author_bio: Tryphiodorus, sometimes identified with Triphiodorus of Panopolis, was a Greek epic poet who composed the 'Sack of Troy' (Ἅλωσις Ἰλίου), a hexameter poem describing the fall of Troy. His work survives intact in a single medieval manuscript.
author_notes: Name appears with variant spellings (Triphiodorus). Conventional identification with the poet of the Ilias Persis is stable.

author_bio: Tryphon I was an Alexandrian grammarian active in the 1st century BCE, known for works on tropes and linguistic pathology. His writings were influential in later grammatical and rhetorical studies.
author_notes: Distinguished from a later homonym (Tryphon II). The fragments include both original treatises and later epitomes.

author_bio: Tryphon II was a Greek grammarian whose work on tropes (De tropis) was transmitted under the name of Gregory of Corinth. He contributed to the systematization of rhetorical figures and remained influential in Byzantine scholarship.
author_notes: Attribution to Tryphon II is based on manuscript evidence; the text was formerly assigned to Gregory of Corinth, but modern scholarship identifies Tryphon as the likely author.
representative_titles: De tropis (olim sub auctore Gregorio Corinthio)

author_bio: Tynnichus of Chalcis is a shadowy Greek poet known from a single brief fragment, preserved as a citation. He is sometimes associated with the dithyrambic tradition, though almost nothing is known of his life.
author_notes: Single-word fragment (3 words); attribution and historicity uncertain due to sparse evidence.

author_bio: A Greek grammarian and textual critic active in Rome, a student of Hestiaeus and a teacher of notable figures; his works survive only in fragments dealing with Homeric language and dialect forms.
author_notes: Often cited in later scholia; the fragments are collected and studied by classical scholars.

author_cultural_context: Greek grammarian tradition; associated with the school of Aristarchus.
author_bio: Tyrannion Junior was a Greek grammarian active during the late Hellenistic or early Roman period, known for his work on Homeric criticism and textual scholarship. His fragments survive mostly in later scholia and lexica.
author_notes: The designation 'Junior Gramm.' distinguishes him from the elder Tyrannion; the source label is ambiguous, and attribution of specific fragments is debated.
