author_bio: Tyrtaeus was a Spartan elegiac poet whose martial verses, the Eunomia, exhorted citizens to bravery and shaped Spartan civic identity.
author_notes: Traditionally associated with Sparta; fragments preserved in quotations by later authors.

author_bio: A Greek rhetorician and commentator, known for a set of prolegomena to Demosthenes' orations. His identity is sometimes confused with the jurist Ulpian, but this is a distinct figure active in the rhetorical schools of the Eastern Roman Empire.
author_notes: Likely a historical teacher of rhetoric; biographical details are sparse and partly inferred from the manuscript tradition.
representative_titles: Prolegomena in Demosthenis orationes Olynthiacas et Philippicas

author_bio: Uranius is a little-known Greek author, possibly a historian or geographer, of whom only fragments survive. The name may indicate Syrian origin, but evidence is insufficient for a secure identification.
author_notes: The author is known only from fragmentary citations; dates and background are uncertain.

author_bio: Favorinus was a Greek sophist, philosopher, and orator from Gaul, active in Rome during the Hadrianic period. A prolific writer, his surviving fragments cover rhetoric, philosophy, and miscellany.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments from numerous works survive, demonstrating his intellectual breadth.

author_bio: An author or compiler of imperial novellae (novels) and theological edicts, often identified with a high-ranking official or jurist in the late Roman/Byzantine court, possibly the same as Flavius Anthemius?
author_notes: The name 'Flavius' is ambiguous; it may refer to a single person or a composite figure. The corpus includes legal and theological texts, partly from the reign of Justinian.
representative_titles: Novellae | Contra monophysitas | Epistula contra tria capitula | Edictum contra Origenem | Edictum rectae fidei | Constitutio contra Anthimum, Severum, Petrum et Zooram | Epistula ad synodum de Theodoro Mopsuesteno et reliquis | Epistula ad synodum de Origene

author_bio: A Jewish historian and diplomat, born Joseph ben Matityahu, who authored the Antiquitates Judaicae, Bellum Judaicum, and Contra Apionem. His works are crucial sources for Second Temple Judaism and the Jewish War against Rome.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; all works securely attributed.
representative_titles: Antiquitates Judaicae | De bello Judaico libri vii | Contra Apionem (= De Judaeorum vetustate) | Josephi vita

author_bio: A compilation of excerpts attributed to Cyril of Alexandria and other Church Fathers, assembled for doctrinal use. No single author.
author_notes: The heading represents the text corpus itself, not a person or collective. Compilation date and origin are uncertain.

author_bio: Fragmenta Adespota is a collective label for unattributed Greek fragments that cannot be assigned to a known author.
author_notes: Explicitly anonymous; 'Adespota' means 'ownerless' in Greek.

author_bio: A corpus of Greek alchemical fragments, including papyri and manuscript excerpts, attributed variously to legendary or historical authors. The collection represents practical and theoretical texts on metallurgy, dyeing, and transmutation.
author_notes: The author field is used for a textual corpus; the label 'Fragmenta Alchemica' is a modern scholarly categorization.
representative_titles: Tractatus alchemicus (fragmenta) (P. Holm.) | Περὶ τῆς τιμιωτάτης καὶ πολυφήμου χρυσοχοϊκῆς (e cod. | Tractatus alchemicus (fragmenta) (P. author_cultural_context: Early Christianity; apostolic literature; Roman church
author_bio: Hermas, a 2nd-century Christian figure in Rome, is the author of 'The Shepherd of Hermas', an apocalyptic and visionary work that was widely read in the early church. Likely a freedman from a Greek-speaking background, his text provides a rare window into lay Christian practice, repentance, and ecclesiology in the post-apostolic period. Multiple fragments from papyri and catenae attest to the work's circulation.
author_notes: Attested in early Christian sources (Muratorian Canon, Irenaeus); the name 'Hermas' is considered that of a historical author, though legendary details appear in later tradition.
representative_titles: Pastor | Fragmenta (ap. Antiochum) | Fragmentum in F (cod. Paris. gr. 1143) | Fragmenta (P. Oxy. 3.404)
